topics.title: Definitions - Robots, Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions - Robots, Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🤖 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of a Robot

The term **Robot** has evolved through decades of technological progress, and consequently, multiple standard bodies and authors have formalized its definition. A robot is best understood as a **reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator** designed to move materials, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Approved Standard Definition (Robot Institute of America - RIA, 1980):**
> *"A robot is a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks."*

### Supplementary Authoritative Definitions

| **Source / Body** | **Definition** |
|---|---|
| **Webster's Dictionary** | An automatic device that performs functions ordinarily ascribed to human beings. |
| **ISO 8373:2021** | An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications. |
| **Merriam-Webster (Modern)** | A machine that resembles a living creature in being capable of moving independently and performing complex actions (e.g., grasping and locomotion). |
| **Karel Čapek (Originator, 1921)** | Derived from the Czech word *robota* meaning "forced labor" or "drudgery." |

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Robotics):**
> Robotics is the **interdisciplinary branch of mechanical engineering, electrical engineering, electronic engineering, and computer science** that deals with the design, construction, operation, and application of robots, as well as computer systems for their control, sensory feedback, and information processing.

The word **Robotics** was coined by the science-fiction author **Isaac Asimov** in his 1941 short story *"Liar!"* and was popularized in his 1950 book *"I, Robot."*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Robot like a Highly Disciplined Apprentice Chef in a Kitchen:**
> - A robot is like a **versatile apprentice** in a restaurant. The apprentice can be reassigned (reprogrammed) from making pasta to baking bread without replacing the entire person.
> - Just as the apprentice has hands (end-effector), arms (manipulator), eyes (sensors), and a brain (controller), a robot has analogous components that mirror human functionality.
> - **Robotics**, on the other hand, is the **entire culinary school** — the science of training, equipping, and managing such apprentices. It includes the curriculum (kinematics, dynamics), the tools (controllers, actuators), and the safety rules (Asimov's Laws).
> - A single chef in the kitchen = **A Robot**. The science of operating a fleet of smart kitchens = **Robotics**.

## 1.4 Key Characteristics that Define a Modern Robot

For a machine to be formally classified as a robot, it must satisfy most of the following criteria:

1. **Sensing** — The ability to perceive its environment through sensors (vision, force, proximity).
2. **Movement** — The ability to move in its environment (locomotion or manipulation) — typically **≥ 3 degrees of freedom (DOF)**.
3. **Energy** — The ability to convert stored energy into physical action.
4. **Intelligence** — The ability to make decisions based on sensory input and pre-programmed logic.
5. **Autonomy** — Some degree of independent task execution without continuous human intervention.
6. **Reprogrammability** — Can be re-tasked for new operations without physical alteration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A washing machine or a remote-controlled toy car is **NOT** a robot, because they lack reprogrammability and multifunctionality across variable programmed tasks. Be cautious in exam definitions!

## 1.5 Isaac Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics ⚖️

A landmark conceptual foundation laid in 1942, often tested in KTU examinations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law 0 (Added Later):** A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm.
>
> **Law 1:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
>
> **Law 2:** A robot must obey orders given by human beings except where such orders would conflict with the First Law.
>
> **Law 3:** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Word "Robot" — Etymological Breakdown

The understanding of the term requires tracing its origin:

- **Slavic Root:** The word originates from the Slavic root *rab-*, meaning "slave" or "servant."
- **Czech Derivative:** *Robota* = "forced labor," "drudgery," or "compulsory service."
- **First Usage:** Introduced by **Karel Čapek** in his 1920 play *R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)* — though he credited his brother **Josef Čapek** for the suggestion.
- **Science-Fiction Coining:** *Robotics* (the discipline) was coined by **Isaac Asimov** in 1941, deriving it from *robot* using the classical suffix *-ics* (denoting a field of study).

## 2.2 Distinguishing "Robot" vs "Robotics" — A Critical KTU Distinction

| **Parameter** | **Robot** | **Robotics** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | A physical, tangible machine/device. | An academic discipline / field of study. |
| **Scope** | Refers to a single entity. | Refers to the entire science and engineering ecosystem. |
| **Components** | Hardware + software integration. | Theory (kinematics, dynamics, AI) + practice. |
| **Time Dimension** | Static entity once manufactured. | Evolving, research-driven field. |
| **Example** | A 6-DOF articulated arm welding car chassis. | The branch of ME/EE/CS that designs, builds, and programs such arms. |
| **Analogy** | A *piano*. | The *science of music* and instrument-making. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet (Definition Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is foundational (no heavy numerical analysis), the "Formula Sheet" here is a **Conceptual Cheat-Sheet** of board-tested facts.

| **Concept** | **Key Statement** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| **RIA Definition Year** | **1980** | Most cited KTU definition. |
| **Word Origin** | Karel Čapek, play *R.U.R.* (**1920**) | Trivia question favorite. |
| **Term "Robotics" Coined** | Isaac Asimov, short story *"Liar!"* (**1941**) | Etymology. |
| **Minimum Axes (ISO 8373)** | **≥ 3 axes** | Distinguishes robots from fixed automation. |
| **First Industrial Robot** | **Unimate**, 1961, General Motors (NJ, USA) | Historical milestone. |
| **First Unimate Designer** | **George Devol** and **Joseph Engelberger** | Father of Robotics. |
| **Asimov's Laws Year** | **1942** (*Runaround* short story) | KTU 2-mark favorite. |
| **Sensors in a Robot** | Vision, Force/Torque, Proximity, Tactile, Encoders | 5 marks elaboration expected. |
| **Reprogrammability** | The defining feature per RIA | Definition keyword. |

## 2.4 Why Definitions Matter in Engineering Practice

> [!TIP]
> The formal definitions are not academic pedantry — they directly inform:
> 1. **Legal & Safety Standards** — Workplace safety laws (e.g., OSHA) use the RIA definition to determine which machines require robotic safeguards.
> 2. **Patent Classification** — The *International Patent Classification (IPC B25J)* uses ISO 8373 criteria to decide what qualifies as a "robot patent."
> 3. **Trade & Tariff Codes** — Customs duties for industrial robots vs. CNC machines are decided based on these definitions.
> 4. **Research Funding** — Government grants (DST, SERB in India) classify "robotics research" using these boundaries.
> 5. **Insurance** — Coverage for "robotic accidents" hinges on whether the device legally meets the robot definition.

## 2.5 Brief Classification Snapshot (Foundation for Later Modules)

A foundational classification helpful before diving deeper:

- **By Application:** Industrial robot, Service robot, Medical robot, Military robot, Space robot, Domestic robot.
- **By Mechanical Structure:** Articulated, Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, SCARA, Delta/Parallel.
- **By Mobility:** Fixed-base, Wheeled mobile, Legged, Aerial (drone), Underwater (AUV/ROV).
- **By Autonomy Level:** Teleoperated, Semi-autonomous, Fully autonomous, Intelligent/Cognitive.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Deconstructing the Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is **conceptual and definitional**, so the "derivation" is replaced by a structured **analytical deconstruction matrix** — the standard KTU examiner's approach for breaking down definition-based 14-mark questions.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Deconstruction of the RIA Definition

The KTU board's valuation pattern requires students to **unpack** the RIA definition and explain each keyword:

**Step 1 — Identify the Core Subject**

$$M_{\text{robot}} \subseteq \{\text{Programmable, Multifunctional, Manipulator}\}$$

The RIA definition is built on three foundational pillars. We extract them:

**Step 2 — Decode the Keyword "Reprogrammable"**

> *Reprogrammable* means the operating instructions of the device can be **altered without physical modification** of the machine itself.

Logical Formulation:

$$\text{If } P = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n\} \text{ are the set of programs,}$$
$$\text{then } \exists \; p_i, p_j \in P \quad \text{such that} \quad p_i \neq p_j \; \text{and} \; M(p_i) \neq M(p_j)$$

where $M$ is the machine's task function. This guarantees that a single robot can perform *Task A* today and *Task B* tomorrow with only software changes.

**Step 3 — Decode the Keyword "Multifunctional"**

The robot must be capable of executing **more than one distinct category of task** (e.g., welding *and* material handling *and* assembly). A device doing only one fixed job is a *special-purpose machine*, not a robot.

$$\text{Tasks} = \{T_1, T_2, T_3, \dots, T_k\}, \quad k \geq 2$$

**Step 4 — Decode the Keyword "Manipulator"**

A manipulator is the mechanical arm structure with a series of **joints and links** that physically moves and orients payloads.

$$\text{DOF}_{\min} = 3 \quad \text{(per ISO 8373:2021 standard)}$$

**Step 5 — Decode "Variable Programmed Motions"**

The motion profile is **not fixed**; it can be parameterized and dynamically changed at runtime.

$$X(t) = f(p_i, t), \quad \text{where } p_i \in P \text{ (the active program)}$$

This means trajectory $X(t)$ is a *function* of both the active program and time.

**Step 6 — Decode "Performance of a Variety of Tasks"**

This is the **outcome clause** — the manipulator's actions result in accomplishing diverse jobs in industrial or service settings.

## 3.2 The Complete Analytical Matrix for KTU Board Valuation

| **Element of Definition** | **Engineering Meaning** | **Example** | **Examiner's Cue Word** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reprogrammable | Software-level re-tasking | Teach pendant re-programming a FANUC arm. | "Can task be changed without rebuilding?" |
| Multifunctional | Multiple task categories | Welding + Painting + Pick & Place. | "≥ 2 distinct job types." |
| Manipulator | Mechanical arm with joints | 6-DOF articulated arm. | "Joint-link chain." |
| Variable Programmed Motions | Parameterized trajectory | Circular, linear, spline paths. | "Trajectory, not fixed." |
| Variety of Tasks | Practical use-case diversity | Automotive assembly line. | "Real-world deployment." |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Logical Argumentation: "Is a CNC Machine a Robot?"

A classic KTU trick question. Here is the analytical walkthrough:

> **Step 1:** A CNC (Computer Numerical Control) machine has reprogrammable instructions — ✅ matches.
> **Step 2:** A CNC machine is multifunctional (can mill, drill, tap) — ✅ matches.
> **Step 3:** A CNC machine is a *fixed-purpose machining center*, not a **manipulator** — ❌ does NOT match.
> **Step 4:** Its motion is constrained to its own work envelope, not a free manipulator moving parts/tools through space.
> **Step 5:** **Conclusion:** Per ISO 8373:2021 and RIA, a CNC is **not a robot** — it is *fixed automation*. Robots are designed to move tools/parts *through space*, not have the work brought to them.

## 3.4 Logical Proof of Asimov's Law Hierarchy

Asimov's laws have a **strict priority order**. We can express this logically:

$$\text{If Law 1 is violated} \Rightarrow \text{Action is forbidden, regardless of Law 2 or 3.}$$

$$\text{If Law 1 is not violated and Law 2 is violated} \Rightarrow \text{Action is forbidden.}$$

$$\text{If Laws 1 and 2 are not violated} \Rightarrow \text{Law 3 may be honored.}$$

The relationship can be expressed in decision-precedence notation:

$$\text{Priority} = L_0 > L_1 > L_2 > L_3$$

This is a **nested conditional** — Law 1 always gates the lower laws.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Foundational Taxonomy of Robotics (Functional Architecture Flow)

Since this is a definitional topic, the diagram below maps the **conceptual hierarchy of Robotics as a discipline** and the **structural anatomy of a Robot as a system**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Robotics as an Interdisciplinary Science"] --> nodeB["Mechanical Engineering Domain"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Electrical Engineering Domain"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Computer Science Domain"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Cognitive Science Domain"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["Manipulator Design"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Kinematics and Dynamics"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["End Effector Design"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["Actuator Systems"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Sensor Integration"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Drive Electronics"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["Control Algorithms"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Path Planning"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Artificial Intelligence"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["Machine Vision"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Speech Recognition"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["Human Robot Interaction"]

    nodeA --> nodeF["Application of Robotics"]
    nodeF --> nodeF1["Industrial Automation"]
    nodeF --> nodeF2["Medical Robotics"]
    nodeF --> nodeF3["Service and Domestic"]
    nodeF --> nodeF4["Space and Defense"]
    nodeF --> nodeF5["Exploration and Research"]
```

## 4.2 Robot System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeI["Human Operator"] -->|Commands| nodeG["Controller CPU"]
    nodeG -->|Motion Signals| nodeH["Drive System"]
    nodeH -->|Power Signals| nodeJ["Actuators"]
    nodeJ -->|Mechanical Motion| nodeK["Manipulator Arm"]
    nodeK -->|Physical Interaction| nodeL["End Effector"]
    nodeL -->|Task Execution| nodeM["Workspace Environment"]

    nodeM -->|Feedback Stimuli| nodeN["Sensor Array"]
    nodeN -->|Sensor Data| nodeG
    nodeN -->|Force Vision Data| nodeI

    nodeK -.->|Position Velocity| nodeN
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Does a Device Qualify as a Robot?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Device Under Evaluation"] --> nodeQ1{"Is it reprogrammable?"}
    nodeQ1 -->|No| nodeNo["Not a Robot. Classified as Fixed Automation."]
    nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodeQ2{"Is it multifunctional?"}
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeNo
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeQ3{"Does it have a manipulator structure?"}
    nodeQ3 -->|No| nodeNo
    nodeQ3 -->|Yes| nodeQ4{"Does it have at least 3 programmable axes?"}
    nodeQ4 -->|No| nodeNo
    nodeQ4 -->|Yes| nodeQ5["Qualifies as a Robot per ISO 8373 and RIA"]
```

> [!TIP]
> This decision flow is an excellent **flowchart answer** that KTU examiners love to see in 14-mark definition questions. Drawing this in your answer sheet can earn you **2–3 bonus marks** for clarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a robot as per the Robot Institute of America (RIA). List any two key features that distinguish a robot from conventional fixed automation.**

**Model Answer:**

A robot, as per the **Robot Institute of America (1980)**, is defined as:

> *"A reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks."*

**Two distinguishing features vs. fixed automation:**

1. **Reprogrammability:** A robot can be re-tasked via software changes (e.g., teach pendant) without any mechanical reconfiguration, whereas a fixed-purpose machine requires physical retooling.
2. **Multifunctionality & Variable Motions:** A single robot can perform multiple task categories (welding, painting, assembly) through variable programmed trajectories, whereas conventional fixed automation performs one dedicated operation only.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain Isaac Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

The Three Laws of Robotics, formulated by **Isaac Asimov in his 1942 short story *Runaround***, are as follows:

1. **Law 1:** A robot may not injure a human being, or through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
2. **Law 2:** A robot must obey orders given by human beings, except where such orders conflict with Law 1.
3. **Law 3:** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with Laws 1 or 2.

A **zeroth law** was added later: *A robot may not harm humanity as a whole, or through inaction, allow humanity to come to harm.* These laws form a **strict priority hierarchy** ($L_0 > L_1 > L_2 > L_3$) that governs the ethical decision-making of autonomous robotic systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### ✦ **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**"Robots and Robotics are often used interchangeably, but they refer to fundamentally different concepts." Discuss in detail with suitable examples. Also explain the historical evolution of the term with key milestones.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Clearly distinguish between a 'Robot' and 'Robotics' with the help of a comparative table and real-world examples.*

**Model Solution:**

| **Basis of Comparison** | **Robot** | **Robotics** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A physical, reprogrammable manipulator. | The science and engineering of designing robots. |
| **Nature** | A tangible machine/system. | An academic discipline. |
| **Disciplines Involved** | Mechanical + Electrical + Software integration. | ME + EE + CS + AI + Cognitive Science + Mathematics. |
| **Scope** | Narrow — refers to a specific machine. | Broad — covers theory, design, control, and application. |
| **Analogy** | A *piano* in a concert hall. | The *science of musicology*. |
| **Real-World Example** | KUKA KR 1000 Titan (6-axis industrial arm). | The research field at Carnegie Mellon's Robotics Institute. |

**Examples for clarity:**
- A **Boston Dynamics Spot** (quadruped robot) is a *robot*; the research area that designs quadruped locomotion algorithms is *robotics*.
- A **da Vinci Surgical System** is a *robot*; the field of robot-assisted minimally invasive surgery is *robotics*.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Trace the historical evolution of the term 'Robot' and 'Robotics' with at least five key milestones.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **1920 — Karel Čapek's Play *R.U.R.*:** The word *robot* (from Czech *robota*, "forced labor") is introduced, describing artificial laborers called *Robots*.
2. **1941 — Isaac Asimov Coins 'Robotics':** In his short story *Liar!*, Asimov first uses the word *robotics* to denote the study of robots.
3. **1942 — Asimov's Three Laws:** Published in *Runaround*, providing the first ethical framework.
4. **1954 — George Devol Patents the Unimate:** The first digitally programmable robot arm, considered the ancestor of all industrial robots.
5. **1961 — Unimate Installed at General Motors (USA):** The first industrial robot deployed on a production line, performing die-casting handling.
6. **1968 — Shakey the Robot (SRI International):** The first mobile robot with reasoning capability, a precursor to modern AI-driven robotics.
7. **1979 — SCARA Robot (Hirata / Sankyo):** Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm developed in Japan for precision assembly.

---

### ✦ **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**"The formal definition of a robot has evolved over decades, and each definition serves a specific engineering or legal purpose." Critically analyze the various definitions of a robot given by RIA, ISO, and Webster, and discuss their practical significance.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Compare the RIA (1980), ISO 8373:2021, and Webster definitions of a robot. Highlight the differences and explain why multiple definitions coexist.*

**Model Solution:**

| **Aspect** | **RIA (1980)** | **ISO 8373:2021)** | **Webster** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Engineering functionality. | Standardization and classification. | Linguistic/general. |
| **Minimum Axes** | Not specified explicitly. | **≥ 3 axes** (explicit). | Not specified. |
| **Reprogrammability** | Explicitly stated. | Explicitly stated. | Implied via "automatic." |
| **Application Domain** | Industrial. | Industrial automation. | General (any). |
| **Formality** | Industry consortium. | International standards body. | Dictionary. |

**Why multiple definitions coexist:**
- **RIA:** Serves the *manufacturing industry's* operational needs.
- **ISO:** Provides *global, legal-grade standardization* for trade, patents, and safety regulation.
- **Webster:** Captures the *cultural and linguistic* perception of robots in everyday life.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Discuss the practical engineering significance of having a formal, standardized definition of a robot. Provide at least four real-world implications.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Workplace Safety Legislation (OSHA/ISO 10218):** Safety standards for robots (e.g., fencing, speed limits, emergency stops) are legally triggered only for devices meeting the ISO robot definition. Without a formal definition, safety regulations cannot be enforced.
2. **Patent Law & Intellectual Property:** Patent offices use the ISO 8373 criteria to grant or reject "robot patents." A precise definition is essential for the *International Patent Classification (IPC B25J)*.
3. **Trade & Customs Classification:** Industrial robots attract different tariff codes than CNC machines or general automation. Customs officials rely on the formal definition to apply the correct duties.
4. **Research Funding & Academic Classification:** Government funding agencies (DST, NSF, EU Horizon) classify "robotics research" using these definitions, determining which projects qualify for grants.
5. **Insurance and Liability:** Insurance claims for robotic accidents are adjudicated based on whether the device legally qualifies as a "robot" under prevailing standards, affecting liability and coverage.
6. **Performance Benchmarking:** Standardized definitions enable apples-to-apples comparison of robot specifications (payload, reach, repeatability) across manufacturers (FANUC, KUKA, ABB, Yaskawa).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Students 3–5 Marks:**
>
> 1. **Quoting the wrong year for the RIA definition** — it is **1980**, not 1979 or 1981. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Attributing the word "robot" to Asimov** — Asimov coined **"robotics"**, not "robot." The latter was Karel Čapek (1920).
> 3. **Listing only 2 Asimov's Laws** — Always state **all three** (and mention Law 0 if asked).
> 4. **Calling a CNC machine a robot** — A CNC is **NOT a robot** per ISO 8373. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Writing definitions without examples** — Every definition in 14-mark answers **must be supported by at least 2 examples** (industrial + service) to score full marks.
> 6. **Skipping the reprogrammability clause** — This is the *single most important* keyword in the RIA definition; omitting it loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Definitions of Robots and Robotics:**
>
> - 🔑 **RIA Definition (1980):** Reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator, variable programmed motions, variety of tasks. *(Memorize verbatim.)*
> - 🔑 **ISO 8373:2021:** Adds the **≥ 3 axes** criterion explicitly.
> - 🔑 **Etymology:** *Robot* = Karel Čapek (1920, *R.U.R.*); *Robotics* = Isaac Asimov (1941, *"Liar!"*).
> - 🔑 **First Industrial Robot:** **Unimate**, 1961, by **George Devol & Joseph Engelberger**, at **General Motors**.
> - 🔑 **Asimov's Laws (1942):** Three laws + a Zeroth Law added later; strict priority hierarchy $L_0 > L_1 > L_2 > L_3$.
> - 🔑 **CNC ≠ Robot** — A CNC is fixed automation; a robot is a manipulator.
> - 🔑 **Six defining characteristics of a robot:** Sensing, Movement, Energy, Intelligence, Autonomy, Reprogrammability.
> - 🔑 **Robotics is interdisciplinary:** ME + EE + CS + AI + Cognitive Science + Mathematics.
> - 🔑 **Drawing a flow diagram or block schematic** in a 14-mark answer can fetch 2–3 extra marks.
> - 🔑 **Always cite the year** alongside the source (e.g., "RIA, 1980" or "Asimov, 1942") — this shows examiner awareness.
> - 🔑 **Applications domains to remember:** Industrial, Medical, Service, Space, Defense, Domestic, Exploration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Robots - Manipulators, Mobile Robots - Wheeled and Legged Robots, Aerial Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Robots - Manipulators, Mobile Robots - Wheeled and Legged Robots, Aerial Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Universal Definition of a Robot

According to the **Robot Institute of America (RIA, 1979)**, a robot is formally defined as:

> *"A reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks."*

A broader ISO/technical definition states that a robot is an **automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes**, which can be either fixed in place (manipulators) or mobile (wheeled, legged, aerial).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Classification of robots is a foundational topic. Examiners frequently test:
> - The exact classification hierarchy (manipulator vs. mobile vs. aerial).
> - Kinematic configurations and their degrees of freedom.
> - Suitability of robot type for specific applications (e.g., "Which robot for terrain exploration?").

---

## 1.2 Top-Level Classification of Robots

Robots can be classified along three major architectural axes:

| **S. No.** | **Primary Class** | **Sub-Classes** | **Typical Environment** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Manipulators (Industrial)** | Articulated, Cartesian, SCARA, Cylindrical, Spherical (Polar) | Structured factory floor |
| 2 | **Mobile Robots – Wheeled** | Differential Drive, Ackermann, Omnidirectional, Tracked | Indoor, paved surfaces |
| 3 | **Mobile Robots – Legged** | 1-leg (Hopper), 2-leg (Biped), 4-leg (Quadruped), 6-leg (Hexapod) | Rough, uneven terrain |
| 4 | **Aerial Robots (UAVs)** | Fixed-wing, Rotary-wing (Single/Multi-rotor), Hybrid VTOL | Open airspace, 3D environments |

---

## 1.3 Manipulators — Definition & Intuition

**Definition:** A manipulator is a robotic mechanism consisting of a **series of rigid links connected by joints**, anchored to a fixed base, whose end-effector is positioned and oriented in space by coordinated joint motions.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Think of a manipulator as a **mechanical human arm bolted to a workbench**. Just as your shoulder (joint 1), elbow (joint 2), and wrist (joint 3) work together to place your hand (end-effector) at any point in a 3D workspace, a manipulator uses serial or parallel kinematic chains to achieve the same goal. However, unlike your arm which has ~7 DOF, manipulators typically have **3 to 6 DOF**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** is the number of independent motions a robot can perform. A rigid body in 3D space needs exactly **6 DOF** (3 for position $\rightarrow$ x, y, z, and 3 for orientation $\rightarrow$ roll, pitch, yaw) to be fully positioned and oriented.

### 1.3.1 Geometric (Kinematic) Configurations of Manipulators

**1. Cartesian (Gantry / PPP) Robot**
- Three prismatic joints, mutually perpendicular.
- Workspace: rectangular box.
- Use: pick-and-place, CNC, 3D printers.

**2. Cylindrical (RPP) Robot**
- One revolute joint (base) + two prismatic joints.
- Workspace: hollow cylinder.

**3. Spherical / Polar (RRP) Robot**
- Two revolute + one prismatic joint.
- Workspace: portion of a sphere.

**4. Articulated (RRR) Robot**
- Three revolute joints (shoulder, elbow, wrist).
- Workspace: sphere-shaped, most dexterous.
- Example: **6-axis industrial arm** (KUKA, ABB, FANUC).

**5. SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm — RRP)**
- Two revolute joints in horizontal plane + one prismatic vertical.
- Compliant in X–Y, rigid in Z. Ideal for vertical assembly tasks (PCB insertion).

---

## 1.4 Mobile Robots — Wheeled Robots

**Definition:** A wheeled mobile robot (WMR) is a robot whose **locomotion is achieved through actuated wheels** in contact with the ground, without a fixed base.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a **remote-controlled toy car** — the wheels roll, the robot translates and rotates on the floor. Now add sensors and autonomy, and you have a WMR like a **warehouse AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle)** or a **Mars rover**.

### 1.4.1 Wheel Configurations (Locomotion Sub-Types)

**a) Differential Drive**
- Two independently driven wheels on a common axis.
- Turning: by differential wheel speeds.
- Example: Roomba, research platforms like Pioneer P3-DX.

**b) Ackermann Steering**
- Mimics car steering (4-wheel with steered front).
- Turning: governed by the Ackermann geometry.
- Example: Autonomous cars (Waymo, MIT's Talos).

**c) Synchronous (Omnidirectional) Drive**
- 3 or 4 mecanum/omni wheels allowing translation in any direction without reorientation.
- Example: KUKA youBot, RoboCup robots.

**d) Tracked (Skid-Steer)**
- Two continuous tracks (like tanks).
- Example: PackBot, search-and-rescue robots.

---

## 1.5 Mobile Robots — Legged Robots

**Definition:** Legged robots use a **discrete number of articulated limbs (legs)** for locomotion, mimicking biological gaits.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Compare a wheeled robot to a **roller-skater** (smooth surfaces only) and a legged robot to a **hiker** (can climb over rocks, stairs, debris). Legged robots trade speed and mechanical simplicity for **terrain adaptability**.

### 1.5.1 Static vs. Dynamic Stability
- **Static Stability (SS):** Robot remains stable even when motion stops (e.g., 4+ legs).
- **Dynamic Stability (DS):** Stability only during motion (e.g., 2-leg bipeds like ASIMO).

> [!NOTE]
> **Gruebler's Criterion for Legged Robot DOF (Planar):**
>
> $$F = 3(n-1) - 2j$$
>
> where $n$ = number of links, $j$ = number of lower-pair joints. For a 6-legged hexapod with 3 DOF per leg: $F = 18$ active DOF total.

### 1.5.2 Common Legged Configurations
- **1-leg (Hopper):** Dynamic only, saltatorial locomotion.
- **2-leg (Biped):** Humanoid robots, dynamic stability, complex control.
- **4-leg (Quadruped):** Static + dynamic stability. Examples: Boston Dynamics **Spot**, MIT Mini-Cheetah.
- **6-leg (Hexapod):** Best static stability, insect-like gaits. Example: BigDog's smaller siblings.
- **8-leg (Octopod):** Spider-inspired, maximum stability.

---

## 1.6 Aerial Robots (Unmanned Aerial Vehicles — UAVs)

**Definition:** Aerial robots are **unmanned aircraft systems** that fly autonomously or semi-autonomously using onboard control systems, sensors, and actuators.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a **robotic hummingbird** — small, agile, hovering, with rapid directional changes. Now scale this up to a **robotic airplane (drone)**, and you have the full range of aerial robots.

### 1.6.1 Categories of Aerial Robots

| **Type** | **Lift Mechanism** | **Hover Capability** | **Example** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Fixed-wing** | Aerodynamic (airfoil) | No | Survey drones, military UAVs (Predator) |
| **Single-rotor** | One main rotor + tail | Yes | Helicopter UAVs (Yamaha RMAX) |
| **Multi-rotor** | 3, 4, 6, 8 rotors | Yes | DJI Phantom, Quadcopter |
| **Hybrid VTOL** | Fixed + rotary combined | Yes | DARPA Tern, Lockheed Stalker |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manipulator Kinematics — The Mathematical Backbone

The position and orientation of a manipulator's end-effector is given by the **forward kinematics equation**:

$$
\mathbf{T}_n^0 = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{A}_i^{i-1}(q_i)
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{A}_i^{i-1}$ = homogeneous transformation matrix from frame $i-1$ to frame $i$.
- $q_i$ = joint variable (angle for revolute, displacement for prismatic).
- $n$ = number of joints.

For a revolute joint, the standard **Denavit–Hartenberg (D-H)** parameter matrix is:

$$
\mathbf{A}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For a prismatic joint, replace $\theta_i$ with $d_i$ as the variable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why D-H Parameters Matter in KTU:** Almost every manipulator question in the KTU paper asks for the **forward kinematic position equation** of a 2- or 3-DOF arm using the D-H method. Memorize the matrix and the 4 parameters $(\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i)$.

---

## 2.2 Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR) — Kinematic Model

### 2.2.1 Differential Drive Kinematics

For a robot with two wheels of radius $r$ separated by distance $L$ (wheelbase), the kinematic equations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) \\
\omega &= \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $v$ = linear velocity of robot center.
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of robot.
- $\omega_R, \omega_L$ = angular velocities of right and left wheels.
- $r$ = wheel radius.
- $L$ = distance between wheels.

The robot's **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** lies on the line through both wheels, and the radius of curvature is:

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Special Cases:**
> - If $\omega_R = \omega_L$: robot moves **straight** ($R \rightarrow \infty$).
> - If $\omega_R = -\omega_L$: robot **rotates in place** ($R = 0$).
> - If $\omega_L = 0$: robot rotates around the left wheel.

### 2.2.2 Ackermann Steering Geometry

For a car-like robot, the **Ackermann condition** ensures all wheels rotate about a common ICR:

$$
\cot \delta_f - \cot \delta_r = \frac{W}{L}
$$

where:
- $\delta_f, \delta_r$ = steering angles of front and rear wheels.
- $W$ = track width.
- $L$ = wheelbase.

For a front-steered, rear-fixed Ackermann vehicle:

$$
\tan \delta = \frac{L}{R}
$$

where $R$ is the turn radius. This geometric relationship prevents tire slip during turning.

### 2.2.3 Maneuverability vs. Controllability (Bullo & Murray's Theorem)

For a wheeled robot with $N$ wheels, the **degree of mobility** $M$ and **degree of steerability** $S$ satisfy:

$$
M + S \le 3
$$

and the **maneuverability** is $\delta_M = M + S$ (a robot is holonomic only if $\delta_M = 3$).

---

## 2.3 Legged Robots — Static Stability Criterion

For a statically stable multi-legged robot, the **Center of Gravity (CoG)** projection must lie **inside the support polygon** formed by the feet in contact with the ground.

For a hexapod walking in a **wave gait**, the minimum number of supporting legs is **3**, forming a tripod. The CoG projection condition:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{CoG} \in \text{ConvexHull}(\mathbf{x}_{f_1}, \mathbf{x}_{f_2}, \mathbf{x}_{f_3})
$$

The **Static Stability Margin (SSM)** is the minimum distance from the CoG projection to the nearest edge of the support polygon:

$$
\text{SSM} = \min_{i=1}^{N_{edges}} d(\mathbf{x}_{CoG}, \text{edge}_i)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Stability Margins:**
> - SSM $> 0$ : statically stable.
> - SSM $= 0$ : marginally stable.
> - SSM $< 0$ : statically unstable (requires dynamic balance, like bipedal walking).

---

## 2.4 Aerial Robots — Fundamental Flight Equations

### 2.4.1 Thrust and Lift Equations

For a **multi-rotor UAV** with $n$ rotors, total thrust must equal weight for hover:

$$
T_{total} = n \cdot T_i = m \cdot g
$$

where:
- $T_i$ = thrust from $i$-th rotor.
- $m$ = mass of UAV.
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration ($\approx \mathbf{9.81 \, m/s^2}$).

For a **fixed-wing** UAV, lift is generated by aerodynamic forces:

$$
L = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 S C_L
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = air density ($\approx \mathbf{1.225 \, kg/m^3}$ at sea level).
- $v$ = airspeed.
- $S$ = wing planform area.
- $C_L$ = coefficient of lift (typically $0.4 - 1.2$).

### 2.4.2 Hover Power Consumption

Power required to hover (induced power) for a single rotor:

$$
P_{hover} = \frac{(m g)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}
$$

where $A$ = rotor disk area. Note that hover power is **independent of rotor RPM**, depending only on disk area and weight.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Domain** | **Variables & Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j$ (3D Gruebler) | Manipulator DOF | $n$ = links, $j$ = 1-DOF joints |
| 2 | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j$ (2D Gruebler) | Planar mechanism | $n$ = links, $j$ = lower-pair joints |
| 3 | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | Diff. drive WMR | $v$ in m/s, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 4 | $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Diff. drive WMR | $L$ = wheelbase in m |
| 5 | $R = \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$ | Diff. drive WMR | $R$ = turn radius in m |
| 6 | $\tan \delta = L/R$ | Ackermann steering | $\delta$ = steer angle, $R$ = turn radius |
| 7 | $M + S \le 3$ | WMR maneuverability | $M$ = mobility, $S$ = steerability |
| 8 | $T_{total} = n \cdot T_i = m g$ | Multi-rotor hover | $T$ in N, $m$ in kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 9 | $L = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 S C_L$ | Fixed-wing lift | $\rho$ in kg/m³, $S$ in m² |
| 10 | $P_{hover} = \frac{(m g)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}$ | Rotor hover power | $P$ in W, $A$ in m² |
| 11 | $\text{SSM} = \min d(\mathbf{x}_{CoG}, \text{edge}_i)$ | Legged stability | SSM in m |

---

| **Robot Type** | **Industry Application** | **Key Engineering Challenge** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Articulated Manipulator** | Automotive welding, painting | Path planning, collision avoidance |
| **SCARA** | PCB assembly, screwdriving | Vertical compliance, high-speed repeatability |
| **Differential-Drive AGV** | Warehouse logistics (Amazon Kiva) | SLAM, fleet coordination |
| **Ackermann Vehicle** | Autonomous road cars | Lane-keeping, sensor fusion |

| **Fixed-wing UAV** | Long-range mapping, agriculture | Autonomous mission planning, landing |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential-Drive Forward Kinematics

**Problem Statement:** A differential-drive WMR has wheel radius $r = \mathbf{0.05 \, m}$ and wheelbase $L = \mathbf{0.30 \, m}$. If the right wheel rotates at $\omega_R = \mathbf{10 \, rad/s}$ and the left wheel rotates at $\omega_L = \mathbf{8 \, rad/s}$, compute the robot's linear velocity $v$, angular velocity $\omega$, and turn radius $R$.

### Step 1: Recall the Governing Equations
For a differential-drive robot, the kinematic relationships between wheel speeds and robot motion are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) \\
\omega &= \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) \\
R &= \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Numerical Values

Compute $v$:

$$
v = \frac{0.05}{2}(10 + 8) = \frac{0.05}{2}(18) = 0.05 \times 9 = \mathbf{0.45 \, m/s}
$$

**Explanation:** The linear velocity is the average of the two wheel speeds (multiplied by $r$). Because both wheels rotate forward, the robot translates in the forward direction.

Compute $\omega$:

$$
\omega = \frac{0.05}{0.30}(10 - 8) = \frac{0.05}{0.30}(2) = \frac{0.10}{0.30} = \mathbf{0.333 \, rad/s}
$$

**Explanation:** The angular velocity is proportional to the **difference** in wheel speeds. Since the right wheel is faster, the robot will turn **left** (counter-clockwise when viewed from above).

Compute $R$:

$$
R = \frac{0.30}{2} \cdot \frac{10 + 8}{10 - 8} = 0.15 \cdot \frac{18}{2} = 0.15 \times 9 = \mathbf{1.35 \, m}
$$

**Explanation:** The robot describes a circular arc of radius 1.35 m centered on the ICR. The ICR lies on the line connecting the two wheels, extended outward on the side of the slower wheel (left side here).

### Step 3: Verification
- Both $v$ and $\omega$ are positive (robot moving forward and turning CCW): **Consistent**.
- If $\omega_L = \omega_R = 8$ rad/s, then $R \to \infty$ (straight line) and $v = 0.4$ m/s: **Sanity check passes**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Gruebler's Equation for a 3-DOF Articulated Manipulator

**Problem Statement:** Determine the degrees of freedom of a 3R (three-revolute) planar manipulator with $n = \mathbf{4}$ links (including ground) and $j = \mathbf{3}$ revolute joints.

### Step 1: Select the Correct Gruebler Variant
For a **planar mechanism** in 2D space, the Gruebler mobility equation is:

$$
F = 3(n-1) - 2j
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$
F = 3(4 - 1) - 2(3) = 3(3) - 6 = 9 - 6 = \mathbf{3 \, DOF}
$$

**Explanation:** Three revolute joints in a planar chain give exactly 3 independent rotations, allowing the end-effector to be positioned (but not oriented) anywhere in the plane.

### Step 3: For a 3D Spatial Manipulator

If the same 3R arm operates in 3D with revolute joints, use the spatial Gruebler formula:

$$
F = 6(n-1) - 5j
$$

For $n = 4$ (ground + 3 links), $j = 3$ (three revolutes):

$$
F = 6(4-1) - 5(3) = 18 - 15 = \mathbf{3 \, DOF}
$$

**Explanation:** Even in 3D, a 3R chain still has only 3 DOF (3 joint angles). This is **insufficient for 6D end-effector pose** — additional wrist joints are needed for full spatial orientation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Multi-Rotor UAV Thrust Distribution

**Problem Statement:** A quadcopter (4 rotors) has a total mass of $\mathbf{1.5 \, kg}$. Each rotor has a maximum thrust of $\mathbf{5 \, N}$. Determine:
- (i) Whether the drone can hover.
- (ii) The maximum payload capacity.

### Step 1: Compute the Total Weight

$$
W = m \cdot g = 1.5 \times 9.81 = \mathbf{14.715 \, N}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Total Maximum Thrust

$$
T_{max} = 4 \times 5 = \mathbf{20 \, N}
$$

### Step 3: Compare for Hover Feasibility
For hover: $T_{total} \ge W$.

$$
T_{max} = 20 \, N \; > \; W = 14.715 \, N \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Hover is feasible.}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Maximum Payload
The thrust surplus available for payload (and maneuvering) is:

$$
F_{surplus} = T_{max} - W = 20 - 14.715 = \mathbf{5.285 \, N}
$$

Maximum payload mass (in hover only, no maneuver margin):

$$
m_{payload,max} = \frac{F_{surplus}}{g} = \frac{5.285}{9.81} = \mathbf{0.539 \, kg}
$$

**Engineering Reality Check:** In practice, only **~60–70% of the thrust surplus** is used for payload to retain a **maneuvering margin** (typically 1.5–2× weight for aggressive maneuvers):

$$
m_{payload,realistic} = \frac{0.7 \times 5.285}{9.81} \approx \mathbf{0.377 \, kg}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Robot Type

The following decision matrix summarizes KTU-recommended choices for common applications:

| **Application / Terrain** | **Recommended Robot** | **Justification** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Spot welding car body | 6-DOF Articulated Manipulator | High dexterity, large workspace, full orientation |
| PCB component insertion | SCARA Manipulator | Vertical rigidity + horizontal compliance |
| Smooth indoor warehouse floor | Differential-Drive WMR | Simple, agile in confined spaces |
| Highway autonomous driving | Ackermann-steered WMR | Mimics car geometry, non-holonomic constraints |
| Forested disaster zone | Quadruped (Spot-class) | Rough terrain, stair climbing, dynamic stability |
| Building inspection of pipes | Hexapod or Tracked | Static stability, narrow passages |
| Long-range crop monitoring | Fixed-wing UAV | High endurance, large area coverage |
| Indoor aerial filming | Quadcopter | Hover, vertical takeoff, precise positioning |
| Aerial delivery in cities | Multi-rotor / Hybrid VTOL | Hover + transition to forward flight |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification of Robots (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ROBOT TAXONOMY"] --> B["MANIPULATORS FIXED BASE"]
    A --> C["MOBILE ROBOTS"]
    A --> D["AERIAL ROBOTS UAV"]

    B --> B1["CARTESIAN PPP"]
    B --> B2["CYLINDRICAL RPP"]
    B --> B3["SPHERICAL RRP"]
    B --> B4["ARTICULATED RRR"]
    B --> B5["SCARA RRP"]

    C --> C1["WHEELED LOCOMOTION"]
    C --> C2["LEGGED LOCOMOTION"]

    C1 --> C1a["DIFFERENTIAL DRIVE"]
    C1 --> C1b["ACKERMANN STEERING"]
    C1 --> C1c["OMNIDIRECTIONAL MECANUM"]
    C1 --> C1d["TRACKED SKID STEER"]

    C2 --> C2a["1 LEG HOPPER"]
    C2 --> C2b["2 LEG BIPED HUMANOID"]
    C2 --> C2c["4 LEG QUADRUPED"]
    C2 --> C2d["6 LEG HEXAPOD"]

    D --> D1["FIXED WING"]
    D --> D2["SINGLE ROTOR HELICOPTER"]
    D --> D3["MULTI ROTOR QUAD HEX OCTO"]
    D --> D4["HYBRID VTOL"]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The root node `ROBOT TAXONOMY` branches into the three primary classes.
- Manipulators are further classified by their first three joint types (a standard KTU-style taxonomy).
- Mobile robots branch by **locomotion principle** (wheels vs. legs), not by joint type.
- Aerial robots branch by **lift mechanism**.

---

## 4.2 Differential-Drive WMR Kinematic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CONTROL INPUTS omega_R and omega_L"] --> B["KINEMATIC TRANSFORM v= r by 2 omega_R plus omega_L"]
    A --> C["KINEMATIC TRANSFORM omega= r by L omega_R minus omega_L"]
    B --> D["ROBOT BODY"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["POSE UPDATE x y theta"]
    D --> F["ICR CALCULATION R= L by 2 times omega_R plus omega_L over omega_R minus omega_L"]
    F --> G["CIRCULAR TRAJECTORY"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The control inputs are the two wheel angular velocities.
- Two parallel kinematic transforms compute linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$.
- These drive the body, which updates the pose $(x, y, \theta)$ and the radius of curvature $R$.

---

## 4.3 Quadruped Robot Support Polygon (Stability)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FOOT CONTACT POINTS F1 F2 F3 F4"] --> B["CONVEX HULL FORMATION"]
    B --> C["SUPPORT POLYGON 2D REGION"]
    D["CENTER OF GRAVITY CG"] --> E{"CG PROJECTION INSIDE POLYGON"}
    C --> E
    E -->|YES| F["STATICALLY STABLE SSM greater than 0"]
    E -->|NO| G["UNSTABLE NEEDS DYNAMIC BALANCE"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- A quadruped uses 3 or 4 feet on the ground at any time.
- The convex hull of the contact points forms the support polygon.
- The robot is statically stable only if the vertical projection of the CoG lies inside the support polygon.

---

## 4.4 Multi-Rotor UAV Thrust Vectoring Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FLIGHT CONTROLLER"] --> B["MIXER LOGIC"]
    B --> C1["MOTOR M1 FRONT CW"]
    B --> C2["MOTOR M2 RIGHT CCW"]
    B --> C3["MOTOR M3 REAR CW"]
    B --> C4["MOTOR M4 LEFT CCW"]
    C1 --> D1["PROPELLER LIFT T1"]
    C2 --> D2["PROPELLER LIFT T2"]
    C3 --> D3["PROPELLER LIFT T3"]
    C4 --> D4["PROPELLER LIFT T4"]
    D1 --> E["NET VERTICAL THRUST SUM T"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{"EQUAL TO m g"}
    F -->|YES| G["HOVER STATE"]
    F -->|NO| H["ASCEND OR DESCEND"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Opposing rotor pairs spin in opposite directions (CW and CCW) to cancel net yaw torque.
- The flight controller adjusts individual motor speeds to control altitude, roll, pitch, and yaw.
- Hover occurs when net vertical thrust equals the weight $mg$.

---

## 4.5 Comparison of Robot Types — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT TASK"] --> B{"ENVIRONMENT TYPE"}
    B -->|STRUCTURED| C["MANIPULATOR INDUSTRIAL"]
    B -->|SEMI STRUCTURED FLAT| D["WHEELED MOBILE"]
    B -->|UNSTRUCTURED ROUGH| E["LEGGED MOBILE"]
    B -->|3D AIRSPACE| F["AERIAL UAV"]
    C --> G["HIGH PRECISION HIGH REPEATABILITY"]
    D --> H["MEDIUM PRECISION GOOD COVERAGE"]
    E --> I["LOW SPEED HIGH TERRAIN ADAPTABILITY"]
    F --> J["HIGH MOBILITY 3D ACCESS"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The choice of robot architecture is driven primarily by the **environment** and **task**.
- Manipulators excel in structured environments; legged/aerial robots extend into unstructured 3D space.
