
## 4.2 Closed-Loop Servo Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Trajectory theta ref]:::ref --> SUM[Summing Junction]:::sum
    F[Feedback theta actual]:::fb --> SUM
    SUM -->|e of t| P[PID Controller]:::pid
    P -->|u of t| V[Power Amplifier]:::amp
    V -->|Voltage or Flow| M[Actuator Motor or Cylinder]:::act
    M --> J[Robot Joint]:::joint
    J --> S[Position Sensor Encoder]:::sens
    S --> F
    classDef ref fill:#fefae0,stroke:#0a1931,color:#0a1931
    classDef sum fill:#ff006e,stroke:#0a1931,color:#ffffff
    classDef pid fill:#16c79a,stroke:#0a1931,color:#0a1931
    classDef amp fill:#3a86ff,stroke:#0a1931,color:#ffffff
    classDef act fill:#533483,stroke:#16c79a,color:#ffffff
    classDef joint fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,color:#ffffff
    classDef sens fill:#f9b208,stroke:#0a1931,color:#0a1931
    classDef fb fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#0a1931,color:#0a1931
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of an Industrial Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Teach Pendant or HMI]:::ui --> B[Controller CPU Path Planner]:::cpu
    B --> C[Motion Control Card Interpolator]:::motion
    C --> D[Servo Drive Amplifier]:::drive
    D --> E[Power Converter DC Bus or Hydraulic Servo Valve]:::power
    E --> F[Actuator DC AC Stepper Hydraulic Motor]:::actuator
    F --> G[Mechanical Linkage Gear Reducer Harmonic Drive]:::mech
    G --> H[Robot Joint Output Shaft]:::joint
    H --> I[Encoder Resolver LVDT Feedback]:::fb
    I --> B
    classDef ui fill:#fefae0,stroke:#0a1931,color:#0a1931
    classDef cpu fill:#3a86ff,stroke:#0a1931,color:#ffffff
    classDef motion fill:#16c79a,stroke:#0a1931,color:#0a1931
    classDef drive fill:#ff006e,stroke:#0a1931,color:#ffffff
    classDef power fill:#533483,stroke:#16c79a,color:#ffffff
    classDef actuator fill:#8338ec,stroke:#0a1931,color:#ffffff
    classDef mech fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,color:#ffffff
    classDef joint fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#f9b208,color:#ffffff
    classDef fb fill:#f9b208,stroke:#0a1931,color:#0a1931
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Trace the arrows to follow the **flow of information** (thin arrows) versus the **flow of power** (thick arrows in real schematics). The feedback arrow from the encoder returning to the controller is the **defining feature** that converts an open-loop system into a closed-loop servo system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Distinguish between servo-controlled and non-servo-controlled robots. Give one example application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Servo-Controlled | Non-Servo Controlled |
|---------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Feedback** | Continuous (encoders, resolvers) | None (limit switches, stops) |
| **Path Control** | PTP, CP, Controlled | PTP only |
| **Accuracy** | High (±0.02 mm) | Lower (±1 mm) |
| **Cost & Complexity** | Higher | Lower |
| **Example Application** | Arc welding of car chassis (CP, servo) | Pick-and-place of plastic bottles (PTP, pneumatic) |

Servo-controlled robots use a **closed-loop error-correction system** with a PID controller, enabling them to follow **continuous spatial paths** with high accuracy. Non-servo robots rely on **mechanical end-stops** and are limited to point-to-point motions. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**List the three main drive technologies used in industrial robots. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hydraulic Drive:** ✓ *Advantage:* Highest power density, ideal for payloads > 50 kg. ✗ *Disadvantage:* Oil leakage, high maintenance.
2. **Pneumatic Drive:** ✓ *Advantage:* Clean, low-cost, suitable for food/pharma. ✗ *Disadvantage:* Low accuracy, limited payload.
3. **Electric Drive:** ✓ *Advantage:* High precision, clean, easy computer interfacing. ✗ *Disadvantage:* Lower power density than hydraulics. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the **closed-loop servo control** of a robot joint. Derive the **error equation** and the **PID control law**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A hydraulic cylinder with bore $D = 100$ mm, rod diameter $d = 60$ mm, supply pressure $P = 100$ bar drives a robot's linear axis. If the mechanical efficiency is $\eta = 0.90$, calculate the **extension force**, **retraction force**, and the **power delivered** when the piston moves at $v = 0.4$ m/s.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

A closed-loop servo system for a robot joint consists of the following blocks (see SECTION 4.2 diagram):

1. **Reference Generator** — produces the desired joint trajectory $\theta_{ref}(t)$.
2. **Feedback Sensor** — measures actual joint position $\theta_{actual}(t)$ via encoder/resolver.
3. **Summing Junction** — computes the **error signal** $e(t) = \theta_{ref}(t) - \theta_{actual}(t)$.
4. **PID Controller** — converts the error into a control command $u(t)$.
5. **Power Amplifier / Servo Drive** — boosts the controller signal to actuator levels.
6. **Actuator (Motor / Hydraulic Cylinder)** — produces torque/force to move the joint.

**Derivation of Error Equation:**

$$
e(t) = \theta_{ref}(t) - \theta_{actual}(t)
$$

**Derivation of PID Control Law:**

$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

where $K_p$ (proportional gain) gives **fast response**, $K_i$ (integral gain) **eliminates steady-state error**, and $K_d$ (derivative gain) **improves damping and stability**. The controller drives $e(t) \to 0$ asymptotically, ensuring the joint follows the reference trajectory. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $D = 0.100$ m, $d = 0.060$ m, $P = 100 \text{ bar} = 1.0 \times 10^7$ Pa, $\eta = 0.90$, $v = 0.4$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Extension area (full bore):**

$$
A_{ext} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Retraction area (annular):**

$$
A_{ret} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0100 - 0.0036) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0064) = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Real extension force:**

$$
F_{ext} = \eta \, P \, A_{ext} = 0.90 \times (1.0 \times 10^7) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 70{,}686 \text{ N} \approx 70.69 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Real retraction force:**

$$
F_{ret} = \eta \, P \, A_{ret} = 0.90 \times (1.0 \times 10^7) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) = 45{,}243 \text{ N} \approx 45.24 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Power delivered (extension stroke):**

$$
P_{mech} = F_{ext} \times v = 70{,}686 \times 0.4 = 28{,}274 \text{ W} \approx 28.27 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $F_{ext} \approx 70.69$ kN, $F_{ret} \approx 45.24$ kN, $P_{mech} \approx 28.27$ kW. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare **hydraulic, pneumatic, and electric drives** for industrial robots across **at least 8 parameters** in a tabular form. State two example applications for each drive type.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a 6-axis servo robot**. List the major subsystems and explain the role of the **interpolator** and the **feedback encoder**.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Tabular Comparison** *(see SECTION 3.3 for the full 12-parameter analytical matrix — reproduce at least 8 rows in exam answer)*.

**Key Applications:**

- **Hydraulic:** (1) Heavy-payload spot welding in automotive body shops (e.g., 500 kg welding gun); (2) Forging cell handling of red-hot billets.
- **Pneumatic:** (1) Pick-and-place of biscuits in a food packaging line; (2) Bottle-capping in pharmaceutical plants.
- **Electric (AC Servo):** (1) Arc welding of car chassis (CP); (2) Precision PCB assembly using SCARA robots; (3) Palletizing with articulated 6-axis arms.

> The choice is governed by the **payload-accuracy-environment** triangle: hydraulics for high payload, electrics for high accuracy, pneumatics for clean/low-cost applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

The functional architecture of a 6-axis servo robot comprises these subsystems (see SECTION 4.3 diagram):

1. **Teach Pendant / HMI** — human interface for programming and manual jogging.
2. **Controller (CPU + Path Planner)** — high-level trajectory generation in Cartesian (X, Y, Z) coordinates.
3. **Interpolator (Motion Control Card)** — converts the Cartesian path into **per-joint setpoints** at a fine time slice (typically 1–4 ms). It performs **linear, circular, or spline interpolation** between taught points.
4. **Servo Drive / Amplifier** — one per joint, closes the local current/torque loop.
5. **Actuators** — typically brushless AC servo motors coupled to the joints through **harmonic-drive or RV reducers**.
6. **Feedback Encoders** — mounted on each motor shaft (and sometimes on the joint output), providing **incremental position pulses** back to the interpolator.
7. **Mechanical Structure** — the links, gears, and bearings forming the kinematic chain.

**Role of the Interpolator:** It is the *bridge* between the slow path planner (Cartesian) and the fast joint servo loops. It samples the desired Cartesian path at high frequency and **inverts the Jacobian** to compute equivalent joint angles for every time instant.

**Role of the Feedback Encoder:** It is the *sensory organ* of the closed loop. Without it, the system is open-loop and cannot correct for disturbances, gravity, or inertial coupling between links. The encoder resolution (e.g., 20-bit = 1,048,576 counts/rev) directly determines the achievable **positioning repeatability** of the robot. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — KTU 2024 examiner's pet peeves:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Non-Servo" with "PTP":** Non-servo robots are *always* PTP, but *servo robots can also be PTP*. Many students write "PTP = non-servo" — this is a **2-mark deduction** error.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert bar to Pa:** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5$ Pa. Writing force in N without unit conversion loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $F = P \cdot A$ without efficiency $\eta$:** A purely theoretical answer. Real robots always have $\eta < 1$; examiners expect $\eta$ in the calculation.
> 4. **Omitting the block diagram in 14-mark answers:** A 14-mark servo/control question without a diagram is capped at ~10 marks per the KTU valuation key.
> 5. **Calling stepper motors "servo" motors:** Stepper motors are **open-loop** by design (no feedback). Conflating them is a common conceptual error.
> 6. **No units in the final answer:** Always write units — N, N·m, W, rad/s. Unit-less answers = auto-1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ **Two independent axes of classification:** *Motion Control* (Servo vs Non-Servo) and *Drive Technology* (Hydraulic / Pneumatic / Electric). They are **orthogonal** — combine freely.
- ✅ **Servo = Closed Loop = Feedback = CP capable.** Non-Servo = Open Loop = Limit Switches = PTP only.
- ✅ **Three path-control modes:** PTP (no intermediate control), Continuous Path (full curve), Controlled Path (interpolated).
- ✅ **Hydraulic → highest power density, heavy payload, messy.** **Pneumatic → cheapest, cleanest, low payload.** **Electric → highest accuracy, modern default.**
- ✅ **Master equations:** $F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta$, $\tau = K_t I_a$, $e_{back} = K_e \omega$, $V_a = e_{back} + I_a R_a$, $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$.
- ✅ **SI conversions to memorize:** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5$ Pa, $1 \text{ HP} = 745.7$ W, $1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3}$ m.
- ✅ **Block diagram is compulsory** for any 14-mark question on servo control or robot architecture.
- ✅ **Key feedback sensors:** Encoder (rotary, optical), Resolver (rugged, analog), LVDT (linear), Tachogenerator (velocity).
- ✅ **PID:** $K_p$ = speed, $K_i$ = steady-state accuracy, $K_d$ = damping.
- ✅ **Stepper motors = open-loop.** **Servo motors = closed-loop.** Never mix them up.
- ✅ **Industry trend (examiner's "current affairs" question):** Modern factories migrate from **hydraulic → electric servo** for cleanliness, and from **pneumatic → small electric servo** for precision, even at low payload.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3R Concurrent Wrist
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** 3R Concurrent Wrist # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **3R Concurrent Wrist** (also called a **Spherical Wrist** or **Euler Wrist**) is a robotic end-effector assembly consisting of **three revolute joints whose rotational axes all intersect at a single common point**, known as the **wrist center (OC)** or the **origin of the wrist frame**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this configuration is treated as the canonical wrist topology because it enables **kinematic decoupling** — the position of the wrist center and the orientation of the end-effector can be computed independently. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > All three joint axes of the wrist (joints 4, 5, 6) must **intersect at one point** for the spherical-wrist property to hold. If the axes are offset (non-concurrent), the inverse kinematics cannot be solved in closed form using decoupling, and the manipulator is classified as a *non-spherical* or *redundant* wrist. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Joint + Knuckle" Analogy Imagine holding a pen and writing your name: - Your **wrist** acts like the spherical center of a 3R wrist — all three rotations (left-right swing, up-down nod, and twisting) happen at the same anatomical pivot point. - The **elbow and shoulder** move the wrist (the "positioning arm") to the correct location on the page. - The **fingers** (the wrist joints) then orient the pen tip at exactly the right angle to write a clean letter. In the robot, the **first three joints (waist, shoulder, elbow)** handle **positioning** the wrist center in 3D space, while the **last three joints (4, 5, 6)** handle only **orientation** of the end-effector. This is the foundation of the famous **Kinematic Decoupling Principle** introduced by Paul, Shimano, and Liegeois. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Concurrent"?** > The word *concurrent* literally means "meeting at one point." In KTU theory, it is a *structural* property of the wrist frame's **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters** — specifically, the link lengths $d_i$ and $a_i$ between joints 4, 5, and 6 must be zero, so that all origins of frames $\{4\}, \{5\}, \{6\}$ coincide at the wrist center $O_C$. ## 1.3 Standard Configuration Metrics (KTU Convention) | Parameter | KTU Standard Value | Units | |---|---|---| | Number of revolute joints | **3** (joints 4, 5, 6) | — | | Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of wrist | **3** | — | | Axes intersection | **All 3 at $O_C$** | point | | Standard axis ordering | **Z-Y-Z (Euler)** or **Z-Y-X (RPY)** | — | | Common singularity | $\theta_5 = 0$ or $\pm 180°$ (ZYZ), $\theta_5 = \pm 90°$ (RPY) | degrees | > [!NOTE] > The "3R" prefix denotes **3 Revolute** joints. This is the most common industrial wrist topology (e.g., found on the **PUMA 560**, **ABB IRB**, and **KUKA KR** series robots). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of a 6-DOF Manipulator A 6-DOF industrial manipulator is conceptually split into two sub-systems: 1. **The Arm (Joints 1, 2, 3):** A positioning chain that places the wrist center $O_C$ at the desired Cartesian position $(p_x, p_y, p_z)$. 2. **The Wrist (Joints 4, 5, 6):** A pure-orientation chain that aligns the tool frame $\{T\}$ to the desired orientation matrix $R$. The forward kinematics of the full manipulator is: $${}^{0}_{T}T \;=\; {}^{0}_{3}T \;\cdot\; {}^{3}_{6}T$$ where ${}^{0}_{3}T$ is the arm's pose matrix (position + orientation at frame 3) and ${}^{3}_{6}T$ is the wrist's relative transformation. ## 2.2 The Decoupling Property — Step-by-Step Logic The decoupling property arises from the **3R concurrent geometry**. Following KTU board-style logical reasoning: - **Step 1:** Because all three wrist axes pass through $O_C$, the position of $O_C$ depends ONLY on the arm joints $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$. - **Step 2:** Once $O_C$ is located, the orientation of the end-effector is governed ONLY by the wrist joints $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$. - **Step 3:** The position sub-problem becomes a **2R planar problem in the shoulder plane** (after projecting the wrist center onto the $X_0Y_0$ plane), and the orientation sub-problem reduces to **Euler-angle extraction** from a 3×3 rotation matrix. - **Step 4:** This separation permits **closed-form analytical solutions** instead of iterative numerical methods (Newton-Raphson), making real-time control feasible. ## 2.3 ZYZ Euler Angle Kinematic Equations (KTU High-Yield) For a 3R concurrent wrist using **ZYZ Euler angles**, the orientation matrix expressed in terms of wrist joints $(\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6)$ is: $${}^{3}_{6}R(\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6) \;=\; R_z(\theta_4) \;\cdot\; R_y(\theta_5) \;\cdot\; R_z(\theta_6)$$ Writing the components explicitly: $${}^{3}_{6}R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_4 c_5 c_6 - s_4 s_6 & -c_4 c_5 s_6 - s_4 c_6 & c_4 s_5 \\ s_4 c_5 c_6 + c_4 s_6 & -s_4 c_5 s_6 + c_4 c_6 & s_4 s_5 \\ -s_5 c_6 & s_5 s_6 & c_5 \end{bmatrix}$$ The inverse problem (extracting joint angles from a known rotation matrix $R = [r_{ij}]$) yields the closed-form solutions: $$\theta_5 \;=\; \text{Atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2},\; r_{33}\right)$$ $$\theta_4 \;=\; \text{Atan2}\!\left(r_{23},\; r_{13}\right)$$ $$\theta_6 \;=\; \text{Atan2}\!\left(r_{32},\; -r_{31}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Singularity Condition (ZYZ):** When $\theta_5 = 0$ or $\theta_5 = 180°$, the matrix $R$ loses rank and the values of $\theta_4$ and $\theta_6$ become coupled (only their sum or difference is uniquely defined). This is the **wrist singularity**, analogous to gimbal lock in aerospace navigation. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $O_C = {}^{0}P_{6ORG}$ | Wrist center position vector | m or mm | | 2 | $O_C = p - d_6 \cdot \hat{a}$ | Position using tool length $d_6$ | m or mm | | 3 | ${}^{0}_{3}R = {}^{0}_{3}R(\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3)$ | Arm orientation matrix | — | | 4 | ${}^{3}_{6}R = ({}^{0}_{3}R)^{-1} \cdot {}^{0}_{6}R$ | Wrist rotation from decoupling | — | | 5 | ZYZ: $\theta_5 = \pm \text{Atan2}(\sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2}, r_{33})$ | Pitch extraction | rad | | 6 | ZYZ: $\theta_4 = \text{Atan2}(r_{23}, r_{13})$ | First roll | rad | | 7 | ZYZ: $\theta_6 = \text{Atan2}(r_{32}, -r_{31})$ | Second roll | rad | | 8 | RPY: $\phi = \text{Atan2}(r_{21}, r_{11})$ | Yaw (about $Z$) | rad | | 9 | RPY: $\theta = \text{Atan2}(-r_{31}, \sqrt{r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2})$ | Pitch (about $Y$) | rad | | 10 | RPY: $\psi = \text{Atan2}(r_{32}, r_{33})$ | Roll (about $X$) | rad | | 11 | Singularity: $r_{33} = \pm 1$ (ZYZ) or $r_{31} = \pm 1$ (RPY) | Gimbal lock condition | — | > [!NOTE] > **Notation Convention:** $c_i = \cos\theta_i$, $s_i = \sin\theta_i$, $r_{ij}$ denotes the $(i,j)$ element of the rotation matrix, and $\hat{a}$ is the approach vector (Z-axis) of the end-effector frame. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The 3R concurrent wrist is the **de-facto standard** in industrial automation because: - **Welding Robots (e.g., MIG/TIG):** The wrist must orient the torch tip at any angle to reach into complex weld seams while the arm positions the torch stand-off. - **Painting Robots:** Continuous reorientation of the spray nozzle without disturbing the spray-pattern distance. - **Assembly Robots (PCB insertion, screw-driving):** Aligning the tool axis with the insertion direction while the arm brings it to the workpiece. - **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** The instruments must enter through a **remote-center-of-motion (RCM)** trocar — the wrist's concurrency point IS the pivot through the patient's body wall. The "3R concurrent" property is so critical that KTU board questions frequently test it as a **design constraint** (e.g., "Why must a 6-DOF robot use a spherical wrist for closed-form IK?"). # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The ZYZ Euler-Angle Forward Map for a 3R Concurrent Wrist ### Step A — Define the elementary rotation matrices We use the standard right-handed rotation matrices about the body-fixed $Z$ and $Y$ axes: $$R_z(\theta) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c & -s & 0 \\ s & c & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_y(\theta) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c & 0 & s \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -s & 0 & c \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step B — Compose the three rotations in order (intrinsic Z-Y-Z) Intrinsic rotations are post-multiplied. The wrist's relative orientation is: $${}^{3}_{6}R \;=\; R_z(\theta_4) \cdot R_y(\theta_5) \cdot R_z(\theta_6)$$ ### Step C — Compute $R_z(\theta_4) \cdot R_y(\theta_5)$ We multiply the first two matrices, labeling $c_4 = \cos\theta_4$, $s_4 = \sin\theta_4$, $c_5 = \cos\theta_5$, $s_5 = \sin\theta_5$: $$R_z(\theta_4) \cdot R_y(\theta_5) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_4 c_5 & -s_4 & c_4 s_5 \\ s_4 c_5 & c_4 & s_4 s_5 \\ -s_5 & 0 & c_5 \end{bmatrix}$$ The conversion logic for the $(1,1)$ element is: $(c_4)(c_5) + (-s_4)(0) + (0)(-s_5) = c_4 c_5$. The $(1,2)$ element: $(c_4)(0) + (-s_4)(1) + (0)(0) = -s_4$. The $(1,3)$ element: $(c_4)(s_5) + (-s_4)(0) + (0)(c_5) = c_4 s_5$. The $(2,1)$ element: $(s_4)(c_5) + (c_4)(0) + (0)(-s_5) = s_4 c_5$. The $(2,2)$ element: $(s_4)(0) + (c_4)(1) + (0)(0) = c_4$. The $(2,3)$ element: $(s_4)(s_5) + (c_4)(0) + (0)(c_5) = s_4 s_5$. The $(3,1)$ element: $(-s_5)(c_5) + (0)(0) + (1)(-s_5) = -s_5 c_5 + (-s_5) = \ldots$ — correction: $(-s_5)(c_5) + (0)(0) + (1)(-s_5)$ gives $-s_5 c_5 - s_5$. Re-checking: The third row of $R_z$ is $(0, 0, 1)$ and the third column of $R_y$ is $(s_5, 0, c_5)^T$. So $(3,1) = 0 \cdot s_5 + 0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot (-s_5) = -s_5$. Similarly $(3,3) = 0 \cdot s_5 + 0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot c_5 = c_5$. The corrected intermediate is: $$R_z(\theta_4) \cdot R_y(\theta_5) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_4 c_5 & -s_4 & c_4 s_5 \\ s_4 c_5 & c_4 & s_4 s_5 \\ -s_5 & 0 & c_5 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step D — Right-multiply by $R_z(\theta_6)$ Now post-multiply the intermediate matrix by $R_z(\theta_6)$: $${}^{3}_{6}R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_4 c_5 & -s_4 & c_4 s_5 \\ s_4 c_5 & c_4 & s_4 s_5 \\ -s_5 & 0 & c_5 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} c_6 & -s_6 & 0 \\ s_6 & c_6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ Expanding the $(1,1)$ element: $(c_4 c_5)(c_6) + (-s_4)(s_6) + (c_4 s_5)(0) = c_4 c_5 c_6 - s_4 s_6$. Expanding the $(1,2)$ element: $(c_4 c_5)(-s_6) + (-s_4)(c_6) + (c_4 s_5)(0) = -c_4 c_5 s_6 - s_4 c_6$. Expanding the $(1,3)$ element: $(c_4 c_5)(0) + (-s_4)(0) + (c_4 s_5)(1) = c_4 s_5$. Expanding the $(2,1)$ element: $(s_4 c_5)(c_6) + (c_4)(s_6) + (s_4 s_5)(0) = s_4 c_5 c_6 + c_4 s_6$. Expanding the $(2,2)$ element: $(s_4 c_5)(-s_6) + (c_4)(c_6) + (s_4 s_5)(0) = -s_4 c_5 s_6 + c_4 c_6$. Expanding the $(2,3)$ element: $(s_4 c_5)(0) + (c_4)(0) + (s_4 s_5)(1) = s_4 s_5$. Expanding the $(3,1)$ element: $(-s_5)(c_6) + (0)(s_6) + (c_5)(0) = -s_5 c_6$. Expanding the $(3,2)$ element: $(-s_5)(-s_6) + (0)(c_6) + (c_5)(0) = s_5 s_6$. Expanding the $(3,3)$ element: $(-s_5)(0) + (0)(0) + (c_5)(1) = c_5$. ### Step E — Final compact form of the forward map $$\boxed{ {}^{3}_{6}R(\theta_4,\theta_5,\theta_6) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_4 c_5 c_6 - s_4 s_6 & -c_4 c_5 s_6 - s_4 c_6 & c_4 s_5 \\ s_4 c_5 c_6 + c_4 s_6 & -s_4 c_5 s_6 + c_4 c_6 & s_4 s_5 \\ -s_5 c_6 & s_5 s_6 & c_5 \end{bmatrix} }$$ This is the **canonical ZYZ forward orientation map** for any 3R concurrent wrist in KTU exam settings. ## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Kinematics (ZYZ Angle Extraction) ### Step F — Set up the known target Given a desired orientation ${}^{3}_{6}R = [r_{ij}]$, equate the symbolic matrix (Step E) element-by-element with the known matrix. The most diagnostic element is $(3,3)$: $$r_{33} \;=\; \cos\theta_5$$ This is the **pitch-angle equation** used by KTU examiners. It gives: $$\theta_5 \;=\; \pm \arccos(r_{33})$$ The two-valued result corresponds to the **"elbow-up / elbow-down"** dual solution. Using the four-quadrant $\text{Atan2}$ form (preserving sign information): $$\theta_5 \;=\; \text{Atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2},\; r_{33}\right)$$ The $\sqrt{\cdot}$ uses the elements $r_{13} = c_4 s_5$ and $r_{23} = s_4 s_5$ to encode the sign of $s_5$. ### Step G — Solve for $\theta_4$ Using the $(1,3)$ and $(2,3)$ elements: $$r_{13} \;=\; c_4 s_5, \quad r_{23} \;=\; s_4 s_5$$ Dividing (provided $s_5 \neq 0$): $$\frac{r_{23}}{r_{13}} \;=\; \frac{s_4}{c_4} \;=\; \tan\theta_4 \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta_4 \;=\; \text{Atan2}(r_{23}, r_{13})$$ ### Step H — Solve for $\theta_6$ Using the $(3,1)$ and $(3,2)$ elements: $$r_{31} \;=\; -s_5 c_6, \quad r_{32} \;=\; s_5 s_6$$ Dividing (again, $s_5 \neq 0$): $$\frac{r_{32}}{-r_{31}} \;=\; \frac{s_5 s_6}{s_5 c_6} \;=\; \tan\theta_6 \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta_6 \;=\; \text{Atan2}(r_{32}, -r_{31})$$ ### Step I — Summarize the inverse map $$\boxed{\theta_5 = \text{Atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2},\; r_{33}\right), \quad \theta_4 = \text{Atan2}(r_{23}, r_{13}), \quad \theta_6 = \text{Atan2}(r_{32}, -r_{31})}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** Given a 3R concurrent wrist with desired orientation matrix $${}^{3}_{6}R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.527 & -0.574 & 0.628 \\ 0.369 & 0.819 & 0.439 \\ -0.766 & 0 & 0.643 \end{bmatrix}$$ find the ZYZ Euler angles $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$. **Solution:** Step 1 — Identify the relevant elements: $r_{13} = 0.628$, $r_{23} = 0.439$, $r_{33} = 0.643$, $r_{31} = -0.766$, $r_{32} = 0$. Step 2 — Compute $\theta_5$: $$\theta_5 \;=\; \text{Atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{(0.628)^2 + (0.439)^2},\; 0.643\right)$$ The intermediate sum: $(0.628)^2 = 0.394384$, $(0.439)^2 = 0.192721$, sum $= 0.587105$, square root $\approx 0.7662$. $$\theta_5 \;=\; \text{Atan2}(0.7662,\; 0.643) \;\approx\; 50.0° \;\approx\; 0.873 \text{ rad}$$ Step 3 — Compute $\theta_4$: $$\theta_4 \;=\; \text{Atan2}(0.439,\; 0.628) \;\approx\; 35.0° \;\approx\; 0.611 \text{ rad}$$ Step 4 — Compute $\theta_6$: $$\theta_6 \;=\; \text{Atan2}(0,\; -(-0.766)) \;=\; \text{Atan2}(0,\; 0.766) \;=\; 0.0° \;=\; 0 \text{ rad}$$ Step 5 — Verify by substitution (sanity check): the diagonal element $r_{33}$ from the symbolic formula is $c_5 = \cos(50°) \approx 0.643$ ✓ matches input. **Final Answer:** $\theta_4 \approx 35.0°$, $\theta_5 \approx 50.0°$, $\theta_6 \approx 0.0°$. ## 3.4 Singularity Analysis — Where the Wrist "Locks Up" Setting $s_5 = 0$ in the forward map collapses the third column of ${}^{3}_{6}R$ to either $(0, 0, 1)^T$ (when $c_5 = +1$, i.e., $\theta_5 = 0$) or $(0, 0, -1)^T$ (when $c_5 = -1$, i.e., $\theta_5 = 180°$). In either case, the rotation matrix has **two identical columns**, and the angles $\theta_4, \theta_6$ lose individual meaning — only their **sum** (or difference) is observable. Physically: - **$\theta_5 = 0$:** Wrist joints 4 and 6 become **collinear**, so the end-effector can be rotated about the same axis using EITHER joint — infinite IK solutions. - **$\theta_5 = 180°$:** Similar degeneracy with reversed hand. This is mathematically identical to the **gimbal lock** of a three-gimbal gyroscope and is the reason industrial robots must avoid commanding the wrist to these configurations at high speeds. ## 3.5 Kinematic Decoupling — Mathematical Justification Consider the 6-DOF arm with wrist center $O_C$ at origin of frame $\{6\}$ (or equivalently, displaced by the tool length $d_6$ from the end-effector origin). The end-effector position is: $$p \;=\; O_C \;+\; d_6 \cdot \hat{a}$$ where $\hat{a}$ is the unit approach vector of the tool frame. Rearranging: $$O_C \;=\; p \;-\; d_6 \cdot \hat{a}$$ This shows $O_C$ depends on $(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$ alone (the arm), while $\hat{a}$ depends on $(\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6)$ alone (the wrist). Hence the position and orientation problems are **separable**, yielding the **two-step inverse kinematic algorithm** of Paul & Shimano (1977) that is universally adopted in KTU textbooks: $$\text{Step A (Position):} \quad \text{Solve } (q_1, q_2, q_3) \text{ such that } {}^{0}P_{3ORG} = O_C.$$ $$\text{Step B (Orientation):} \quad \text{Compute } {}^{3}_{6}R = ({}^{0}_{3}R)^{-1} \cdot R, \text{ then extract } (\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6).$$ # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 3R Concurrent Wrist The diagram below maps the **control flow** and **mathematical flow** of a 3R concurrent wrist system, as taught in KTU Module 1. It is intentionally a **block-level functional architecture** rather than a physical sketch, because the wrist is best understood through its input-output signal/data relationships. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Desired End-Effector Pose
Position p and Orientation R] --> B{Split into Two Sub-Problems} B --> C[Position Sub-Problem
Locate Wrist Center OC] B --> D[Orientation Sub-Problem
Compute Wrist Rotation Matrix 3-6 R] C --> E[Arm IK Solver
Joints q1, q2, q3] D --> F[Decoupling Equation
3-6 R equals 0-3 R inverse times R] F --> G[Euler Angle Extractor
ZYZ or RPY convention] G --> H[Wrist Joint Angles
q4, q5, q6] E --> I[Concatenated Joint Vector
q1 to q6] H --> I I --> J{Singularity Check
q5 equals 0 or 180 deg} J -->|No Singularity| K[Valid Joint Solution] J -->|Singularity| L[Apply IK Redundancy Resolution
Set q4 to 0 and combine into q6] L --> K K --> M[Joint Servo Controllers] M --> N[Physical 3R Concurrent Wrist Hardware] ``` ## 4.2 Kinematic Decoupling Schematic — The 6-DOF Manipulator Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ARM[Positioning Arm - Joints 1, 2, 3] A1[Base Frame 0] --> A2[Shoulder Joint q1] A2 --> A3[Elbow Joint q2] A3 --> A4[Arm Tip and Wrist Center OC] end subgraph WRIST[Orienting Wrist - Joints 4, 5, 6] A4 --> W1[Roll Joint q4 about Z4] W1 --> W2[Pitch Joint q5 about Y5] W2 --> W3[Spin Joint q6 about Z6] end W3 --> END[End Effector Frame T
Position p and Orientation R] style ARM fill:#f0f8ff style WRIST fill:#fff5e6 style END fill:#e8f5e8 ``` ## 4.3 Singularity Condition Map — The "Forbidden Zone" of $\theta_5$ ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Wrist Joint Command q5 Received] --> S1{Is q5 approximately 0 deg} S1 -->|Yes| S2[Singularity Type A
Wrist Flip Configuration
Apply q4 equals 0 and absorb into q6] S1 -->|No| S3{Is q5 approximately 180 deg} S3 -->|Yes| S4[Singularity Type B
Reversed Approach Vector
Use dual elbow solution] S3 -->|No| S5[Safe Operating Region
Solve IK in closed form
q4 from Atan2, q6 from Atan2] S2 --> S6[Controller Output] S4 --> S6 S5 --> S6 style S2 fill:#ffe0e0 style S4 fill:#ffe0e0 style S5 fill:#e0ffe0 ``` ## 4.4 Modular Mapping — DH Parameters to Concurrency The structural beauty of a 3R concurrent wrist lies in its Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameter table. **All link lengths and offsets between joints 4, 5, 6 are zero** (except for the joint angle $\theta_i$). ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DHTABLE[DH Parameters for 3R Concurrent Wrist] T1[i equals 4
a sub i-1 equals 0
alpha sub i-1 equals plus 90 deg
d sub i equals d4
theta sub i equals q4] T2[i equals 5
a sub i-1 equals 0
alpha sub i-1 equals minus 90 deg
d sub i equals 0
theta sub i equals q5] T3[i equals 6
a sub i-1 equals 0
alpha sub i-1 equals 0 deg
d sub i equals d6
theta sub i equals q6] end T1 --> T2 --> T3 style DHTABLE fill:#fafafa ``` > [!NOTE] > In the DH table above, the entries $a_{i-1} = 0$ for all three rows are what physically realize the **concurrent** property — there is no translational offset between successive wrist joints, so all rotation axes intersect at a single point. The values $d_4$ and $d_6$ represent the **pre-wrist offset** and **tool length**, not offsets between wrist joints. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B] **Define a 3R concurrent (spherical) wrist. State the primary kinematic advantage it provides in a 6-DOF robot manipulator.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **3R concurrent wrist** is a robotic wrist assembly consisting of **three revolute joints whose axes all intersect at a single common point**, called the wrist center $O_C$. This is also known as a **spherical wrist** or **Euler wrist**. The primary kinematic advantage is the **kinematic decoupling property**: the position of the wrist center depends only on the first three (arm) joints, and the orientation of the end-effector depends only on the last three (wrist) joints. This allows the inverse kinematics to be solved in **closed form** (analytically) rather than through iterative numerical methods, enabling real-time motion planning and control. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper] **What is "wrist singularity" in a 3R concurrent wrist? At which joint configuration does it occur?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Wrist singularity (also called **gimbal lock**) is a configuration in which the 3R concurrent wrist loses one effective degree of freedom. It occurs when the middle wrist joint $\theta_5 = 0°$ or $\theta_5 = 180°$ (for ZYZ Euler convention), or $\theta_5 = \pm 90°$ (for RPY convention). At this configuration, the rotation matrix becomes **rank-deficient** (two columns become identical), and the individual values of $\theta_4$ and $\theta_6$ lose physical meaning — only their algebraic sum or difference is observable. Industrial robots typically route the wrist around this zone to avoid control instabilities. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU End-Semester Model Paper **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]** **(a)** For a 6-DOF robot manipulator with a 3R concurrent (spherical) wrist, derive the **ZYZ Euler angle forward kinematic equation** for the wrist sub-chain. Show all intermediate matrix multiplications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given a desired wrist orientation matrix $${}^{3}_{6}R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0.866 & -0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ extract the ZYZ Euler angles $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ using closed-form inverse kinematics. State any singularity conditions if applicable. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(a) **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the elementary rotation matrices $R_z(\theta)$ and $R_y(\theta)$ as given in Section 3.1, Steps A. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the composition ${}^{3}_{6}R = R_z(\theta_4) \cdot R_y(\theta_5) \cdot R_z(\theta_6)$ and perform the first product: $$R_z(\theta_4) \cdot R_y(\theta_5) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_4 c_5 & -s_4 & c_4 s_5 \\ s_4 c_5 & c_4 & s_4 s_5 \\ -s_5 & 0 & c_5 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3 (3 marks):** Post-multiply by $R_z(\theta_6)$ and expand all 9 elements as shown in Section 3.1, Steps C and D, to obtain the final symbolic matrix: $${}^{3}_{6}R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_4 c_5 c_6 - s_4 s_6 & -c_4 c_5 s_6 - s_4 c_6 & c_4 s_5 \\ s_4 c_5 c_6 + c_4 s_6 & -s_4 c_5 s_6 + c_4 c_6 & s_4 s_5 \\ -s_5 c_6 & s_5 s_6 & c_5 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude by stating that this is the **canonical ZYZ forward map** for a 3R concurrent wrist, valid for any spherical-wrist robot. --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(b) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the relevant elements from the given matrix: $r_{11} = -0.5$, $r_{12} = -0.866$, $r_{13} = 0$, $r_{21} = 0.866$, $r_{22} = -0.5$, $r_{23} = 0$, $r_{31} = 0$, $r_{32} = 0$, $r_{33} = 1$. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $\theta_5$ using the formula $\theta_5 = \text{Atan2}(\sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2}, r_{33})$: $$\theta_5 \;=\; \text{Atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{0^2 + 0^2},\; 1\right) \;=\; \text{Atan2}(0, 1) \;=\; 0°$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Identify the singularity. Since $\theta_5 = 0$, the wrist is at the **ZYZ singularity** (gimbal lock). In this case, the joint angles $\theta_4$ and $\theta_6$ cannot be independently resolved — they are **infinitely many solutions** to the equation. The standard convention is to **set $\theta_4 = 0$** and absorb the net rotation into $\theta_6$: $$\theta_4 \;=\; 0°, \quad \theta_6 \;=\; \text{Atan2}(r_{32}, -r_{31}) \;=\; \text{Atan2}(0, 0) \;=\; \text{undefined}$$ By convention, set $\theta_6 = 0$ and recognize that any $(\theta_4, \theta_6)$ pair with $\theta_4 + \theta_6 = 60°$ would be valid (one of the infinitely many equivalent solutions). For instance, $\theta_4 = 0°, \theta_6 = 60°$. **Step 4 (2 marks):** Verify by substituting into the symbolic matrix with $\theta_4 = 0, \theta_5 = 0, \theta_6 = 60°$: $${}^{3}_{6}R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.5 - 0 \cdot 0.866 & -1 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.866 - 0 \cdot 0.5 & 1 \cdot 0 \\ 0 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.5 + 1 \cdot 0.866 & -0 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.866 + 1 \cdot 0.5 & 0 \cdot 0 \\ -0 \cdot 0.5 & 0 \cdot 0.866 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 0 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ This corresponds to a 60° rotation about $Z$, not the given 120° rotation. The correct choice is therefore $\theta_4 + \theta_6 = 120°$ (e.g., $\theta_4 = 0°, \theta_6 = 120°$), which yields the input matrix. Final answer: $\theta_5 = 0°$ (singular), $\theta_4 + \theta_6 = 120°$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Alternative Set]** **(a)** Explain the **kinematic decoupling principle** for a 6-DOF robot with a 3R concurrent wrist. Derive the position equation for the wrist center $O_C$ in terms of the end-effector position $p$, tool length $d_6$, and approach vector $\hat{a}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a PUMA 560-like manipulator with DH parameters for the arm: $a_2 = 0.5$ m, $d_3 = 0.1$ m, and wrist center located at $O_C = (0.4, 0.3, 0.5)$ m, compute the **arm joint angles** $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ using the geometric (planar 2R) approach after projecting onto the shoulder plane. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(a) **Step 1 (2 marks):** Define the kinematic decoupling principle: in a 6-DOF manipulator with spherical wrist, the position of the wrist center $O_C$ (origin of frame $\{3\}$ or $\{4\}$) is a function of the **arm joints** $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ alone, while the orientation of the end-effector is a function of the **wrist joints** $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ alone. This separation allows the inverse kinematics to be solved as two independent sub-problems. **Step 2 (2 marks):** State the position equation. Let the end-effector position be $\mathbf{p}$ and the tool length (along the approach axis) be $d_6$. The wrist center is located at: $$O_C \;=\; \mathbf{p} \;-\; d_6 \cdot \hat{\mathbf{a}}$$ where $\hat{\mathbf{a}} = (a_x, a_y, a_z)^T$ is the unit approach vector of the tool frame, which is the **third column** of the orientation matrix $R = [\, \hat{\mathbf{n}} \mid \hat{\mathbf{o}} \mid \hat{\mathbf{a}} \,]$. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Justify the equation. The end-effector origin is offset from $O_C$ by $d_6$ along the approach axis. Since all wrist joint axes pass through $O_C$, varying $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ rotates the tool but never translates $O_C$. Hence the wrist-center position depends only on the arm joints. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Note that this is the **Paul-Shimano decoupling algorithm** (1977) universally used in KTU-recommended textbooks and the foundation of closed-form IK for PUMA, ABB, and KUKA robots. --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(b) **Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute $\theta_1$ from the projection of $O_C$ onto the $X_0Y_0$ plane: $$\theta_1 \;=\; \text{Atan2}(O_{C,y},\; O_{C,x}) \;=\; \text{Atan2}(0.3,\; 0.4) \;\approx\; 36.87° \;\approx\; 0.6435 \text{ rad}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the radial and vertical distances in the shoulder plane: $$r \;=\; \sqrt{O_{C,x}^2 + O_{C,y}^2} \;=\; \sqrt{0.16 + 0.09} \;=\; \sqrt{0.25} \;=\; 0.5 \text{ m}$$ $$h \;=\; O_{C,z} \;=\; 0.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the cosine rule in the 2R planar arm (with link lengths $a_2 = 0.5$ m, $d_3 = 0.1$ m): For a standard 2R planar linkage with the wrist offset $d_3$ along the last link's $X$-axis, the effective "reach" to the wrist center from the shoulder is: $$D \;=\; \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} \;=\; \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} \;=\; \sqrt{0.5} \;\approx\; 0.7071 \text{ m}$$ $$\cos\theta_3 \;=\; \frac{D^2 - a_2^2 - d_3^2}{2 a_2 d_3} \;=\; \frac{0.5 - 0.25 - 0.01}{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.1} \;=\; \frac{0.24}{0.1} \;=\; 2.4$$ This is **infeasible** (cosine > 1), indicating the wrist center is **out of the workspace** for the given link lengths. The student should note this and conclude that no valid solution exists for the requested configuration. **(0 marks for invalid angle, but full credit for detecting infeasibility.)** **Step 4 (1 mark):** State the lesson: the decoupling algorithm requires **pre-validation** of the wrist-center reachability against the arm's kinematic workspace before attempting to solve for joint angles. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Skipping the singularity check:** Many students extract $\theta_4$ and $\theta_6$ blindly even when $r_{33} = \pm 1$ (ZYZ) or $r_{31} = \pm 1$ (RPY). This loses **at least 1 mark** for not recognizing gimbal lock. > 2. **Sign errors in $r_{32}$ vs. $-r_{31}$:** The argument order of the $\text{Atan2}$ function is **strictly** $(r_{32}, -r_{31})$ for ZYZ — mixing this up gives a sign-flipped $\theta_6$. > 3. **Forgetting the dual solution:** $\theta_5$ has **two valid values** ($\pm$); a complete answer must mention both the "elbow-up" and "elbow-down" configurations. > 4. **Not justifying "why concurrent":** When asked to define a spherical wrist, students often describe it but fail to **justify the decoupling benefit** in 1–2 lines. Always include the equation $O_C = p - d_6 \hat{a}$. > 5. **Mixing ZYZ and RPY conventions:** Picking the wrong extraction formulas for the given rotation order is a **disastrous 4–5 mark loss** in the ESE. Always re-derive from first principles if unsure. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] A **3R concurrent wrist** has all three revolute axes intersecting at a single point $O_C$ (the wrist center). - [ ] The property is also called a **spherical wrist** or **Euler wrist**. - [ ] **Kinematic decoupling** separates the 6-DOF IK into a **position sub-problem** (3 arm joints) and an **orientation sub-problem** (3 wrist joints). - [ ] Wrist center position: $O_C = \mathbf{p} - d_6 \hat{\mathbf{a}}$, where $\hat{\mathbf{a}}$ is the third column of the rotation matrix. - [ ] The DH parameters $a_4 = a_5 = a_6 = 0$ and $d_5 = 0$ are the structural signature of concurrency. - [ ] **ZYZ forward map** of the wrist: a 3×3 matrix with elements built from $c_4, s_4, c_5, s_5, c_6, s_6$ (see Section 3.1, Step E). - [ ] **ZYZ inverse map**: $\theta_5 = \text{Atan2}(\sqrt{r_{13}^2 + r_{23}^2}, r_{33})$; $\theta_4 = \text{Atan2}(r_{23}, r_{13})$; $\theta_6 = \text{Atan2}(r_{32}, -r_{31})$. - [ ] **RPY (ZYX) inverse map**: $\phi = \text{Atan2}(r_{21}, r_{11})$; $\theta = \text{Atan2}(-r_{31}, \sqrt{r_{11}^2 + r_{21}^2})$; $\psi = \text{Atan2}(r_{32}, r_{33})$. - [ ] **Singularity** (ZYZ): $\theta_5 = 0°$ or $180°$ — gimbal lock, infinite IK solutions. - [ ] **Singularity** (RPY): $\theta = \pm 90°$ — gimbal lock, similar degeneracy. - [ ] Industrial examples: PUMA 560, ABB IRB, KUKA KR — all use 3R concurrent wrists. - [ ] Surgical robots (Da Vinci) exploit the wrist center as a **remote-center-of-motion** for minimally invasive tools. - [ ] Always verify the **workspace feasibility** of the wrist center before solving IK — out-of-reach cases are common exam traps. - [ ] When the given matrix is a pure $Z$-rotation, expect singularity; choose the convention $\theta_4 = 0$ and absorb into $\theta_6$. - [ ] The decoupling approach is the **Paul-Shimano (1977) algorithm** — cite this name in ESE answers for examiner brownie points. - [ ] Common exam trap: confusing **end-effector position $\mathbf{p}$** with **wrist-center position $O_C$** — they differ by the tool length $d_6$.

topics.title: Classification of End Effectors - Mechanical Grippers, Special Tools, Magnetic Grippers, Vacuum Grippers, Adhesive Grippers, Active and Passive Grippers, Selection and Design Considerations of Grippers in Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of End Effectors - Mechanical Grippers, Special Tools, Magnetic Grippers, Vacuum Grippers, Adhesive Grippers, Active and Passive Grippers, Selection and Design Considerations of Grippers in Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of End Effector

An **End Effector** is the device attached to the free end (wrist) of a robotic manipulator that interacts directly with the workpiece or the environment to perform the intended task. It is the functional "business end" of the robot — analogous to a human hand fitted with the appropriate tool for a specific job.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** End effectors are broadly classified into two categories:
> 1. **Grippers (Mechanical / Special-purpose)** — used for grasping, holding, and manipulating objects.
> 2. **Tools (Process End Effectors)** — used for carrying out manufacturing processes such as welding, painting, drilling, deburring, etc.

A **Gripper** is a specialized end effector whose primary function is to grasp, hold, lift, transport, and release workpieces of varying shape, size, and material without causing damage to the surface.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Human Hand**
> Consider a human hand performing different tasks. The *hand* (palm + fingers) acts as a **mechanical gripper** when it lifts a cup. When you attach a *screwdriver* to that hand, it becomes a **special tool**. When you press a *suction cup* against a glass, you are mimicking a **vacuum gripper**. When you use a *magnet* to pick up a pin, you replicate a **magnetic gripper**. Just as the human hand can be re-equipped with various tools, the robot wrist can be fitted with different end effectors — making it a flexible, reprogrammable manipulator in a manufacturing cell.

The end effector is usually mounted on the **flange** of the robot's last axis (Axis 6 in a 6-DOF articulated robot), and its design is heavily influenced by the **task**, **workpiece geometry**, **material**, **mass**, and **production cycle time**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Favourite Terminology)

The following engineering parameters are the **standard evaluation metrics** examiners expect students to recognize and use:

- **Payload (P):** The maximum mass the end effector can reliably hold, expressed in **kg** or **N**.
- **Gripping Force (F_grip):** The clamping force applied per jaw on the workpiece, in **N**.
- **Finger Stroke (S):** The maximum opening/closing travel of the gripper jaws, in **mm**.
- **Cycle Time (t_c):** Time to complete one open–close–grip–release cycle, in **seconds**.
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** A dimensionless property between the finger pad and workpiece material.
- **Safety Factor (n):** Typically between **1.5 and 3.0**, used to multiply the theoretical minimum grip force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Default assumptions for KTU numericals:** Standard gravity **g = 9.81 m/s²**, friction coefficient values are usually tabulated (e.g., rubber on steel: μ ≈ 0.7, steel on steel: μ ≈ 0.4, soft jaw on glass: μ ≈ 0.2).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of End Effectors

End effectors are classified structurally and functionally as follows:

**A. By Function / Task Performed:**

1. **Mechanical Grippers (Grasping Type)**
   - Two-finger (parallel jaw) grippers
   - Three-finger (centric / angular) grippers
   - Multi-finger (anthropomorphic) grippers
2. **Special Tools (Process Type)**
   - Welding torches (Spot, MIG, TIG, Plasma)
   - Paint spray guns
   - Drilling spindles / Deburring tools
   - Hot-air / flame guns
   - Cutting torches (laser, water-jet, plasma)
3. **Magnetic Grippers**
   - Electromagnetic (energize-deenergize)
   - Permanent magnet (mechanical switch ON/OFF)
4. **Vacuum Grippers**
   - Single-cup and multi-cup arrays
5. **Adhesive Grippers**
   - Gecko-inspired dry adhesive / micro-spine arrays

**B. By Actuation / Control Mode:**

1. **Active Grippers** — Externally powered (pneumatic, hydraulic, electric, magnetic). Force and position are *actively controlled*.
2. **Passive Grippers** — Rely on stored mechanical energy (springs, gravity, compliance) and *no external power* is needed to maintain grip.

### 2.2 Mechanical Grippers — Deep Analysis

Mechanical grippers are the most common type. They use mechanical jaws (fingers) to clamp the workpiece by applying normal force. Gripping action is achieved by converting input energy from an actuator into jaw motion.

**Sub-classification of Mechanical Grippers:**

- **Cam-Operated Gripper:** A cam mechanism transforms rotational motion of the actuator into linear jaw motion. Simple, self-locking, but limited stroke.
- **Wedge / Scissor Gripper:** A wedge slides between angled surfaces, producing symmetrical jaw closure. High force amplification.
- **Toggle-Linkage Gripper:** Uses a knee-joint toggle mechanism. Near the toggle's dead-centre, the mechanical advantage becomes very high, producing large gripping forces from a small actuator input. Highly preferred in KTU problems.
- **Screw / Lead-Screw Gripper:** Rotary screw motion is converted to linear jaw motion. Self-locking, high force, but slow.
- **Spring-Loaded (Passive) Gripper:** Grip is maintained by stored spring energy; release requires actuator input.
- **Three-Finger (Centric) Gripper:** Three jaws close on the central axis, ideal for cylindrical workpieces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mechanical Grippers Dominate:** They offer the highest reliability, repeatable positioning, ability to handle a wide range of geometries (with changeable fingers), and are insensitive to surface flatness compared to vacuum/magnetic types.

### 2.3 Special Tools — Process End Effectors

These end effectors **do not grasp** the workpiece; instead, they perform a manufacturing process on it.

- **Welding Torches:** Carry electrical current and shielding gas; payload = weight of the torch itself.
- **Paint Spray Guns:** Atomize paint using compressed air; electrostatic versions improve transfer efficiency.
- **Drilling / Tapping Spindles:** Carries a rotating tool; needs high stiffness and accuracy.
- **Deburring Tools:** Compliant spindles that follow edges to remove burrs.
- **Cutting Tools:** Laser, plasma, or water-jet nozzles mounted on the wrist.

> [!NOTE]
> For process tools, the design emphasis shifts from "grip force" to "stiffness, accuracy, reach, and tool-center-point (TCP) calibration."

### 2.4 Magnetic Grippers

Used primarily for **ferromagnetic workpieces** (steel, iron). Two main types:

- **Electromagnetic Gripper:** A DC-excited coil generates magnetic flux. Energize → grip; de-energize → release. Continuous power consumption; risk of workpiece drop on power failure.
- **Permanent Magnet Gripper:** Uses permanent magnets with a movable flux-concentrator. Mechanical knob or pneumatic actuator moves the flux path to switch grip ON/OFF. **Fail-safe** — grip remains even during power loss.

**Force equation (per unit area of contact):**
$$F_{mag} = \frac{B^{2} \cdot A}{2\mu_{0}}$$

where **B** is flux density (T), **A** is pole-face area (m²), **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**.

### 2.5 Vacuum Grippers

Used for **smooth, flat, non-porous** workpieces (glass sheets, PCBs, cardboard boxes). A vacuum pump or Venturi ejector reduces the pressure inside a flexible elastomer cup. Atmospheric pressure pushes the cup against the workpiece, generating the holding force.

**Force equation:**
$$F_{vac} = \Delta P \cdot A_{cup}$$

where **ΔP = P_atm − P_internal** (Pa) and **A_cup** is the effective cup area (m²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Limitation: Vacuum grippers fail on **porous, rough, or perforated** surfaces. They are also sensitive to altitude (lower atmospheric pressure reduces the available differential).

### 2.6 Adhesive Grippers

Bio-inspired by **gecko feet** and certain insect pads. The grip relies on **van der Waals forces** generated by arrays of microscopic fibres (micro-spines or mushroom-shaped pillars). They can grip smooth or even rough surfaces **without** suction, glue, or external power.

**Advantages:** No power required, residue-free, gentle on fragile surfaces, works in vacuum (NASA applications).
**Limitation:** Lower holding force per unit area compared to mechanical/magnetic grippers.

### 2.7 Active vs Passive Grippers — Operational Logic

| Feature | Active Gripper | Passive Gripper |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | External (pneumatic, electric, hydraulic) | Mechanical spring / gravity / compliance |
| **Holding Force** | Continuously variable via control | Fixed (determined by spring constant) |
| **Failure Mode** | Lose force if power fails (unless fail-safe) | Fail-safe (spring holds the workpiece) |
| **Complexity** | High | Low |
| **Cost** | High | Low |
| **KTU Example** | Pneumatic two-finger gripper | Spring-closed, pneumatic-open gripper |

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Two-finger gripper minimum grip force | $F_{grip} \geq \dfrac{m(g+a)}{2\mu n}$ | m = mass (kg), a = accel. (m/s²), μ = friction coeff., n = safety factor |
| 2 | Two-finger gripper holding (vertical lift) | $F_{grip} \geq \dfrac{W}{2\mu}$ | W = weight (N) |
| 3 | Magnetic holding force (per pole pair) | $F_{mag} = \dfrac{B^{2} A}{2\mu_{0}}$ | B (T), A (m²), μ₀ (H/m) |
| 4 | Vacuum holding force | $F_{vac} = \Delta P \cdot A_{cup}$ | ΔP (Pa), A_cup (m²) |
| 5 | Pneumatic piston gripping force | $F_{piston} = P_{air} \cdot A_{piston}$ | P_air (Pa), A_piston (m²) |
| 6 | Number of vacuum cups required | $N_{cups} = \left\lceil \dfrac{m(g+a) \cdot n}{F_{vac,\,single}} \right\rceil$ | Ceiling of safety-factored demand |
| 7 | Toggle mechanism mechanical advantage | $MA = \dfrac{1}{2\tan(\theta/2)}$ | θ = toggle included angle (deg) |
| 8 | Cam gripper velocity ratio | $VR = \dfrac{\omega_{cam}}{\dot{x}_{jaw}}$ | ω (rad/s), ẋ_jaw (m/s) |
| 9 | Spring-closure grip force | $F_{spring} = k \cdot \delta$ | k (N/m), δ = compression (m) |
| 10 | Payload (W) of gripper system | $W = m \cdot g$ | m (kg), g = 9.81 m/s² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Table-Formatting:** The vertical bar symbol `|` is intentionally avoided inside the formulae above. Use `\vert` or `\mid` whenever an absolute value or division must be expressed inside a markdown table.

### 2.9 Selection & Design Considerations — Decision Matrix

The selection of an end effector for a given robotic task is governed by the following engineering criteria (frequently tested in KTU short-answer and 14-mark questions):

1. **Workpiece geometry & weight** — shape, size, surface finish, mass.
2. **Material compatibility** — magnetic, porous, brittle, hot, oily.
3. **Required gripping force** — function of weight + inertial + safety factor.
4. **Production environment** — temperature, humidity, presence of dust/fluids, cleanroom, explosive atmosphere.
5. **Cycle time & throughput** — influences actuator type (pneumatic is fastest, hydraulic has highest force, electric is most precise).
6. **Compliance & surface protection** — soft finger pads vs hard jaws.
7. **Power & control availability** — dictates active vs passive.
8. **Cost, maintainability, and standardisation** — modular fingers vs custom tool.
9. **Robot payload capacity** — end effector mass must be ≤ robot's rated payload minus workpiece mass.
10. **Repeatability and accuracy** — affects TCP calibration and part placement tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Heuristic:** A design is considered acceptable only if the end effector's **mass + workpiece mass ≤ robot's payload**, and the **maximum gripping force ≤ the workpiece's crush / deformation limit** to prevent damage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Gripping Force for a Two-Finger Parallel-Jaw Gripper

**Problem Setup:** A two-finger gripper must lift a workpiece of mass *m* vertically while the robot arm accelerates upward at *a*. Each finger applies a normal force *F_N* on a vertical face. Friction coefficient between finger pad and workpiece is *μ*. A safety factor *n* is applied.

**Step 1 — Identify forces acting on the workpiece in the vertical (lift) direction:**

Two upward friction forces from the two jaws:
$$F_{friction,\,total} = 2 \cdot \mu \cdot F_N$$

One downward weight force:
$$W = m \cdot g$$

One downward inertial force (D'Alembert) due to upward acceleration *a*:
$$F_{inertia} = m \cdot a$$

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium of the workpiece in the vertical direction (so it neither slips up nor down):**

The two friction forces must balance both the weight and the inertial force:
$$2 \cdot \mu \cdot F_N = m \cdot g + m \cdot a$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the normal force per finger:**

$$F_N = \dfrac{m(g + a)}{2\mu}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the safety factor (n ≥ 1):**

To prevent slip under varying conditions (vibration, oily surface, wear), multiply the theoretical force by the safety factor:
$$F_{grip} = n \cdot F_N = \dfrac{n \cdot m(g + a)}{2\mu}$$

**Step 5 — Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{F_{grip} \geq \dfrac{n \cdot m(g + a)}{2\mu}}$$

This is the **most frequently tested derivation** in KTU Robotics papers.

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Vacuum Cups Required for a Flat Workpiece

**Step 1 — Determine the demand force (weight + inertial + safety):**
$$F_{demand} = n \cdot m(g + a)$$

**Step 2 — Determine the supply force from one vacuum cup:**
$$F_{cup} = \Delta P \cdot A_{cup}$$

**Step 3 — Divide and round up to the next integer (you cannot use a fractional cup):**
$$N_{cups} = \left\lceil \dfrac{n \cdot m(g + a)}{\Delta P \cdot A_{cup}} \right\rceil$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ceiling function `⌈ ⌉` is essential — KTU problems will accept the next whole number; choosing a fraction indicates a design error.

### 3.3 Derivation: Toggle-Linkage Mechanical Advantage

A toggle linkage consists of two links of equal length *L* joined at the slider, making an included angle of 2θ with the line of actuator force *F_actuator*.

**Step 1 — Resolve the actuator force along each link:**

Each link carries an axial compressive force *F_link*:
$$F_{link} = \dfrac{F_{actuator}}{2\cos\theta}$$

**Step 2 — Resolve the link force into the horizontal (clamping) direction:**

The horizontal component of *F_link* closes the jaws:
$$F_{clamp} = F_{link} \cdot \cos\theta = \dfrac{F_{actuator}}{2\cos\theta} \cdot \cos\theta = \dfrac{F_{actuator}}{2\cos^{2}\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Define Mechanical Advantage (MA):**
$$MA = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{F_{actuator}} = \dfrac{1}{2\cos^{2}\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent form (using included angle 2θ):**

$$\boxed{MA = \dfrac{1}{2\cos^{2}\theta} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \cos(2\theta)}}$$

**Step 5 — Limiting case:** As the toggle approaches straight alignment (θ → 0), MA → ∞. This is why toggles are preferred for high-force grippers.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Question:** A robot gripper must pick a steel part of mass 5 kg. The robot accelerates upward at 2 m/s² during the lift. The friction coefficient between the rubber finger pads and the steel part is 0.7. Calculate the minimum gripping force per finger, using a safety factor of 2.

**Given:** m = 5 kg, a = 2 m/s², g = 9.81 m/s², μ = 0.7, n = 2

**Step 1 — Compute weight + inertia term:**
$$m(g + a) = 5 \times (9.81 + 2) = 5 \times 11.81 = 59.05 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**
$$F_{grip} = \dfrac{n \cdot m(g + a)}{2\mu} = \dfrac{2 \times 59.05}{2 \times 0.7} = \dfrac{118.1}{1.4} = 84.36 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Answer:**

$$\boxed{F_{grip,\,per\,finger} \approx 84.36 \text{ N}}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Gripper Types

| Property | Mechanical | Magnetic | Vacuum | Adhesive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Workpiece material** | Most solids | Ferromagnetic only | Smooth, non-porous | Smooth to slightly rough |
| **Holding force per area** | Very high | High | Moderate | Low–Moderate |
| **Power needed?** | Yes (active) | Yes (electromag.) / No (PM) | Yes (pump) | No |
| **Fail-safe?** | Possible (spring) | No (electromag.) / Yes (PM) | No | Yes |
| **Speed** | Fast (pneumatic) | Fast | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Cleanroom OK?** | With care | Yes | Limited | Yes |
| **Cost** | Medium | Medium | Medium–High | High (new tech) |
| **KTU Mark weight** | High | High | High | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
