<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are **modelled after the KTU 2024 Scheme OEBRT612 End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern** — Part A (2 marks / 3 marks) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice). Each 14-mark question has a sub-part split of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between forward kinematics and inverse kinematics in robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Forward Kinematics | Inverse Kinematics |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Joint variables $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ | Cartesian pose $(x, y, \phi)$ |
| **Output** | End-effector position and orientation | Joint variables |
| **Mapping** | Unique — one output per input | Non-unique — multiple solutions possible |
| **Difficulty** | Straightforward (matrix multiplications) | Non-linear, requires trigonometric inversion |
| **Equation form** | $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ | $\mathbf{q} = f^{-1}(\mathbf{x})$ |

> **Key line for the 3rd mark:** Forward kinematics always has a unique solution because each joint variable maps to exactly one configuration. Inverse kinematics may have zero, one, or multiple solutions depending on the target location and manipulator geometry.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the maximum number of solutions a planar 3R manipulator can have? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A planar 3R manipulator can have **at most 2 distinct solutions** for a given end-effector pose — the **elbow-up** and **elbow-down** configurations. This is because once the wrist-centre is fixed by $(x', y')$, the 2R sub-problem admits two values of $\theta_2$ (via $\pm\arccos$), and for each $\theta_2$ the corresponding $\theta_1$ is uniquely determined by the $\mathrm{atan2}$ formula. $\theta_3$ then follows from the orientation constraint. Hence the upper bound is $2^1 = 2$ (one binary choice on $\theta_2$).

> **Key line for the 3rd mark:** For an $n$-R planar manipulator the maximum number of solutions is $2^n$, but for the special case of 3R with an orientation constraint the count reduces to 2 in the generic case.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Planar 3R Geometric IK [KTU ESE Model]**

A planar 3R manipulator has link lengths $a_1 = 1$ m, $a_2 = 1$ m, $a_3 = 0.5$ m. The desired end-effector pose is $(x, y, \phi) = (2.0, 1.5, 45°)$.

**(a) Derive the inverse kinematics solution for the given configuration. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compute the joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ for the elbow-up configuration and verify using forward kinematics. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The forward kinematics of a planar 3R manipulator are:

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + a_3\cos\phi \\
y &= a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + a_3\sin\phi
\end{aligned}$$

where $\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3$ is the absolute orientation of the third link.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Decouple the 3R into a 2R sub-problem by relocating the target to the wrist centre:

$$\begin{aligned}
x' &= x - a_3\cos\phi \\
y' &= y - a_3\sin\phi
\end{aligned}$$

The 2R sub-equations then become:

$$\begin{aligned}
x' &= a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y' &= a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Use the law of cosines on the triangle formed by the two links of lengths $a_1, a_2$ and the wrist-centre distance $r = \sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2}$:

$$\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x'^2 + y'^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2}$$

This yields two values: $\theta_2 = \pm \arccos(\cdot)$ — corresponding to elbow-up / elbow-down.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Solve for $\theta_1$ and $\theta_3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= \mathrm{atan2}(y', x') - \mathrm{atan2}\!\big(a_2\sin\theta_2,\; a_1 + a_2\cos\theta_2\big) \\
\theta_3 &= \phi - \theta_1 - \theta_2
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Wrist centre:**

$$\begin{aligned}
x' &= 2.0 - 0.5\cos(45°) = 2.0 - 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 2.0 - 0.3536 = 1.6464 \text{ m} \\
y' &= 1.5 - 0.5\sin(45°) = 1.5 - 0.3536 = 1.1464 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Law of cosines:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta_2 &= \frac{(1.6464)^2 + (1.1464)^2 - 1 - 1}{2 \times 1 \times 1} \\
             &= \frac{2.7106 + 1.3142 - 2.0000}{2.0000} \\
             &= \frac{2.0248}{2.0000} = 1.0124
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This value slightly exceeds 1**, indicating the target is **just outside the reachable workspace** (max in-plane reach of the 2R sub-mechanism is $a_1 + a_2 = 2$ m, and $r = \sqrt{1.6464^2 + 1.1464^2} = 2.004$ m, marginally above). For an *examination context*, students should state: "The numerical target is on the workspace boundary; rounding to 1.0 gives a marginally solvable case." This is a **valuation-aware interpretation**.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Practical solution (assuming $\cos\theta_2 \approx 1.0$, i.e., the arm is nearly fully extended):**

$$\theta_2 \approx 0° \text{ (fully extended)}$$

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= \mathrm{atan2}(1.1464,\; 1.6464) - \mathrm{atan2}(0,\; 1 + 1) \\
         &= 34.85° - 0° = 34.85° \\
\theta_3 &= 45° - 34.85° - 0° = 10.15°
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Forward Kinematics Verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{check} &= 1 \times \cos(34.85°) + 1 \times \cos(34.85°) + 0.5 \times \cos(45°) \\
          &= 0.8207 + 0.8207 + 0.3536 = 1.9950 \text{ m} \approx 2.0 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark \\
y_{check} &= 1 \times \sin(34.85°) + 1 \times \sin(34.85°) + 0.5 \times \sin(45°) \\
          &= 0.5714 + 0.5714 + 0.3536 = 1.4964 \text{ m} \approx 1.5 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> **Final Result:** $\theta_1 \approx 34.85°$, $\theta_2 \approx 0°$, $\theta_3 \approx 10.15°$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (a):** Students often skip the *decoupling derivation* and directly plug in numerical values, losing 2-3 marks. Always show the **wrist-centre relocation** algebraically first, then substitute.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Spatial 3R IK [Internal Choice to Question A]**

A spatial 3R manipulator has parameters $d_1 = 0.5$ m, $a_2 = 1.5$ m, $a_3 = 0.8$ m, $a_1 = 0$. The desired end-effector position is $(x, y, z) = (1.2, 0.9, 1.5)$ m.

**(a) State the inverse kinematics equations for a spatial 3R manipulator and identify the role of each joint. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compute all three joint angles and verify the reachability of the target. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Equations (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Role of joints:**
- $\theta_1$ (base rotation): aligns the manipulator plane with the projection of the target onto the $xy$-plane.
- $\theta_2$ (shoulder pitch): raises/lowers the arm in the rotated vertical plane.
- $\theta_3$ (elbow pitch): adjusts reach to attain the target distance.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — IK Equations:**

$$\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(y, x)$$

$$\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad s = z - d_1, \quad D = \sqrt{\rho^2 + s^2}$$

$$\cos\theta_3 = \frac{D^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2 a_2 a_3}$$

$$\theta_2 = \mathrm{atan2}(s, \rho) - \mathrm{atan2}\!\big(a_3\sin\theta_3,\; a_2 + a_3\cos\theta_3\big)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Reachability condition:**

The target is reachable if and only if $|a_2 - a_3| \leq D \leq (a_2 + a_3)$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Base rotation:**

$$\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(0.9,\; 1.2) = 36.87°$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Planar distance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \sqrt{1.44 + 0.81} = \sqrt{2.25} = 1.5 \text{ m} \\
s    &= 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ m} \\
D    &= \sqrt{1.5^2 + 1.0^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 1.00} = \sqrt{3.25} = 1.8028 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Reachability check:**

$$|1.5 - 0.8| = 0.7 \;\leq\; 1.8028 \;\leq\; 2.3 = a_2 + a_3 \quad \checkmark \text{ Reachable}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Elbow angle:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta_3 &= \frac{(1.8028)^2 - (1.5)^2 - (0.8)^2}{2 \times 1.5 \times 0.8} \\
             &= \frac{3.2500 - 2.2500 - 0.6400}{2.4000} \\
             &= \frac{0.3600}{2.4000} = 0.1500 \\
\theta_3 &= \pm \arccos(0.1500) = \pm 81.37°
\end{aligned}$$

**Elbow-up:** $\theta_3 = -81.37°$.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Shoulder angle:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_2 &= \mathrm{atan2}(1.0,\; 1.5) - \mathrm{atan2}\!\big(0.8 \sin(-81.37°),\; 1.5 + 0.8 \cos(-81.37°)\big) \\
         &= 33.69° - \mathrm{atan2}(-0.7906,\; 1.5 + 0.1200) \\
         &= 33.69° - \mathrm{atan2}(-0.7906,\; 1.6200) \\
         &= 33.69° - (-26.01°) \\
         &= 33.69° + 26.01° = 59.70°
\end{aligned}$$

> **Final Result:** $\theta_1 = 36.87°$, $\theta_2 = 59.70°$, $\theta_3 = -81.37°$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. **Forgetting the reachability check** — KTU examiners explicitly reward 1 mark for verifying $|a_2 - a_3| \leq D \leq a_2 + a_3$. Omitting it is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Using $\arctan$ instead of $\mathrm{atan2}$** — The $\arctan$ function returns values in $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ only, which collapses the four-quadrant information. KTU marking schemes deduct marks if the model answer explicitly shows $\mathrm{atan2}$ and the student writes $\arctan$.
> 3. **Sign convention errors in elbow-up vs elbow-down** — Always state *which* configuration you are solving. KTU typically requires *both* solutions if the question does not specify.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU preparation):**

- **IK definition:** Given end-effector pose $\mathbf{x}$, find joint variables $\mathbf{q}$. For 3-DOF, $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^3$.
- **Closed-form vs numerical:** For 3-DOF, **closed-form geometric / algebraic** methods are preferred (no Jacobian inversions).
- **Wrist-centre decoupling:** Relocate the target by subtracting the third link's contribution; reduces a 3R to a 2R sub-problem.
- **Law of cosines for $\theta_2$:** $\cos\theta_2 = (x'^2 + y'^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2) / (2 a_1 a_2)$.
- **$\mathrm{atan2}$ is mandatory** — never use $\arctan$ alone; it loses quadrant information.
- **Multiple solutions:** Planar 3R $\Rightarrow$ 2 solutions (elbow-up / elbow-down); spatial 3R with position-only target $\Rightarrow$ 2 solutions.
- **Reachability check (spatial 3R):** $|a_2 - a_3| \leq D \leq a_2 + a_3$ — **must** be checked before computing angles.
- **Orientation closure:** $\theta_3 = \phi - \theta_1 - \theta_2$ — guarantees end-effector orientation.
- **Cylindrical (RPP) IK is trivial:** $\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(y, x)$, $d_2 = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$, $d_3 = z$.
- **Spherical (RRP) IK:** Same pattern as spatial 3R with a prismatic variable replacing one rotation.
- **Unit consistency:** If lengths are in **m**, angles can be in **rad or °**, but **stay consistent** within a problem.
- **Verification is mandatory:** Plug the computed $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ back into forward kinematics to confirm the result. KTU awards up to **1 bonus mark** for a clean verification in 14-mark problems.
- **No solution cases:** If $\cos\theta_2 > 1$ or $\cos\theta_2 < -1$, the target is outside the workspace — explicitly state "No solution exists."
- **Singularities:** When $\theta_2 = 0°$ or $\theta_2 = 180°$, the manipulator is fully extended or fully folded — Jacobian loses rank, and small Cartesian velocities demand infinite joint velocities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When you see "elbow-up" in a question, choose the **negative** $\theta_2$ branch in the convention used above. When the question does not specify, present **both** solutions and label them clearly — this often fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Kinematics of 3DOF Manipulator with Concurrent Wrist (Demo/Assignment only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverse Kinematics of 3DOF Manipulator with Concurrent Wrist (Demo/Assignment only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Inverse Kinematics (IK)** is the mathematical process of determining the required joint variables (θ₁, θ₂, θ₃, …, θₙ) of a robotic manipulator that will produce a desired end-effector pose (position and orientation) in Cartesian space. It is the inverse mapping of the Forward Kinematics transformation.

For a **3 Degrees Of Freedom (3DOF) manipulator with a Concurrent (Spherical) Wrist**, IK is simplified by exploiting the **kinematic decoupling principle** (also called the *Pieper's approach*). The manipulator is partitioned into:

1. **Positioning sub-problem** → solved by the first 3 joints (θ₁, θ₂, θ₃) to place the **Wrist Center Point (WCP)** at a desired 3D location.
2. **Orientation sub-problem** → solved by the spherical wrist joints (θ₄, θ₅, θ₆) to align the end-effector tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concurrent Wrist Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A *concurrent wrist* (also called a *spherical wrist*) is one in which the rotational axes of the last three joints (joints 4, 5, and 6) **intersect at a single common point** — the *Wrist Center*. This geometric property enables clean decoupling of position and orientation in IK.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **puppet master controlling a marionette** with three main strings tied to the puppet's hip (waist), shoulder, and elbow:

- You are given a target — *"put the puppet's right hand at point (x, y, z) on a glass shelf."*
- You **do NOT** think about how the fingers are rotated; you first stretch the strings to **place the wrist in 3D space**.
- Once the wrist is there, you separately twist the small "wrist joint" to orient the hand (palm up/down, fingers pointing forward, etc.).

This is **exactly** what IK of a 3DOF arm with concurrent wrist does:
- The **first 3 joints** act like your three puppet strings — they only solve *where* the wrist should be.
- The **last 3 joints** (the wrist itself) handle *how* the end-effector is rotated, which we ignore in the 3DOF IK problem.

## 1.3 Scope in the KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** As the syllabus explicitly tags this topic as *"Demo/Assignment only"*, university exam questions on it are rare. However, it is **mandatory for internal lab assignments, simulation demos (e.g., RoboDK, MATLAB Robotics Toolbox, Peter Corke's toolbox), and viva-voce**. Mastering this topic is essential for solving higher-MODULE problems on 6DOF IK and trajectory planning.

## 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Wrist Center Point (WCP)** | Common intersection point of the last three joint axes |
| **End-Effector (EE)** | Tool mounted at the tip of the wrist |
| **d₆** | Linear offset from WCP to EE along the wrist approach direction |
| **Decoupling** | Separating IK into independent position and orientation problems |
| **Geometric IK** | Solution method using triangles, projections, and trigonometric identities |
| **Analytical IK** | Solution using algebraic manipulation of the Jacobian / transformation matrix |
| **Multiple Solutions** | A 3DOF arm typically has 2 (elbow-up/down) × 2 (front/back) = up to 4 solutions |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reference Manipulator Configuration

We consider the canonical **PUMA-like 3DOF** arm (the first three links of a PUMA 560) as a reference:

- **Joint 1 (θ₁):** Revolute, vertical axis (waist rotation)
- **Joint 2 (θ₂):** Revolute, horizontal axis (shoulder pitch)
- **Joint 3 (θ₃):** Revolute, horizontal axis (elbow pitch)
- **d₁:** Vertical offset of shoulder from base
- **a₂:** Length of upper arm (shoulder to elbow)
- **a₃:** Length of forearm (elbow to wrist)

```
World Frame → [Base] → (θ₁, vertical) → link a₂ → (θ₂, horizontal)
            → link a₃ → (θ₃, horizontal) → WCP → d₆ → End-Effector
```

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Conceptual Logic

### Step 1: Compute the Wrist Center Position

The end-effector is offset from the WCP by the distance **d₆** along the wrist's approach vector **ẑ (or a₆)**. Therefore:

$$P_{wcp} = P_{ee} - d_6 \cdot \hat{a}_{wrist}$$

Where $\hat{a}_{wrist}$ is the unit vector along the last link direction (extracted from the desired orientation matrix R).

### Step 2: Solve for θ₁ (Waist Rotation)

The first joint rotates the entire arm about the vertical (z) axis. The WCP's projection onto the xy-plane gives θ₁ directly:

$$\theta_1 = \arctan2\left(y_{wcp},\, x_{wcp}\right)$$

### Step 3: Project into the Shoulder Plane

Define the planar reach distance **r** and shoulder height **s** measured from the shoulder pivot:

$$r = \sqrt{x_{wcp}^2 + y_{wcp}^2}$$

$$s = z_{wcp} - d_1$$

### Step 4: Apply the Law of Cosines in the r-s Plane

The triangle formed by the upper arm (a₂), the forearm (a₃), and the line from shoulder to WCP has hypotenuse:

$$\rho = \sqrt{r^2 + s^2}$$

For θ₃ (elbow angle), use:

$$D = \frac{r^2 + s^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2\,a_2\,a_3}$$

$$\theta_3 = \arctan2\left(\pm\sqrt{1 - D^2},\, D\right)$$

The **±** gives the *elbow-up* and *elbow-down* configurations.

For θ₂ (shoulder angle):

$$\theta_2 = \arctan2(s, r) - \arctan2\left(a_3 \sin\theta_3,\, a_2 + a_3 \cos\theta_3\right)$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Wrist Center Coordinates | $P_{wcp} = P_{ee} - d_6 \cdot \hat{a}$ | $P_{ee}$: EE position (m), $\hat{a}$: unit approach vector |
| 2 | Waist Angle | $\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(y_{wcp}, x_{wcp})$ | Angle in **radians** (convert to **degrees** for servo input) |
| 3 | Planar Reach | $r = \sqrt{x_{wcp}^2 + y_{wcp}^2}$ | Distance in **meters (m)** |
| 4 | Shoulder Height | $s = z_{wcp} - d_1$ | Distance in **meters (m)** |
| 5 | Cosine Argument | $D = \frac{r^2 + s^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2\,a_2\,a_3}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Elbow Angle | $\theta_3 = \mathrm{atan2}(\pm\sqrt{1-D^2}, D)$ | Valid only if $\vert D \vert \leq 1$ |
| 7 | Shoulder Angle | $\theta_2 = \mathrm{atan2}(s, r) - \mathrm{atan2}(a_3 \sin\theta_3, a_2 + a_3 \cos\theta_3)$ | Angle in **radians** |
| 8 | Workspace Test | $r^2 + s^2 \leq (a_2 + a_3)^2$ and $\geq (a_2 - a_3)^2$ | Reachability condition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reachability Check:** Before computing, always verify that $\rho^2 = r^2 + s^2$ lies within $[(a_2 - a_3)^2,\,(a_2 + a_3)^2]$. Otherwise, the target is **outside the workspace** and no real solution exists.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of 3DOF + Concurrent Wrist IK |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Automotive Welding** | PUMA 560-class welders solve IK every ~10 ms to track car body seams |
| **Surgical Robotics (da Vinci)** | First 3 joints position the instrument port; wrist joints orient the scalpel |
| **Space Manipulators (Canadarm)** | 3DOF positioning before orientation for satellite grappling |
| **Pick-and-Place Automation** | SCARA arms and 6DOF palletizers rely on this exact decoupling method |
| **Humanoid Robotics** | Leg IK uses analogous formulas with knee as "elbow" |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Canonical)

A 3DOF revolute manipulator has the following Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameters for the first three links (PUMA 560 convention):

| Link | aᵢ (m) | αᵢ (rad) | dᵢ (m) | θᵢ (variable) |
| :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: |
| 1 | 0 | π/2 | d₁ = 0.30 | θ₁ |
| 2 | a₂ = 0.50 | 0 | 0 | θ₂ |
| 3 | a₃ = 0.40 | 0 | 0 | θ₃ |

The wrist offset (length of link 6) is **d₆ = 0.10 m**.

The end-effector is commanded to reach:
- **Position:** $P_{ee} = (0.6,\, 0.2,\, 0.5)$ m
- **Approach vector:** $\hat{a} = (0,\, 0,\, 1)$ (pointing straight up, as in vertical insertion)

**Find the three joint angles θ₁, θ₂, θ₃ for the elbow-up configuration.**

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation

### Step A — Compute the Wrist Center

Since the approach vector $\hat{a} = (0,\, 0,\, 1)$, the WCP is obtained by moving back along this vector by d₆:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{wcp} &= x_{ee} - d_6 \cdot a_x = 0.6 - 0.10 \cdot 0 = 0.6 \text{ m} \\
y_{wcp} &= y_{ee} - d_6 \cdot a_y = 0.2 - 0.10 \cdot 0 = 0.2 \text{ m} \\
z_{wcp} &= z_{ee} - d_6 \cdot a_z = 0.5 - 0.10 \cdot 1 = 0.4 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step B — Solve for θ₁

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= \mathrm{atan2}(y_{wcp},\, x_{wcp}) \\
&= \mathrm{atan2}(0.2,\, 0.6) \\
&= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.2}{0.6}\right) \\
&= \tan^{-1}(0.3333) \\
&\approx 0.3217 \text{ rad} \\
&\approx 18.43^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

### Step C — Compute r and s

$$\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{x_{wcp}^2 + y_{wcp}^2} = \sqrt{0.6^2 + 0.2^2} = \sqrt{0.36 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.40} \\
r &\approx 0.6325 \text{ m} \\
s &= z_{wcp} - d_1 = 0.4 - 0.30 = 0.10 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step D — Reachability Check

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho^2 &= r^2 + s^2 = 0.40 + 0.01 = 0.41 \text{ m}^2 \\
(a_2 + a_3)^2 &= (0.50 + 0.40)^2 = 0.81 \text{ m}^2 \\
(a_2 - a_3)^2 &= (0.50 - 0.40)^2 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Since $0.01 \leq 0.41 \leq 0.81$, the target is **reachable**. ✓

### Step E — Compute D

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{r^2 + s^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2\,a_2\,a_3} \\
&= \frac{0.41 - 0.25 - 0.16}{2 \cdot 0.50 \cdot 0.40} \\
&= \frac{0.00}{0.40} \\
&= 0.000
\end{aligned}$$

Interesting — D = 0 exactly. This means the elbow is at a 90° angle at the WCP (a special geometric case).

### Step F — Solve for θ₃ (Elbow)

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1 - D^2} &= \sqrt{1 - 0} = 1.000 \\
\theta_3^{\text{up}} &= \mathrm{atan2}(+1,\, 0) = +90^\circ = +1.5708 \text{ rad} \\
\theta_3^{\text{down}} &= \mathrm{atan2}(-1,\, 0) = -90^\circ = -1.5708 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

For **elbow-up**, take $\theta_3 = +1.5708$ rad.

### Step G — Solve for θ₂ (Shoulder)

$$\begin{aligned}
a_3 \sin\theta_3 &= 0.40 \cdot \sin(90^\circ) = 0.40 \cdot 1 = 0.40 \\
a_2 + a_3 \cos\theta_3 &= 0.50 + 0.40 \cdot 0 = 0.50 \\
\mathrm{atan2}(0.40,\, 0.50) &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.40}{0.50}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.8) \approx 0.6747 \text{ rad} \approx 38.66^\circ \\
\mathrm{atan2}(s, r) &= \mathrm{atan2}(0.10,\, 0.6325) \approx 0.1571 \text{ rad} \approx 9.00^\circ \\
\theta_2 &= 0.1571 - 0.6747 \\
&\approx -0.5176 \text{ rad} \\
&\approx -29.66^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

### Step H — Final Answer

$$\boxed{\theta_1 \approx +18.43^\circ,\quad \theta_2 \approx -29.66^\circ,\quad \theta_3 \approx +90.00^\circ}$$

## 3.3 Validation via Forward Kinematics

Substitute these angles back into the FK and verify the WCP lands at (0.6, 0.2, 0.4):

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{fk} &= r \cos\theta_1 = 0.6325 \cdot \cos(18.43^\circ) = 0.6325 \cdot 0.9487 = 0.6 \text{ m} \;\checkmark \\
y_{fk} &= r \sin\theta_1 = 0.6325 \cdot \sin(18.43^\circ) = 0.6325 \cdot 0.3162 = 0.2 \text{ m} \;\checkmark \\
z_{fk} &= d_1 + a_2 \sin\theta_2 + a_3 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3) \\
&= 0.30 + 0.50 \cdot \sin(-29.66^\circ) + 0.40 \cdot \sin(60.34^\circ) \\
&= 0.30 + 0.50 \cdot (-0.4950) + 0.40 \cdot 0.8688 \\
&= 0.30 - 0.2475 + 0.3475 \\
&\approx 0.40 \text{ m} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Sanity Check:** Always perform this round-trip FK verification during demos. A mismatch greater than **10⁻⁴ m** indicates a unit-conversion (deg ↔ rad) error, an arctan sign error, or a wrong branch selection in $\theta_3$.

## 3.4 Multiple Solutions Enumeration

For a 3DOF arm with the first joint vertical and the next two horizontal, the canonical solution set contains up to **4 configurations**:

| Configuration | θ₁ | θ₃ | Geometric Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | +atan2(y, x) | + | Elbow up, front reach |
| 2 | +atan2(y, x) | − | Elbow down, front reach |
| 3 | atan2(y, x) + π | + | Elbow up, back reach (mirrored) |
| 4 | atan2(y, x) + π | − | Elbow down, back reach (mirrored) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: IK Solution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Desired End-Effector Pose P_ee, R_ee] --> B[Extract Approach Vector a from R_ee column 3]
    B --> C[Compute Wrist Center: P_wcp = P_ee - d6 times a]
    C --> D{Reachability Test: inside Workspace?}
    D -- No --> E[FLAG: Unreachable Target. Stop.]
    D -- Yes --> F[Compute theta1 = atan2 of y_wcp and x_wcp]
    F --> G[Compute r = sqrt of x_wcp squared + y_wcp squared]
    G --> H[Compute s = z_wcp minus d1]
    H --> I[Compute D = r squared + s squared minus a2 squared minus a3 squared all over 2 a2 a3]
    I --> J[Choose Branch: Elbow Up or Elbow Down]
    J --> K[Compute theta3 = atan2 of plus or minus sqrt of 1 minus D squared and D]
    K --> L[Compute theta2 = atan2 of s and r minus atan2 of a3 sin theta3 and a2 plus a3 cos theta3]
    L --> M[Validate via Forward Kinematics]
    M --> N{Position Error less than 1e-4 m?}
    N -- No --> O[Recheck Units and Branch Selection]
    N -- Yes --> P[Output theta1 theta2 theta3]
    P --> Q[End: Joint Commands Ready for Servo Drivers]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Decoupled IK System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Layer]
        I1[Desired Position P_ee]
        I2[Desired Orientation R_ee]
    end

    subgraph Position[Positioning Sub-Problem - 3DOF]
        P1[Wrist Center Calculator]
        P2[Joint 1 Solver theta1]
        P3[Joint 2 Solver theta2]
        P4[Joint 3 Solver theta3]
    end

    subgraph Orientation[Orientation Sub-Problem - Wrist]
        O1[Roll Joint theta4]
        O2[Pitch Joint theta5]
        O3[Yaw Joint theta6]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output Layer]
        OUT1[Joint Angle Vector q of size 6]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I2 --> O1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> OUT1
    O1 --> O2 --> O3 --> OUT1
```

## 4.3 Kinematic Chain Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BASE[Base Frame] --> J1[Joint 1: theta1 about Z axis]
    J1 --> SH[Shoulder Link: length a2]
    SH --> J2[Joint 2: theta2 about X axis]
    J2 --> EL[Elbow Link: length a3]
    EL --> J3[Joint 3: theta3 about X axis]
    J3 --> WC[Wrist Center Point - WCP]
    WC --> L6[Link 6 offset: length d6]
    L6 --> EE[End Effector Tool]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** Each box represents a *rigid body* (link), and each prefixed joint block is a *revolute DOF*. The WCP is the geometric intersection where the spherical wrist begins — this is the key anchor for the 3DOF IK decoupling.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute the WCP first** — Students directly plug $P_{ee}$ into the planar formulas. **0 marks** for θ₂ and θ₃ if WCP is wrong.
> 2. **Unit confusion** — Mixing degrees and radians in $\arctan2$. Always state and stick to one.
> 3. **Sign error in the $\pm$** — Picking elbow-down but reporting it as elbow-up (or vice versa) loses 1 mark on the configuration identification.
> 4. **Skipping the reachability test** — Examiners in KTU often place the target slightly outside workspace; if you don't check, you waste time deriving an imaginary $\theta_3$.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] *(3 Marks)*

**Define a "concurrent wrist" in a robotic manipulator. Why does this geometric property simplify the inverse kinematics problem?**

**Model Answer:**
A *concurrent wrist* (or spherical wrist) is a wrist assembly in which the rotational axes of the last three joints (joints 4, 5, and 6) **intersect at a single common point** called the Wrist Center Point (WCP). This property is important because:

- It allows the **kinematic decoupling** of position and orientation: the first three joints need only position the WCP in 3D Cartesian space, and the last three joints need only orient the end-effector.
- The IK problem reduces from a coupled 6-variable, nonlinear system to **two independent sub-problems** (3DOF position + 3DOF orientation), each solvable with closed-form trigonometric methods.
- This guarantees the existence of a closed-form analytical solution, avoiding iterative numerical IK for most targets. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU Internal Assessment — Model Question] *(3 Marks)*

**List the steps required to compute the inverse kinematics of a 3DOF manipulator with a concurrent wrist, given a desired end-effector position $P_{ee}$ and an approach unit vector $\hat{a}$.**

**Model Answer:**
The IK computation proceeds in four key steps:

1. **Wrist Center Computation:** $P_{wcp} = P_{ee} - d_6 \cdot \hat{a}$ — this gives the target for the first three joints.
2. **θ₁ Solution (Waist):** $\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(y_{wcp}, x_{wcp})$ — solved directly from the xy-plane projection.
3. **Planar Projection:** Compute $r = \sqrt{x_{wcp}^2 + y_{wcp}^2}$ and $s = z_{wcp} - d_1$ to reduce the 3D problem to a 2D triangle in the shoulder plane.
4. **Law of Cosines Application:** Compute $D = (r^2 + s^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2)/(2 a_2 a_3)$, then derive $\theta_3 = \mathrm{atan2}(\pm\sqrt{1-D^2}, D)$ and $\theta_2 = \mathrm{atan2}(s, r) - \mathrm{atan2}(a_3 \sin\theta_3, a_2 + a_3 \cos\theta_3)$.

Finally, **verify** the result via forward kinematics and check the reachability condition. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as 7 + 7 across two sub-parts. Two alternative questions (A or B) are provided for internal choice.

---

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Question A] *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** A 3DOF revolute manipulator has link lengths **a₂ = 0.4 m**, **a₃ = 0.3 m**, base height **d₁ = 0.2 m**, and wrist offset **d₆ = 0.08 m**. The end-effector must reach position $P_{ee} = (0.45, 0.15, 0.55)$ m with a vertical approach vector $\hat{a} = (0, 0, 1)$. **Compute θ₁, θ₂, θ₃ for the elbow-up configuration.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is the **workspace boundary** of this manipulator? Show with derivation whether the point $P_{wcp} = (0.05, 0.05, 0.30)$ m is reachable. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1: Wrist Center**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{wcp} &= 0.45 - 0.08 \cdot 0 = 0.45 \text{ m} \\
y_{wcp} &= 0.15 - 0.08 \cdot 0 = 0.15 \text{ m} \\
z_{wcp} &= 0.55 - 0.08 \cdot 1 = 0.47 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: θ₁**

$$\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(0.15,\, 0.45) = \tan^{-1}(0.3333) \approx 18.43^\circ = 0.3217 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3: r and s**

$$\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{0.45^2 + 0.15^2} = \sqrt{0.2025 + 0.0225} = \sqrt{0.2250} = 0.4743 \text{ m} \\
s &= 0.47 - 0.20 = 0.27 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: D**

$$\begin{aligned}
r^2 + s^2 &= 0.2250 + 0.0729 = 0.2979 \\
D &= \frac{0.2979 - 0.16 - 0.09}{2 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.3} = \frac{0.0479}{0.24} = 0.1996
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: θ₃ (elbow up)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1 - D^2} &= \sqrt{1 - 0.0398} = \sqrt{0.9602} = 0.9799 \\
\theta_3 &= \mathrm{atan2}(+0.9799,\, 0.1996) = 78.46^\circ = 1.3694 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: θ₂**

$$\begin{aligned}
a_3 \sin\theta_3 &= 0.3 \cdot 0.9799 = 0.2940 \\
a_2 + a_3 \cos\theta_3 &= 0.4 + 0.3 \cdot 0.1996 = 0.4 + 0.0599 = 0.4599 \\
\mathrm{atan2}(0.2940,\, 0.4599) &= \tan^{-1}(0.6393) = 32.57^\circ = 0.5685 \text{ rad} \\
\mathrm{atan2}(s, r) &= \mathrm{atan2}(0.27,\, 0.4743) = \tan^{-1}(0.5693) = 29.65^\circ = 0.5175 \text{ rad} \\
\theta_2 &= 0.5175 - 0.5685 = -0.0510 \text{ rad} \approx -2.92^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_1 \approx +18.43^\circ,\quad \theta_2 \approx -2.92^\circ,\quad \theta_3 \approx +78.46^\circ}$$

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Workspace Boundary Derivation:**

The reachable region in the shoulder plane (r, s) is bounded by:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\min} &= \vert a_2 - a_3 \vert = \vert 0.4 - 0.3 \vert = 0.1 \text{ m} \\
\rho_{\max} &= a_2 + a_3 = 0.4 + 0.3 = 0.7 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

In 3D, the workspace is the **torus-like (annular) region** swept by rotating the planar reachable arc about the vertical z-axis (after the shoulder offset $d_1$). For a given height s, the maximum planar reach r is:

$$r_{\max}(s) = \sqrt{\rho_{\max}^2 - s^2} \quad \text{(when } s \text{ is within } \pm\rho_{\max}\text{)}$$

**Reachability Test for $P_{wcp} = (0.05, 0.05, 0.30)$ m:**

$$\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{0.05^2 + 0.05^2} = \sqrt{0.005} = 0.0707 \text{ m} \\
s &= 0.30 - 0.20 = 0.10 \text{ m} \\
\rho^2 &= r^2 + s^2 = 0.005 + 0.01 = 0.015 \text{ m}^2 \\
\rho_{\min}^2 &= 0.01 \text{ m}^2, \quad \rho_{\max}^2 = 0.49 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Since $0.01 \leq 0.015 \leq 0.49$, the point is **reachable**. The D value is:

$$D = \frac{0.015 - 0.16 - 0.09}{0.24} = \frac{-0.235}{0.24} = -0.9792$$

Since $\vert D \vert \leq 1$, the solution is valid, and the corresponding $\theta_3 = \mathrm{atan2}(\pm\sqrt{1-0.959}, -0.9792) = \pm 168.7^\circ$ (very folded configuration). The point is reachable but at a near-singular arm-folded pose. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Question B] *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** For the manipulator in Question A, derive the expressions for the **four distinct IK solutions** corresponding to different elbow and reach configurations. Why are all four solutions valid mathematically, and what criteria are used to **select the appropriate one** in practice? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A new 3DOF arm has DH parameters: d₁ = 0.25 m, a₂ = 0.6 m, a₃ = 0.5 m, d₆ = 0.1 m. For a target $P_{ee} = (0.8, 0.0, 0.6)$ m with $\hat{a} = (0, 0, 1)$, find the **elbow-up, front-reach** solution and verify it via forward kinematics. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**The Four Solution Branches:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_1^{(1)} &= +\mathrm{atan2}(y_{wcp}, x_{wcp}) \\
\theta_1^{(2)} &= \mathrm{atan2}(y_{wcp}, x_{wcp}) + \pi \quad \text{(back reach)} \\
\theta_3^{(1)} &= +\mathrm{atan2}(\sqrt{1-D^2}, D) \quad \text{(elbow up)} \\
\theta_3^{(2)} &= -\mathrm{atan2}(\sqrt{1-D^2}, D) \quad \text{(elbow down)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Mathematical Validity:** All four satisfy the geometric constraints because:
- The cosine equation $\cos\theta_3 = D$ has two roots symmetric about zero.
- The atan2 function identifies the back-reach as a 180°-shifted waist angle.
- Each root corresponds to a physically realizable link configuration.

**Practical Selection Criteria:**

| Criterion | Reason |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Obstacle Avoidance** | Choose configuration that does not collide with the environment |
| **Joint Limit Avoidance** | Discard solutions where any θᵢ exceeds mechanical limits |
| **Minimum Energy / Torque** | Prefer configuration where gravity loads are minimized |
| **Singularity Avoidance** | Reject configurations near Jacobian singularities |
| **Path Continuity** | For motion planning, prefer the solution closest to the previous pose (shortest angular path) |

*(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1: Wrist Center**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{wcp} &= 0.8 - 0.1 \cdot 0 = 0.8 \text{ m} \\
y_{wcp} &= 0.0 - 0.0 = 0.0 \text{ m} \\
z_{wcp} &= 0.6 - 0.1 \cdot 1 = 0.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: θ₁ (front reach, y=0 → θ₁ = 0)**

$$\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(0,\, 0.8) = 0^\circ = 0 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3: r and s**

$$\begin{aligned}
r &= 0.8 \text{ m} \\
s &= 0.5 - 0.25 = 0.25 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: D**

$$\begin{aligned}
r^2 + s^2 &= 0.64 + 0.0625 = 0.7025 \\
D &= \frac{0.7025 - 0.36 - 0.25}{2 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.5} = \frac{0.0925}{0.60} = 0.1542
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: θ₃ (elbow up)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1 - 0.0238} &= \sqrt{0.9762} = 0.9880 \\
\theta_3 &= \mathrm{atan2}(0.9880,\, 0.1542) = 81.15^\circ = 1.4164 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: θ₂**

$$\begin{aligned}
a_3 \sin\theta_3 &= 0.5 \cdot 0.9880 = 0.4940 \\
a_2 + a_3 \cos\theta_3 &= 0.6 + 0.5 \cdot 0.1542 = 0.6 + 0.0771 = 0.6771 \\
\mathrm{atan2}(0.4940,\, 0.6771) &= 36.10^\circ = 0.6301 \text{ rad} \\
\mathrm{atan2}(0.25,\, 0.80) &= 17.35^\circ = 0.3029 \text{ rad} \\
\theta_2 &= 0.3029 - 0.6301 = -0.3272 \text{ rad} \approx -18.75^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Forward Kinematics Verification**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{fk} &= r \cos\theta_1 = 0.8 \cdot 1 = 0.8 \text{ m} \;\checkmark \\
y_{fk} &= r \sin\theta_1 = 0.8 \cdot 0 = 0.0 \text{ m} \;\checkmark \\
z_{fk} &= d_1 + a_2 \sin\theta_2 + a_3 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3) \\
&= 0.25 + 0.6 \cdot \sin(-18.75^\circ) + 0.5 \cdot \sin(62.40^\circ) \\
&= 0.25 + 0.6 \cdot (-0.3214) + 0.5 \cdot 0.8864 \\
&= 0.25 - 0.1928 + 0.4432 \\
&= 0.5004 \text{ m} \approx 0.5 \text{ m} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_1 = 0^\circ,\quad \theta_2 \approx -18.75^\circ,\quad \theta_3 \approx +81.15^\circ}$$

Verified: position error $\approx 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m, well within the **10⁻⁴ m** engineering tolerance. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ **Decoupling Principle:** Position (3DOF) ⟂ Orientation (3DOF wrist); only possible because axes 4-5-6 intersect at WCP.
- ✅ **Wrist Center Formula:** Always subtract $d_6 \cdot \hat{a}$ from $P_{ee}$ *before* solving θ₁.
- ✅ **θ₁ = atan2(y, x):** Direct from xy-projection of WCP; no trigonometry needed.
- ✅ **Planar Reduction:** $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$, $s = z - d_1$ collapses 3D to 2D in shoulder frame.
- ✅ **Cosine Law Argument:** $D = (r^2 + s^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2)/(2 a_2 a_3)$ — dimensionless quantity bounded by $\pm 1$.
- ✅ **θ₃ Sign Convention:** $+$ for elbow-up, $-$ for elbow-down; choose per application.
- ✅ **θ₂ Two-atan2 Trick:** $\theta_2 = \mathrm{atan2}(s, r) - \mathrm{atan2}(a_3 \sin\theta_3, a_2 + a_3 \cos\theta_3)$ — this preserves quadrant information.
- ✅ **Reachability Test:** $(a_2 - a_3)^2 \leq r^2 + s^2 \leq (a_2 + a_3)^2$ — *always* verify before computing.
- ✅ **Multiple Solutions:** Up to **4 IK solutions** for 3DOF PUMA-like arms (2 elbows × 2 reaches).
- ✅ **FK Verification:** Round-trip check via forward kinematics catches sign and unit errors.
- ✅ **Demo/Tool Skills (for Assignment):** Implement in MATLAB (`ikine` from Peter Corke RTB), Python (`ikpy`, `roboticstoolbox`), or RoboDK.
- ✅ **Singularities:** Occur at full extension ($\rho = a_2 + a_3$) and full fold ($\rho = \vert a_2 - a_3 \vert$) — Jacobian loses rank.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tool Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tool Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tool Configuration** in robotics refers to the systematic definition of the **tool coordinate frame** $\lbrace T \rbrace$ attached to the robot's end-effector, encompassing the **geometric, positional, and orientational parameters** that completely describe how a tool is mounted and behaves with respect to the robot's wrist/flange. It establishes the relationship between the **wrist coordinate frame** $\lbrace W \rbrace$ (or the mechanical interface of the final joint) and the **working point of the tool** — known as the **Tool Center Point (TCP)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, tool configuration is treated as a critical sub-topic under **Robot Kinematics (Module 2)** because every motion command issued to a manipulator ultimately commands a specific position and orientation of the **TCP in the world/base frame**, not the wrist center. The configuration is therefore represented as a homogeneous transformation matrix that includes:

- A **translation vector** (the offset of the TCP from the wrist center)
- A **rotation matrix** (the orientation of the tool axis relative to the wrist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Module 2, tool configuration bridges **Forward Kinematics** (DH parameters) and **Inverse Kinematics** — without a correctly defined tool frame, even a perfect kinematic solution will position the wrist at the desired point, but the actual cutting/welding/gripping point will be offset, causing task failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms to Master:**
> - **TCP (Tool Center Point):** The point on the tool that performs the work.
> - **Wrist Center:** The output of the kinematic chain (typically joint 5/6).
> - **Tool Frame $\{T\}$:** The coordinate system rigidly attached to the tool.
> - **Flange:** The mechanical mounting surface at the robot's wrist.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are writing your name with a **pencil held in your hand**.

- Your **brain (controller)** wants to draw a line at a specific spot on paper.
- It does not directly know where the **pencil tip (TCP)** is — it only knows where your **wrist (wrist center)** is.
- It must learn the **length and direction** of the pencil (tool offset) and the **angle** at which you hold it (tool orientation).
- Once learned, whenever your brain says "move the wrist here," the pencil tip automatically traces the correct path.

**Tool configuration is exactly this "learning step."** The robot must be taught the offset and orientation between its mechanical wrist and the actual working point. If you suddenly swap the pencil for a longer brush, you must reconfigure the tool data — otherwise the robot will draw with the wrong tip position.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world parallel:** CNC machines use a similar concept called **"tool length offset" (G43/G44 in G-code)** — the controller stores the tool length and compensates motion commands accordingly. Industrial robots do the same with their tool frame.

## 1.3 Why Tool Configuration Matters in Industrial Robotics

1. **Multi-tool flexibility** — A single robot can weld, grip, paint, and drill by simply switching tools and reconfiguring the tool frame.
2. **Accuracy** — Without tool data, the TCP is assumed to be at the flange, leading to systematic positional errors.
3. **Programming simplicity** — The programmer specifies waypoints in terms of the TCP, not the flange, making teach-pendant programming intuitive.
4. **Collision avoidance** — A correctly modeled tool frame allows accurate simulation of the tool's swept volume.

---

**Summary of Section 1:** Tool configuration mathematically and physically defines *where* the tool's working point is and *how* it is oriented, relative to the robot's wrist — analogous to a person knowing the length and angle of a pencil before writing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Tool Configuration

A complete tool configuration in robotics consists of **four fundamental elements**:

### A. Tool Center Point (TCP) — The Working Point
The TCP is the point on the tool that:
- Performs the actual task (e.g., weld point, gripper center, drill tip).
- Is the reference point used in all motion commands.
- Is defined as the **origin of the tool coordinate frame** $\lbrace T \rbrace$.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **welding gun**, the TCP is typically at the **electrode tip** (touch point with the workpiece). For a **gripper**, the TCP is at the **midpoint between the fingers when fully closed**. For a **suction cup**, the TCP is the **cup center on the contact surface**.

### B. Tool Offset Vector (Translation)
A $3 \times 1$ vector that locates the TCP relative to the wrist center:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}} = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ z_t \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $(x_t, y_t, z_t)$ are the **Cartesian offsets** measured along the wrist frame's axes.

### C. Tool Orientation Matrix (Rotation)
A $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix that describes how the tool's local axes are aligned with respect to the wrist frame:

$$
\mathbf{R}_{\text{tool}} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} \end{bmatrix}
$$

For a typical tool, $\mathbf{R}_{\text{tool}}$ is often a pure rotation about one axis (e.g., the tool points along the wrist's $z$-axis).

### D. Combined Tool Transformation
The full tool configuration is expressed as a $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation:

$$
^{\text{World}}_{\text{Tool}}\mathbf{T} = \,^{\text{World}}_{\text{Wrist}}\mathbf{T} \cdot \,^{\text{Wrist}}_{\text{Tool}}\mathbf{T}
$$

where $\,^{\text{Wrist}}_{\text{Tool}}\mathbf{T}$ is the **tool frame transformation** we are configuring.

## 2.2 The Tool Transformation Matrix

The tool transformation, expressed in the wrist frame, is:

$$
\,^{\text{Wrist}}_{\text{Tool}}\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & x_t \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & y_t \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & z_t \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU-Trapped Mistake:** Many students apply the tool transformation in the **wrong order**. Always remember: the tool frame is *multiplied on the right* of the wrist transformation, because the tool is a *child* of the wrist in the kinematic chain.

## 2.3 Methods of Defining Tool Configuration

### Method 1 — Numerical Entry (Direct)
The programmer enters $(x_t, y_t, z_t)$ and the orientation angles manually. Suitable when tool dimensions are known exactly.

### Method 2 — Multi-Point Teach (TCP Calibration)
The robot is jogged so that a **single physical reference point** on the tool (e.g., a sharp tip) touches a **fixed reference point** on a calibration jig from **4 to 6 different robot poses**. The controller solves a least-squares system to recover the TCP position relative to the wrist.

### Method 3 — World Frame Definition
Define both the **wrist origin** and the **TCP origin** in the world frame, then compute the tool transformation as the inverse of the wrist transformation, multiplied by the TCP transformation.

## 2.4 Robot Arm Configurations (Joint Geometry Context)

While "tool configuration" in the strict KTU sense refers to the tool frame, the syllabus also treats the **robot's geometric configuration** as a related concept that determines the kinematic structure onto which the tool is attached.

| Configuration Type | Structure | Typical Use | Workspace Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian / Gantry | 3 prismatic (PPP) | Pick-and-place, CNC loading | Rectangular box |
| Cylindrical | 2 prismatic + 1 revolute (RPP) | Heavy assembly, coating | Cylinder |
| Spherical / Polar | 1 prismatic + 2 revolute (RRP) | Welding, foundry | Sphere sector |
| Articulated / Anthropomorphic | 3 revolute (RRR) | General-purpose, painting | Sphere (large) |
| SCARA | 2 revolute + 1 prismatic (RRP) | PCB assembly, pick-place | Horizontal disc |
| Parallel / Delta | Multiple closed-loop chains | High-speed packaging | Dome above base |

> [!NOTE]
> The acronym for the SCARA arm is **Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm** — it is compliant in the horizontal plane and stiff vertically, ideal for vertical-insertion assembly tasks.

## 2.5 Master–Slave Manipulator Configuration (Historical Context)

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, a brief reference to **master–slave configurations** appears in some editions. This is a teleoperation setup where:

- The **master arm** is moved by the human operator.
- The **slave arm** mimics the master's motion, often scaled in force or position.
- The slave is typically the one *equipped with the tool* (e.g., handling radioactive material).

The slave is configured as a **mirror image** of the master in terms of joint axes, allowing intuitive control.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive welding lines:** The tool frame stores the geometry of spot-welding guns; rapid reconfiguration is needed when switching car models.
- **Aerospace drilling:** The TCP is the drill bit tip; even a 1 mm error in tool length causes unacceptable hole misalignment on large fuselage panels.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci):** Tool configuration defines the precise entry point of the endoscope and the tip of each instrument, scaled down by a factor of 10×.
- **3D printing robotic arms:** The TCP is the extruder nozzle; the tool frame must be re-taught whenever the nozzle is changed.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Tool offset vector | $\mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}} = [x_t, y_t, z_t]^T$ | Length: **mm** or **m** |
| Tool rotation matrix | $\mathbf{R}_{\text{tool}} \in SO(3)$ | $3 \times 3$, orthogonal, $\det = +1$ |
| Full tool transform | $\,^{W}_{T}\mathbf{T} = \,^{W}_{\text{Wrist}}\mathbf{T} \cdot \,^{\text{Wrist}}_{T}\mathbf{T}$ | $4 \times 4$ homogeneous matrix |
| TCP position in world | $\mathbf{p}_{\text{TCP}} = \,^{W}_{\text{Wrist}}\mathbf{T} \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}}$ | Result is a $3 \times 1$ position |
| Pitch angle (Z-Y-X Euler) | $\alpha = \arctan2(\pm r_{31}, \sqrt{r_{32}^2 + r_{33}^2})$ | Rotation about world $X$ |
| Yaw angle (Z-Y-X Euler) | $\gamma = \arctan2(\pm r_{21}, \pm r_{11})$ | Rotation about world $Z$ |
| Roll angle (Z-Y-X Euler) | $\beta = \arctan2(\pm r_{32}, \pm r_{33})$ | Rotation about world $Y$ |
| Workspace volume (Cartesian) | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | Cubic, $\text{mm}^3$ |
| Workspace volume (Spherical) | $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi r_{\max}^3$ | Polar, $\text{mm}^3$ |
| SCARA reach condition | $\vert d_1 - d_2 \vert \le r \le d_1 + d_2$ | Two-link planar reach |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When you see the symbol $\vert x \vert$ in a formula, in your answer sheet write it as $\vert x \vert$ using vertical bars, but **inside markdown tables** (as above) use the spelled form $\vert x \vert$ only when the context demands it — this avoids breaking the table layout.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Tool Transformation from TCP Calibration

A common KTU numerical problem is: *"A robot is moved into 4 poses such that a fixed reference point is touched by the tool tip. The wrist-center coordinates for each pose and the corresponding joint angles are given. Compute the tool offset vector."*

### Step 1 — Set Up the General TCP Equation

Let the **world frame** be $\lbrace 0 \rbrace$, the **wrist frame** be $\lbrace W \rbrace$, and the **tool frame** be $\lbrace T \rbrace$. For any configuration $i$ of the robot:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{\text{TCP}}^{(i)} = \,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{T}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_i) \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{p}_{\text{TCP}}^{(i)}$ is the **fixed world coordinate** of the reference point (constant across all poses).
- $\,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{T}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_i)$ is the forward-kinematic wrist transformation at pose $i$.
- $\mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}} = [x_t, y_t, z_t, 1]^T$ is the unknown tool offset (in homogeneous form).

### Step 2 — Rearrange into a Linear System

Writing the equation explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{\text{TCP},x}^{(i)} &= r_{11}^{(i)} x_t + r_{12}^{(i)} y_t + r_{13}^{(i)} z_t + d_x^{(i)} \\
p_{\text{TCP},y}^{(i)} &= r_{21}^{(i)} x_t + r_{22}^{(i)} y_t + r_{23}^{(i)} z_t + d_y^{(i)} \\
p_{\text{TCP},z}^{(i)} &= r_{31}^{(i)} x_t + r_{32}^{(i)} y_t + r_{33}^{(i)} z_t + d_z^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $[r_{11} \dots r_{33}]^{(i)}$ are the elements of $\,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{R}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_i)$ and $[d_x, d_y, d_z]^{(i)}$ is the wrist center position in the world frame.

### Step 3 — Stack Multiple Poses

For $N$ calibration poses (typically $N = 4$ minimum), we form the matrix equation:

$$
\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}} = \mathbf{b}
$$

where

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11}^{(1)} & r_{12}^{(1)} & r_{13}^{(1)} \\ r_{21}^{(1)} & r_{22}^{(1)} & r_{23}^{(1)} \\ r_{31}^{(1)} & r_{32}^{(1)} & r_{33}^{(1)} \\ r_{11}^{(2)} & r_{12}^{(2)} & r_{13}^{(2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ r_{31}^{(N)} & r_{32}^{(N)} & r_{33}^{(N)} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}} = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ z_t \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} p_{\text{TCP},x}^{(1)} - d_x^{(1)} \\ p_{\text{TCP},y}^{(1)} - d_y^{(1)} \\ p_{\text{TCP},z}^{(1)} - d_z^{(1)} \\ p_{\text{TCP},x}^{(2)} - d_x^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ p_{\text{TCP},z}^{(N)} - d_z^{(N)} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve Using Least Squares (Overdetermined Case)

If $N > 1$ (and we have $3N$ equations in 3 unknowns), the system is overdetermined. The **least-squares solution** is:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}
$$

This minimizes the squared error between the measured TCP positions and the predicted ones.

### Step 5 — Recover the Tool Rotation (Optional)

The tool's orientation can be found by comparing the tool frame's known axis (e.g., the approach direction) with the wrist's last-axis direction across multiple poses. If the tool points along the wrist $Z$-axis:

$$
\mathbf{R}_{\text{tool}} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}_T & \mathbf{y}_T & \mathbf{z}_T \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\mathbf{x}_T, \mathbf{y}_T, \mathbf{z}_T$ are the columns of the tool frame's basis vectors expressed in the wrist frame.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Tool Offset for a 2-DOF Planar Arm

**Problem:** A planar RR robot has link lengths $L_1 = 400\,\text{mm}$ and $L_2 = 300\,\text{mm}$. The wrist frame is attached at the end of link 2. A tool of length $L_t = 100\,\text{mm}$ is mounted along the local $X$-axis of the wrist. Compute the TCP position when $\theta_1 = 30°$ and $\theta_2 = 45°$.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute the wrist position using forward kinematics.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_W &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y_W &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_W &= 400 \cos 30° + 300 \cos(30° + 45°) \\
    &= 400 \times 0.8660 + 300 \times \cos 75° \\
    &= 400 \times 0.8660 + 300 \times 0.2588 \\
    &= 346.41 + 77.64 = 424.05 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_W &= 400 \sin 30° + 300 \sin(30° + 45°) \\
    &= 400 \times 0.5000 + 300 \times \sin 75° \\
    &= 400 \times 0.5000 + 300 \times 0.9659 \\
    &= 200.00 + 289.78 = 489.78 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the orientation of the wrist frame.**

The wrist's $X$-axis (in the world frame) makes an angle $(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = 75°$ with the world $X$-axis.

**Step 3: Add the tool offset along the wrist's $X$-axis.**

The tool is mounted along the wrist's $X$-axis with length $L_t = 100\,\text{mm}$, so its unit vector in world coordinates is $[\cos 75°, \sin 75°]^T$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{TCP}} &= x_W + L_t \cos 75° = 424.05 + 100 \times 0.2588 = 424.05 + 25.88 = 449.93 \text{ mm} \\
y_{\text{TCP}} &= y_W + L_t \sin 75° = 489.78 + 100 \times 0.9659 = 489.78 + 96.59 = 586.37 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Write the full tool transformation matrix in homogeneous form.**

$$
\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 75° & -\sin 75° & 0 & 449.93 \\ \sin 75° & \cos 75° & 0 & 586.37 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final Answer:** The TCP is located at $(449.93, 586.37)\,\text{mm}$ in the world frame, with the tool frame rotated by $75°$ relative to the world $X$-axis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Workspace Volume for Robot Configurations

### Spherical (Polar) Arm Workspace

A polar robot has:
- A revolute joint (base rotation): $\theta$ range $= 0$ to $2\pi$ rad
- A revolute joint (shoulder pitch): $\phi$ range $= 0$ to $\pi$ rad
- A prismatic joint (arm extension): $r$ range $= 0$ to $r_{\max}$

The workspace is a sector of a sphere with maximum radius $r_{\max}$ and a "blind" cone of half-angle $\phi_{\min}$ beneath the shoulder.

$$
V_{\text{spherical}} = \frac{2}{3}\pi r_{\max}^3 (1 - \cos\phi_{\min})
$$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\phi_{\min}} \int_0^{r_{\max}} r^2 \sin\phi \, dr \, d\phi \, d\theta \\
  &= \int_0^{2\pi} d\theta \int_0^{\phi_{\min}} \sin\phi \, d\phi \int_0^{r_{\max}} r^2 \, dr \\
  &= 2\pi \cdot [-\cos\phi]_0^{\phi_{\min}} \cdot \frac{r_{\max}^3}{3} \\
  &= 2\pi (1 - \cos\phi_{\min}) \cdot \frac{r_{\max}^3}{3} \\
  &= \frac{2}{3}\pi r_{\max}^3 (1 - \cos\phi_{\min})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Cartesian Workspace Volume

$$
V_{\text{Cartesian}} = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z
$$

where $L_x, L_y, L_z$ are the maximum travel of the three prismatic joints.

### Articulated (RRR) Approximate Workspace

For an articulated arm with all three revolute joints having $\pm 180°$ range and equal link lengths $L_1 = L_2 = L_3 = L$, the workspace is approximately a sphere of radius $3L$:

$$
V_{\text{articulated}} \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi (3L)^3 = 36\pi L^3
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Table

| Step | Operation | Mathematical Form | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Define wrist position | $\mathbf{p}_W = f(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | FK problem |
| 2 | Define tool offset in wrist frame | $\mathbf{p}_t = [x_t, y_t, z_t]^T$ | Given/measured |
| 3 | Express TCP in world frame | $\mathbf{p}_{\text{TCP}} = \,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{T} \cdot \mathbf{p}_t$ | Position verification |
| 4 | Combine rotations | $\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{R} = \,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{R} \cdot \,^{W}_{T}\mathbf{R}$ | Orientation check |
| 5 | Solve for tool offset (calibration) | $\mathbf{p}_t = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$ | 14-mark problem |
| 6 | Compute workspace | $V = \int dV$ over joint limits | 3-mark question |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comprehensive Coverage Note:** All derivations above are complete and self-contained. The next section translates these mathematical structures into visual block diagrams for intuitive understanding.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tool Frame Kinematic Chain — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Frame at ID 0] --> B[Joint 1 Theta1]
    B --> C[Joint 2 Theta2]
    C --> D[Joint 3 Theta3]
    D --> E[Joint 4 Theta4]
    E --> F[Joint 5 Theta5]
    F --> G[Joint 6 Theta6]
    G --> H[Wrist Center Frame at ID W]
    H --> I[Tool Offset Vector p_tool]
    I --> J[Tool Frame at ID T with TCP]
    J --> K[Workpiece in World]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each joint contributes a rotation/translation, the wrist center is the output of the manipulator's kinematic chain, and the tool frame is offset from the wrist by the tool's geometric data. The TCP at the tip of the tool is the point that interacts with the workpiece.

---

## 4.2 Tool Configuration Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input[Input Layer]
        A1[User Waypoint Specification in World Frame]
        A2[Known Tool Geometry from Data Sheet]
    end

    subgraph Computation[Computation Layer]
        B1[Forward Kinematics Solver]
        B2[Tool Transform Calculator]
        B3[TCP Position in World Frame]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output Layer]
        C1[Joint Angle Commands to Motors]
        C2[Verified TCP Path on Display]
        C3[Collision Free Motion]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
```

**Interpretation:** The user specifies the TCP's desired path, the controller uses the tool configuration data to translate those waypoints into joint-level commands, and verifies that the resulting TCP motion is collision-free.

---

## 4.3 TCP Calibration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with Nominal Tool Offset] --> S2[Jog Robot to Pose 1 and Touch Reference Point]
    S2 --> S3[Record Wrist Transform and Save Pose 1]
    S3 --> S4[Jog Robot to Pose 2 Touch Reference and Record]
    S4 --> S5[Repeat for Poses 3 and 4]
    S5 --> S6[Form the Overdetermined Matrix Equation]
    S6 --> S7{Number of Poses Greater than 1}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Compute Least Squares Solution for Tool Offset]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Direct Solve for Tool Offset]
    S8 --> S10[Update Tool Configuration in Controller Memory]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Verify by Retracing Path with New TCP]
    S11 --> S12[End of Calibration]
```

**Workflow explanation:** This is the standard **four-point TCP calibration** procedure used by ABB, FANUC, KUKA, and Yaskawa controllers. The more poses recorded, the more accurate the recovered tool offset.

---

## 4.4 Robot Arm Configuration Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cartesian[Cartesian Gantry PPP]
        CA1[Base Slide X]
        CA2[Vertical Slide Y]
        CA3[Horizontal Slide Z]
    end

    subgraph Cylindrical[Cylindrical RPP]
        CY1[Revolute Base]
        CY2[Prismatic Vertical]
        CY3[Prismatic Radial]
    end

    subgraph Spherical[Spherical Polar RRP]
        SP1[Revolute Base]
        SP2[Revolute Shoulder]
        SP3[Prismatic Extension]
    end

    subgraph Articulated[Articulated RRR plus Wrist]
        AR1[Revolute Waist]
        AR2[Revolute Shoulder]
        AR3[Revolute Elbow]
        AR4[Wrist Roll Pitch Yaw]
    end

    subgraph SCARA[SCARA RRP]
        SC1[Revolute Shoulder]
        SC2[Revolute Elbow]
        SC3[Prismatic Vertical]
    end

    subgraph Parallel[Parallel Delta]
        PA1[Fixed Base Platform]
        PA2[Three Parallel Arms]
        PA3[Moving End Effector Platform]
    end

    TCP[TCP and Tool Frame Mounted on End Effector] -.-> Cartesian
    TCP -.-> Cylindrical
    TCP -.-> Spherical
    TCP -.-> Articulated
    TCP -.-> SCARA
    TCP -.-> Parallel
```

**Diagram interpretation:** The tool frame $\lbrace T \rbrace$ and its TCP are attached to the **end-effector of any robot configuration**. The internal joint structure (Cartesian, cylindrical, spherical, articulated, SCARA, or parallel) determines the shape of the reachable workspace, but the tool configuration logic remains identical.
