<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term Tool Center Point (TCP) in a robotic manipulator. Why is the TCP, and not the wrist center, used as the reference point for motion programming?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Tool Center Point (TCP)** is the precisely defined point on the robot's end-effector or tool that physically performs the task — for example, the tip of a welding electrode, the midpoint of a closed gripper, or the tip of a drill bit. It is the **origin of the tool coordinate frame** $\lbrace T \rbrace$.

The TCP is used (rather than the wrist center) for motion programming because:

1. **Functional relevance:** The task is performed at the TCP, not at the wrist flange. Specifying a weld point or a hole position in terms of the wrist would require the programmer to manually compensate for tool length and orientation — an error-prone process.
2. **Tool-change flexibility:** When a tool is replaced (e.g., a 100 mm welding gun swapped for a 50 mm gripper), only the tool frame data needs updating; the motion programs remain valid.
3. **Intuitive programming:** Operators on a shop floor think in terms of where the *tool tip* must go, not where the mechanical flange is.
4. **Accurate simulation:** Collision-detection software models the tool's swept volume using the TCP and tool-frame orientation, ensuring realistic off-line programming.

> **Word count guideline:** Approximately 100 words. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three methods of defining the tool configuration in an industrial robot. Briefly explain the multi-point teach method.**

**Model Answer:**

The three main methods of defining tool configuration in industrial robots are:

1. **Numerical (Direct) Entry:** The programmer enters the tool offset $(x_t, y_t, z_t)$ and orientation angles directly into the controller, based on the tool's known mechanical dimensions from a data sheet.
2. **Multi-Point Teach (TCP Calibration):** The robot is jogged to touch a fixed reference point on a calibration jig from 4 to 6 different orientations, and the controller solves a least-squares system to recover the TCP.
3. **World-Frame Definition:** The wrist and the TCP are both expressed in the world frame, and the tool transformation is computed algebraically as the inverse of the wrist transformation multiplied by the TCP transformation.

**Multi-point teach method (in brief):** A sharp physical point on the tool (e.g., the drill tip) is touched against a **fixed reference target** (e.g., a dowel pin) from several different robot poses. The controller records the wrist position and orientation for each pose, and knowing that the TCP is at a fixed point in the world frame for every pose, solves the linear system:

$$
\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}} = \mathbf{b}
$$

using least-squares to obtain the unknown tool offset vector $\mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}} = [x_t, y_t, z_t]^T$. The more poses used, the more accurate the calibration.

> **Word count guideline:** Approximately 130 words. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**A 6-DOF articulated robot has a wrist center position at $\mathbf{p}_W = [600, 200, 800]^T$ mm and wrist orientation matrix:**

$$
\,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**A welding torch is mounted such that its TCP is offset $150\,\text{mm}$ along the wrist's $Z$-axis and rotated $30°$ about the wrist's $Y$-axis.**

#### (a) Derive the tool transformation matrix $\,^{W}_{T}\mathbf{T}$. (7 Marks)

#### (b) Compute the TCP position and orientation in the world frame, and present the final homogeneous matrix $\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{T}$. (7 Marks)

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Tool Transformation Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the translation vector.**

The TCP is offset $150\,\text{mm}$ along the wrist's $Z$-axis, so:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 150 \end{bmatrix} \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Construct the rotation matrix for a $30°$ rotation about the wrist's $Y$-axis.**

The standard rotation matrix about the $Y$-axis by angle $\beta = 30°$ is:

$$
\mathbf{R}_Y(30°) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 30° & 0 & \sin 30° \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin 30° & 0 & \cos 30° \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 & 0 & 0.5000 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -0.5000 & 0 & 0.8660 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the $4 \times 4$ tool transformation matrix.**

$$
\,^{W}_{T}\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 & 0 & 0.5000 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ -0.5000 & 0 & 0.8660 & 150 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix describes how the tool frame is positioned and oriented relative to the wrist frame.

> *(7 marks: 2 for translation, 3 for rotation matrix derivation, 2 for assembly.)*

---

#### (b) Model Solution — TCP in World Frame

**Step 1 — Construct the wrist homogeneous transformation matrix.**

$$
\,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 & 600 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & 200 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 800 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply the matrices: $\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{T} = \,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{T} \cdot \,^{W}_{T}\mathbf{T}$.**

Performing the multiplication block by block:

**Rotation block:**

$$
\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{R} = \,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{R}_Y(30°)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{R} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 & 0 & 0.5000 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -0.5000 & 0 & 0.8660 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot 0.8660 + (-1) \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-0.5000) & 0 \cdot 0 + (-1) \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 & 0 \cdot 0.5 + (-1) \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0.866 \\ 1 \cdot 0.866 + 0 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-0.5) & 1 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 & 1 \cdot 0.5 + 0 + 0 \\ 0 \cdot 0.866 + 0 + 1 \cdot (-0.5) & 0 + 0 + 1 \cdot 0 & 0 \cdot 0.5 + 0 + 1 \cdot 0.866 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0.8660 & 0 & 0.5000 \\ -0.5000 & 0 & 0.8660 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Translation block:**

$$
\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{p} = \,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}} + \mathbf{p}_W
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 150 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 150 \end{bmatrix} \\
\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{p} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 150 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 600 \\ 200 \\ 800 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 600 \\ 200 \\ 950 \end{bmatrix} \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final homogeneous matrix.**

$$
\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 & 600 \\ 0.8660 & 0 & 0.5000 & 200 \\ -0.5000 & 0 & 0.8660 & 950 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Interpretation:** The TCP is located at $(600, 200, 950)\,\text{mm}$ in the world frame, and the tool frame is oriented such that its $Z$-axis (the welding direction) points along the rotated $Y$-axis of the wrist.

> *(7 marks: 2 for matrix setup, 3 for rotation multiplication, 2 for translation and final assembly.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**A 2-DOF planar RR robot with link lengths $L_1 = 500\,\text{mm}$ and $L_2 = 400\,\text{mm}$ carries a gripper whose TCP is located $120\,\text{mm}$ from the wrist center, along the local $X$-axis of the wrist.**

#### (a) Derive the forward-kinematic expressions for the wrist-center position and orientation in terms of $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. (7 Marks)

#### (b) For $\theta_1 = 45°$ and $\theta_2 = -30°$, compute the wrist position, the TCP position in the world frame, and the orientation of the gripper's tool frame. (7 Marks)

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Forward Kinematics

**Step 1 — Apply the standard 2-DOF planar RR forward kinematics.**

For a planar RR arm with revolute joints at the base and elbow, the wrist-center position $(x_W, y_W)$ in the world frame is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_W &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y_W &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Orientation of the wrist frame.**

The wrist frame's $X$-axis is aligned with the second link, so the wrist orientation angle (with respect to the world $X$-axis) is:

$$
\phi_W = \theta_1 + \theta_2
$$

**Step 3 — Wrist rotation matrix.**

$$
\,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & 0 \\ \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> *(7 marks: 3 for position expressions, 2 for orientation, 2 for rotation matrix.)*

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** $L_1 = 500$ mm, $L_2 = 400$ mm, $L_t = 120$ mm, $\theta_1 = 45°$, $\theta_2 = -30°$.

**Step 1 — Compute the wrist position.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_W &= 500 \cos 45° + 400 \cos(45° - 30°) \\
    &= 500 \times 0.7071 + 400 \times \cos 15° \\
    &= 500 \times 0.7071 + 400 \times 0.9659 \\
    &= 353.55 + 386.37 = 739.92 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_W &= 500 \sin 45° + 400 \sin(45° - 30°) \\
    &= 500 \times 0.7071 + 400 \times \sin 15° \\
    &= 500 \times 0.7071 + 400 \times 0.2588 \\
    &= 353.55 + 103.53 = 457.08 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the wrist orientation angle.**

$$
\phi_W = \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 45° + (-30°) = 15°
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the TCP position using the tool offset along the wrist $X$-axis.**

The tool unit vector in world coordinates is $[\cos 15°, \sin 15°]^T$. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{TCP}} &= x_W + L_t \cos 15° = 739.92 + 120 \times 0.9659 = 739.92 + 115.91 = 855.83 \text{ mm} \\
y_{\text{TCP}} &= y_W + L_t \sin 15° = 457.08 + 120 \times 0.2588 = 457.08 + 31.06 = 488.14 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final tool transformation.**

$$
\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 15° & -\sin 15° & 0 & 855.83 \\ \sin 15° & \cos 15° & 0 & 488.14 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Numerically:

$$
\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9659 & -0.2588 & 0 & 855.83 \\ 0.2588 & 0.9659 & 0 & 488.14 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Interpretation:** The gripper's TCP is at $(855.83, 488.14)\,\text{mm}$ in the world frame, and the gripper is rotated by $15°$ from the world $X$-axis.

> *(7 marks: 2 for wrist position, 2 for tool offset calculation, 3 for final homogeneous matrix and interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Tool Configuration Problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong multiplication order** — Many students multiply $\,^{W}_{T}\mathbf{T} \cdot \,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{T}$ instead of $\,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{T} \cdot \,^{W}_{T}\mathbf{T}$. **Memorize the rule:** the *parent* (world) is on the left, the *child* (tool) is on the right.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the homogeneous row** — The last row of every $4 \times 4$ transformation must be $[0, 0, 0, 1]$. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Sign error in rotation matrices** — A $30°$ rotation about $Y$ has $\sin(-30°) = -0.5$ in the $[3,1]$ position; confusing $\sin$ and $\cos$ signs is a classic $0.5$–$1$ mark loss.
>
> 4. **Confusing the order of tool offset application** — Some students add the tool length along the *world* $X$-axis instead of the *wrist* $X$-axis. Always express the tool vector in the *wrist frame* before transforming.
>
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners often deduct $0.5$ marks for missing "mm" or "m" in final position answers.
>
> 6. **Misinterpreting "TCP" as wrist center** — When the problem says "compute the position of the tool," it means the TCP, not the flange. Reading the question carefully is worth $1$–$2$ marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tool Configuration**

- **Tool Center Point (TCP):** The functional working point on the tool; the origin of the tool frame $\{T\}$.
- **Wrist Center:** Output of the manipulator's kinematic chain (last joint output).
- **Tool Configuration** = the homogeneous transformation from the wrist frame to the tool frame, containing both a 3D translation vector $\mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}}$ and a 3×3 rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}_{\text{tool}}$.
- **Master Equation:** $\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{T} = \,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{T} \cdot \,^{W}_{T}\mathbf{T}$ — the *parent-on-left, child-on-right* rule.
- **Tool offset vector** components $(x_t, y_t, z_t)$ are always expressed in the **wrist frame**, not the world frame.
- **TCP Calibration:** 4-point (minimum) teach method using a fixed reference and least-squares solution to recover the tool offset.
- **Workspace shapes by robot configuration:**
  - Cartesian → rectangular box
  - Cylindrical → cylinder
  - Spherical / Polar → spherical sector
  - Articulated → large sphere
  - SCARA → horizontal disc with vertical reach
  - Parallel / Delta → dome-shaped volume
- **TCP-related formulas** to memorize: $\,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{T}$, $\mathbf{p}_{\text{TCP}} = \,^{0}_{W}\mathbf{T} \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{tool}}$, and the workspace volume integrals.
- **SCARA reach condition:** $\vert d_1 - d_2 \vert \le r \le d_1 + d_2$.
- **Polar workspace:** $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi r_{\max}^3 (1 - \cos\phi_{\min})$.
- **Articulated workspace approximation:** $V \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi (3L)^3 = 36\pi L^3$.
- **Examiner's red flags to avoid:** wrong multiplication order, missing homogeneous row, sign errors in rotation matrices, unit omissions, TCP-vs-wrist confusion.
- **Industrial relevance:** tool configuration underpins multi-tool cells, accurate welding/drilling, and teach-pendant programming in automotive and aerospace manufacturing.
- **Cross-link:** Tool configuration feeds directly into Module 2 topics — **forward kinematics** (output the wrist transform), **inverse kinematics** (input the desired TCP pose), and **path planning** (TCP trajectory must be collision-free).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jacobian
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Jacobian

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
In the context of manipulator kinematics, the **Jacobian Matrix** (denoted as **J(q)**) is a fundamental time-varying linear mapping that relates the **joint-space velocities** $(\dot{q})$ of a robotic manipulator to the **Cartesian (task-space) linear and angular velocities** of its end-effector. It is the matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of the forward kinematics position/orientation vector with respect to the joint variables.

Mathematically, the relationship is expressed as:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = J(q) \, \dot{q}
$$

Where **v** is the **6 m/s** linear velocity, **ω** is the **rad/s** angular velocity, **J(q)** is a **6 × n** matrix (for an n-DOF robot), and **q̇** is the **n × 1** joint velocity vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Jacobian is not just a single entity — it is split into the **Linear Velocity Jacobian ($J_v$)** and the **Angular Velocity Jacobian ($J_\omega$)**, stacked vertically to form the full Jacobian: $J = \begin{bmatrix} J_v \\ J_\omega \end{bmatrix}$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Steering Wheel of the Robot"
Imagine driving a car. When you turn the steering wheel by a small angle, the car turns by some related angle at the wheels, which translates to a certain turning radius. The Jacobian is mathematically that exact relationship — but for a robot arm.

- **Joint velocities ($\dot{q}$)** = how fast the "steering" (joint motors) spin.
- **End-effector velocity (v, ω)** = how fast the robot's "fingertip" actually moves in 3D space.
- **Jacobian J(q)** = the instantaneous "steering ratio" that converts motor speeds into fingertip motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in KTU exams?** Almost every velocity, force, and singularity problem in Robotics requires the Jacobian. Without it, you cannot answer questions on differential motion, force transmission, or path planning.

### 1.3 Physical Significance & Engineering Utility
- **Velocity Control:** Used to compute joint velocities required for a desired end-effector trajectory.
- **Force Control:** Provides the transpose $J^T$ which maps end-effector forces to joint torques (static equilibrium).
- **Singularity Detection:** Locates configurations where the robot loses a degree of freedom (rank deficiency).
- **Trajectory Planning:** Converts smooth Cartesian paths into feasible joint-space trajectories.

**Standard Notation used in KTU Board Exams:**
- $o_i$ → position vector of frame *i* origin
- $z_{i-1}$ → axis of joint *i* (from frame *i-1*)
- $o_n$ → end-effector origin position
- Bold lowercase → vectors; Bold UPPERCASE → matrices.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Geometric Origin of the Jacobian
The Jacobian is derived using **vector cross-product geometry** applied iteratively from frame *i* to the end-effector frame *n*. Each column of the Jacobian corresponds to the velocity contribution of **one joint**.

For an n-DOF robot, the Jacobian is constructed column-by-column. For the **i-th column** $J_i$:

**Case 1: Revolute Joint (R)**
$$
J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1}) \\ z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Case 2: Prismatic Joint (P)**
$$
J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:
- $z_{i-1}$ is the unit vector along the axis of joint *i* (expressed in the base frame).
- $o_n - o_{i-1}$ is the vector from the origin of frame *i-1* to the end-effector origin.
- The first 3 rows give linear velocity contribution, last 3 rows give angular velocity contribution.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Formula
- For a **revolute** joint, the end-effector moves in a circular arc around the joint axis. The linear velocity is therefore $\omega \times r$ where $r$ is the position vector — hence the cross product $z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1})$.
- The angular velocity contribution is simply $z_{i-1} \cdot \dot{q}_i$, i.e., the axis itself.
- For a **prismatic** joint, there is no rotation — only translation along $z_{i-1}$, hence no angular velocity.

### 2.3 Properties of the Jacobian

| Property | Description | Engineering Implication |
|----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Size** | 6 × n (for spatial robot) | More DOF = more columns |
| **Time-varying** | Depends on configuration $q$ | Recomputed at every time step |
| **Non-square** | Usually not square (m ≠ n) | Inversion requires pseudo-inverse |
| **Rank-deficient** | Loses rank at singularities | Robot cannot move in certain directions |
| **Transpose** | $J^T$ maps forces to torques | Used in static force analysis |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Expression | Use Case |
|---------|------------|----------|
| Velocity mapping | $v = J(q) \, \dot{q}$ | Forward differential kinematics |
| Static force/torque | $\tau = J^T(q) \, F$ | Force control, joint torque computation |
| Joint velocity from Cartesian | $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q) \, v$ (square case) | Inverse differential kinematics |
| Pseudo-inverse (non-square) | $\dot{q} = J^+(q) \, v$ | Redundant/under-actuated robots |
| Singularities | $\det(J J^T) = 0$ | Detect loss of DOF |
| Manipulability index | $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^T)}$ | Measure dexterity (Yoshikawa) |
| Jacobian derivative | $\dot{J} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial q} \dot{q}$ | Acceleration analysis |
| Acceleration relation | $\dot{v} = J \ddot{q} + \dot{J} \dot{q}$ | Full dynamic analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Board:** Always separate $J_v$ (top 3 rows) and $J_\omega$ (bottom 3 rows) explicitly. Examiners look for the **vertical stacking** $J = \begin{bmatrix} J_v \\ J_\omega \end{bmatrix}$.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Industry
- Used in **CNC machines** for tool-path velocity calculation.
- Central to **haptic feedback** in surgical robots (da Vinci Surgical System).
- Used in **NASA's Canadarm** for real-time trajectory compensation.
- Essential in **self-driving cars** for steering kinematics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Jacobian Column for a Revolute Joint

**Step 1: Position of End-Effector**
Let the end-effector position be $o_n$ in the base frame, and the position of the *i-1* frame origin be $o_{i-1}$.

**Step 2: Joint Rotation Contribution**
When joint *i* rotates by an infinitesimal angle $d\theta_i$ about its axis $z_{i-1}$, the end-effector undergoes an infinitesimal displacement:

$$
d o_n = z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1}) \, d\theta_i
$$

This is the classic **rigid body rotation** formula where linear velocity equals angular velocity crossed with the radius vector.

**Step 3: Time Derivative (Divide by dt)**
$$
v_i = \frac{d o_n}{dt} = z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1}) \, \dot{\theta}_i
$$

**Step 4: Identify the Jacobian Column**
Comparing with $v = J_v \dot{q}$, the contribution of joint *i* to linear velocity is:

$$
J_{vi} = z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1})
$$

**Step 5: Angular Velocity Component**
Since the end-effector rotates about the same axis as the joint:

$$
\omega_i = z_{i-1} \, \dot{\theta}_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad J_{\omega i} = z_{i-1}
$$

**Step 6: Combine into Full Column Vector**

$$
J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1}) \\ z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix} \quad \blacksquare
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: 2-Link Planar Manipulator (R-R)

**Forward Kinematics:**

$$
x = l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Jacobian via Partial Derivatives:**

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1} & \frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2} \\ \frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} & \frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step-by-Step Differentiation:**

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1} = -l_1 \sin\theta_1 - l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2} = -l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} = l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} = l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Final Jacobian:**

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 \sin\theta_1 - l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Determinant (for Singularity Analysis):**

$$
\det(J) = (-l_1 \sin\theta_1 - l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)) \cdot l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) - (-l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)) \cdot (l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2))
$$

Expanding and simplifying using trigonometric identities:

$$
\det(J) = l_1 l_2 \sin\theta_2
$$

**Singularities:** Occur when $\sin\theta_2 = 0$, i.e., $\theta_2 = 0$ (arm fully extended) or $\theta_2 = \pi$ (arm folded back on itself).

### 3.3 Verification Using Vector Method (Cross-Product Formula)

For joint 1 (revolute about $z_0 = [0, 0, 1]^T$):

$$
o_2 - o_1 = \begin{bmatrix} l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
J_{v1} = z_0 \times (o_2 - o_1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matches the first column of the Jacobian derived analytically. ✓

### 3.4 Static Force Analysis Derivation

**Principle of Virtual Work:** In static equilibrium, the virtual work done by joint torques equals the virtual work done by end-effector forces:

$$
\tau^T \delta q = F^T \delta x
$$

Substituting $\delta x = J \delta q$:

$$
\tau^T \delta q = F^T J \delta q
$$

$$
\tau^T = F^T J \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = J^T F \quad \blacksquare
$$

This is the **Jacobian Transpose Principle** — the foundation of force control in robotic manipulators.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Differential Kinematics Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[inputQdot: Joint Velocities qdot] --> B[Multiply: J of q times qdot]
    B --> C[Extract: v Linear Velocity]
    B --> D[Extract: w Angular Velocity]
    C --> E[outputV: End Effector Linear Velocity m/s]
    D --> F[outputW: End Effector Angular Velocity rad/s]
    G[inputF: End Effector Force] --> H[Multiply: J transpose times F]
    H --> I[outputTau: Joint Torques Nm]
    J[inputQ: Joint Variables] --> K[Compute Forward Kinematics o of n]
    K --> L[Extract z of i minus 1 and o of n minus o of i minus 1]
    L --> M[Apply Cross Product Rule]
    M --> N[Construct Column J of i]
    N --> O[Stack Columns: Full J of q]
    O --> B
    O --> H
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Jacobian Construction Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given n DOF Robot] --> B[Initialize: i equals 1]
    B --> C{Is Joint i Revolute or Prismatic}
    C -->|Revolute R| D[Compute Jv of i equals z of i minus 1 cross o of n minus o of i minus 1]
    D --> E[Compute Jw of i equals z of i minus 1]
    C -->|Prismatic P| F[Compute Jv of i equals z of i minus 1]
    F --> G[Compute Jw of i equals zero vector]
    E --> H[Stack into Column: J of i]
    G --> H
    H --> I{i less than n}
    I -->|Yes| J[Increment i equals i plus 1]
    J --> C
    I -->|No| K[Stack All Columns: J equals matrix of size 6 by n]
    K --> L[Check: det of J J transpose equals 0]
    L -->|Yes| M[Warning: Singularity Detected]
    L -->|No| N[Output: Valid Jacobian J of q]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Force-Velocity Dual Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPACE1 [Joint Space Domain]
        A1[tau: Joint Torques] 
        A2[qdot: Joint Velocities]
    end
    subgraph MATRIX [Jacobian J of q and J transpose]
        B1[J of q: 6 by n]
        B2[J transpose: n by 6]
    end
    subgraph SPACE2 [Cartesian Task Space]
        C1[F: End Effector Force]
        C2[v and w: End Effector Velocity]
    end
    A1 -->|Maps to| B2
    B2 -->|Maps to| C1
    A2 -->|Maps to| B1
    B1 -->|Maps to| C2
    A1 -.Virtual Work.-> C1
    A2 -.Differential Motion.-> C2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Jacobian matrix of a robotic manipulator. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**
The Jacobian matrix $J(q)$ of a robotic manipulator is a time-varying matrix that relates the joint velocities to the end-effector linear and angular velocities in Cartesian space.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}_{6 \times 1} = J(q)_{6 \times n} \, \dot{q}_{n \times 1}
$$

**Physical Significance:**
- It is the **first-order partial derivative** of the forward kinematics equation with respect to the joint variables.
- It maps infinitesimal changes in joint space to infinitesimal changes in Cartesian (task) space.
- Its transpose $J^T$ maps end-effector forces to joint torques (using the principle of virtual work).
- It is used to detect **singularities** of the manipulator (where the robot loses one or more degrees of freedom).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for physical significance)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between linear velocity Jacobian and angular velocity Jacobian. Write the Jacobian column for a prismatic joint.**

**Model Answer:**
The full Jacobian $J(q)$ is a $6 \times n$ matrix constructed by vertically stacking two sub-matrices:

$$
J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} J_v(q) \\ J_\omega(q) \end{bmatrix}
$$

- **Linear Velocity Jacobian ($J_v$):** A $3 \times n$ matrix whose *i*-th column gives the linear velocity of the end-effector due to unit velocity at joint *i*. For revolute joint: $J_{vi} = z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1})$.
- **Angular Velocity Jacobian ($J_\omega$):** A $3 \times n$ matrix whose *i*-th column gives the angular velocity of the end-effector due to unit velocity at joint *i*. For revolute joint: $J_{\omega i} = z_{i-1}$.

**Jacobian Column for a Prismatic Joint (P):**

$$
J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \\ 0_{3 \times 1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for difference, 1.5 marks for prismatic column derivation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Jacobian matrix for a 2-link planar manipulator with revolute joints, having link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$, and joint variables $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the joint torques required to balance a force of 10 N applied at the end-effector in the X-direction when the manipulator is at configuration $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, with $l_1 = l_2 = 1$ m.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Jacobian [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Forward Kinematics**
Position of end-effector:
$$
x = l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$
$$
y = l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Step 2: Partial Derivatives**

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1} & \frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2} \\ \frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} & \frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 \sin\theta_1 - l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Verification via Cross-Product Method** [optional 1 mark]
For joint 1: $z_0 = [0, 0, 1]^T$, $o_2 - o_1 = [l_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2), l_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2), 0]^T$
$J_{v1} = z_0 \times (o_2 - o_1)$ gives the first column — matches.

**Step 4: Determinant (Singularity Analysis) [1 mark]**
$$
\det(J) = l_1 l_2 \sin\theta_2
$$
Singular at $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\pi$.

---

**(b) Joint Torque Calculation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Compute Jacobian at given configuration**
$\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, so $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 75°$.

$\sin 30° = 0.5$, $\cos 30° = 0.866$
$\sin 45° = 0.7071$, $\cos 45° = 0.7071$
$\sin 75° = 0.9659$, $\cos 75° = 0.2588$

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -(1)(0.5) - (1)(0.9659) & -(1)(0.9659) \\ (1)(0.866) + (1)(0.2588) & (1)(0.2588) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -1.4659 & -0.9659 \\ 1.1248 & 0.2588 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Force Vector**
Force $F = [10, 0]^T$ N (only X-direction).

**Step 3: Compute Torques via $\tau = J^T F$**

$$
\tau = \begin{bmatrix} -1.4659 & 1.1248 \\ -0.9659 & 0.2588 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -14.659 \\ -9.659 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}
$$

**Result:** $\tau_1 = -14.66$ N·m, $\tau_2 = -9.66$ N·m (the negative signs indicate torques opposing the applied force direction).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a 3-DOF manipulator with all revolute joints, derive the general expression for the Jacobian column $J_i$ using the vector cross-product method. Show the contribution from each joint axis vector $z_{i-1}$ and the position vector $(o_n - o_{i-1})$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2-DOF RR manipulator has $l_1 = 0.5$ m, $l_2 = 0.4$ m. At configuration $\theta_1 = 60°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$, find:
(i) The end-effector velocity when joint velocities are $\dot{\theta}_1 = 1$ rad/s and $\dot{\theta}_2 = 0.5$ rad/s.
(ii) Identify whether the configuration is singular.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) General Jacobian Derivation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1:** The end-effector position is $o_n = [x, y, z]^T$. The position of frame $i-1$ origin is $o_{i-1}$.

**Step 2:** For a revolute joint rotating by $\theta_i$ about axis $z_{i-1}$, the end-effector linear velocity is obtained by differentiating $o_n$ w.r.t. $\theta_i$:

$$
J_{vi} = \frac{\partial o_n}{\partial \theta_i} = z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1})
$$

**Step 3:** The angular velocity contributed by joint $i$ is along its own axis:

$$
J_{\omega i} = z_{i-1}
$$

**Step 4:** Stacking gives the column:

$$
J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1}) \\ z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** For a 3-DOF robot (R-R-R), the full Jacobian is:

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} z_0 \times (o_3 - o_0) & z_1 \times (o_3 - o_1) & z_2 \times (o_3 - o_2) \\ z_0 & z_1 & z_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem [7 Marks]:**

**(i) End-Effector Velocity [4 Marks]:**

Compute the Jacobian elements:
- $\theta_1 = 60°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$, $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 90°$
- $\sin 60° = 0.866$, $\cos 60° = 0.5$
- $\sin 30° = 0.5$, $\cos 30° = 0.866$
- $\sin 90° = 1$, $\cos 90° = 0$

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -(0.5)(0.866) - (0.4)(1) & -(0.4)(1) \\ (0.5)(0.5) + (0.4)(0) & (0.4)(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.833 & -0.4 \\ 0.25 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply $v = J \dot{q}$ where $\dot{q} = [1, 0.5]^T$:

$$
v = \begin{bmatrix} -0.833 \cdot 1 + (-0.4) \cdot 0.5 \\ 0.25 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.033 \\ 0.25 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m/s}
$$

Magnitude: $|v| = \sqrt{(-1.033)^2 + (0.25)^2} = \sqrt{1.129} = 1.063$ m/s.

**(ii) Singularity Check [3 Marks]:**
$$
\det(J) = (-0.833)(0) - (-0.4)(0.25) = 0.1
$$
Since $\det(J) = 0.1 \neq 0$, the configuration is **NOT singular**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the vertical stacking** of $J_v$ and $J_\omega$ into a $6 \times n$ matrix — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you only write $J_v$ without combining.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in cross products** — remember $a \times b = -b \times a$. Always verify the right-hand rule.
>
> 3. **Confusing $o_n$ and $o_{i-1}$** — $o_n$ is always the END-EFFECTOR origin, not joint $n$ origin. This is the #1 conceptual error in KTU answer sheets.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to evaluate $J$ at the given configuration** in numerical problems. Symbolic $J$ alone does not earn full marks.
>
> 5. **Using $J^{-1}$ instead of $J^+$ (pseudo-inverse)** for non-square or rank-deficient Jacobians. The Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse is required for redundant robots.
>
> 6. **Not identifying the joint type (R or P)** before applying the column formula — prismatic joints have $J_{\omega i} = 0$ and $J_{vi} = z_{i-1}$ (no cross product).
>
> 7. **Singularity answer must include both the math ($\det = 0$) AND the physical interpretation** (e.g., "the arm is fully extended and cannot move in certain directions").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**
>
> - The Jacobian is a **$6 \times n$ matrix** mapping joint velocities to Cartesian velocities.
> - For **revolute joint**: $J_i = [z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1}) \;\; z_{i-1}]^T$ (a $6 \times 1$ column).
> - For **prismatic joint**: $J_i = [z_{i-1} \;\; 0]^T$ (a $6 \times 1$ column).
> - Velocity relation: **$v = J(q) \dot{q}$** — direct (forward) differential kinematics.
> - Force relation: **$\tau = J^T(q) F$** — derived from the principle of virtual work.
> - Singularity condition: **$\det(J J^T) = 0$** (works for non-square Jacobians too).
> - For 2-link planar: $\det(J) = l_1 l_2 \sin\theta_2$; singular at $\theta_2 = 0, \pi$.
> - Manipulability index (Yoshikawa): $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^T)}$ — measure of dexterity.
> - Jacobian can be derived **two ways**: (1) partial derivatives of FK, (2) vector cross-product method.
> - Always **compute $J$ at the given configuration** before applying numerical values.
> - **Acceleration equation** (advanced): $\dot{v} = J \ddot{q} + \dot{J} \dot{q}$ — the $\dot{J} \dot{q}$ term captures the Coriolis effect.
> - **Transpose vs Inverse**: $\tau = J^T F$ (forces), $\dot{q} = J^{-1} v$ or $J^+ v$ (velocities) — never confuse!
> - Real-world apps: CNC machines, surgical robots (da Vinci), space manipulators (Canadarm), haptic devices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — RELATION BETWEEN JOINT AND END EFFECTOR VELOCITIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — The Manipulator Jacobian**
> The **Manipulator Jacobian** $J(q)$ is a matrix-valued function that maps the joint velocity vector $\dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to the linear and angular velocity of the end-effector $\dot{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ in task space (Cartesian space). The fundamental velocity relationship is:
> $$\dot{x} = J(q)\,\dot{q}$$
> where $n$ = number of joints, and $m$ = dimension of task space (typically $m = 6$ for a spatial manipulator: 3 linear + 3 angular).

The Jacobian is, by definition, the **first-order partial derivative of the forward kinematic function** with respect to the joint variables. If the forward kinematics equation is $x = f(q)$, then

$$J_{ij}(q) = \frac{\partial f_i(q)}{\partial q_j}$$

In other words, $J$ is the multi-dimensional slope of the function $f$ — it tells us how the end-effector's pose changes instantaneously when each joint is nudged by a small amount.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Steering Wheel of a Robot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Car Steering Wheel**
> Imagine driving a car. The steering wheel (joints) turns a little, but the car (end-effector) moves in a complex, curved path. The relationship between *how much* you turn the wheel and *how the car actually moves* depends on the car's current geometry (is the car parked? moving fast? on a curve?). That *geometry-dependent mapping* is exactly what the **Jacobian** does for a robot manipulator.
> - At one configuration, turning a joint may move the end-effector a lot (lever arm is long).
> - At another configuration (e.g., arm folded), the same joint motion may produce almost no end-effector motion.
> The Jacobian captures this geometry-dependent "sensitivity" of the end-effector to joint motions.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Robotics

| Aspect | Role of the Joint-velocity / End-effector Relationship |
|---|---|
| **Trajectory planning** | Converts desired Cartesian path velocities into joint motor commands |
| **Force control** | Maps contact forces at end-effector into equivalent joint torques |
| **Singularity detection** | Detects configurations where the manipulator loses a degree of freedom |
| **Sensor-based control** | Resolves measured end-effector velocities back to joint rates |
| **Redundancy resolution** | For $n > m$ arms, finds joint motions that optimize secondary criteria |

### 1.4 Two Variants of the Jacobian

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Jacobian** $J_g(q)$ — relates joint rates to the *linear velocity $v$* and *angular velocity $\omega$* of the end-effector expressed in the **base frame**.
>
> **Analytical Jacobian** $J_a(q)$ — relates joint rates to the *time derivative of the chosen task-space parameterization* (e.g., $\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{z}, \dot{\phi}, \dot{\theta}, \dot{\psi}$ for ZYZ Euler angles). They coincide when Euler rates equal angular velocity (only at certain conventions).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of the Velocity Relationship

The forward kinematic equation maps joint coordinates to Cartesian coordinates:

$$x = f(q) \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$$

Differentiating with respect to time using the **multivariate chain rule**:

$$\dot{x} = \frac{d}{dt}f(q(t)) = \frac{\partial f}{\partial q}\cdot \frac{dq}{dt}$$

This is the very definition of the Jacobian, leading to the canonical equation

$$\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$$

## 2.2 Construction of the Jacobian — Column-by-Column

For an $n$-joint manipulator, the Jacobian is an $m \times n$ matrix where the **$i$-th column** $J_i$ represents the contribution of joint $i$ to the end-effector velocity. The rules below are derived from rigid-body kinematics using DH parameters and are extremely high-yield for KTU board exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Column Construction Rule (For joint $i$ with axis $z_{i-1}$ in the base frame):**

### 2.2.1 Revolute Joint (Rotation about $z_{i-1}$)

When joint $i$ is revolute, rotating it by $\dot{q}_i$ produces:
- A linear velocity of the end-effector $v = z_{i-1} \times (p_e - p_{i-1})\;\dot{q}_i$
- An angular velocity $\omega = z_{i-1}\;\dot{q}_i$

Therefore the $i$-th column of the geometric Jacobian is

$$J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \times (p_e - p_{i-1}) \\ z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(6x1, for revolute joint)}$$

### 2.2.2 Prismatic Joint (Translation along $z_{i-1}$)

When joint $i$ is prismatic, sliding it by $\dot{q}_i$ produces:
- A linear velocity $v = z_{i-1}\;\dot{q}_i$
- Zero angular velocity $\omega = 0$

Therefore

$$J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(6x1, for prismatic joint)}$$

## 2.3 Two Reference Frames — Important Distinction

| Frame Convention | Symbol | Common in |
|---|---|---|
| Base (world) frame Jacobian | $^0J(q)$ | KTU textbooks (Spong, Hutchinson, Vidyasagar) |
| End-effector frame Jacobian | $^eJ(q)$ | Force control, resolved rate |

**Conversion rule:** $^0J = \begin{bmatrix} ^0R_e & 0 \\ 0 & ^0R_e \end{bmatrix} \,^eJ$

## 2.4 Singularities — When the Jacobian Fails

> [!WARNING]
> **Singularity Condition**
> A manipulator configuration $q_s$ is a **singular configuration** if the Jacobian $J(q_s)$ is rank-deficient:
> $$\det\!\left(J(q)\,J^{T}(q)\right) = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \sigma_{\min}(J) = 0$$
> At a singularity, joint motions may produce zero end-effector motion, or finite end-effector motion may require infinite joint rates.

**Three classes of singularities** (Yoshikawa's classification):
1. **Boundary singularities** — occur at the limits of the reachable workspace.
2. **Interior singularities** — occur strictly inside the workspace due to alignment of joint axes.
3. **Algorithmic singularities** — arise purely from the chosen task-space parameterization (e.g., Euler-angle representation).

## 2.5 Manipulability — A Scalar Performance Index

> [!NOTE]
> **Yoshikawa's Manipulability Index**
> $$w(q) = \sqrt{\det\!\left(J(q)\,J^{T}(q)\right)}$$
> Measures the "distance" of $J$ from singularity. Used to optimize postures away from singular configurations.

## 2.6 Static Force Relationship — The Dual of the Jacobian

Using the **Principle of Virtual Work**, joint torques and end-effector forces are dual to the velocity relationship:

$$\tau = J^{T}(q)\,F$$

where $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the joint torque vector and $F \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the Cartesian force/torque wrench applied at the end-effector.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$ | Velocity mapping (joint → Cartesian) | $m/s$ ↔ $rad/s$ |
| 2 | $J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1}\times(p_e - p_{i-1}) \\ z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$ | Column for revolute joint | — |
| 3 | $J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | Column for prismatic joint | — |
| 4 | $\tau = J^{T}(q)\,F$ | Static force/torque relation | $N\cdot m$ ↔ $N$ |
| 5 | $\det(JJ^{T}) = 0$ | Singularity condition | dimensionless |
| 6 | $w(q) = \sqrt{\det(JJ^{T})}$ | Manipulability index | $m \cdot rad$ |
| 7 | $\dot{q} = J^{+}\dot{x} + (I - J^{+}J)\dot{q}_0$ | General solution (Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse $J^{+}$) | $rad/s$ |
| 8 | $V = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^{T}M(q)\dot{q}$ | Kinetic energy (used to derive $M(q)$ matrix) | Joules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** Linear velocity has units of **m/s**, angular velocity has units of **rad/s**. The Jacobian $J$ therefore carries mixed units; in rigid columns the units cancel appropriately when computing $\dot{x} = J\dot{q}$.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial welding robots** use resolved-rate control to maintain constant tool-tip speed along curved seams.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci)** rely on the Jacobian to convert surgeon hand motion into precise instrument-tip motion.
- **Humanoid balance control** uses $J$ to map desired center-of-mass velocities to joint commands.
- **Haptic devices** exploit $\tau = J^{T}F$ to render virtual contact forces back to the operator.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity Relationship from First Principles

**Starting point.** Let the forward kinematics give the end-effector position and orientation as a function of joint variables:

$$x(t) = f(q(t)) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$$

where $q(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the vector of joint variables.

**Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to time.** Using the multivariate chain rule on each component $x_i$:

$$\frac{dx_i}{dt} = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{\partial f_i}{\partial q_j}\cdot \frac{dq_j}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Write in matrix form.** Stacking all $m$ components into a single vector:

$$\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial q_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial q_n} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial q_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial q_n} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{q}_n \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Jacobian.** By definition, the matrix on the right is $J(q)$:

$$\dot{x} = J(q)\,\dot{q}$$

**Step 4 — Note the structural interpretation.** Each element $J_{ij}$ is the partial derivative of the $i$-th Cartesian coordinate with respect to the $j$-th joint variable — i.e., the *instantaneous sensitivity* of end-effector coordinate $i$ to motion of joint $j$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Column Form for a Revolute Joint

**Physical setup.** Consider joint $i$ of a serial chain, with axis $z_{i-1}$ and a point on the axis at position $p_{i-1}$. The end-effector is at position $p_e$.

**Step 1 — Velocity of any point on a rotating rigid body.** A point on a rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ about a point $r_0$ has velocity:

$$v = \omega \times (p - r_0)$$

**Step 2 — Apply to joint $i$.** When only joint $i$ moves (with $\dot{q}_i$), the angular velocity of the link is $\omega = z_{i-1}\dot{q}_i$. The end-effector linear velocity becomes:

$$v_e = z_{i-1}\dot{q}_i \times (p_e - p_{i-1})$$

Using the cyclic property of the scalar triple product, this is equivalently written using the cross product:

$$v_e = (z_{i-1} \times (p_e - p_{i-1}))\,\dot{q}_i$$

**Step 3 — Express angular velocity of the end-effector.** The end-effector's angular velocity due to joint $i$ is simply:

$$\omega_e = z_{i-1}\,\dot{q}_i$$

**Step 4 — Stack into a 6×1 column.** Combining linear and angular parts:

$$J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \times (p_e - p_{i-1}) \\ z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

This column is multiplied by $\dot{q}_i$ to give the contribution of joint $i$ to the end-effector velocity. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Jacobian of a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

**Manipulator description.** Two revolute joints in the $xy$-plane with link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$.

**Step 1 — Forward kinematics.** Position of the end-effector:

$$x = l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$
$$y = l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

Orientation (end-effector angle): $\phi = q_1 + q_2$

**Step 2 — Form the analytical Jacobian by direct differentiation.**

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} = -l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)$$
$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} = -l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)$$
$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} = l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)$$
$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} = l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)$$
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial q_1} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial q_2} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the 3×2 Jacobian.**

$$J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity equation.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\phi} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Find singularities.** The manipulator is in a singular configuration when $J$ loses rank. Computing the determinant of the upper 2×2 block:

$$\det\begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

Expanding:

$$= -l_1 l_2\sin q_1 \cos(q_1+q_2) + l_1 l_2\cos q_1 \sin(q_1+q_2)$$
$$= l_1 l_2\left[\sin(q_1+q_2)\cos q_1 - \cos(q_1+q_2)\sin q_1\right]$$
$$= l_1 l_2 \sin\!\left((q_1+q_2) - q_1\right) = l_1 l_2 \sin q_2$$

**Step 6 — Singularity condition.** Setting the determinant to zero:

$$l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad q_2 = 0 \text{ or } q_2 = \pi$$

Physically: **arm fully stretched out** ($q_2 = 0$) or **arm fully folded back** ($q_2 = \pi$). $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Static Force Relationship

**Step 1 — Apply the Principle of Virtual Work.** A set of joint torques $\tau$ producing a virtual joint displacement $\delta q$ does work:

$$\delta W = \tau^{T}\delta q$$

A Cartesian force $F$ producing a virtual end-effector displacement $\delta x$ does work:

$$\delta W = F^{T}\delta x$$

**Step 2 — Equate both expressions.**

$$\tau^{T}\delta q = F^{T}\delta x$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\delta x = J(q)\delta q$:**

$$\tau^{T}\delta q = F^{T}J(q)\delta q = (J^{T}F)^{T}\delta q$$

**Step 4 — Equate coefficients of $\delta q$:**

$$\tau = J^{T}(q)F \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Joint-Velocity / End-Effector Relationships

| Component | Symbol | Maps From | Maps To | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward kinematics | $f(q)$ | Joint space | Task space | Always defined |
| Velocity mapping | $J(q)\dot{q}$ | Joint rates | End-effector twist | Defined everywhere |
| Inverse velocity (square $J$) | $\dot{q} = J^{-1}\dot{x}$ | End-effector twist | Joint rates | Requires $\det J \neq 0$ |
| Inverse velocity (rectangular $J$) | $\dot{q} = J^{+}\dot{x}$ | End-effector twist | Joint rates (least-squares) | Uses Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse |
| Force mapping | $\tau = J^{T}F$ | End-effector wrench | Joint torque | Dual of velocity mapping |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
Define the manipulator Jacobian. State the relationship between joint velocities and end-effector velocities.

**Model Answer:**

The **Manipulator Jacobian** $J(q)$ is the matrix of first-order partial derivatives of the forward kinematic function $f(q)$ with respect to the joint variables $q$. It is configuration-dependent and maps joint velocities to the linear and angular velocities of the end-effector.

The velocity relationship is:

$$\dot{x} = J(q)\,\dot{q}$$

where $\dot{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ is the end-effector twist (linear and angular velocity) and $\dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the joint velocity vector. For a non-redundant manipulator with a square Jacobian, the inverse mapping is $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\dot{x}$, provided the configuration is non-singular.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
What is a singular configuration of a robot manipulator? How is it detected using the Jacobian?

**Model Answer:**

A **singular configuration** is a joint configuration $q_s$ at which the Jacobian matrix $J(q_s)$ loses full rank, i.e., $\text{rank}(J) < \min(m, n)$. At such configurations, the manipulator loses one or more degrees of freedom in Cartesian space, and:

- Infinitely small joint motions can produce large end-effector velocities (or no end-effector motion at all in the lost direction).
- Conversely, a finite end-effector velocity may demand **infinite joint rates**, which is physically impossible.

**Detection:** A configuration is singular when

$$\det\!\left(J(q)\,J^{T}(q)\right) = 0$$

equivalently, when the smallest singular value of $J$ equals zero. For a square Jacobian, simply $\det J = 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, 2024 Scheme | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the general expression for the geometric Jacobian of an $n$-DOF serial manipulator, stating clearly the column construction rules for revolute and prismatic joints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $l_1 = 1$ m, $l_2 = 1$ m, with both joints revolute. Compute the Jacobian at the configuration $q_1 = 90°$, $q_2 = -90°$ and find the end-effector velocity when $\dot{q} = [0.5,\; 0.3]^{T}$ rad/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The forward kinematics gives the end-effector pose as a function of joint variables: $x = f(q)$.

Differentiating with respect to time using the chain rule:

$$\dot{x} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial q}\dot{q} = J(q)\dot{q}$$

The Jacobian $J(q)$ is an $m \times n$ matrix (typically $m = 6$) whose $i$-th column is the contribution of joint $i$ alone. From rigid-body kinematics, with $z_{i-1}$ being the axis of joint $i$ and $p_{i-1}$ a point on that axis, $p_e$ the end-effector position:

**For a revolute joint $i$:**

$$J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \times (p_e - p_{i-1}) \\ z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

**For a prismatic joint $i$:**

$$J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Stacking all $n$ columns side by side gives the full geometric Jacobian:

$$J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} J_1 & J_2 & \cdots & J_n \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$$

The $z_{i-1}$ and $p_{i-1}$ are obtained from the homogeneous transformation $T^{i-1}_0(q)$ derived using DH parameters.

**Model Solution for (b):**

The forward kinematics is:
- $x = l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2)$
- $y = l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)$
- $\phi = q_1 + q_2$

The Jacobian is:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 - l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -l_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & l_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting $l_1 = l_2 = 1$, $q_1 = 90° = \pi/2$, $q_2 = -90° = -\pi/2$:

- $q_1 + q_2 = 0$
- $\sin q_1 = 1$, $\cos q_1 = 0$
- $\sin(q_1+q_2) = 0$, $\cos(q_1+q_2) = 1$

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \cdot 1 - 1 \cdot 0 & -1 \cdot 0 \\ 1 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 1 & 1 \cdot 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

End-effector velocity:

$$\dot{x} = J\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 \\ 0.8 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

So $\dot{x} = -0.5$ m/s, $\dot{y} = 0.8$ m/s, $\dot{\phi} = 0.8$ rad/s.

---

### Question B — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, 2024 Scheme | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** State and prove the principle of virtual work as applied to a robotic manipulator, and hence derive the static force-torque relationship $\tau = J^{T}F$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the 2-DOF planar manipulator of Question A, an end-effector force $F = [5,\; 0,\; 0]^{T}$ (N, N, N·m) is applied at the configuration $q_1 = 60°$, $q_2 = 30°$. Compute the equivalent joint torques. Take $l_1 = l_2 = 0.5$ m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Principle of Virtual Work** states that for a system in static equilibrium, the total virtual work done by all forces and torques through any virtual displacement consistent with the constraints is zero.

For the manipulator, if joint torques $\tau$ act through virtual joint displacement $\delta q$ and end-effector force $F$ acts through virtual end-effector displacement $\delta x$, then:

$$\delta W_{\text{total}} = \tau^{T}\delta q - F^{T}\delta x = 0$$

(The minus sign reflects $F$ being external work done *on* the manipulator, equivalent to work done *by* joint torques.)

Since the virtual displacements are related by $\delta x = J(q)\delta q$, we substitute:

$$\tau^{T}\delta q = F^{T}J(q)\delta q = (J^{T}F)^{T}\delta q$$

Equating the coefficients of $\delta q$ (which is arbitrary and non-zero):

$$\tau = J^{T}(q)F$$

This is the **static force-torque relationship** — the dual of the velocity relationship. It tells us that a force at the end-effector produces joint torques equal to the Jacobian transpose times that force.

**Model Solution for (b):**

At $q_1 = 60°$, $q_2 = 30°$, with $l_1 = l_2 = 0.5$ m:

- $q_1 + q_2 = 90°$
- $\sin 60° = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$, $\cos 60° = 0.5$
- $\sin 90° = 1$, $\cos 90° = 0$
- $\sin 30° = 0.5$, $\cos 30° = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$

Substituting into the Jacobian (multiplying by 0.5 for the link lengths):

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5\sin 60° - 0.5\sin 90° & -0.5\sin 90° \\ 0.5\cos 60° + 0.5\cos 90° & 0.5\cos 90° \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5(0.866) - 0.5(1) & -0.5(1) \\ 0.5(0.5) + 0.5(0) & 0.5(0) \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.933 & -0.5 \\ 0.25 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Transpose:

$$J^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.933 & 0.25 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Applied force: $F = [5,\; 0,\; 0]^{T}$

$$\tau = J^{T}F = \begin{bmatrix} -0.933 & 0.25 & 1 \\ -0.5 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.933 \times 5 \\ -0.5 \times 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4.665 \\ -2.5 \end{bmatrix}\text{ N·m}$$

So $\tau_1 = -4.665$ N·m, $\tau_2 = -2.5$ N·m.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign errors in cross products:** When computing $z_{i-1} \times (p_e - p_{i-1})$, forgetting that the result is in the base frame and not the $i$-th frame is a common mistake.
> 2. **Units confusion:** Mixing linear and angular quantities in $J$ without proper unit tracking leads to mark deductions.
> 3. **Forgetting the orientation row:** The angular-velocity rows of the Jacobian are easily omitted, especially in the 6-DOF case.
> 4. **Singularity miscalculation:** Students often compute $\det J = 0$ on a rectangular Jacobian (rectangular matrices do not have a determinant in the usual sense); use $\det(JJ^{T}) = 0$ instead.
> 5. **Inverse vs. Pseudoinverse:** For redundant manipulators ($n > m$), one **must** use the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse $J^{+}$, not $J^{-1}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Joint & End-Effector Velocities**
> - ⭐ The **Jacobian** $J(q)$ is the cornerstone of manipulator velocity analysis: $\dot{x} = J\dot{q}$.
> - ⭐ Each **column** of $J$ corresponds to a single joint:
>   - Revolute → $\begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \times (p_e - p_{i-1}) \\ z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$
>   - Prismatic → $\begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$
> - ⭐ The Jacobian is computed from the **DH table** — extract $z_{i-1}$ and $p_{i-1}$ from $T^{i-1}_0$.
> - ⭐ The **dual relationship** between velocity and force: $\tau = J^{T}F$ (Principle of Virtual Work).
> - ⭐ **Singularities** occur when $\text{rank}(J) < \min(m, n)$ → manipulator loses a degree of freedom.
> - ⭐ For **redundant** manipulators, use the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse $J^{+}$ for inverse velocity.
> - ⭐ **Yoshikawa's manipulability** $w = \sqrt{\det(JJ^{T})}$ measures the distance from singularity.
> - ⭐ At a singularity, joint rates can become unbounded for a desired finite end-effector velocity.
> - ⭐ For 2-DOF planar arms with link lengths $l_1, l_2$, the determinant is $l_1 l_2 \sin q_2$ → singular when $q_2 = 0$ or $\pi$.
> - ⭐ The **analytical Jacobian** depends on the chosen orientation parameterization (Euler angles, RPY, etc.); the **geometric Jacobian** always gives the true angular velocity $\omega$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manipulator Dynamics - Lagrange's Formulation - Kinetic Energy Expression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Manipulator Dynamics - Lagrange's Formulation - Kinetic Energy Expression # Module 3 | Manipulator Dynamics: Lagrange's Formulation — Kinetic Energy Expression ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Manipulator Dynamics** is the mathematical study of the relationship between the generalized forces (joint torques/forces) acting on a robotic manipulator and the resulting motion of its links, accounting for **inertial, Coriolis, centrifugal, gravitational, and frictional effects**. The **Lagrangian formulation** (also called the *Lagrange–Euler formulation*) of manipulator dynamics expresses the equations of motion in a compact, systematic, and coordinate-free form. It is based on the principle of **virtual work** and **d'Alembert's principle**, and is derived from the **Lagrangian function**: $$L(q, \dot{q}) = K(q, \dot{q}) - P(q)$$ where $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the vector of generalized joint coordinates, $K$ is the total **kinetic energy** of the manipulator, and $P$ is the total **potential energy** of the manipulator. The general **Lagrange equation** of the second kind for the $i^{th}$ generalized coordinate is: $$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$ where $\tau_i$ is the generalized force/torque applied at joint $i$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** In this topic, we are concerned with the derivation of the **kinetic energy term** $K(q, \dot{q})$ for an $n$-DOF serial-chain manipulator. The kinetic energy term is the **foundation** of the entire dynamic model, because once $K$ is expressed, the manipulator inertia matrix $M(q)$ follows directly. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Skaters Holding Hands" Intuition Imagine **$n$ skaters** (representing the $n$ links of a robotic arm) holding hands in a chain on a frictionless ice rink. When the first skater pushes (joint torque), the whole chain moves. The energy of the system is **not just the sum of individual energies** — it is the total kinetic energy of all skaters, each moving with **coupled velocities** that depend on **every** joint's motion. - The **velocity of the $i^{th}$ skater** (link $i$) is a combination of **every** skater's push (joint velocity). This coupling is mathematically captured by the **Jacobian matrix** $J_i$. - The **mass of each skater** and how their mass is distributed (heavy backpack near shoulders vs. light hands) corresponds to the link's **mass and rotational inertia tensor**. **The kinetic energy of the entire chain** = sum over all skaters of (translational KE of that skater) + (rotational KE of that skater about its center of mass). > [!NOTE] > **Why Lagrange over Newton–Euler?** > - Newton–Euler: recursive, computationally efficient, but equation-by-equation. > - **Lagrange–Euler: closed-form, structured, suitable for symbolic derivation, control law design (e.g., computed torque), and stability analysis** — preferred in KTU theory examinations. ### 1.3 Standard Nomenclature (KTU Convention) The following constants, variables, and metrics are used throughout this topic: - **$n$** — number of joints / degrees of freedom (DOF) of the manipulator. - **$q_i$** — generalized coordinate (joint variable) of joint $i$ (angle for revolute, displacement for prismatic). **Unit:** rad or m. - **$\dot{q}_i$** — generalized velocity of joint $i$. **Unit:** rad/s or m/s. - **$m_i$** — mass of link $i$. **Unit:** kg. - **$I_i$** — inertia tensor of link $i$ about its center of mass, expressed in the base/inertial frame. **Unit:** kg·m². - **$v_i$** — linear velocity of the center of mass of link $i$. **Unit:** m/s. - **$\omega_i$** — angular velocity of link $i$. **Unit:** rad/s. - **$J_{v_i}$** — **linear (translational) Jacobian** of link $i$ (3×n matrix). - **$J_{\omega_i}$** — **angular Jacobian** of link $i$ (3×n matrix). - **$K_i$** — kinetic energy of link $i$. **Unit:** J (joule). - **$K$** — total kinetic energy of the manipulator. **Unit:** J (joule). - **$M(q)$** — **Manipulator Inertia Matrix** (n×n, symmetric, positive-definite). **Unit:** kg·m². - **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** (used later in potential energy). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Philosophy Behind Lagrange's Formulation Unlike Newton's second law (which is a vector equation in Cartesian space), the Lagrangian method uses **scalar energy functions** as the starting point. The key steps in the philosophy are: 1. **Choose generalized coordinates** ($q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n$) that completely describe the configuration of the manipulator. For a serial chain, these are naturally the joint variables. 2. **Express the kinetic and potential energies** as scalar functions of $q$ and $\dot{q}$ — i.e., $K(q, \dot{q})$ and $P(q)$. 3. **Apply the Euler–Lagrange equation** to each generalized coordinate to obtain one scalar equation of motion. 4. **Combine** the $n$ scalar equations into a compact matrix form. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The kinetic energy expression we derive here will eventually lead to the **manipulator equation of motion** in the canonical form: > $$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$ > where $M(q)$ comes from $K$, $C$ contains Coriolis/centrifugal terms, and $G$ comes from $P$. This single equation is the cornerstone of **model-based robot control**. ## 2.2 Building the Kinetic Energy — Step-by-Step Logic ### Step 1: Decompose the Manipulator into Rigid Links A serial-chain manipulator with $n$ joints has exactly $n$ rigid links. Link $i$ is the rigid body located **between** joint $i$ and joint $i+1$. The total kinetic energy is the sum of the kinetic energies of all individual links: $$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i$$ **Why sum?** Because kinetic energy is a **scalar quantity**, and energy is additive across non-interpenetrating rigid bodies. This is the **superposition principle** applied to energy. ### Step 2: Express the Kinetic Energy of a Single Link For a rigid body (link $i$) performing general 3D motion, the kinetic energy is the sum of: - **Translational kinetic energy** of the center of mass: $\frac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i}$ - **Rotational kinetic energy** about the center of mass: $\frac{1}{2} \omega_i^T \, I_i \, \omega_i$ Therefore: $$K_i = \frac{1}{2} \, m_i \, v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2} \, \omega_i^T \, I_i \, \omega_i$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Point:** The inertia tensor $I_i$ must be expressed in the **same reference frame** as the angular velocity $\omega_i$. By convention in the KTU/standard robotics texts (Spong, Hutchinson, Vidyasagar), this is the **base (inertial) frame**. ### Step 3: Relate Link Velocities to Joint Velocities via the Jacobian The velocities $v_{c_i}$ and $\omega_i$ of link $i$ depend on **all** the joint velocities $\dot{q}$ (because of the kinematic chain coupling). This functional relationship is given by the **Jacobian matrices**: $$v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q) \, \dot{q}$$ $$\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q) \, \dot{q}$$ where $J_{v_i}$ and $J_{\omega_i}$ are 3×n matrices whose entries are functions of $q$. Substituting: $$K_i = \frac{1}{2} \, m_i \, \dot{q}^T \, J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} \, \dot{q} + \frac{1}{2} \, \dot{q}^T \, J_{\omega_i}^T \, I_i \, J_{\omega_i} \, \dot{q}$$ ### Step 4: Sum Over All Links — The Total Kinetic Energy $$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \frac{1}{2} \, \dot{q}^T \, \left( m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right) \dot{q} \right]$$ Factoring out $\frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T$ from the left and $\dot{q}$ from the right: $$K = \frac{1}{2} \, \dot{q}^T \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right) \right] \dot{q}$$ ### Step 5: Identify the Manipulator Inertia Matrix $M(q)$ Defining the **manipulator mass matrix** (also called the **kinetic energy matrix** or **inertia matrix**): $$M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right)$$ we obtain the elegant **quadratic form**: $$\boxed{K = \frac{1}{2} \, \dot{q}^T \, M(q) \, \dot{q}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Box:** The expression $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$ is the **most exam-critical formula** in this entire module. The matrix $M(q)$ is $n \times n$, **symmetric** ($M = M^T$), and **positive-definite** ($x^T M x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$). Positive-definiteness guarantees that $K > 0$ for any non-zero velocity, which is physically meaningful. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $L = K - P$ | Lagrangian | J | | 2 | $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | Euler–Lagrange equation (scalar form) | N·m or N | | 3 | $K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i$ | Total KE = sum of link KEs | J | | 4 | $K_i = \frac{1}{2}m_i v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2}\omega_i^T I_i \omega_i$ | KE of single link $i$ | J | | 5 | $v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q) \dot{q}$ | Linear velocity of COM of link $i$ | m/s | | 6 | $\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q) \dot{q}$ | Angular velocity of link $i$ | rad/s | | 7 | $K_i = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T\!\left(m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i}\right)\!\dot{q}$ | KE of link $i$ in joint space | J | | 8 | $M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\!\left(m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i}\right)$ | Manipulator inertia matrix | kg·m² (or kg) | | 9 | $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$ | Total KE (canonical form) | J | | 10 | $M = M^T$ (symmetric) | Property of inertia matrix | — | | 11 | $x^T M x > 0, \, x \neq 0$ | Positive-definiteness | — | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The kinetic energy expression $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$ and the underlying $M(q)$ matrix are not just theoretical constructs — they power real engineering applications: - **Computed-Torque Control:** The control law $\tau = M(q)\!\left[\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e\right] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ requires $M(q)$ explicitly. - **Energy-Based Control:** Passivity-based and energy-shaping controllers exploit the storage function $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$ to prove Lyapunov stability. - **Trajectory Optimization:** Direct collocation methods (e.g., in industrial software like **RoboDK, MATLAB Robotics Toolbox, MoveIt**) need symbolic or numerical evaluation of $M(q)\ddot{q}$ terms. - **Industrial Robots (KUKA, ABB, FANUC):** Their internal motion controllers run simplified versions of Lagrangian dynamics at 1 kHz or higher. - **Humanoid Robots (ASIMO, Atlas, Digit):** Energy-based whole-body controllers critically rely on the structure of $M(q)$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation A — From Newton's Rigid-Body KE to the Quadratic Form We start with the most fundamental definition of kinetic energy of a rigid body and show how the manipulator inertia matrix emerges naturally. **Given:** A serial-chain manipulator with $n$ revolute and/or prismatic joints, with joint coordinate vector $q = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$. **Step A.1:** The total kinetic energy of the manipulator is the scalar sum of the kinetic energies of its $n$ rigid links: $$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i$$ **Step A.2:** For a single rigid link $i$ moving freely in 3D, the kinetic energy is the sum of translational KE of the center of mass and rotational KE about the COM. The general rigid-body KE theorem states: $$K_i = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} m_i \left(v_{c_i} \cdot v_{c_i}\right)}_{\text{translational}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \left(\omega_i \cdot I_i \cdot \omega_i\right)}_{\text{rotational}}$$ In matrix/vector notation (using the dot-product as $a^T a$ and $a^T B a$): $$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i \, v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2} \omega_i^T \, I_i \, \omega_i$$ **Step A.3:** The forward kinematics of the manipulator give the position and orientation of every link as a function of $q$. Differentiating these with respect to time, the velocities of link $i$ are linear functions of $\dot{q}$, with the Jacobian matrices as the coefficients: $$v_{c_i} = \frac{\partial p_{c_i}}{\partial q} \, \dot{q} \;\equiv\; J_{v_i}(q) \, \dot{q}$$ $$\omega_i = \frac{\partial \phi_i}{\partial q} \, \dot{q} \;\equiv\; J_{\omega_i}(q) \, \dot{q}$$ where $p_{c_i}$ is the position of the COM of link $i$ and $\phi_i$ represents the orientation parameters. **Step A.4:** Substitute Step A.3 into Step A.2: $$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i \left(J_{v_i} \dot{q}\right)^T \left(J_{v_i} \dot{q}\right) + \frac{1}{2} \left(J_{\omega_i} \dot{q}\right)^T I_i \left(J_{\omega_i} \dot{q}\right)$$ Using the identity $(AB)^T = B^T A^T$: $$K_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i \, \dot{q}^T J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} \, \dot{q} + \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \, \dot{q}$$ **Step A.5:** Factor out the scalar $\frac{1}{2}$ and the vector $\dot{q}^T$ from the left and $\dot{q}$ from the right: $$K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \left( m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right) \dot{q}$$ **Step A.6:** Sum over all $n$ links. Because scalar addition is commutative and the bracketed term is a sum, we can factor out $\frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \ldots \dot{q}$ as a common structure: $$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right) \right] \dot{q}$$ **Step A.7:** Define the **manipulator inertia matrix** as the bracketed sum: $$M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right)$$ This yields the final compact expression: $$\boxed{K(q, \dot{q}) = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}}$$ **Step A.8 — Symmetry proof:** Consider the $(j,k)$-th entry of $M(q)$: $$M_{jk} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( m_i \left(J_{v_i}^T\right)_{j,:} \left(J_{v_i}\right)_{:,k} + \left(J_{\omega_i}^T\right)_{j,:} I_i \left(J_{\omega_i}\right)_{:,k} \right)$$ Swapping $j$ and $k$: $$M_{kj} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( m_i \left(J_{v_i}^T\right)_{k,:} \left(J_{v_i}\right)_{:,j} + \left(J_{\omega_i}^T\right)_{k,:} I_i \left(J_{\omega_i}\right)_{:,j} \right)$$ Note that $\left(J_{v_i}^T\right)_{j,:} = \left(J_{v_i}\right)_{:,j}^T$ and the scalar $m_i \left(J_{v_i}^T\right)_{j,:} \left(J_{v_i}\right)_{:,k} = m_i \left(J_{v_i}^T\right)_{k,:} \left(J_{v_i}\right)_{:,j}$ (since both equal $m_i$ times a real scalar product of column $j$ and column $k$ of $J_{v_i}$). The same argument holds for the rotational term. Therefore $M_{jk} = M_{kj}$, i.e., $M = M^T$. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation B — Worked Example: 2-DOF Planar Manipulator (KTU Exam Standard) Consider a **2-DOF planar RR manipulator** with link lengths $l_1, l_2$, link masses $m_1, m_2$, treated as point masses at the link ends (a common KTU simplification), with joint angles $q_1, q_2$. **Step B.1:** The position of COM of link 1 (at distance $l_1$ from joint 1): $$p_{c_1} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 c_1 \\ l_1 s_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{where } c_1 = \cos q_1, \; s_1 = \sin q_1$$ **Step B.2:** Velocity of COM of link 1: $$v_{c_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 s_1 \dot{q}_1 \\ l_1 c_1 \dot{q}_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \;\Rightarrow\; |v_{c_1}|^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2$$ Angular velocity of link 1 (revolute joint ⇒ $\omega_1 = \dot{q}_1 \hat{z}$): $$\omega_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \dot{q}_1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step B.3:** Position of COM of link 2 (at distance $l_1 + l_2$ from base in 2D, or at $l_2$ from joint 2 — here we use point-mass at joint 2 location): $$p_{c_2} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} \\ l_1 s_1 + l_2 s_{12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad c_{12} = \cos(q_1+q_2), \; s_{12} = \sin(q_1+q_2)$$ **Step B.4:** Velocity of COM of link 2 (partial derivatives with respect to $q_1$ and $q_2$): $$v_{c_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 s_1 \dot{q}_1 - l_2 s_{12}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) \\ l_1 c_1 \dot{q}_1 + l_2 c_{12}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ Squaring the magnitude: $$|v_{c_2}|^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) \cos q_2$$ Angular velocity of link 2: $\omega_2 = (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \hat{z}$. **Step B.5:** Total kinetic energy (point-mass assumption: ignore rotational inertia, keep only translational): $$K = \frac{1}{2} m_1 |v_{c_1}|^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 |v_{c_2}|^2$$ $$K = \frac{1}{2} m_1 l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 \left[ l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) \cos q_2 \right]$$ **Step B.6:** Collect terms in the quadratic form $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$: $$K = \frac{1}{2} \left[ (m_1+m_2) l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 \right] \dot{q}_1^2 + m_2 \left[ l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 \right] \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 l_2^2 \dot{q}_2^2$$ Reading off the matrix entries (using $K = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j,k} M_{jk} \dot{q}_j \dot{q}_k$): $$\boxed{M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 c_2 & m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 c_2 \\ m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 c_2 & m_2 l_2^2 \end{bmatrix}}$$ **Step B.7 — Verification of symmetry:** Clearly $M_{12} = M_{21}$ ✓. The off-diagonal terms depend on $q_2$, confirming that $M$ is **configuration-dependent** ✓. With $m_1 = m_2 = m$ and $l_1 = l_2 = l$: $$M(q) = ml^2 \begin{bmatrix} 3 + 2c_2 & 1 + c_2 \\ 1 + c_2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ This is a well-known result in Spong's textbook and is highly likely to appear in a 14-mark KTU question. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Manipulator Inertia Matrices (Standard Results) | Manipulator Type | DOF $n$ | Inertia Matrix $M(q)$ (entries) | |------------------|---------|---------------------------------| | Single revolute joint | 1 | $\left[ I_{zz,1} \right]$ (scalar) | | 2-DOF planar RR (point masses) | 2 | $M_{11} = (m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 c_2$
$M_{12} = M_{21} = m_2 l_1 l_2 c_2 + m_2 l_2^2$
$M_{22} = m_2 l_2^2$ | | 2-DOF Cartesian (PPP) | 2 | $\text{diag}(m_1, m_2)$ (constant) | | Spherical (RRP) | 3 | Config-dependent (see Spong Ch. 7) | | General $n$-DOF | $n$ | $M_{jk} = \sum_{i=\max(j,k)}^{n}\left[ m_i (J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i})_{jk} + (J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i})_{jk} \right]$ | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Off-diagonal entries $M_{jk}$ ($j \neq k$) represent **coupling inertias** between joints $j$ and $k$. When $M_{jk} = 0$, joints $j$ and $k$ are **dynamically decoupled** (e.g., Cartesian robot has decoupled, constant inertia). # 4.
