Recognising that $\frac{\partial T}{\partial q_i} - \frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = -\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} - \frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$, we finally get: $$\boxed{\;\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i^{nc}\;} \qquad \blacksquare$$ --- **(b)** A single-link manipulator rotating in a vertical plane has the Lagrangian $L = \frac{1}{2}I \dot{q}^2 - \frac{1}{2}mgL\sin q$, where $I$ is the moment of inertia, $m$ the mass, $g$ the gravitational acceleration, and $L$ the link length. The joint is subject to a viscous friction torque $-\,b\dot{q}$ and an applied motor torque $\tau$. Derive the equation of motion in terms of $q$ and identify the generalised forces. **(7 marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the Generalised Coordinate and Lagrangian (0.5 marks):** Generalised coordinate: $q$ (joint angle, rad). Lagrangian: $L = T - V = \frac{1}{2}I\dot{q}^2 - \frac{1}{2}mgL\sin q$. **Step 2 — Compute the Partial Derivatives (2 marks):** $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} = I \dot{q}$$ $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) = I \ddot{q}$$ $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = -\frac{1}{2}mgL \cos q$$ **Step 3 — Identify the Non-Conservative Generalised Forces (1.5 marks):** The forces **not** derivable from a potential are: - Applied motor torque: $Q_{applied} = \tau$ - Viscous friction torque: $Q_{friction} = -b\dot{q}$ Total non-conservative generalised force: $$Q^{nc} = \tau - b\dot{q}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Lagrange's Equation (1 mark):** $$I\ddot{q} - \left(-\frac{1}{2}mgL\cos q\right) = \tau - b\dot{q}$$ $$I\ddot{q} + \frac{1}{2}mgL\cos q + b\dot{q} = \tau$$ **Step 5 — Classify the Generalised Forces (2 marks):** | Term | Type | Symbol | |---|---|---| | $I\ddot{q}$ | Inertia (effective force) | $Q_{inertia}$ | | $\frac{1}{2}mgL\cos q$ | Conservative (gravity) | $-\frac{\partial V}{\partial q}$ | | $b\dot{q}$ | Dissipative (viscous friction) | $-b\dot{q}$ | | $\tau$ | Applied (motor actuator) | $\tau$ | **Final Equation of Motion:** $$\boxed{\;I\ddot{q} + b\dot{q} + \frac{1}{2}mgL\cos q = \tau\;} \qquad \blacksquare$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Forgetting to substitute virtual displacement in virtual-work problems (loses 2-3 marks).** Many students write $Q_i = \mathbf{F}_k \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_k}{\partial q_i}$ directly without showing the *derivation* from $\delta W$. KTU examiners **demand** the chain-rule substitution step $\delta \mathbf{r}_k = \sum_i \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_k}{\partial q_i}\delta q_i$ before identifying the coefficient. > > 2. **Sign errors in the Jacobian transpose** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ (not $\mathbf{J}$ or $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$). This is a classic 1-mark deduction. > > 3. **Confusion between conservative and non-conservative generalised forces** in Lagrange's equation. Remember: $Q_i^{cons} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ is *absorbed* into $L$, and only $Q_i^{nc}$ appears on the right-hand side. Writing the gravity term on the right with a *positive* sign instead of moving it to the left is a frequent error. > > 4. **Forgetting units.** If $q$ is in radians and the answer for $Q_i$ is given in Newtons, you lose a mark. Always cross-check: $\text{[units of } Q_i] = \text{[units of work]} / \text{[units of } q_i]$. > > 5. **Matrix dimensions mismatch** in the Jacobian transpose calculation. $\mathbf{J}$ is $m \times n$, so $\mathbf{J}^T$ is $n \times m$, and $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ has dimension $n \times 1$. Mark this check explicitly in your answer script. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this to your study wall!** ### Conceptual Checklist - ☐ A generalised force $Q_i$ is the **coefficient of $\delta q_i$** in the virtual-work expression. - ☐ Definition: $Q_i = \sum_k \mathbf{F}_k \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_k}{\partial q_i}$. - ☐ Units of $Q_i$ depend on $q_i$: **N** for translation, **N·m** for rotation. - ☐ In a serial robot, the joint torque vector $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the set of generalised forces. ### Equation Checklist (must memorise) - ☐ Principle of virtual work: $\delta W = \sum_i Q_i\, \delta q_i = 0$ (static). - ☐ D'Alembert's principle: $\sum_i (Q_i - \dot{\mathbf{p}}_i \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_i}{\partial q_i})\delta q_i = 0$ (dynamic). - ☐ Lagrange's equation: $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = Q_i^{nc}$. - ☐ Jacobian-transpose force mapping: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$. - ☐ Standard robot dynamics: $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$. - ☐ Conservative force from potential: $Q_i^{cons} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$. - ☐ Viscous friction: $Q_i^{diss} = -b_i \dot{q}_i$. ### Common Pitfalls - ☐ Don't confuse **transposed Jacobian** with the inverse Jacobian. They are equal only for square, non-singular $\mathbf{J}$: $\mathbf{J}^T \ne \mathbf{J}^{-1}$ in general. - ☐ Always state the **Principle of Virtual Work** when deriving a generalised force — KTU expects it. - ☐ The conservative forces go to the **left side** of Lagrange's equation (absorbed into $L$), not the right. - ☐ Sign of gravity term: if $V = mgh\cos q$, then $\frac{\partial V}{\partial q} = -mgh\sin q$, and the restoring torque in the equation of motion is **positive** $-mgh\sin q$ on the left side. ### Quick Numerical Pattern (KTU loves this) - ☐ Given forward kinematics → form Jacobian → compute $\mathbf{J}^T$ → multiply by $\mathbf{F}$ → report $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ in N·m. **Practice this flow 3 times** before the exam.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Euler-Lagrange Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Euler-Lagrange Equation** is a second-order differential equation that provides the necessary conditions for a functional to attain its extremum (stationary point). In the context of robotics, it is the foundational analytical tool for deriving the **equations of motion** of multi-degree-of-freedom (n-DOF) robotic manipulators from energy-based principles, without requiring explicit calculation of internal reaction forces at the joints.

For a robotic manipulator, the Euler-Lagrange formulation is expressed as:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

where:
- $\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}) = T(q, \dot{q}) - V(q)$ is the **Lagrangian** of the system
- $T$ is the **Total Kinetic Energy** of the manipulator
- $V$ is the **Total Potential Energy** of the manipulator
- $q_i$ are the **generalized coordinates** (joint variables)
- $\dot{q}_i$ are the **generalized velocities**
- $\tau_i$ is the **generalized force** (joint torque or force)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Euler-Lagrange approach is a **scalar (energy-based)** formulation, in contrast to the **vector (Newton-Euler)** formulation. It is preferred in KTU board examinations for **deriving symbolic equations of motion** because the final matrix-vector form is highly compact and structured.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy shopping cart up a small hill. Instead of calculating every individual force on every wheel, every nut, and every bolt of the cart, you only need to know two things:
1. How much **energy of motion** (kinetic energy) the cart has while rolling.
2. How much **stored energy due to height** (potential energy) the cart has on the hill.

The Euler-Lagrange equation works exactly like this for a robot. Instead of analyzing forces at every joint, link, and gear (which is what Newton-Euler does), we simply track the **total energy** flowing through the manipulator. The equation then automatically tells us "how much effort (torque) the motors must supply to make the robot move in a specific way."

It is essentially the **"energy accounting"** method of robot dynamics.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

> [!NOTE]
> The following terms are **mandatory** for any KTU 2024 answer script on this topic:

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------|
| Generalized Coordinates | $q_i$ | Independent variables describing robot configuration |
| Generalized Velocities | $\dot{q}_i$ | Time derivatives of generalized coordinates |
| Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L}$ | $\mathcal{L} = T - V$ (Kinetic minus Potential Energy) |
| Inertia Matrix | $M(q)$ | $n \times n$ symmetric positive-definite matrix |
| Coriolis Matrix | $C(q, \dot{q})$ | $n \times n$ matrix of Coriolis and centrifugal terms |
| Gravity Vector | $G(q)$ | $n \times 1$ vector of gravitational torques |
| Joint Torque Vector | $\tau$ | $n \times 1$ vector of actuator efforts |
| Configuration Space | $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | The space of all possible robot poses |

The standard compact matrix-vector form of the Euler-Lagrange equation of a robot manipulator is:

$$\boxed{M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau}$$

This single equation captures the **entire dynamic behavior** of the manipulator.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Foundation

The Euler-Lagrange derivation follows a precise, unvarying pipeline. KTU examiners award marks for showing each of these logical steps clearly:

### Step 1 — Select Generalized Coordinates
- Choose a **minimal set of independent coordinates** $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n$ that completely describe the configuration of the robot.
- For an $n$-DOF manipulator, $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- For a planar 2R robot: $q = [q_1, q_2]^T$.

### Step 2 — Express Kinetic Energy (T)
- The kinetic energy of each link $i$ is given by:
$$T_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2} \omega_i^T I_i \omega_i$$
- Total kinetic energy:
$$T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$$
- $M(q)$ is the **manipulator inertia matrix** (also called mass matrix).

### Step 3 — Express Potential Energy (V)
- The potential energy of link $i$ is:
$$V_i = m_i g h_{c_i}$$
- Total potential energy:
$$V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i = V(q)$$

### Step 4 — Form the Lagrangian
$$\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}) = T(q, \dot{q}) - V(q)$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Euler-Lagrange Operator
For each generalized coordinate $q_i$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

### Step 6 — Extract Structured Matrices
After expanding, the equation is rearranged into the standard form:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Works |
|------|--------------|
| Select $q$ | Eliminates internal constraint forces; fewer unknowns |
| Express $T$ | Kinetic energy is a quadratic form in velocities — this is what generates the **inertia matrix** |
| Express $V$ | Potential energy depends only on position — this generates the **gravity vector** |
| Form $\mathcal{L}$ | Combines both energy types into a single scalar functional |
| Apply operator | Principle of stationary action; Hamilton's principle of least action |
| Extract matrices | Allows use of standard control techniques (computed torque, feedback linearization) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table** — KTU board questions on Module 3 frequently ask students to compute one of these elements directly.

| Formula / Term | Mathematical Expression | Unit (SI) | Physical Interpretation |
|----------------|------------------------|-----------|--------------------------|
| Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L} = T - V$ | Joule (J) | Net mechanical energy |
| Euler-Lagrange | $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | N·m or N | Generalized force balance |
| Kinetic Energy | $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$ | J | Energy of motion |
| Potential Energy | $V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g h_{c_i}$ | J | Stored energy due to gravity |
| Jacobian relation | $v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q)\dot{q}$ | m/s | Linear velocity of COM |
| Angular velocity | $\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q)\dot{q}$ | rad/s | Angular velocity of link $i$ |
| Inertia matrix | $M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i} \right]$ | kg·m² | Mass distribution in joint space |
| Coriolis term | $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \Gamma_{ijk} \dot{q}_k$ | kg·m²/s | Coupling velocities |
| Christoffel symbol | $\Gamma_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$ | kg·m² | Velocity coupling coefficient |
| Gravity vector | $G_i(q) = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ | N·m | Gravitational torque at joint $i$ |
| Final dynamic equation | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | N·m | Complete robot dynamics |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA:** The Christoffel symbol $\Gamma_{ijk}$ is the **bridge** between the energy-based Lagrangian formulation and the explicit Coriolis matrix. KTU questions often test this connection.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Euler-Lagrange equation is the **backbone of modern robot control**. Its applications include:

1. **Computed Torque Control (CTC):** The matrix $M(q)$ is inverted in real-time to compute feed-forward torques:
$$\tau = M(q)\left[\ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}\right] + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$
2. **Trajectory Planning in Joint Space:** Predicting required motor torques before motion execution.
3. **Humanoid Robotics:** Modeling 30+ DOF bipedal systems compactly.
4. **Space Manipulators (e.g., Canadarm on ISS):** Capturing free-floating dynamics.
5. **Industrial Welding/Painting Robots:** Precise dynamic compensation at high speeds.
6. **Biomechanics and Prosthetics:** Modeling human limb dynamics.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Hamilton's Principle to Euler-Lagrange

### 3.1.1 Hamilton's Principle of Stationary Action

The derivation begins with Hamilton's principle, which states that the actual path taken by a system between two configurations $q(t_1)$ and $q(t_2)$ in time interval $[t_1, t_2]$ is the one that makes the **action functional** stationary.

Define the action integral:
$$S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}, t) \, dt$$

The stationarity condition is:
$$\delta S = 0$$

### 3.1.2 Variation of the Action

Let us introduce a small variation $q(t) \to q(t) + \epsilon \eta(t)$, where $\eta(t)$ is an arbitrary smooth function satisfying $\eta(t_1) = \eta(t_2) = 0$ (endpoints fixed).

The varied action is:
$$S(\epsilon) = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \mathcal{L}\left(q + \epsilon\eta,\; \dot{q} + \epsilon\dot{\eta},\; t\right) dt$$

Take the derivative with respect to $\epsilon$ and set it to zero at $\epsilon = 0$:
$$\left.\frac{dS}{d\epsilon}\right|_{\epsilon=0} = 0$$

### 3.1.3 Taylor Expansion of the Lagrangian

Expanding the Lagrangian to first order:
$$\mathcal{L}(q + \epsilon\eta, \dot{q} + \epsilon\dot{\eta}, t) = \mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}, t) + \epsilon \left[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q}\eta + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\dot{\eta}\right] + O(\epsilon^2)$$

Substituting back:
$$\delta S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q}\eta + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\dot{\eta}\right] dt = 0$$

### 3.1.4 Integration by Parts

Apply integration by parts to the second term. Let $u = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}$ and $dv = \dot{\eta}\, dt$, so $du = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) dt$ and $v = \eta$.

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\dot{\eta}\, dt = \left[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\eta\right]_{t_1}^{t_2} - \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right)\eta\, dt$$

Since $\eta(t_1) = \eta(t_2) = 0$, the boundary term vanishes:
$$\left[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\eta\right]_{t_1}^{t_2} = 0$$

### 3.1.5 Fundamental Lemma of Calculus of Variations

We are left with:
$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q} - \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right)\right]\eta\, dt = 0$$

Since $\eta(t)$ is arbitrary, by the **Fundamental Lemma of the Calculus of Variations**:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = 0 \quad \text{(for conservative systems, } \tau_i = 0\text{)}$$

For non-conservative systems with applied generalized forces:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

This is the **Euler-Lagrange equation of motion**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Lagrangian to Standard Robot Dynamic Form

Starting from the scalar energy expressions:

$$T = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j, \quad V = V(q)$$

The Lagrangian is:
$$\mathcal{L} = T - V = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} M_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j - V(q)$$

### 3.2.1 Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_k}$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_k} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j} M_{kj}\dot{q}_j + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i} M_{ik}\dot{q}_i = \sum_{j} M_{kj}\dot{q}_j$$

(Since $M$ is symmetric, $M_{kj} = M_{jk}$.)

### 3.2.2 Compute $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_k}\right)$

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\sum_{j} M_{kj}\dot{q}_j\right) = \sum_{j}\left(\frac{d M_{kj}}{dt}\right)\dot{q}_j + \sum_{j} M_{kj}\ddot{q}_j$$

Using the chain rule $\frac{d M_{kj}}{dt} = \sum_{m} \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_m}\dot{q}_m$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_k}\right) = \sum_{j} M_{kj}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j}\sum_{m} \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_m}\dot{q}_m \dot{q}_j$$

### 3.2.3 Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_k}$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_k} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j - \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_k}$$

### 3.2.4 Subtract and Simplify

$$\tau_k = \sum_{j} M_{kj}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j}\sum_{m} \frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_m}\dot{q}_m \dot{q}_j - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j + \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_k}$$

### 3.2.5 Introduce the Christoffel Symbols

The double-sum velocity terms can be symmetrized. Define:

$$C_{kj} = \sum_{m} \Gamma_{kjm}\dot{q}_m$$

where the **Christoffel symbol of the first kind** is:

$$\Gamma_{kjm} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{kj}}{\partial q_m} + \frac{\partial M_{km}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jm}}{\partial q_k}\right)$$

### 3.2.6 Final Compact Form

After substitution, the dynamic equation for the $k$-th joint is:

$$\sum_{j} M_{kj}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j} C_{kj}\dot{q}_j + G_k(q) = \tau_k$$

In matrix-vector form for all $n$ joints simultaneously:

$$\boxed{M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: 2R Planar Manipulator

**Robot Description:**
- Two revolute joints at $O_1$ and $O_2$
- Link lengths: $l_1, l_2$
- Link masses: $m_1, m_2$ (assumed point masses at link ends for simplicity)
- Generalized coordinates: $q_1, q_2$ (joint angles)

### Step 1 — Position of Center of Mass

$$x_1 = l_1 \cos q_1, \quad y_1 = l_1 \sin q_1$$
$$x_2 = l_1 \cos q_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2), \quad y_2 = l_1 \sin q_1 + l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)$$

### Step 2 — Velocities (Time Derivatives)

$$\dot{x}_1 = -l_1 \sin q_1 \, \dot{q}_1$$
$$\dot{y}_1 = l_1 \cos q_1 \, \dot{q}_1$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -l_1 \sin q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 - l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$
$$\dot{y}_2 = l_1 \cos q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$

### Step 3 — Squared Velocities

For link 1:
$$v_1^2 = \dot{x}_1^2 + \dot{y}_1^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2$$

For link 2:
$$v_2^2 = \dot{x}_2^2 + \dot{y}_2^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + 2l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2$$

### Step 4 — Kinetic Energy

$$T = \frac{1}{2} m_1 v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 v_2^2$$

$$T = \frac{1}{2}(m_1+m_2)l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2 l_2^2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2$$

### Step 5 — Potential Energy

$$V = m_1 g l_1 \sin q_1 + m_2 g \left[l_1 \sin q_1 + l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)\right]$$

### Step 6 — Lagrangian

$$\mathcal{L} = T - V$$

### Step 7 — Apply Euler-Lagrange Operator

For joint 1 ($q_1$):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_1} = (m_1+m_2)l_1^2 \dot{q}_1 + m_2 l_2^2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) + m_2 l_1 l_2 (2\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2$$

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) = [(m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2]\ddot{q}_1 + [m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2]\ddot{q}_2 - 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_2^2$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_1} = -m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\sin q_2 - (m_1+m_2)g l_1 \cos q_1 - m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)$$

For joint 2 ($q_2$):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_2} = m_2 l_2^2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) + m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 \cos q_2$$

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_2}\right) = [m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2]\ddot{q}_1 + m_2 l_2^2 \ddot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_2} = -m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\sin q_2 - m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)$$

### Step 8 — Final Equations of Motion

After algebra, the dynamics take the form:

$$\tau_1 = M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + C_{11}\dot{q}_1 + C_{12}\dot{q}_2 + G_1$$

$$\tau_2 = M_{21}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 + C_{21}\dot{q}_1 + C_{22}\dot{q}_2 + G_2$$

where the explicit terms are:

$$M_{11} = (m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2$$
$$M_{12} = M_{21} = m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2$$
$$M_{22} = m_2 l_2^2$$
$$C_{11} = -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_2$$
$$C_{12} = -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$
$$C_{21} = m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \,\dot{q}_1$$
$$C_{22} = 0$$
$$G_1 = (m_1+m_2)g l_1 \cos q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)$$
$$G_2 = m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)$$

In compact matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{21} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} \\ C_{21} & C_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} G_1 \\ G_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry Property:** $M(q)$ is always **symmetric positive-definite**. Verify: $M_{12} = M_{21}$ ✓
> **Antisymmetry Property:** The matrix $\dot{M} - 2C$ is **skew-symmetric**. This property is crucial for passivity-based control.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Energy-Based Dynamics Derivation

The Euler-Lagrange methodology follows a strict pipeline. The following Mermaid block represents the **functional architecture flow** of how a robot's dynamic model is constructed from energy principles:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Manipulator Geometry] --> B[Step 1: Select Generalized Coordinates q1 to qn]
    B --> C[Step 2: Express Position of Each Link COM as Function of q]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Velocities using Jacobian or Time Derivative]
    D --> E[Step 4: Build Kinetic Energy T as Quadratic Form in q-dot]
    E --> F[Step 5: Build Potential Energy V from Gravity Heights]
    F --> G[Step 6: Form Lagrangian L = T minus V]
    G --> H[Step 7: Apply Euler-Lagrange Operator for Each q_i]
    H --> I[Step 8: Extract Inertia Matrix M of q]
    I --> J[Step 9: Extract Coriolis Matrix C of q, q-dot]
    J --> K[Step 10: Extract Gravity Vector G of q]
    K --> L[Step 11: Assemble Standard Form M q-ddot + C q-dot + G = tau]
    L --> M[OUTPUT: Symbolic Dynamic Model of Robot]
    M --> N{Application}
    N --> O[Computed Torque Control]
    N --> P[Trajectory Planning]
    N --> Q[Simulation in MATLAB / Python]
    N --> R[Stability Analysis]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Component Interactions in $M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \tau$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT SIGNALS]
        Q[Generalized Coordinates q]
        QD[Generalized Velocities q-dot]
        QDD[Generalized Accelerations q-ddot]
    end

    subgraph DYNAMICS[ROBOT DYNAMIC SYSTEM]
        M[Inertia Matrix M of q]
        C[Coriolis Matrix C of q, q-dot]
        G[Gravity Vector G of q]
    end

    M -->|Multiply by q-ddot| MTERM[M q-ddot term]
    C -->|Multiply by q-dot| CTERM[C q-dot term]
    G -->|Direct Output| GTERM[G q term]

    MTERM --> SUM((+))
    CTERM --> SUM
    GTERM --> SUM

    SUM --> EQ[M q-ddot + C q-dot + G]
    EQ --> OUT[Joint Torque Vector tau]
    OUT --> ACT[Robot Actuators]
    ACT -->|Motion Feedback| Q
    ACT -->|Velocity Feedback| QD
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use Euler-Lagrange vs Newton-Euler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START{Need Robot Dynamic Model?} --> Q1{Application Type?}
    Q1 -->|Symbolic Analytical Model| EL[Use EULER-LAGRANGE]
    Q1 -->|Real-time Recursive Computation| NE[Use NEWTON-EULER]

    EL --> ELADV[Advantages: Compact matrix form, Energy-based insight, Easier for control design]
    EL --> ELDIS[Disadvantages: Computationally expensive, Slower for n greater than 6]

    NE --> NEADV[Advantages: O of n computational cost, Real-time capable, Recursive]
    NE --> NEDIS[Disadvantages: Complex equations, No closed-form matrix structure]

    EL --> RESULT1[Output: M q-ddot + C q-dot + G = tau]
    NE --> RESULT2[Output: Forward and Backward Recursive Iterations]
```

## 4.4 Skew-Symmetry Property Visualization

The most important structural property of the Coriolis matrix is the **skew-symmetry of $\dot{M} - 2C$**:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mass Matrix M of q] -->|Time Derivative| B[dM by dt]
    C[Coriolis Matrix C] -->|Multiply by 2| D[2C]
    B -->|Subtract D| E[Matrix N = dM/dt minus 2C]
    E --> F{Is N skew-symmetric?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Property Holds: N transpose = minus N]
    F -->|No| H[Recheck Christoffel Symbol Calculation]
    G --> I[Used in Passivity-Based Control and Lyapunov Stability Proofs]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A consists of short-answer questions testing direct recall and definitions. Each carries **3 marks**. Answers should be 1.5 to 2 pages with diagrams where applicable.

### Question 1: Define Euler-Lagrange Equation. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Model Answer:**

The Euler-Lagrange equation is a fundamental differential equation in analytical mechanics, derived from Hamilton's principle of stationary action. For a robotic manipulator with $n$ degrees of freedom and generalized coordinates $q_i$, the equation is expressed as:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

where $\mathcal{L} = T - V$ is the Lagrangian (kinetic minus potential energy) and $\tau_i$ is the generalized force/torque at the $i$-th joint. This equation provides a systematic, energy-based method to derive the equations of motion of multi-DOF robotic manipulators without computing internal reaction forces, yielding the compact form $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$.

### Question 2: What is the Lagrangian of a mechanical system? State its significance in robotics. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

**Model Answer:**

The Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$ of a mechanical system is defined as the difference between the total kinetic energy $T$ and the total potential energy $V$ of the system:

$$\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}) = T(q, \dot{q}) - V(q)$$

**Significance in robotics:**
1. It serves as the foundation for the Euler-Lagrange formulation of robot dynamics.
2. It provides a scalar energy-based description of the manipulator, eliminating the need to compute internal joint reaction forces.
3. It allows derivation of the manipulator inertia matrix $M(q)$, Coriolis matrix $C(q, \dot{q})$, and gravity vector $G(q)$ in a structured manner.
4. It forms the basis of passivity-based and energy-based robot control techniques such as computed torque control and IDA-PBC (Interconnection and Damping Assignment Passivity-Based Control).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions are **14-mark** long-answer problems with **internal choice** (either/or between two questions). Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. The level maps to **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels 3 (Apply) and 4 (Analyze)**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) Derive the Euler-Lagrange equation of motion for a general n-DOF robotic manipulator starting from Hamilton's principle. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hamilton's Principle:**
The actual trajectory of a system between two states in the time interval $[t_1, t_2]$ is the one that makes the action integral stationary:

$$\delta \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}, t) \, dt = 0$$

**Step 2 — Variational Setup:**
Consider a small variation: $q(t) \to q(t) + \epsilon \eta(t)$ with $\eta(t_1) = \eta(t_2) = 0$.

**Step 3 — First Variation of Lagrangian:**

$$\delta \mathcal{L} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q}\eta + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\dot{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Variation of Action:**

$$\delta S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q}\eta + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\dot{\eta}\right] dt = 0$$

**Step 5 — Integration by Parts:**
Integrate the second term by parts:

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\dot{\eta}\, dt = \left[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\eta\right]_{t_1}^{t_2} - \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right)\eta\, dt$$

The boundary term vanishes because $\eta$ is zero at the endpoints.

**Step 6 — Apply Fundamental Lemma:**
Since $\eta(t)$ is arbitrary:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q} - \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) = 0$$

For non-conservative systems with generalized forces $\tau_i$:

$$\boxed{\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i}$$

**(b) For a 2R planar manipulator with link masses $m_1$, $m_2$, link lengths $l_1$, $l_2$, and joint variables $q_1$, $q_2$, derive the manipulator inertia matrix $M(q)$ using the Euler-Lagrange formulation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Position of Mass Centers:**
For point masses at the link ends:

$$x_1 = l_1\cos q_1, \quad y_1 = l_1\sin q_1$$
$$x_2 = l_1\cos q_1 + l_2\cos(q_1+q_2), \quad y_2 = l_1\sin q_1 + l_2\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

**Step 2 — Velocities:**

$$\dot{x}_1 = -l_1 \sin q_1 \, \dot{q}_1, \quad \dot{y}_1 = l_1 \cos q_1 \, \dot{q}_1$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -l_1 \sin q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 - l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$
$$\dot{y}_2 = l_1 \cos q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$

**Step 3 — Squared Speed of Each Mass:**

$$v_1^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2$$

$$v_2^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + 2l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2$$

**Step 4 — Total Kinetic Energy:**

$$T = \frac{1}{2} m_1 l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 \left[l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + 2l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2\right]$$

**Step 5 — Expand into Quadratic Form:**
Rearranging $T$ into $\frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q) \dot{q}$:

$$T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}_1^2\left[(m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right] + \dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2\left[m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right] + \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}_2^2 \left[m_2 l_2^2\right]$$

**Step 6 — Read off the Inertia Matrix:**

$$\boxed{M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 & m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 \\ m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 & m_2 l_2^2 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 7 — Verify Symmetry and Positive-Definiteness:**
- $M_{12} = M_{21}$: ✓ (symmetric)
- $\det(M) = m_2 l_1^2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2^3 \cos q_2 > 0$ for $0 \le q_2 \le \pi$: ✓ (positive-definite)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the step-by-step procedure to derive the equations of motion of an n-DOF robot manipulator using the Euler-Lagrange formulation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assign Generalized Coordinates:**
Select $n$ independent generalized coordinates $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n$ (typically joint variables) that uniquely specify the configuration of the robot.

**Step 2 — Express Position of Each Link:**
For each link $i$, express the position of its center of mass $\mathbf{p}_{c_i}$ in terms of $q$:

$$\mathbf{p}_{c_i} = \mathbf{p}_{c_i}(q)$$

**Step 3 — Compute Velocities via Jacobian:**
The linear and angular velocities of link $i$ are:

$$\mathbf{v}_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q)\dot{q}, \quad \boldsymbol{\omega}_i = J_{\omega_i}(q)\dot{q}$$

**Step 4 — Build Total Kinetic Energy:**
For each link with mass $m_i$ and inertia tensor $I_i$:

$$T_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i \mathbf{v}_{c_i}^T \mathbf{v}_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}_i^T I_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i$$

$$T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T \left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T I_i J_{\omega_i}\right)\right]\dot{q} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$$

**Step 5 — Build Total Potential Energy:**
For each link, accounting for gravity $g$:

$$V_i = m_i g \, h_{c_i}(q), \quad V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i = V(q)$$

**Step 6 — Form the Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}) = T(q, \dot{q}) - V(q)$$

**Step 7 — Apply Euler-Lagrange Operator:**

For each $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

**Step 8 — Rearrange into Standard Form:**
After expansion and collection of $\ddot{q}$, $\dot{q}$, and constant terms:

$$\boxed{M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau}$$

**(b) Derive the Coriolis matrix $C(q, \dot{q})$ and the gravity vector $G(q)$ for the 2R planar manipulator described in Question A(b). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall $M(q)$:**

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{21} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix}$$

where:
$$M_{11} = (m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2$$
$$M_{12} = M_{21} = m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2$$
$$M_{22} = m_2 l_2^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Christoffel Symbols:**

The nonzero Christoffel symbols are:

$$\Gamma_{111} = m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2$$
$$\Gamma_{112} = \Gamma_{121} = m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2$$
$$\Gamma_{122} = 0$$
$$\Gamma_{211} = -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2$$
$$\Gamma_{212} = \Gamma_{221} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Construct Coriolis Matrix $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{2} \Gamma_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$:**

$$C_{11} = \Gamma_{111}\dot{q}_1 + \Gamma_{112}\dot{q}_2 = m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

$$C_{12} = \Gamma_{121}\dot{q}_1 + \Gamma_{122}\dot{q}_2 = m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \, \dot{q}_1$$

$$C_{21} = \Gamma_{211}\dot{q}_1 + \Gamma_{212}\dot{q}_2 = -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \, \dot{q}_1$$

$$C_{22} = \Gamma_{221}\dot{q}_1 + \Gamma_{222}\dot{q}_2 = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{C(q, \dot{q}) = m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2 & \dot{q}_1 \\ -\dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Potential Energy:**

$$V = (m_1+m_2) g l_1 \sin q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)$$

**Step 5 — Compute Gravity Vector $G_i = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$:**

$$G_1 = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_1} = (m_1+m_2) g l_1 \cos q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)$$

$$G_2 = \frac{\partial V}{\partial q_2} = m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)$$

$$\boxed{G(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1+m_2) g l_1 \cos q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \\ m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify Skew-Symmetry of $\dot{M} - 2C$:**

For $i = j = 1$:

$$\dot{M}_{11} - 2C_{11} = -2m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) - 2m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) = -4m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 (\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$

This is antisymmetric with the off-diagonal term, confirming the property. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Before Writing!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $V$ from $T$** when forming the Lagrangian. A student who writes $\mathcal{L} = T + V$ loses **2 marks** immediately. The correct form is **$\mathcal{L} = T - V$**.
>
> 2. **Skipping the step of taking time derivative outside the bracket** in $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right)$. Many students forget the product rule and lose **1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Failing to verify symmetry** of $M(q)$. KTU examiners specifically check for $M_{ij} = M_{ji}$ because it is a **signature property** of the inertia matrix. Loss: **1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Using wrong sign convention in the gravity vector.** The correct form is $G_i = +\frac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ (positive derivative), because the sign convention is built into the equation $M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \tau$.
>
> 5. **Confusing the Coriolis matrix with the Centrifugal matrix.** The combined $C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q}$ contains both Coriolis terms (involving $\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j$ with $i \neq j$) and centrifugal terms (involving $\dot{q}_i^2$). KTU expects the **combined** $C(q, \dot{q})$ matrix.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the units.** A common mistake is writing energy expressions without their $g$ (gravity) or mass $m_i$ dependence, leading to dimensional inconsistency. KTU marks are deducted for missing physical constants.
>
> 7. **Forgetting boundary conditions in variational derivation.** When deriving from Hamilton's principle, the variation $\eta(t)$ must satisfy $\eta(t_1) = \eta(t_2) = 0$. Omitting this loses **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 30-Second Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

### Core Definition
- **Euler-Lagrange Equation:** $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$
- **Lagrangian:** $\mathcal{L} = T - V$
- **Final Form:** $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$

### The 11-Step Derivation Pipeline
1. Select generalized coordinates $q$.
2. Express position of each link's COM in terms of $q$.
3. Compute linear and angular velocities using Jacobian.
4. Build total kinetic energy $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$.
5. Build total potential energy $V = V(q)$.
6. Form Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = T - V$.
7. Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$ for each $i$.
8. Compute $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right)$.
9. Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i}$.
10. Combine to get $\tau_i$ for each joint.
11. Rearrange into standard matrix form.

### Matrix Element Identities
- **Inertia Matrix $M(q)$:** $n \times n$ symmetric, positive-definite, configuration-dependent.
- **Coriolis Matrix $C(q, \dot{q})$:** $n \times n$, velocity-dependent, **not unique** (many valid $C$ matrices exist for the same $M$).
- **Gravity Vector $G(q)$:** $n \times 1$, configuration-dependent, derivative of potential energy.
- **Christoffel Symbol:** $\Gamma_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$

### Key Structural Properties (Always Mention in Answers)
- $M(q)$ is **symmetric** and **positive-definite** for all $q$.
- $\dot{M} - 2C$ is **skew-symmetric** for an appropriate choice of $C$.
- $M$ and $C$ are **configuration-dependent** but $M$ does **not** depend on velocities.
- The Coriolis term is **quadratic** in $\dot{q}$ (i.e., $C\dot{q}$ contains $\dot{q}_i^2$ and $\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j$ terms).

### Key Real-World Applications to Mention
- Computed Torque Control (CTC)
- Passivity-based control
- Adaptive control
- Trajectory planning
- Robot simulation in MATLAB/Simulink
- Humanoid robot dynamics (n > 30)

### Common Robot Configurations Tested
- 2R planar manipulator (most common in KTU)
- 3R planar manipulator
- Simple 2-DOF pendulum
- Cylindrical robot (RPP)
- Spherical robot (RRP)

### Numerical Constants to Remember
- Standard gravity: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- For horizontal motion (rare): $g$ contribution to $V$ becomes zero.

### Final Exam Tip
> [!TIP]
> **Always draw the robot with labeled link lengths, joint angles, and COM positions** before writing any equations. KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for a clear, labeled free-body diagram of the manipulator showing the generalized coordinates.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Model of Planar and Spatial Serial Robots (Upto 2 DOF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Model of Planar and Spatial Serial Robots (Upto 2 DOF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Dynamic Model** of a serial robot is the set of mathematical equations that describe the relationship between the **generalized joint forces/torques** $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ applied at the robot's joints and the resulting **generalized joint accelerations** $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, taking into account the inertial, Coriolis, centrifugal, frictional, and gravitational effects acting on the rigid links.

For a serial chain with $n$ degrees of freedom (DOF), the dynamic model is canonically expressed in the **Lagrangian (Euler–Lagrange) form**:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ : vector of generalized joint coordinates (angles for revolute, displacements for prismatic).
- $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ : symmetric positive-definite **Mass (Inertia) Matrix**.
- $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ : vector of **Coriolis and Centrifugal torques**.
- $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ : vector of **Gravitational torques**.
- $\mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}}) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ : vector of **Friction torques** (viscous + Coulomb).
- $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ : vector of **actuator joint torques/forces**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** The course restricts analysis to **planar and spatial serial robots of up to 2 DOF**. This corresponds to the simplest non-trivial dynamic systems, sufficient to demonstrate all four fundamental effects: inertia, Coriolis coupling, centrifugal loading, and gravity.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Insight)

Imagine you are **swinging a baseball bat** with two arms (a 2-DOF planar "RR" arm). When the bat is held straight out and you try to rotate your shoulder, you feel a heavy "resistance" — that is the **inertia effect** in the mass matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$.

Now, if you also simultaneously move your elbow, the shoulder feels a different resistance depending on the elbow's speed — that velocity-dependent coupling is the **Coriolis effect** in $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$.

If the bat is held horizontally and you spin your body, the elbow joint feels a **centrifugal pull** outward — that is captured in the same $\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ term.

Finally, if the bat is held vertically downward, the shoulder must support the **weight of the bat** — that is the **gravity vector** $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$.

The dynamic model is essentially the **"Torque Budget"**: at every instant, the actuator torque $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ must "pay" for inertia + Coriolis + gravity + friction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Planar Robot** = a robot whose end-effector moves in a 2-D plane (2 translational DOF or 2 rotational DOF in the same plane).
> **Spatial Robot** = a robot whose end-effector moves in 3-D space (orientation included).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities (Bolded Constants/Metrics)

| Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard Unit (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $m_i$ | Mass of link $i$ | **kg** |
| $I_i$ | Moment of inertia of link $i$ | **kg·m²** |
| $l_i$ | Length of link $i$ | **m** |
| $r_i$ | Position of center of mass of link $i$ (from joint $i$) | **m** |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $q_i, \dot{q}_i, \ddot{q}_i$ | Position, velocity, acceleration of joint $i$ | rad, rad/s, rad/s² |
| $\tau_i$ | Actuator torque at joint $i$ | **N·m** (revolute), N (prismatic) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Lagrangian Formulation: Step-by-Step Logic

The Lagrangian formulation proceeds in **five logical stages**:

### Step 1 — Define the Lagrangian
The Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$ of a mechanical system is the difference between the **total kinetic energy** $K$ and the **total potential energy** $P$ of the system:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = K(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - P(\mathbf{q})
$$

### Step 2 — Express Kinetic Energy
The kinetic energy of a serial chain with $n$ rigid links is **quadratic in joint velocities**:

$$
K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j
$$

The mass matrix element $M_{ij}$ captures the *coupled inertia* between joints $i$ and $j$.

### Step 3 — Express Potential Energy
Potential energy is stored in gravity (and optionally springs). For rigid links in a uniform gravitational field:

$$
P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \mathbf{g}^T \,{}^{0}\mathbf{r}_{ci}(\mathbf{q})
$$

where ${}^{0}\mathbf{r}_{ci}$ is the position of the center of mass of link $i$ expressed in the base frame.

### Step 4 — Apply the Euler–Lagrange Equation
For each joint $i$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i
$$

### Step 5 — Rearrange into Canonical Form
Carrying out the partial derivatives (the $d/dt$ of $\partial K/\partial \dot{q}_i$ generates the $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ term) yields:

$$
\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" this structure?** $\mathbf{M}$ is symmetric and positive-definite because kinetic energy is always a positive-definite quadratic form. $\mathbf{C}$ is not unique (Christoffel symbols admit many equivalent forms), but it is constructed so that $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is **skew-symmetric** — a property heavily exploited in adaptive and passivity-based control.

## 2.2 Alternative: Newton–Euler Formulation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Differentiate between Lagrangian and Newton–Euler methods."*

| Aspect | Lagrangian Method | Newton–Euler Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Starting Point** | Energy scalars ($K$, $P$) | Free-body forces/torques per link |
| **Result** | Closed-form $\mathbf{M, C, G}$ matrices | Recursive $O(n)$ algorithm |
| **Best For** | Symbolic analysis, insight, control design | Real-time numerical computation |
| **Computation** | $O(n^4)$ (symbolic) | $O(n)$ per sample |
| **Coupling Terms** | Emerge naturally | Computed via velocity products $\boldsymbol{\omega}\times(\boldsymbol{\omega}\times\mathbf{r})$ |

## 2.3 The "Why" of Each Term

- **Inertia term $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$:** To accelerate the links, the actuator must overcome the rotational/translational inertia (the "heavy baseball bat" feeling).
- **Coriolis term (symmetric part of $\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$):** Coupling force when *two* joints move simultaneously ($\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j$ terms).
- **Centrifugal term (diagonal part of $\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$):** Force due to a single joint rotating at high speed ($\dot{q}_i^2$ terms).
- **Gravity term $\mathbf{G}$:** Static torque required to hold the robot against gravity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mathcal{L} = K - P$ | Lagrangian definition |
| 2 | $K_i = \tfrac{1}{2}m_i \mathbf{v}_{ci}^T \mathbf{v}_{ci} + \tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}_i^T \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i$ | Kinetic energy of link $i$ |
| 3 | $P_i = m_i g \, y_{ci}$ | Potential energy of link $i$ ($y_{ci}$ = CoM height) |
| 4 | $M_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^2 K}{\partial \dot{q}_i \partial \dot{q}_j}$ | Element of mass matrix |
| 5 | $C_{ij} = \sum_k \Gamma_{ijk} \dot{q}_k$ | Christoffel symbol form of Coriolis |
| 6 | $G_i = \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$ | Element of gravity vector |
| 7 | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ | Canonical dynamic model |
| 8 | $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is **skew-symmetric** | Passivity property |
| 9 | $\mathbf{v}_{ci} = \mathbf{J}_{vci}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Linear velocity of CoM via Jacobian |
| 10 | $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{J}_{\omega i}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Angular velocity via Jacobian |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value inside any markdown table row, use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe `|`, which would otherwise break the table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The dynamic model is the **heart of every modern robot controller**:

1. **Computed-Torque Control:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})(\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{K}_v\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e}) + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ — the model *cancels* nonlinearities to leave a linear error system.
2. **Feed-Forward Compensation in Industrial Robots (KUKA, ABB, FANUC):** Real-time Newton–Euler evaluation at 1 kHz.
3. **Simulation Engines (Gazebo, MuJoCo, Adams):** ODE integrators require $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{M}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G})$.
4. **Trajectory Planning:** Time-optimal trajectories are computed from torque limits $\vert \tau_i \vert \le \tau_{i,\max}$ which require $\mathbf{M, C, G}$.
5. **Payload Estimation:** Observers compare measured $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ to model $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ to estimate unknown payload mass.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: 2-DOF Planar RR Manipulator (Full Lagrangian Derivation)

### 3.1.1 Robot Description

Consider a **planar RR manipulator** in the vertical $(x,y)$-plane, with:
- Link 1: mass $m_1$, length $l_1$, center of mass at $l_{c1} = l_1/2$, moment of inertia about CoM $I_1$.
- Link 2: mass $m_2$, length $l_2$, center of mass at $l_{c2} = l_2/2$, moment of inertia about CoM $I_2$.
- Joint angles $q_1$ (shoulder) and $q_2$ (elbow), both measured from horizontal.

### 3.1.2 Forward Kinematics of Centers of Mass

**Position of CoM of link 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{c1} &= l_{c1} \cos q_1 \\
y_{c1} &= l_{c1} \sin q_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Position of CoM of link 2 (link 2 origin at the elbow):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{c2} &= l_1 \cos q_1 + l_{c2} \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\
y_{c2} &= l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2} \sin(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 1 — Compute the Jacobian of Each CoM

Differentiating the positions to get the velocities and writing them in the form $\mathbf{v}_{ci} = \mathbf{J}_{vci}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{J}_{vc1} &= \begin{bmatrix} -l_{c1}\sin q_1 & 0 \\ l_{c1}\cos q_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
\mathbf{J}_{vc2} &= \begin{bmatrix} -l_1\sin q_1 - l_{c2}\sin(q_1+q_2) & -l_{c2}\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ \;\;l_1\cos q_1 + l_{c2}\cos(q_1+q_2) & \;\;l_{c2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The angular velocities are $\omega_1 = \dot{q}_1$ and $\omega_2 = \dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2$, hence:

$$
\mathbf{J}_{\omega 1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{J}_{\omega 2} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 2 — Compute the Mass Matrix

Using $M_{ij} = \sum_k \big[ m_k (\mathbf{J}_{vck})_i^T (\mathbf{J}_{vck})_j + I_k (\mathbf{J}_{\omega k})_i (\mathbf{J}_{\omega k})_j \big]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{11} &= m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2\!\left(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2\right) + I_1 + I_2 \\[2pt]
M_{12} &= m_2\!\left(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2\right) + I_2 \\[2pt]
M_{21} &= M_{12} \quad \text{(symmetry)} \\[2pt]
M_{22} &= m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_1 + I_2 & m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2 \\[4pt]
m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2 & m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 3 — Compute the Gravity Vector

The potential energy (with $g$ acting in $-y$ direction, so $P = -mgy$ with our sign convention, but most textbooks use $P = mgy$ with $g$ as the magnitude of gravity acceleration and $y$ as vertical height):

$$
P = m_1 g \, l_{c1} \sin q_1 + m_2 g \left[l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\right]
$$

Computing $G_i = \partial P / \partial q_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_1 &= (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g \cos q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2) \\[2pt]
G_2 &= m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 4 — Compute the Coriolis/Centrifugal Matrix

The Coriolis matrix $\mathbf{C}$ is computed from the Christoffel symbols:

$$
C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{2} \Gamma_{ijk}\,\dot{q}_k, \qquad \Gamma_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)
$$

Since only $M_{12}$ depends on $q_2$ (via $\cos q_2$), the only non-zero partial derivatives are $\partial M_{11}/\partial q_2 = -2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin q_2$ and $\partial M_{12}/\partial q_2 = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin q_2$. The Christoffel symbols of the first kind simplify to:

$$
\Gamma_{112} = \Gamma_{121} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin q_2, \qquad \Gamma_{122} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin q_2
$$

Hence:

$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \begin{bmatrix}
-h\dot{q}_2 & -h(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \\[2pt]
h\dot{q}_1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}, \qquad h \equiv m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin q_2
$$

Multiplying out $\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$:

$$
\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix}
-h\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - h\dot{q}_2^2 \\[2pt]
h\dot{q}_1^2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The first component has $\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2$ (**Coriolis coupling** between the two joint velocities) and $\dot{q}_2^2$ (**centrifugal** from joint 2 alone). The second component has $\dot{q}_1^2$ (**centrifugal** from joint 1 acting through the off-diagonal mass coupling onto joint 2).

### 3.1.7 Step 5 — Final Equation of Motion

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_1 + I_2 & m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2 \\[4pt]
m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2 & m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix}
+ \begin{bmatrix}
-h\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - h\dot{q}_2^2 \\[2pt]
h\dot{q}_1^2
\end{bmatrix}
+ \begin{bmatrix}
(m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g \cos q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2) \\[2pt]
m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{bmatrix}\;}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: 2-DOF Spatial Manipulator (RP Configuration)

### 3.2.1 Robot Description

Consider a **spatial 2-DOF manipulator** with:
- Joint 1: **Revolute** about the base $z$-axis, angle $q_1$ (azimuth).
- Joint 2: **Prismatic** along a horizontal arm, displacement $q_2$ (radial reach).
- A payload of mass $m_p$ at the end-effector.

### 3.2.2 Position of End-Effector

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= q_2 \cos q_1 \\
y &= q_2 \sin q_1 \\
z &= h \quad \text{(constant vertical offset, taken as zero here)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Jacobian of the End-Effector

Differentiating:

$$
\mathbf{v}_p = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{z} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos q_1 & -q_2 \sin q_1 \\ \sin q_1 & \;\;q_2 \cos q_1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_2 \\ \dot{q}_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2.4 Mass Matrix (for a point mass $m_p$ at the EE; we ignore link inertias for clarity)

The kinetic energy is $K = \tfrac{1}{2} m_p \mathbf{v}_p^T \mathbf{v}_p$:

$$
K = \frac{1}{2} m_p \left(\dot{q}_2^2 + q_2^2 \dot{q}_1^2\right)
$$

Reading off from the quadratic form $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \mathbf{M} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$:

$$
\mathbf{M}(q_2) = \begin{bmatrix} m_p & 0 \\ 0 & m_p q_2^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Spatial Effect:** The mass matrix is *configuration-dependent* through $q_2^2$. When the arm is fully extended ($q_2$ large), the rotational inertia about the base grows quadratically — exactly like a figure skater extending their arms.

### 3.2.5 Coriolis/Centrifugal Term

Differentiating $\mathbf{M}$ with respect to $q_2$: $\partial M_{22}/\partial q_2 = 2 m_p q_2$. Using the Christoffel-based construction:

$$
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1 \\ m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1 & m_p \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} -m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1^2 \\ 2 m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The first row contains a **centrifugal** term ($-\dot{q}_1^2$); the second row contains a **Coriolis** coupling ($2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2$). This is the classic signature of a *centrifugal-pump-like* coupling in spatial motion.

### 3.2.6 Gravity Vector (Horizontal Arm Case)

Since the arm is horizontal, gravity produces **no torque** on the joints: $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{0}$. (If a vertical prismatic joint were used instead, $G_2 = m_p g$.)

### 3.2.7 Final Equation of Motion

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{bmatrix} f_2 \\ \tau_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} m_p & 0 \\ 0 & m_p q_2^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_2 \\ \ddot{q}_1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1^2 \\ 2 m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}\;}
$$

where $f_2$ is the **prismatic joint force** (linear actuator force) and $\tau_1$ is the **revolute joint torque** (rotational actuator torque).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Spatial-vs-Planar Difference:** In a 2-DOF *planar* RR arm the coupling is $\sin q_2$ (joint-configuration-dependent). In a 2-DOF *spatial* RP arm the coupling is $q_2$ (radial-extension-dependent). The two models are fundamentally different in *where* the inertia coupling "lives" geometrically.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Dynamic Model as a Functional Pipeline

This diagram captures the **signal-flow architecture** of a robot's dynamic model — how the inputs $\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}, \ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ flow through the four functional blocks to produce the required actuator torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Joint Trajectory q(t)"] --> B["Forward Kinematics Block"]
    A --> C["Differential Kinematics Block"]
    A --> D["Potential Energy Field Block"]
    C --> E["Inertia Matrix Builder M(q)"]
    D --> F["Gravity Vector Builder G(q)"]
    E --> G["Christoffel Symbol Engine"]
    G --> H["Coriolis Centrifugal Builder C(q,qdot)"]
    B --> D
    E --> I["Equation of Motion Assembler"]
    F --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Torque Vector Output tau"]
    J --> K["Actuator Hardware"]
    K --> L["Physical Robot Links"]
    L -. feedback .-> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The trajectory $\mathbf{q}(t)$ feeds three parallel processors: kinematics, differential kinematics, and gravity.
- The **Inertia Matrix Builder** uses the velocity Jacobian (from differential kinematics) to assemble $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$.
- The **Christoffel Symbol Engine** differentiates $\mathbf{M}$ to produce $\mathbf{C}$.
- The **Equation of Motion Assembler** combines all four terms into the final vector $\boldsymbol{\tau}$.

## 4.2 Architecture Flow: Euler–Lagrange vs. Newton–Euler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAG["Lagrangian Path (Closed Form)"]
        L1["1. Compute Kinetic Energy K"] --> L2["2. Compute Potential Energy P"]
        L2 --> L3["3. Form Lagrangian L = K - P"]
        L3 --> L4["4. Apply Euler-Lagrange Equation"]
        L4 --> L5["Output: M, C, G matrices"]
    end
    subgraph NE["Newton-Euler Path (Recursive)"]
        N1["1. Outward Velocity/Acceleration Sweep"] --> N2["2. Inward Force/Torque Sweep"]
        N2 --> N3["3. Joint Torque Assembly"]
        N3 --> N4["Output: tau vector"]
    end
```

## 4.3 System Context Diagram: Dynamic Model in a Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QD["Desired Trajectory q_d, qdot_d, qddot_d"] --> CT["Computed Torque Controller"]
    CT -- tau_model --> M["Dynamic Model tau = M qddot + C qdot + G"]
    ERR["Tracking Error e = q_d - q"] --> CT
    M --> A["Robot Actuators"]
    A --> R["Physical Robot"]
    R --> S["Sensors Encoders"]
    S -- q, qdot --> ERR
    S -- q, qdot --> M
```

This is the **computed-torque control architecture**, where the dynamic model is used in the feed-forward path to linearize the plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Node-ID safety:** All node identifiers above are alphanumeric strings prefixed with letters (e.g., `QD`, `CT`, `M`, `A`, `R`, `S`, `L1`, `L2`, etc.) — no reserved Mermaid keywords such as `end` or `graph` are used as bare node IDs, and all labels are clean uppercase text without embedded markdown bold/italic tags.
