# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**State the canonical form of the dynamic model of a serial robot and identify the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
The dynamic model of an $n$-DOF serial robot in joint space is given by

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ — generalized inertial torque required to accelerate the links.
- $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ — Coriolis and centrifugal torques arising from coupling between joint velocities.
- $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ — gravitational torques due to the weight of the links.
- $\mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ — frictional torques (viscous and Coulomb).

*(3 marks: 1 for equation, 2 for meaning of terms.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between the Lagrangian and Newton–Euler methods of deriving the dynamic model of a robot.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Lagrangian | Newton–Euler |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Basis | Energy (scalar) | Forces and moments (vector) |
| Result | Closed-form $\mathbf{M, C, G}$ matrices | Recursive $O(n)$ algorithm |
| Computation cost | High (symbolic) | Low (numerical, real-time) |
| Used for | Analysis, control design | Real-time torque computation |

*(3 marks: 1 for basis, 1 for result type, 1 for typical use.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice between two alternative questions. Below we provide **Question A** and **Question B** with complete model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]

**(a)** *Derive the equation of motion of a 2-DOF planar RR manipulator using the Euler–Lagrange formulation. Assume uniform links of mass $m_1, m_2$, lengths $l_1, l_2$, with centers of mass at $l_1/2$ and $l_2/2$ respectively. Neglect friction. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For the manipulator in part (a), evaluate the joint torques $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ at the instant $q_1 = \pi/4$, $q_2 = \pi/6$, $\dot{q}_1 = 0.5$ rad/s, $\dot{q}_2 = 0.2$ rad/s, $\ddot{q}_1 = \ddot{q}_2 = 0$. Take $m_1 = m_2 = 2$ kg, $l_1 = l_2 = 1$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s², and treat links as thin rods with $I_i = m_i l_i^2/12$. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark) — Forward kinematics of the centers of mass:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{c1} &= \tfrac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1, & y_{c1} &= \tfrac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1 \\
x_{c2} &= l_1 \cos q_1 + \tfrac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2), & y_{c2} &= l_1 \sin q_1 + \tfrac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 (2 marks) — Kinetic energy (linear + rotational contributions):*

$$
K = \frac{1}{2}m_1(\dot{x}_{c1}^2 + \dot{y}_{c1}^2) + \frac{1}{2}I_1 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2(\dot{x}_{c2}^2 + \dot{y}_{c2}^2) + \frac{1}{2}I_2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2
$$

After algebraic simplification (substituting velocities and grouping terms):

$$
K = \frac{1}{2}\left[m_1\!\left(\tfrac{l_1}{2}\right)^{\!2} + m_2\!\left(l_1^2 + \left(\tfrac{l_2}{2}\right)^{\!2} + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right) + I_1 + I_2\right]\dot{q}_1^2
+ \left[m_2\!\left(\left(\tfrac{l_2}{2}\right)^{\!2} + \tfrac{l_1 l_2}{2}\cos q_2\right) + I_2\right]\dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2
+ \frac{1}{2}\left[m_2\!\left(\tfrac{l_2}{2}\right)^{\!2} + I_2\right]\dot{q}_2^2
$$

*Step 3 (1 mark) — Potential energy:*

$$
P = m_1 g \tfrac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1 + m_2 g\!\left[l_1 \sin q_1 + \tfrac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\right]
$$

*Step 4 (1 mark) — Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = K - P$.*

*Step 5 (1 mark) — Euler–Lagrange equations $\frac{d}{dt}(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{q}_i) - \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial q_i = \tau_i$:*

This yields, after carrying out the differentiation:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
m_1\!\left(\tfrac{l_1}{2}\right)^{\!2} + m_2\!\left(l_1^2 + \left(\tfrac{l_2}{2}\right)^{\!2} + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right) + I_1 + I_2 & m_2\!\left(\left(\tfrac{l_2}{2}\right)^{\!2} + \tfrac{l_1 l_2}{2}\cos q_2\right) + I_2 \\[3pt]
m_2\!\left(\left(\tfrac{l_2}{2}\right)^{\!2} + \tfrac{l_1 l_2}{2}\cos q_2\right) + I_2 & m_2\!\left(\tfrac{l_2}{2}\right)^{\!2} + I_2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix}
-m_2 l_1 \tfrac{l_2}{2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 \tfrac{l_2}{2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_2^2 \\[3pt]
m_2 l_1 \tfrac{l_2}{2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1^2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
(m_1 \tfrac{l_1}{2} + m_2 l_1) g \cos q_1 + m_2 \tfrac{l_2}{2} g \cos(q_1+q_2) \\[3pt]
m_2 \tfrac{l_2}{2} g \cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 6 (1 mark) — Final equation of motion in canonical form $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$.*

**Part (b) — Numerical evaluation (7 marks):**

*Substitute $l_{c1} = l_1/2 = 0.5$ m, $l_{c2} = l_2/2 = 0.5$ m, $I_i = m_i l_i^2/12 = 2 \cdot 1/12 = 1/6$ kg·m².*

*Compute $h = m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin q_2 = 2 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot \sin(\pi/6) = 1 \cdot 0.5 = 0.5$ kg·m². (1 mark)*

*Mass matrix elements (1 mark):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{11} &= 2(0.25) + 2(1 + 0.25 + 2\cdot 1\cdot 0.5 \cdot \cos(\pi/6)) + 1/6 + 1/6 \\
&= 0.5 + 2(1.25 + 0.866) + 0.333 \\
&= 0.5 + 2(2.116) + 0.333 = 0.5 + 4.232 + 0.333 = 5.065 \text{ kg·m}^2 \\[2pt]
M_{12} = M_{21} &= 2(0.25 + 0.5 \cdot 0.866) + 1/6 = 2(0.683) + 0.167 = 1.533 \text{ kg·m}^2 \\[2pt]
M_{22} &= 2(0.25) + 1/6 = 0.5 + 0.167 = 0.667 \text{ kg·m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

*With $\ddot{q}_1 = \ddot{q}_2 = 0$, the inertia term vanishes. (1 mark)*

*Coriolis term (1 mark):*

$$
\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix}
-0.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.2 - 0.5 \cdot 0.04 \\[2pt]
0.5 \cdot 0.25
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
-0.05 - 0.02 \\[2pt]
0.125
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
-0.07 \\[2pt]
0.125
\end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}
$$

*Gravity vector (1 mark):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_1 &= (2 \cdot 0.5 + 2 \cdot 1) \cdot 9.81 \cdot \cos(\pi/4) + 2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \cos(5\pi/12) \\
&= 3 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.707 + 1 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.259 \\
&= 20.80 + 2.54 = 23.34 \text{ N·m} \\[2pt]
G_2 &= 2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \cos(5\pi/12) = 9.81 \cdot 0.259 = 2.54 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Final torques (1 mark each):*

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_1 = 0 - 0.07 + 23.34 \approx 23.27 \text{ N·m}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_2 = 0 + 0.125 + 2.54 \approx 2.66 \text{ N·m}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):**
> - Students frequently **forget the rotational kinetic energy** ($\tfrac{1}{2}I_i \omega_i^2$). If omitted, the diagonal mass-matrix elements become **incorrect by a constant offset**. (Lose 2 marks.)
> - **Sign errors** in the Coriolis term are common — carefully check whether $\sin q_2$ is positive or negative at the given configuration.
> - Numerical mistakes in the **gravity term** typically arise from confusing $\cos(q_1+q_2)$ with $\cos q_1$ (Lose 1 mark.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Internal Choice]

**(a)** *Derive the dynamic model of a 2-DOF spatial RP manipulator (revolute about the base $z$-axis, then prismatic along the resulting horizontal direction). Assume a point payload of mass $m_p$ at the end-effector. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For the manipulator in part (a), compute the joint force $f_2$ and joint torque $\tau_1$ when $q_2 = 0.5$ m, $\dot{q}_2 = 0.1$ m/s, $\ddot{q}_2 = 0$, $q_1 = \pi/3$, $\dot{q}_1 = 1$ rad/s, $\ddot{q}_1 = 0$. Take $m_p = 5$ kg. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark) — End-effector position:*

$$
x = q_2 \cos q_1, \qquad y = q_2 \sin q_1, \qquad z = 0
$$

*Step 2 (1 mark) — End-effector velocity:*

$$
\mathbf{v}_p = \begin{bmatrix} \cos q_1 & -q_2\sin q_1 \\ \sin q_1 & q_2\cos q_1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_2 \\ \dot{q}_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 3 (1 mark) — Kinetic energy of the point payload:*

$$
K = \frac{1}{2} m_p \mathbf{v}_p^T \mathbf{v}_p = \frac{1}{2} m_p\!\left(\dot{q}_2^2 + q_2^2 \dot{q}_1^2\right)
$$

*Step 4 (1 mark) — Potential energy and Lagrangian:*

Since the manipulator moves in a horizontal plane, $P = 0$, and $\mathcal{L} = K$.

*Step 5 (1 mark) — Euler–Lagrange equations:*

For $q_1$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) = \frac{d}{dt}(m_p q_2^2 \dot{q}_1) = m_p q_2^2 \ddot{q}_1 + 2 m_p q_2 \dot{q}_2 \dot{q}_1
$$

Since $\mathcal{L}$ does not depend on $q_1$ explicitly, the second term is zero, giving $\tau_1 = m_p q_2^2 \ddot{q}_1 + 2 m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2$.

For $q_2$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_2}\right) = m_p \ddot{q}_2, \qquad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_2} = m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1^2
$$

So $f_2 = m_p \ddot{q}_2 - m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1^2$.

*Step 6 (2 marks) — Matrix form:*

$$
\begin{bmatrix} f_2 \\ \tau_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} m_p & 0 \\ 0 & m_p q_2^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_2 \\ \ddot{q}_1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1^2 \\ 2 m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is a clean **2-DOF spatial model with no gravity term and configuration-dependent inertia**.

**Part (b) — Numerical evaluation (7 marks):**

*Substitute $m_p = 5$ kg, $q_2 = 0.5$ m, $\dot{q}_2 = 0.1$ m/s, $\ddot{q}_2 = 0$, $\dot{q}_1 = 1$ rad/s, $\ddot{q}_1 = 0$. (1 mark)*

*Inertia term:* since $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$, this term vanishes. (1 mark)

*Coriolis/Centrifugal term:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{2,\text{vel}} &= -m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1^2 = -5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1^2 = -2.5 \text{ N} \\[2pt]
\tau_{1,\text{vel}} &= 2 m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 = 2 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.1 = 0.5 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

*Gravity term:* horizontal arm $\Rightarrow$ $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{0}$. (1 mark)

*Final results (2 marks):*

$$
\boxed{\;f_2 = -2.5 \text{ N}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_1 = 0.5 \text{ N·m}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> - Many students **invert the Jacobian columns** when computing the velocity, mixing up $q_1$ and $q_2$. Always verify: a positive $\dot{q}_1$ (base rotation) should produce a *tangential* velocity at the end-effector, while a positive $\dot{q}_2$ (extension) should produce a *radial* velocity. (Lose 2 marks if swapped.)
> - The sign of the **centrifugal term** in the prismatic equation must be **negative** (it is a fictitious "pulling-in" force when the arm is rotating). Incorrect sign loses 1 mark.
> - Don't forget the factor of **2** in the Coriolis coupling $2 m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2$ — a common student error is to write it as $m_p q_2 \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2$. (Lose 1 mark.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Canonical Dynamic Equation:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})$.
- **Mass Matrix** $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **symmetric** and **positive-definite** for all $\mathbf{q}$.
- **Inertia** = resistance to acceleration; **Coriolis** = coupling when *two* joints move together; **Centrifugal** = fictitious force from a joint rotating alone at speed.
- **Gravity** depends only on configuration $\mathbf{q}$, **not** on velocity.
- **Skew-Symmetry Property:** $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is skew-symmetric — essential for passivity-based and adaptive control proofs.
- **Planar RR (2-DOF):** coupling appears as $\sin q_2$ in $\mathbf{M}_{11}, \mathbf{M}_{12}$; gravity appears as $\cos q_i$ in $\mathbf{G}$.
- **Spatial RP (2-DOF):** coupling appears as $q_2^2$ in $M_{22}$ (variable rotational inertia); gravity may vanish for a horizontal arm.
- **Lagrangian vs. Newton–Euler:** Lagrangian gives *insight and matrices*; Newton–Euler gives *real-time numbers*.
- **Always include rotational KE** $\tfrac{1}{2}I_i\omega_i^2$ — forgetting it is the #1 cause of lost marks.
- **Cross-check signs** of Coriolis/centrifugal terms by considering a simple configuration (e.g., $q_2 = 90°$ with a fast $\dot{q}_1$).
- **Computed-Torque Control** is the most common industrial application of the dynamic model.
- For a **2-DOF** system the mass matrix is $2\times 2$; the Coriolis matrix has the form $h \begin{bmatrix} -\dot{q}_2 & -(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2) \\ \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ for a planar RR arm.
- The **spatial RP arm** is a clean illustration of *configuration-dependent inertia* — its rotational inertia about the base grows as $q_2^2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling Including Motor and Gearbox
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modelling Including Motor and Gearbox

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Motor-Gearbox Modelling** in robotics is the mathematical representation of the electromechanical dynamics that govern an actuator assembly consisting of an **armature-controlled DC motor** coupled to a **rotational mechanical load through a gear train (gearbox)**. The model is expressed as a set of coupled **ordinary differential equations (ODEs)** linking the applied armature voltage to the resulting angular position, velocity, and torque at the load shaft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> Modelling a robot joint actuator involves combining the *electrical dynamics* of the DC motor (armature inductance, resistance, and back-EMF) with the *mechanical dynamics* of the rotor and load (inertia, viscous friction, and external load torque), and further *reflecting* the load parameters through the gear train to the motor shaft for a unified transfer function.

The governing state variables are:
- **$i_a(t)$** → Armature current (Amperes)
- **$\omega_m(t)$** → Motor shaft angular velocity (rad/s)
- **$\theta_m(t)$** → Motor shaft angular position (rad)
- **$\theta_L(t)$** → Load shaft angular position (rad)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The Bicycle & Steering Wheel:**  
> Imagine turning a heavy ship's steering wheel connected to the rudder through a long shaft. The steering wheel (your **motor shaft**) spins many times for every small movement of the rudder (the **load**). This is a *gear reduction* — you trade speed for **torque amplification**. Now imagine the steering wheel has its own friction (viscous damping) and the rudder pushes water back (load torque). The electrical part is like the voltage you apply across the motor's terminals — push more voltage, more current flows, more torque is produced, and the whole system (wheel + rudder) moves.

The **gearbox** is the translator between two mechanical worlds: the *fast, low-torque motor world* and the *slow, high-torque load world*. Modelling captures this translation mathematically so we can design controllers.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| $R$ | Armature resistance | $\Omega$ (Ohm) |
| $L$ | Armature inductance | H (Henry) |
| $K_b$ | Back-EMF constant | V·s/rad |
| $K_t$ | Motor torque constant | N·m/A |
| $J_m$ | Motor rotor inertia | kg·m² |
| $J_L$ | Load inertia | kg·m² |
| $B_m$ | Motor viscous damping | N·m·s/rad |
| $B_L$ | Load viscous damping | N·m·s/rad |
| $n$ | Gear ratio (motor:load) | dimensionless |
| $\tau$ | Torque | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> In SI units, $K_t$ (N·m/A) and $K_b$ (V·s/rad) are **numerically equal** for an ideal DC motor. This is a direct consequence of energy conservation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Motor-Gearbox System

The system is decomposed into three interacting subsystems:

### 2.1.1 Electrical Subsystem (Armature Circuit)
- The applied voltage $V_a$ drives current $i_a$ through resistance $R$ and inductance $L$.
- The rotating armature in the magnetic field generates a **back-EMF** $e_b = K_b \cdot \omega_m$, which opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's law).
- Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the armature loop yields the **electrical ODE**.

### 2.1.2 Mechanical Subsystem (Rotor Dynamics)
- The motor torque $\tau_m = K_t \cdot i_a$ accelerates the rotor inertia $J_m$ against viscous friction $B_m$ and reflected load torque $\tau_{L}'$.
- Newton's Second Law for rotation yields the **mechanical ODE**.

### 2.1.3 Gearbox (Mechanical Transformer)
- The gear train with ratio $n = \frac{\omega_m}{\omega_L} = \frac{\theta_m}{\theta_L}$ transforms torques, speeds, and inertias.
- **Speed decreases** by factor $n$ at the load.
- **Torque increases** by factor $n$ at the load.
- **Inertia/damping reflect back** to the motor shaft as $J_L/n^2$ and $B_L/n^2$.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How"

- **Why back-EMF?** As the motor spins, it acts as a generator. This voltage opposes the input, creating a natural *speed-regulating effect* — the faster it spins, the more it resists further acceleration.
- **Why gears?** DC motors are inherently *high-speed, low-torque* devices. Robotic joints need *low-speed, high-torque* motion. Gears bridge this mismatch.
- **Why model inertia reflection?** When the load is heavy, its inertia is felt at the motor shaft *scaled down* by $n^2$. This dramatically changes motor response and is critical for controller design.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_a = L\dfrac{di_a}{dt} + R i_a + K_b \omega_m$ | Electrical (armature) equation | V |
| 2 | $J_m \dfrac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B_m \omega_m + \tau_L' = K_t i_a$ | Mechanical (rotor) equation | N·m |
| 3 | $\tau_L' = \dfrac{\tau_L}{n}$ | Reflected load torque to motor | N·m |
| 4 | $J_{eq} = J_m + \dfrac{J_L}{n^2}$ | Equivalent inertia at motor shaft | kg·m² |
| 5 | $B_{eq} = B_m + \dfrac{B_L}{n^2}$ | Equivalent damping at motor shaft | N·m·s/rad |
| 6 | $J_{eq} \dfrac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B_{eq} \omega_m = K_t i_a - \dfrac{\tau_L}{n}$ | Combined mechanical equation | N·m |
| 7 | $n = \dfrac{\omega_m}{\omega_L} = \dfrac{\theta_m}{\theta_L}$ | Gear ratio definition | — |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{n \cdot s \left[L J_{eq} s^2 + (L B_{eq} + R J_{eq})s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b)\right]}$ | Full transfer function (motor→load position) | rad/V |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{L J_{eq} s^2 + (L B_{eq} + R J_{eq})s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b)}$ | Velocity transfer function (no integration) | rad/(V·s) |
| 10 | $K_{tf} = \dfrac{K_t}{R B_{eq} + K_t K_b}$ | Steady-state velocity gain | rad/(V·s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule for KTU:** When $L \to 0$ (inductance neglected, valid for many small DC motors), the transfer function **degenerates from 3rd-order to 2nd-order** and simplifies to:
>
> $$\dfrac{\theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t/n}{s \left[ R J_{eq} s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b) \right]}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This model is the **backbone of robot joint controller design** (PID, state-feedback). It is used in:
- **Industrial robot arms** (e.g., KUKA, ABB) for joint servo design.
- **CNC machine tools** for feed-drive position control.
- **Electric vehicle traction** where the motor-gearbox-wheel assembly must be precisely controlled.
- **Drone BLDC motor control** (though three-phase, the same modelling philosophy applies).
- **Haptic devices and surgical robots** where accurate low-speed torque delivery is non-negotiable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Armature-Controlled DC Motor Equations

### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the Armature Circuit

The armature circuit consists of a voltage source $V_a$, a resistor $R$, an inductor $L$, and the back-EMF source $e_b$ in series.

$$
V_a - V_R - V_L - e_b = 0
$$

Substituting $V_R = R i_a$, $V_L = L \dfrac{di_a}{dt}$, and $e_b = K_b \omega_m$:

$$
V_a = L \frac{di_a}{dt} + R i_a + K_b \omega_m \quad \text{...(Electrical ODE)}
$$

**Logic:** The applied voltage must overcome the resistive drop, the inductive drop (which resists change in current), and the back-EMF (which is proportional to motor speed).

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law for Rotation to the Motor Shaft

The motor torque $K_t i_a$ is balanced by the rotational inertia of the rotor $J_m$, the viscous friction $B_m \omega_m$, and the load torque reflected to the motor shaft $\tau_L'$.

$$
K_t i_a = J_m \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B_m \omega_m + \tau_L' \quad \text{...(Mechanical ODE)}
$$

**Logic:** Net torque equals rate of change of angular momentum plus friction losses plus the torque that the load takes from the motor.

### Step 3: Define the Gear Train and Reflection Law

For a gear train with ratio $n$ (where the motor turns $n$ times for every 1 turn of the load):

$$
\theta_m = n \cdot \theta_L, \quad \omega_m = n \cdot \omega_L
$$

Power conservation across an ideal gear train (no losses):

$$
\tau_m \cdot \omega_m = \tau_L \cdot \omega_L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_L = n \cdot \tau_m
$$

Therefore, the **load torque reflected to the motor shaft** is:

$$
\tau_L' = \frac{\tau_L}{n}
$$

Similarly, the **load inertia and damping reflected to the motor shaft** (using the kinetic energy equivalence $E = \frac{1}{2}J\omega^2$):

$$
\frac{1}{2} J_L' \omega_m^2 = \frac{1}{2} J_L \omega_L^2 = \frac{1}{2} J_L \left(\frac{\omega_m}{n}\right)^2
$$

Solving:

$$
J_L' = \frac{J_L}{n^2}
$$

The same logic gives:

$$
B_L' = \frac{B_L}{n^2}
$$

**Logic:** A spinning load viewed from the fast-spinning motor shaft appears to have *less* rotational inertia and damping because the motor sees it spinning much faster — the effective energy stored is distributed.

### Step 4: Combine into a Single Mechanical Equation

Substituting $\tau_L' = \tau_L/n$, $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L/n^2$, and $B_{eq} = B_m + B_L/n^2$:

$$
J_{eq} \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B_{eq} \omega_m + \frac{\tau_L}{n} = K_t i_a \quad \text{...(Combined Mechanical ODE)}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to the Laplace Domain (Zero Initial Conditions)

Taking the Laplace transform of the electrical equation:

$$
V_a(s) = (Ls + R) I_a(s) + K_b \Omega_m(s) \quad \text{...(E1)}
$$

Taking the Laplace transform of the mechanical equation (with $\tau_L = 0$ for free motion):

$$
(J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) \Omega_m(s) = K_t I_a(s) \quad \text{...(E2)}
$$

### Step 6: Solve for $I_a(s)$ from E2

$$
I_a(s) = \frac{(J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) \Omega_m(s)}{K_t}
$$

### Step 7: Substitute into E1

$$
V_a(s) = (Ls + R) \cdot \frac{(J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) \Omega_m(s)}{K_t} + K_b \Omega_m(s)
$$

Factor out $\Omega_m(s)$:

$$
V_a(s) = \Omega_m(s) \left[ \frac{(Ls + R)(J_{eq} s + B_{eq})}{K_t} + K_b \right]
$$

### Step 8: Simplify the Bracket

Common denominator $K_t$:

$$
V_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_m(s)}{K_t} \left[ (Ls + R)(J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) + K_t K_b \right]
$$

Expand $(Ls + R)(J_{eq} s + B_{eq})$:

$$
(Ls + R)(J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) = L J_{eq} s^2 + (L B_{eq} + R J_{eq}) s + R B_{eq}
$$

Add $K_t K_b$:

$$
V_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_m(s)}{K_t} \left[ L J_{eq} s^2 + (L B_{eq} + R J_{eq}) s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b) \right]
$$

### Step 9: Write the Velocity Transfer Function $\Omega_m(s)/V_a(s)$

$$
\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{L J_{eq} s^2 + (L B_{eq} + R J_{eq}) s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b)}
$$

### Step 10: Integrate to Get Position Transfer Function

Since $\theta_m(s) = \Omega_m(s)/s$:

$$
\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ L J_{eq} s^2 + (L B_{eq} + R J_{eq}) s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b) \right]}
$$

### Step 11: Convert Motor-Shaft Position to Load-Shaft Position

Using $\theta_L = \theta_m / n$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\Theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t / n}{s \left[ L J_{eq} s^2 + (L B_{eq} + R J_{eq}) s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b) \right]}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Special Cases & Simplifications

| Case | Condition | Simplified Transfer Function | Order |
|------|-----------|-------------------------------|-------|
| (a) | Neglect $L$ (inductance) and $B_{eq}$ (friction) | $\dfrac{\theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t / n}{s (R J_{eq} s + K_t K_b)}$ | 2nd |
| (b) | Neglect $L$ only | $\dfrac{\theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t / n}{s \left[ R J_{eq} s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b) \right]}$ | 2nd |
| (c) | Neglect $B_{eq}$ and $L$ | $\dfrac{\theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t / n}{s (R J_{eq} s + K_t K_b)}$ | 2nd |
| (d) | Full model (no simplifications) | $\dfrac{K_t / n}{s [L J_{eq} s^2 + (L B_{eq} + R J_{eq}) s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b)]}$ | 3rd |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The motor constant $K_t$ and back-EMF constant $K_b$ have **numerically equal values in SI units**. The product $K_t K_b$ represents a *self-loading* effect — the motor intrinsically damps its own rotation through electromagnetic induction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Motor-Gearbox-Load System (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the *signal-flow* architecture of the complete motor-gearbox-load system, showing how the electrical input becomes mechanical output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Va["ARMATURE VOLTAGE Va"] --> sumNode1(("+"))
    backEMF["BACK EMF: Kb * wm"] --> sumNode1(("+"))
    sumNode1(("+")) --> R_L_block["ARMATURE IMPEDANCE: Ls + R"]
    R_L_block --> Ia["ARMATURE CURRENT ia"]
    Ia --> Kt_gain["TORQUE GAIN: Kt"]
    Kt_gain --> Tmotor["MOTOR TORQUE: Kt * ia"]
    Tmotor --> sumNode2(("+"))
    reflectedLoad["REFLECTED LOAD TORQUE: tau_L / n"] --> sumNode2(("+"))
    sumNode2(("+")) --> Mech["ROTATIONAL MECHANICS: 1 / Jeq*s + Beq"]
    Mech --> wm["MOTOR VELOCITY wm"]
    wm --> gearBox["GEARBOX RATIO: / n"]
    gearBox --> wL["LOAD VELOCITY wL"]
    wL --> integrator["INTEGRATOR: 1 / s"]
    integrator --> thetaL["LOAD POSITION theta_L"]
    wm --> feedbackPath["FEEDBACK PATH"]
    feedbackPath --> backEMF
    gearBox -.-> reflectInertia["INERTIA REFLECTION: J_L / n^2"]
    gearBox -.-> reflectDamping["DAMPING REFLECTION: B_L / n^2"]
    reflectInertia --> Mech
    reflectDamping --> Mech
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Simplified Open-Loop Transfer Function Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Va["Va (Input Voltage)"] --> TF["TRANSFER FUNCTION BLOCK"]
    TF["K_t / n / s * (L*Jeq*s^2 + ... + Kt*Kb)"] --> thetaL["theta_L (Load Position)"]
    Va2["Va (Input Voltage)"] --> TF2["Velocity TF: K_t / (L*Jeq*s^2 + ...)"] --> wm["wm (Motor Velocity)"]
```

## 4.3 Physical Schematic Description (Mermaid Block Form)

The following diagram represents the *physical architecture* of the motor-gearbox-load chain:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph electrical["ELECTRICAL DOMAIN"]
        V_src["DC Voltage Source Va"] --> armature["Armature Winding L, R"]
        armature --> brushes["Brush-Commutator Interface"]
    end
    subgraph magnetic["MAGNETIC DOMAIN"]
        brushes --> field["Field Flux (Permanent Magnet or Winding)"]
        field --> torqueGen["Torque Generation: tau = Kt * ia"]
    end
    subgraph mechanical["MECHANICAL DOMAIN - MOTOR SHAFT"]
        torqueGen --> Jm["Rotor Inertia Jm"]
        Jm --> Bm["Bearing Friction Bm"]
        Bm --> motorShaft["Motor Shaft: wm, theta_m"]
        motorShaft --> backEMFgen["Back-EMF Generation: eb = Kb * wm"]
        backEMFgen -.-> armature
    end
    subgraph gearbox["GEARBOX STAGE"]
        motorShaft --> pinion["Pinion Gear Z1"]
        pinion --> gearMesh["Gear Mesh Ratio n = Z2 / Z1"]
        gearMesh --> outputGear["Output Gear Z2"]
    end
    subgraph load["MECHANICAL DOMAIN - LOAD SHAFT"]
        outputGear --> JL["Load Inertia JL"]
        JL --> BL["Load Friction BL"]
        BL --> loadShaft["Load Shaft: wL = wm / n, theta_L = theta_m / n"]
    end
    loadShaft --> endNode["JOINT OUTPUT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The **electrical** domain (top-left) and **mechanical** domain are coupled by *two* ports: the *forward path* ($i_a \to \tau_m$ via $K_t$) and the *feedback path* ($\omega_m \to e_b$ via $K_b$).
> - The **gearbox** is the *impedance transformation device* — it scales speed down and torque up.
> - The **feedback** from $\omega_m$ to the back-EMF source is what makes the motor *self-regulating* and is the origin of the $K_t K_b$ damping term.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the back-EMF constant $K_b$ of a DC motor. What is its SI unit? How is it related to the motor torque constant $K_t$?**

**Model Answer:**

The **back-EMF constant $K_b$** is defined as the back electromotive force induced in the armature per unit angular velocity of the rotor. Mathematically:

$$
e_b = K_b \cdot \omega_m
$$

Its SI unit is **V·s/rad** (equivalently, N·m/A).

**Relationship with $K_t$:** From energy conservation, in SI units:

$$
K_t = K_b
$$

That is, the numerical value of the torque constant (N·m/A) equals the numerical value of the back-EMF constant (V·s/rad). This is why both are often written as a single constant $K_m$ in many textbooks.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for unit, 1 for relationship)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A gear train has a ratio $n = 50:1$ (motor:load). If the load inertia is $J_L = 25 \text{ kg·m}^2$, what is the inertia reflected to the motor shaft?**

**Model Answer:**

The inertia reflection law is:

$$
J_L' = \frac{J_L}{n^2}
$$

Substituting $J_L = 25$ kg·m² and $n = 50$:

$$
J_L' = \frac{25}{50^2} = \frac{25}{2500} = 0.01 \text{ kg·m}^2
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The heavy load appears only as $0.01$ kg·m² inertia at the motor shaft because the motor turns 50 times faster than the load. This is why a small motor can drive a large robotic joint.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for interpretation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice 1]`

**(a) Derive the transfer function $\dfrac{\theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)}$ of an armature-controlled DC motor driving a load through a gear train of ratio $n$. Clearly state all assumptions.**  *(7 marks)*

**(b) Starting from the general transfer function, derive the simplified form when armature inductance $L \to 0$ and comment on the order of the system.** *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Full Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. The motor is armature-controlled (field current is constant).
2. The gear train is **ideal** (no backlash, no friction, no compliance).
3. All parameters are **linear and time-invariant**.
4. Initial conditions are zero.

**Step 1: Armature KVL equation** *(1 mark)*

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature loop:

$$
V_a = L \frac{di_a}{dt} + R i_a + K_b \omega_m
$$

**Step 2: Motor shaft torque balance** *(1 mark)*

Applying Newton's second law for rotation at the motor shaft:

$$
K_t i_a = J_m \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B_m \omega_m + \frac{\tau_L}{n}
$$

where the load torque is reflected by $1/n$.

**Step 3: Define equivalent parameters at the motor shaft** *(1 mark)*

$$
J_{eq} = J_m + \frac{J_L}{n^2}, \qquad B_{eq} = B_m + \frac{B_L}{n^2}
$$

Combined mechanical ODE (with $\tau_L = 0$ for unloaded):

$$
(J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) \Omega_m(s) = K_t I_a(s)
$$

**Step 4: Laplace-domain electrical equation** *(1 mark)*

$$
(Ls + R) I_a(s) + K_b \Omega_m(s) = V_a(s)
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $I_a(s)$ and substitute** *(1 mark)*

$$
I_a(s) = \frac{(J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) \Omega_m(s)}{K_t}
$$

Substituting into the electrical equation:

$$
V_a(s) = \frac{(Ls + R)(J_{eq} s + B_{eq})}{K_t} \Omega_m(s) + K_b \Omega_m(s)
$$

**Step 6: Form the velocity transfer function** *(1 mark)*

$$
\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{L J_{eq} s^2 + (L B_{eq} + R J_{eq}) s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b)}
$$

**Step 7: Integrate and convert to load position** *(1 mark)*

Since $\theta_m(s) = \Omega_m(s)/s$ and $\theta_L = \theta_m/n$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t / n}{s \left[ L J_{eq} s^2 + (L B_{eq} + R J_{eq}) s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b) \right]}}
$$

This is a **third-order system** due to the $s$ in the numerator of the integration and the $s^2$ from the motor dynamics.

---

#### Part (b) — Simplification when $L \to 0$ (7 marks)

**Step 1: Substitute $L = 0$ in the general transfer function** *(1 mark)*

$$
\frac{\theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t / n}{s \left[ 0 \cdot J_{eq} s^2 + (0 \cdot B_{eq} + R J_{eq}) s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b) \right]}
$$

$$
= \frac{K_t / n}{s \left[ R J_{eq} s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b) \right]}
$$

**Step 2: Expand the denominator** *(1 mark)*

$$
= \frac{K_t / n}{R J_{eq} s^2 + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b) s}
$$

**Step 3: Final simplified form** *(1 mark)*

$$
\boxed{\frac{\theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t / n}{s (R J_{eq} s + R B_{eq} + K_t K_b)}}
$$

**Step 4: Identify the system order** *(1 mark)*

The denominator has degree 2 in $s$ (i.e., $s^2$ and $s$ terms). This is a **second-order system** with one pole at $s = 0$ (integrator) and one real pole at:

$$
s = -\frac{R B_{eq} + K_t K_b}{R J_{eq}}
$$

**Step 5: Identify the time constant and DC gain** *(1 mark)*

- **Mechanical time constant:**
$$
\tau_m = \frac{R J_{eq}}{R B_{eq} + K_t K_b}
$$

- **DC gain (steady-state position per unit voltage):**
$$
K_{dc} = \frac{K_t / n}{R B_{eq} + K_t K_b} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{rad}}{\text{V}} \right]
$$

**Step 6: Comment on the effect of neglecting $L$** *(1 mark)*

Neglecting $L$ removes the fast electrical transient from the response. This is valid when the **electrical time constant $\tau_e = L/R$** is much smaller than the **mechanical time constant $\tau_m = R J_{eq}/(R B_{eq} + K_t K_b)$**. For most small DC servo motors used in robotics, this is a good approximation.

**Step 7: Comment on the physical meaning** *(1 mark)*

The simplified model captures the essential *position-control* behavior: the motor acts as an integrator (one pole at origin) cascaded with a first-order lag representing rotor inertia, damping, and back-EMF. This is the form typically used to design **PID joint controllers** in KTU-typical robotics coursework.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice 2]`

**(a) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the block diagram representation of an armature-controlled DC motor driving a robotic joint load through a gear train. Clearly show all coupling variables.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A DC motor has $R = 1 \, \Omega$, $L = 0.01 \, \text{H}$, $K_t = K_b = 0.1 \, \text{N·m/A}$, $J_m = 0.001 \, \text{kg·m}^2$, $B_m = 0.0001 \, \text{N·m·s/rad}$. The motor is connected to a load with $J_L = 0.5 \, \text{kg·m}^2$, $B_L = 0.05 \, \text{N·m·s/rad}$ through a gear train of ratio $n = 10$. Compute the equivalent inertia and damping at the motor shaft, and write the full velocity transfer function.** *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4.1 and 4.3 for visual reference.)*

**Block Diagram Description** *(3 marks)*

The block diagram consists of four major blocks:

1. **Armature Block** $(L s + R)$: Represents the electrical impedance of the armature winding. Input is voltage $V_a$, output is armature current $I_a$.

2. **Torque Conversion Block** $(K_t)$: Converts armature current to electromagnetic torque. The forward coupling is $I_a \to \tau_m = K_t I_a$.

3. **Mechanical Block** $\left(\dfrac{1}{J_{eq} s + B_{eq}}\right)$: Represents the rotational dynamics of motor + reflected load. Input is net torque, output is angular velocity $\omega_m$.

4. **Gearbox Block** $(1/n)$: Maps motor shaft velocity/position to load shaft.

**Coupling Variables** *(2 marks)*

- **Forward path:** $V_a \to I_a \to \tau_m \to \omega_m \to \theta_m \to \theta_L$.
- **Inner feedback loop:** $\omega_m \to e_b = K_b \omega_m$ is fed back to the input of the armature block. This is the **back-EMF feedback loop**, which makes the motor self-regulating.
- **Outer feedback (load reflection):** $J_L/n^2$ and $B_L/n^2$ are added to $J_m$ and $B_m$ inside the mechanical block.

**Why these couplings exist** *(2 marks)*

- The **back-EMF** coupling is due to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction — a moving conductor in a magnetic field generates a voltage opposing the motion.
- The **load reflection** is due to Newton's third law and energy conservation in the gear mesh.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $R = 1 \, \Omega$, $L = 0.01 \, \text{H}$
- $K_t = K_b = 0.1 \, \text{N·m/A}$
- $J_m = 0.001 \, \text{kg·m}^2$, $B_m = 0.0001 \, \text{N·m·s/rad}$
- $J_L = 0.5 \, \text{kg·m}^2$, $B_L = 0.05 \, \text{N·m·s/rad}$
- $n = 10$

**Step 1: Compute reflected load inertia** *(1 mark)*

$$
J_L' = \frac{J_L}{n^2} = \frac{0.5}{10^2} = \frac{0.5}{100} = 0.005 \, \text{kg·m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute reflected load damping** *(1 mark)*

$$
B_L' = \frac{B_L}{n^2} = \frac{0.05}{100} = 0.0005 \, \text{N·m·s/rad}
$$

**Step 3: Compute equivalent inertia at motor shaft** *(1 mark)*

$$
J_{eq} = J_m + J_L' = 0.001 + 0.005 = 0.006 \, \text{kg·m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Compute equivalent damping at motor shaft** *(1 mark)*

$$
B_{eq} = B_m + B_L' = 0.0001 + 0.0005 = 0.0006 \, \text{N·m·s/rad}
$$

**Step 5: Write the velocity transfer function form** *(1 mark)*

$$
\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{L J_{eq} s^2 + (L B_{eq} + R J_{eq}) s + (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b)}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute all values** *(1 mark)*

- $L J_{eq} = 0.01 \times 0.006 = 0.00006$
- $L B_{eq} + R J_{eq} = (0.01)(0.0006) + (1)(0.006) = 0.000006 + 0.006 = 0.006006$
- $R B_{eq} + K_t K_b = (1)(0.0006) + (0.1)(0.1) = 0.0006 + 0.01 = 0.0106$

$$
\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.1}{0.00006 \, s^2 + 0.006006 \, s + 0.0106}
$$

**Step 7: Final form and physical interpretation** *(1 mark)*

$$
\boxed{\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.1}{0.00006 \, s^2 + 0.006006 \, s + 0.0106} \;\; \left[\frac{\text{rad/s}}{\text{V}}\right]}
$$

The DC gain is $\dfrac{0.1}{0.0106} \approx 9.43$ rad/s per volt, meaning a constant 1 V input drives the motor (no load) to a steady-state speed of about 9.43 rad/s. The system is **stable** (all coefficients positive) and **overdamped** or **underdamped** depending on the discriminant $b^2 - 4ac$:

$$
\Delta = (0.006006)^2 - 4(0.00006)(0.0106) = 3.607 \times 10^{-5} - 2.544 \times 10^{-6} \approx 3.35 \times 10^{-5} > 0
$$

Since $\Delta > 0$, the system has **two distinct real poles** → **overdamped** response.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by $n$** for the position transfer function $\theta_L$ (only the velocity TF at the motor shaft has no $n$).
> 2. **Mixing up torque reflection** ($/n$ at motor shaft) and **inertia reflection** ($/n^2$). They are *different* powers of $n$ — this is a frequent mistake.
> 3. **Forgetting the $K_t K_b$ term** in the denominator — this *self-damping* term is essential and is the reason the motor is stable in open loop.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions explicitly** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks just for clean listing of assumptions in derivation questions.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** in numerical problems — always carry units in intermediate steps; KTU 2024 valuations penalize unit errors.
> 6. **Confusing motor shaft and load shaft quantities** — when the question says "motor velocity," do not divide by $n$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Modelling Including Motor and Gearbox**

- **Core Model:** The motor is a coupled *electrical + mechanical* second-order system; the gearbox makes the *load* appear scaled at the motor shaft.

- **Two Fundamental ODEs:**
  - Electrical: $V_a = L \dot{i_a} + R i_a + K_b \omega_m$
  - Mechanical: $J_{eq} \dot{\omega_m} + B_{eq} \omega_m = K_t i_a - \tau_L/n$

- **Gearbox Reflections (do not confuse powers of $n$):**
  - Velocity: $\omega_L = \omega_m / n$  →  scales by $1/n$
  - Torque: $\tau_L = n \cdot \tau_{motor-side}$  →  scales by $n$
  - Inertia: $J_L' = J_L / n^2$  →  scales by $1/n^2$
  - Damping: $B_L' = B_L / n^2$  →  scales by $1/n^2$

- **In SI units:** $K_t = K_b$ numerically (energy conservation). This means $K_t K_b$ is the *self-damping coefficient* in N·m·s/rad.

- **Transfer Function Hierarchy:**
  - Velocity TF $\omega_m(s)/V_a(s)$ → 2nd order (if $L$ retained) or 1st order (if $L$ neglected)
  - Position TF $\theta_L(s)/V_a(s)$ → 3rd order (if $L$ retained) or 2nd order (if $L$ neglected) **PLUS** the $1/n$ factor

- **Simplification validity:** Setting $L \to 0$ is valid when the **electrical time constant $\tau_e = L/R$** is much smaller than the **mechanical time constant $\tau_m = R J_{eq}/(R B_{eq} + K_t K_b)$**. For typical small servo motors, $\tau_e \ll \tau_m$.

- **Open-loop behaviour:** A voltage-step input gives a *final steady-state velocity* of $K_t / (R B_{eq} + K_t K_b)$ and a *continuously growing* position — the system is **type-1** (one integrator) → *zero steady-state position error for a step input*.

- **Stability:** The motor-gearbox-load system is **inherently stable** because the denominator polynomial has all positive coefficients (Routh-Hurwitz criterion satisfied automatically for a 2nd-order characteristic equation with $R, L, J, B, K_t, K_b > 0$).

- **Practical design insight:** Increasing gear ratio $n$ reduces reflected inertia, making the motor feel "lighter" and respond faster — but it also reduces the position transfer function gain (factor of $1/n$), so the controller gain must be increased accordingly.

- **Key exam words to use:** *armature-controlled*, *back-EMF*, *reflected parameters*, *equivalent inertia*, *type-1 system*, *self-damping*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Control - The Control Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robot Control - The Control Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Robot Control Problem** is formally defined as the determination of the time-history of joint forces/torques (or actuator inputs) required to cause the robot's end-effector (or tool point) to execute a prescribed motion (trajectory), while satisfying specified force/torque constraints at the tool, in the presence of uncertainties in the model, the environment, and the measurements.

In mathematical terms, given a desired trajectory $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$ in joint space (or $\mathbf{x}_d(t)$ in Cartesian space), the control problem reduces to finding the control law $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ such that:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \mathbf{q}(t) = \mathbf{q}_d(t) \quad \text{with bounded tracking error.}$$

Where $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the vector of joint actuator torques/forces, and the system is governed by the nonlinear, coupled manipulator dynamics:

$$M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Driving a Car in Hilly Terrain**
> 
> Imagine you are driving a car with a manual gearbox up a winding hill, and you have to follow an exact path marked on the road within a specific time. The **steering wheel, accelerator, brake, and gear** are your **control inputs** (like robot joint torques). The **car's mass, road slope, wind, tire grip, and engine response** create **nonlinearities and disturbances** (just like inertia, gravity, Coriolis, and friction in a robot).
> 
> - You do **not** pre-compute every gear change in advance and "blindly" execute it (that would be **open-loop control**, which fails when conditions change).
> - Instead, you **constantly look at the road (sensors)**, compare your position with the desired lane (the **error signal**), and adjust the wheel/pedals (the **controller**).
> - When a hill gets steeper, you press the accelerator more; when a curve tightens, you turn the wheel more.
> 
> This continuous **sensing → comparison → correction** loop is exactly what a robot controller does — except it must do it for **6 joints simultaneously** (for a 6-DOF manipulator) in **real time at 100–1000 Hz**.

### 1.3 Why is the Robot Control Problem "Hard"? — The Core Challenges

The control of robotic manipulators is intrinsically difficult because of the following structural features of the manipulator dynamics:

| S.No. | Challenge | Physical Origin | Engineering Consequence |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Nonlinearity** | Centrifugal, Coriolis, gravitational, and frictional terms depend nonlinearly on $\mathbf{q}$ and $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. | Linear control theory (e.g., classical Bode/Nichols design) cannot be applied directly. |
| 2 | **Coupling (MIMO)** | Motion of one joint induces forces/torques on the other joints via the inertia matrix $M(\mathbf{q})$. | SISO design techniques fail; the system is a true multi-input multi-output plant. |
| 3 | **Configuration-Dependent Inertia** | $M(\mathbf{q})$ varies with the arm posture (e.g., arm extended vs retracted). | A single fixed-gain controller is sub-optimal; gains must adapt to configuration. |
| 4 | **Uncertainty & Disturbances** | Unknown payloads, friction, backlash, and unmodeled dynamics. | Controller must be **robust** to these unmodeled effects. |
| 5 | **Real-Time Constraint** | Sampling periods of **1 ms to 10 ms** are typical for industrial robots. | Computational efficiency of the control algorithm is critical. |

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used to Evaluate the Control Problem

> [!TIP]
> The success of any control solution is judged by the following standard criteria (used in KTU university questions and viva):
> - **Tracking Accuracy**: Steady-state and transient position error $e(t) = \mathbf{q}_d(t) - \mathbf{q}(t)$.
> - **Stability**: Bounded response to bounded initial conditions (BIBO stability).
> - **Robustness**: Insensitivity to bounded disturbances and parameter variations.
> - **Settling Time & Overshoot**: Speed and smoothness of response.
> - **Control Effort**: Magnitude of $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ — must respect actuator saturation limits.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Robot Control Problem — Formal Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim expected in answers):**
> "The robot control problem consists of generating the necessary joint torques/forces to follow a desired trajectory, where the trajectory is specified either in joint space or in Cartesian space, subject to the nonlinear, coupled manipulator dynamics and bounded uncertainties."

### 2.2 Two Distinct Formulations of the Control Problem

#### (A) Joint-Space Control (Trajectory given as $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$)

This is the **simpler and more common** formulation. The desired path is directly specified as a time-history of joint angles.

**Control Block Diagram Concept:**
```
Trajectory Generator → q_d(t) → [+ -] → Controller → τ(t) → Robot M(q)q̈ + ... → q(t)
                                              ↑                                ↓
                                              └──── Sensor (Encoder) ←─────────┘
```

- Error: $\mathbf{e}(t) = \mathbf{q}_d(t) - \mathbf{q}(t)$
- Controller computes $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ from $\mathbf{e}$ and $\dot{\mathbf{e}}$.

#### (B) Task-Space (Cartesian) Control (Trajectory given as $\mathbf{x}_d(t)$)

Here the desired path is given in the Cartesian world frame. The desired joint trajectory is computed in real time using the **inverse kinematics**:

$$\mathbf{q}_d(t) = \text{IK}(\mathbf{x}_d(t))$$

Then the joint-space controller is applied. Alternatively, **resolved motion control** uses the Jacobian transpose/inverse to map Cartesian errors to joint torques.

### 2.3 The Manipulator Dynamics Equation — The Core Object to be Controlled

The full nonlinear equation of motion for an $n$-joint rigid-link serial manipulator, derived using the **Euler-Lagrange formulation**, is:

$$M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}_v\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{F}_s\,\text{sgn}(\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

Where each term has a precise physical meaning:

| Symbol | Name | Physical Meaning |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $M(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ | **Inertia Matrix** | Symmetric, positive-definite; represents the configuration-dependent effective inertia seen at each joint. |
| $C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | **Coriolis & Centripetal Vector** | Forces arising from the rotational coupling between joint velocities; quadratic in $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. |
| $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$ | **Gravity Vector** | Torques needed to hold the arm stationary against gravity; depends only on $\mathbf{q}$. |
| $F_v\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | **Viscous Friction** | Linear damping in the joints. |
| $F_s\,\text{sgn}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | **Coulomb Friction** | Static/sliding friction, direction-dependent. |
| $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | **Actuator Torque/Force Vector** | The control input produced by the controller. |

### 2.4 The Control Hierarchy in Industrial Robots

Real robot controllers are organized in a layered architecture:

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 1 — Trajectory Planner (Path Generator):** Produces a smooth, time-parameterized reference $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$ from sparse via-points using interpolation (cubic polynomials, trapezoidal, or quintic splines).
> 
> **Layer 2 — Servo Controller (Inner Loop, ~1 kHz):** Computes the actuator torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ at every sampling instant to minimize the tracking error. This is the layer that "solves" the control problem.
> 
> **Layer 3 — Supervisory Controller (Outer Loop):** Coordinates the robot with sensors, vision systems, and other machines; handles task-level decisions.

### 2.5 The Five Canonical Solutions to the Control Problem

| S.No. | Controller | Idea | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Open-Loop (Feedforward)** | Pre-compute $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ from the inverse dynamics model. | No sensors needed; simple. | Cannot reject disturbances. |
| 2 | **PID (Decentralized Joint Control)** | Independent SISO PID loop on each joint. | Simple, well-understood. | Ignores coupling → slow. |
| 3 | **Computed-Torque (Inverse-Dynamics)** | Cancel nonlinearities using the model, then add a linear servo. | Excellent tracking. | Requires accurate model. |
| 4 | **Adaptive Control** | Online estimation of unknown parameters (mass, payload). | Handles payload changes. | Complex; convergence proof needed. |
| 5 | **Robust / Sliding-Mode Control** | Designs controller for worst-case uncertainty bounds. | Insensitive to bounded disturbances. | Chattering; high control effort. |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Board Exams**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Full rigid-body manipulator dynamics | SI (N·m, rad/s²) |
| 2 | $\mathbf{e}(t) = \mathbf{q}_d(t) - \mathbf{q}(t)$ | Tracking error vector | rad or m |
| 3 | $\dot{\mathbf{e}}(t) = \dot{\mathbf{q}}_d(t) - \dot{\mathbf{q}}(t)$ | Velocity error vector | rad/s or m/s |
| 4 | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = K_p \mathbf{e} + K_d \dot{\mathbf{e}} + K_i \int \mathbf{e}\, dt$ | Decentralized PID joint control | N·m |
| 5 | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = M(\mathbf{q})[\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + K_v \dot{\mathbf{e}} + K_p \mathbf{e}] + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$ | Computed-Torque Control law | N·m |
| 6 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Differential kinematics (forward) | m/s |
| 7 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = J^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ | Inverse differential kinematics | rad/s |
| 8 | $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{cart}} = J^T(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{F}$ | Static force transformation | N·m |
| 9 | $\tau_{\text{PD}} = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ | Single-joint PD control law | N·m |
| 10 | $M_{ij}(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{k=\max(i,j)}^{n} \text{tr}\left(\frac{\partial {}^{k}T_i}{\partial q_j} I_k \frac{\partial ({}^{k}T_i)^T}{\partial q_i}\right)$ | Inertia matrix element (Lagrangian form) | kg·m² |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The robot control problem is the **core of every industrial automation cell**: from automotive welding robots that follow a seam at 2 m/s with sub-millimeter accuracy, to surgical robots that compensate for heart-beat-induced motion, to space-robotic arms that must track a tumbling satellite. Modern approaches like **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** and **Reinforcement Learning-based control** are direct descendants of this foundational problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Simplified 1-DOF "Robot" Control Problem (PD Servo)

This derivation reduces the general MIMO problem to the simplest SISO analog that retains the essential physics, and is the standard KTU expected derivation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Find $\tau(t)$ for a single rotational joint whose inertia is $J$, viscous friction is $b$, and a disturbance torque $d(t)$ is present.

**Step 1 — Write the equation of motion (Newton's second law for rotation).**

$$J\ddot{q} + b\dot{q} = \tau + d(t)$$

**Step 2 — State the control objective.**

We want $q(t) \to q_d(t)$ as $t \to \infty$. Define the error and its derivatives:

$$e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$$
$$\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$$
$$\ddot{e} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q}$$

**Step 3 — Choose a candidate control law: Proportional + Derivative (PD) action.**

The most basic feedback law that ensures error decay is:

$$\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the control law into the plant equation.**

$$J\ddot{q} + b\dot{q} = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + d(t)$$

**Step 5 — Express the closed-loop dynamics purely in terms of the error $e$.**

Substitute $\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}$ and $\dot{q} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{e}$:

$$J(\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}) + b(\dot{q}_d - \dot{e}) = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + d(t)$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to obtain the error differential equation.**

Group all error terms on the left and the reference/disturbance terms on the right:

$$J\ddot{e} + (b + K_d)\dot{e} + K_p e = J\ddot{q}_d + b\dot{q}_d - d(t)$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result.**

The left side is a **second-order linear differential equation in $e$** with characteristic equation:

$$Js^2 + (b + K_d)s + K_p = 0$$

For a desired natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$, the gains are chosen as:

$$K_p = J \omega_n^2$$
$$K_d = 2J\zeta\omega_n - b$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion of the derivation:** The PD controller converts a nonlinear, uncertain plant into a **stable, second-order, linear closed-loop system** — provided the gains are chosen so the characteristic polynomial has roots with negative real parts. The disturbance $d(t)$ appears as a bounded forcing term on the right side and produces only a small bounded steady-state error.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Computed-Torque Control Law (Inverse-Dynamics Linearization)

This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark answer** derivation for the multi-DOF case.

**Step 1 — Start with the full nonlinear dynamics.**

$$M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

**Step 2 — Define an auxiliary control input $\mathbf{u} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.**

The control law is chosen as:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = M(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{u} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the dynamics equation.**

$$M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = M(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{u} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 4 — Cancel identical terms on both sides.**

The nonlinear terms cancel exactly (because we have an accurate model):

$$M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = M(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{u}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $M^{-1}(\mathbf{q})$ (always invertible since $M$ is positive definite).**

$$\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{u}$$

**Step 6 — Design $\mathbf{u}$ as a PD servo on the tracking error.**

$$\mathbf{u} = \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + K_v \dot{\mathbf{e}} + K_p \mathbf{e}$$

**Step 7 — Write the complete computed-torque control law.**

$$\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\tau} = M(\mathbf{q})\left[\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + K_v \dot{\mathbf{e}} + K_p \mathbf{e}\right] + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})\;}$$

**Step 8 — Closed-loop error dynamics (substitute $\mathbf{u}$ into $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{u}$).**

$$\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + K_v \dot{\mathbf{e}} + K_p \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$$

This is a **decoupled, linear, second-order system** in each joint — exactly the goal of the control problem.

**Step 9 — Gain selection for a critically damped response with natural frequency $\omega_n$.**

$$K_p = \omega_n^2 I_n \quad \text{and} \quad K_v = 2\zeta \omega_n I_n, \quad \zeta = 1 \text{ (critical)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Assumption:** The computed-torque law requires **perfect knowledge** of $M(\mathbf{q})$, $C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$, and $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$. Any modelling error degrades performance — this motivates the robust and adaptive extensions.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Control Architectures

| Property | Open-Loop | Decentralized PID | Computed-Torque | Adaptive |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Uses feedback? | **No** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Cancels nonlinearities? | No | No | **Yes (exactly)** | Yes (approx.) |
| Handles payload changes? | Poorly | Poorly | Poorly | **Excellent** |
| Computational load | Low | Low | **High** | Very High |
| Tracking accuracy | Low | Medium | **High** | High |
| Robustness to disturbance | None | Medium | Medium | **High** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — General Closed-Loop Robot Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical structure of any feedback solution to the robot control problem. Every block is a functional unit in the control loop; the arrows are signal flows.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT[Trajectory Generator]:::gen
    nodeQd[Desired Joint Trajectory q_d of t]:::ref
    nodeSum[Summing Junction]:::sum
    nodeE[Error Signal e equals q_d minus q]:::sig
    nodeU[Controller: PID or Computed Torque]:::ctrl
    nodeTau[Control Input: Torque vector tau of t]:::sig
    nodeR[Robot Plant: M q q double dot plus C plus g equals tau]:::plant
    nodeQ[Actual Joint Position q of t]:::sig
    nodeS[Sensor: Encoders Resolvers]:::sensor
    nodeD[Disturbance d of t: Payload Friction]:::dist

    nodeT --> nodeQd
    nodeQd --> nodeSum
    nodeS --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeU
    nodeU --> nodeTau
    nodeTau --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeS
    nodeD -.-> nodeR

    classDef gen fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef dist fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The trajectory generator produces the reference $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$. The sensor measures the actual position $\mathbf{q}(t)$. The summing junction computes the error $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{q}_d - \mathbf{q}$. The controller (PID or Computed-Torque block) converts this error into joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$. The robot plant transforms these torques into actual joint motion. The disturbance $d(t)$ (payload, friction) enters additively at the plant.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Computed-Torque Controller Internals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OuterLoop[Outer Trajectory Loop]
        nodeA[Desired q_d, q_d_dot, q_d_ddot]:::ref
        nodeErr[e = q_d minus q, e_dot]:::sig
    end

    subgraph InnerLoop[Inner Computed Torque Controller]
        nodePD[PD Servo: u = q_d_ddot + Kv e_dot + Kp e]:::ctrl
        nodeMat[Inertia Matrix M of q]:::plant
        nodeCor[Coriolis Vector C of q, q_dot times q_dot]:::plant
        nodeGrav[Gravity Vector g of q]:::plant
        nodeTauOut[Final Torque tau]:::sig
    end

    subgraph Plant[Physical Robot Manipulator]
        nodeArm[Rigid Link Serial Manipulator: 6 DOF]:::arm
        nodeSens[Joint Encoders]:::sensor
    end

    nodeA --> nodeErr
    nodeErr --> nodePD
    nodePD --> nodeMat
    nodeCor --> nodeTauOut
    nodeGrav --> nodeTauOut
    nodeMat --> nodeTauOut
    nodeTauOut --> nodeArm
    nodeArm --> nodeSens
    nodeSens --> nodeErr

    classDef ref fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef arm fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef sensor fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Three-stage logic visualized:**
> 1. The **outer loop** computes the tracking error and its derivative.
> 2. The **computed-torque controller** in the middle stage cancels all nonlinearities by using the exact model terms $M(\mathbf{q})$, $C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$, and $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$.
> 3. The **physical robot** at the bottom executes the motion; its sensors close the loop.

---

### 4.3 Control Hierarchy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L3[Layer 3: Task Planner]:::top
    L2[Layer 2: Trajectory Generator]:::mid
    L1[Layer 1: Servo Controller]:::low
    L0[Layer 0: Actuators and Motors]:::base
    FB[Feedback from Encoders]:::fb

    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L0
    L0 --> FB
    FB --> L1

    classDef top fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4500,color:#000
    classDef low fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef fb fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
```
