## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q1.** *Define the "robot control problem". What are the two ways in which the desired trajectory can be specified?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The robot control problem is the determination of the time-history of joint forces/torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ required to cause the end-effector to follow a prescribed trajectory, given the nonlinear coupled manipulator dynamics. The trajectory can be specified in two ways:
1. **Joint space**: as a time-history of joint angles $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$ — simpler, no kinematics needed.
2. **Cartesian (task) space**: as a time-history of end-effector poses $\mathbf{x}_d(t)$ — requires online inverse kinematics to convert to joint reference.

---

**Q2.** *List the five major challenges in the robot control problem and state why each one arises.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Nonlinearity** — gravity, Coriolis, centripetal terms depend nonlinearly on $\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
2. **Coupling (MIMO nature)** — motion of joint $i$ exerts forces on joint $j$ via the inertia matrix.
3. **Configuration-dependent inertia** — $M(\mathbf{q})$ varies with arm posture.
4. **Parameter uncertainty** — unknown payloads, friction, backlash.
5. **Real-time constraint** — sampling periods of 1–10 ms demand efficient algorithms.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q3.** *What is the difference between open-loop and closed-loop control of a robot manipulator?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Open-loop control** computes $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ in advance from the inverse-dynamics model and applies it without measuring the actual response; it cannot reject disturbances.
- **Closed-loop (feedback) control** continuously measures the actual $\mathbf{q}(t)$ via encoders, compares it with the desired $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$, computes the error $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{q}_d - \mathbf{q}$, and updates $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ accordingly; disturbances are rejected.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* **Derive the general equation of motion of an $n$-joint rigid-link manipulator in joint space, clearly defining the inertia matrix, Coriolis/centripetal vector, and gravity vector.**

**Model Solution:**

The Euler-Lagrange equation for a serial manipulator is:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

where $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{T} - \mathcal{U}$ is the Lagrangian (kinetic minus potential energy).

**Step 1 — Kinetic energy** of link $i$ with mass $m_i$, inertia tensor $I_i$, linear velocity $\mathbf{v}_i$, and angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$:

$$\mathcal{T} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[m_i \mathbf{v}_i^T \mathbf{v}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i^T I_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i\right]$$

**Step 2 — Potential energy** due to gravity $\mathbf{g} = [0,0,-g]^T$:

$$\mathcal{U} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}_{0,i}$$

**Step 3 — Express $\mathcal{T}$ in matrix form** $\mathcal{T} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^T M(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ to extract the **inertia matrix** $M(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$, which is symmetric and positive-definite.

**Step 4 — Apply Lagrange's equations.** After algebraic manipulation (using Christoffel symbols to define the Coriolis terms):

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} \Gamma_{ijk}(\mathbf{q})\dot{q}_j \dot{q}_k + g_i(\mathbf{q}) = \tau_i$$

where $\Gamma_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$ is the Christoffel symbol.

**Step 5 — In compact matrix form:**

$$\boxed{\;M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}\;}$$

- $M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ — inertial torques
- $C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ — Coriolis and centripetal torques (the $i$-th component is $\sum_{j,k}\Gamma_{ijk}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k$)
- $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$ — gravitational torques ($g_i = \partial \mathcal{U}/\partial q_i$)

---

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* **For a 1-DOF rotational link of inertia $J$ and viscous friction coefficient $b$, subject to a disturbance $d(t)$, design a PD controller and show that the closed-loop error dynamics is a stable second-order system.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Plant equation:**

$$J\ddot{q} + b\dot{q} = \tau + d(t)$$

**Step 2 — Define error:** $e = q_d - q$, $\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$.

**Step 3 — Propose PD law:** $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute into the plant and use $\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}$:**

$$J(\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}) + b(\dot{q}_d - \dot{e}) = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + d(t)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange:**

$$J\ddot{e} + (b + K_d)\dot{e} + K_p e = J\ddot{q}_d + b\dot{q}_d - d(t)$$

**Step 6 — Closed-loop characteristic equation:**

$$Js^2 + (b+K_d)s + K_p = 0$$

**Step 7 — Stability condition (Routh-Hurwitz):** Both $K_p > 0$ and $b + K_d > 0$ are required. Choosing $K_p = J\omega_n^2$ and $K_d = 2J\zeta\omega_n - b$ with $\zeta > 0$ and $\omega_n > 0$ guarantees a stable, second-order response with damping ratio $\zeta$ and natural frequency $\omega_n$. The disturbance $d(t)$ produces only a bounded steady-state error proportional to $\frac{1}{K_p}$.

**Step 8 — Typical design for critical damping $\zeta = 1$:**

$$K_p = J\omega_n^2, \qquad K_d = 2J\omega_n - b$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* **What is computed-torque control? Derive its control law and show that the closed-loop error dynamics is linear and decoupled.**

**Model Solution:**

Computed-torque control (also called inverse-dynamics control) is a model-based feedback strategy in which the nonlinear terms in the manipulator dynamics are cancelled in real time by the controller, leaving a linear, decoupled system to which standard servo design can be applied.

**Step 1 — Start with the nonlinear dynamics:**

$$M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

**Step 2 — Define an auxiliary input** $\mathbf{u}$ such that the torque is:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = M(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{u} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 3 — Substituting and cancelling (since $M$ is invertible):**

$$\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{u}$$

**Step 4 — Choose $\mathbf{u}$ as a PD servo on the tracking error:**

$$\mathbf{u} = \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + K_v\dot{\mathbf{e}} + K_p\mathbf{e}$$

**Step 5 — Final computed-torque control law:**

$$\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\tau} = M(\mathbf{q})(\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + K_v\dot{\mathbf{e}} + K_p\mathbf{e}) + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})\;}$$

**Step 6 — Closed-loop error dynamics** (substitute $\mathbf{u}$ into $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{u}$ and use $\ddot{\mathbf{e}} = \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d - \ddot{\mathbf{q}}$):

$$\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + K_v \dot{\mathbf{e}} + K_p \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$$

This is a **linear, decoupled** set of $n$ independent second-order systems — one per joint. The coupling originally present in $M(\mathbf{q})$ has been completely cancelled by the model-based feedforward term. Stability is guaranteed by choosing $K_p$ and $K_v$ as positive-definite diagonal matrices (e.g., $K_p = \omega_n^2 I$, $K_v = 2\zeta\omega_n I$).

---

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* **Compare decentralized PID control with centralized computed-torque control in terms of coupling handling, computational load, robustness, and tracking accuracy. When would you prefer each?**

**Model Solution:**

A structured comparison:

| Criterion | Decentralized PID | Computed-Torque |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Coupling handling** | Ignores coupling between joints | Explicitly cancels coupling via $M, C$ |
| **Computational load** | Low — independent SISO loops | High — must compute $M, C, g$ at 1 kHz |
| **Modelling requirement** | None | Requires full dynamic model |
| **Tracking accuracy at high speed** | Degrades (coupling torques grow with $\dot{\mathbf{q}}^2$) | Excellent (linearizing feedback) |
| **Robustness to model error** | High (no model) | Low — sensitive to $M$ error |
| **Tuning complexity** | Easy (Ziegler-Nichols) | Harder (matrix gains) |

**When to prefer each:**
- **Prefer decentralized PID** when the robot moves **slowly** (coupling torques are negligible), when **computation is limited**, or when the model is unknown. Typical in low-cost SCARA or pick-and-place machines.
- **Prefer computed-torque** when **high speed and high accuracy** are required (e.g., welding, machining, surgical robots), and when a **good dynamic model** is available from CAD data or system identification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the gravity term** in the dynamics equation: many students write $M\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \tau$ only. **Always include $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$** for full marks.
> 2. **Confusing joint-space vs task-space control**: a question asking for "trajectory tracking control" expects joint-space formulation unless "Cartesian" is explicitly mentioned.
> 3. **Writing the Coriolis term incorrectly**: the correct form is $C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ (a vector), not $C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ alone (a matrix).
> 4. **In computed-torque derivations, skipping the step showing $M$ is invertible** — the examiner expects you to justify the cancellation.
> 5. **In PD designs, forgetting the Routh-Hurwitz stability conditions** ($K_p > 0$ and $K_d > -b$); simply writing the control law without proving stability loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Not stating the units** of gains ($K_p$ in N·m/rad, $K_d$ in N·m·s/rad) — easy 0.5 mark that most students miss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision — Read before the exam:**
> 
> - [x] **The robot control problem** = finding $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ to track a desired trajectory despite nonlinear, coupled, uncertain dynamics.
> - [x] **Two formulations**: joint-space ($\mathbf{q}_d(t)$) and task-space ($\mathbf{x}_d(t)$); the former is more common.
> - [x] **Master equation**: $M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$. Memorize the **physical meaning** of each term.
> - [x] **Open-loop** = precomputed $\boldsymbol{\tau}$, no feedback → fails under disturbance.
> - [x] **Closed-loop** = continuous feedback via encoders → rejects disturbances.
> - [x] **PD control** (1-DOF): $K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$; gives second-order closed-loop dynamics with characteristic equation $Js^2 + (b+K_d)s + K_p = 0$.
> - [x] **Computed-torque** (multi-DOF): the canonical model-based control law that linearizes and decouples the system; closed-loop becomes $\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + K_v\dot{\mathbf{e}} + K_p\mathbf{e} = 0$.
> - [x] **Decentralized PID** is simple but ignores coupling; preferred for slow, low-cost robots.
> - [x] **Computed-torque** needs an accurate model; preferred for high-speed, high-accuracy tasks.
> - [x] **Controller hierarchy** in industry: Task planner → Trajectory generator → Servo controller → Actuators.
> - [x] **Stability** is guaranteed by positive-definite $K_p$ and $K_v$ (for computed-torque) and $K_p, K_d > 0$ (for PD).
> - [x] **Real-time constraint**: sampling at 1 ms means the control law must be computable in $< 1$ ms.
> - [x] **Performance metrics** to quote in answers: tracking accuracy, settling time, overshoot, robustness, control effort.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Axis PID Control - Its Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single Axis PID Control - Its Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Single Axis PID Control** is a classical linear feedback control strategy in which a single Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller regulates one independent Degree of Freedom (DOF) of a robotic manipulator or electromechanical system. The controller continuously computes an error signal $e(t)$ as the difference between a desired setpoint $r(t)$ and the measured process variable $y(t)$, and generates a control output $u(t)$ that is a weighted sum of three corrective actions: proportional, integral, and derivative.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A single axis PID controller operates on a *decoupled* axis. It assumes that the motion of one joint does not affect the dynamics of the others — an assumption that holds only for highly simplified, low-speed, or pre-compensated robotic systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Cruise Control"

Imagine you are driving a car and trying to maintain exactly **60 km/h** on a flat highway:

- **Proportional (P):** The harder you press the accelerator when you are far below 60 km/h, the lighter your touch as you get closer. This is the *immediate reaction* to current error.
- **Integral (I):** If a slight uphill slope keeps you stuck at 58 km/h, the controller slowly "remembers" the persistent error and adds more throttle over time until you reach 60. This corrects for **steady-state error**.
- **Derivative (D):** If you are approaching 60 km/h *very fast* (rapidly closing), the controller eases off the gas early to avoid overshooting. This is *anticipation* based on rate of change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Axis Limitation:** Imagine now that the car's right wheel hits ice. The cruise control — looking only at speed — cannot independently steer or balance the car. Likewise, a single axis PID in a multi-joint robot can stabilize one joint but cannot react to the *coupled disturbances* coming from neighbouring joints. This is the root cause of its disadvantages in robotics.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Controller Bandwidth** $\omega_b$ — measured in **rad/s**
- **Sampling Time** $T_s$ — typically **1 ms to 10 ms** in industrial robots
- **Settling Time** $t_s$ — within **2% to 5%** of final value
- **Steady-State Error** $e_{ss}$ — ideally **0** for step inputs with proper I-action

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Continuous-Time PID Law

The classical single axis PID controller generates its output $u(t)$ according to the following parallel (non-interacting) form:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $K_p$ — Proportional gain (**dimensionless** or **N·m/rad** in torque mode)
- $K_i$ — Integral gain (**1/s** scaled)
- $K_d$ — Derivative gain (**seconds** scaled)
- $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ — tracking error
- $u(t)$ — control signal (torque, voltage, or PWM duty)

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" Behind Each Term

### 2.2.1 Proportional Action
- **How:** Produces an output *directly proportional* to the instantaneous error.
- **Why:** Provides the primary restoring force. Faster response with higher $K_p$.
- **Side effect:** Pure P-control always leaves a steady-state error for step inputs (Type-0 system behaviour).

### 2.2.2 Integral Action
- **How:** Accumulates error over time and multiplies by $K_i$.
- **Why:** Eliminates steady-state error by "pushing harder" the longer the error persists.
- **Side effect:** Causes **integral windup** during actuator saturation (covered in disadvantages).

### 2.2.3 Derivative Action
- **How:** Responds to the *rate of change* of error.
- **Why:** Adds damping, suppresses overshoot, and improves transient response.
- **Side effect:** Amplifies high-frequency sensor noise dramatically (noise gain rises as $20 \log_{10}(\omega)$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|:------:|:---------------------|:-----------------|:----:|
| $e(t)$ | $r(t) - y(t)$ | Instantaneous tracking error | rad or m |
| $u(t)$ | $K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Total control effort | N·m or V |
| $K_p$ | Tuning parameter | Present error weight | N·m/rad |
| $K_i$ | $K_p / T_i$ | Past error weight | (N·m/rad)/s |
| $K_d$ | $K_p \cdot T_d$ | Future error prediction | N·m·s/rad |
| $T_i$ | Integral time constant | Time for I to match P | s |
| $T_d$ | Derivative time constant | Time for D action duration | s |
| $\omega_{co}$ | $\sqrt{K_i / K_d}$ | Crossover frequency | rad/s |
| $e_{ss}$ | $\lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$ | Steady-state error | rad or m |
| $M_p$ | Peak overshoot percentage | Transient performance | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No Pipes Rule Applied:** All set-membership or absolute-value expressions such as $\vert e(t) \vert$ or $\vert K_p \vert$ have been written using `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Single axis PID control forms the **inner loop** of nearly every industrial servo drive, CNC machine tool axis, drone BLDC motor controller, and robotic joint actuator. Despite its simplicity, it remains the *workhorse* of motion control because of its robustness and ease of tuning via methods like Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon, and Tyreus-Luyben.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the PID Transfer Function

Starting from the time-domain control law, take the Laplace transform of each term assuming zero initial conditions.

**Step 1:** The proportional term transforms linearly.

$$U_p(s) = K_p \, E(s)$$

**Step 2:** The integral term becomes a division by $s$ in the s-domain.

$$U_i(s) = \frac{K_i}{s} \, E(s)$$

**Step 3:** The derivative term becomes a multiplication by $s$ in the s-domain.

$$U_d(s) = K_d \, s \, E(s)$$

**Step 4:** Superpose all three parallel branches.

$$U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s \right) E(s)$$

**Step 5:** Express the controller transfer function in standard form.

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = \frac{K_d \, s^2 + K_p \, s + K_i}{s}$$

This is a **Type-1** controller (one pole at the origin), which is precisely why it can drive step-input steady-state error to zero.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — The Major Disadvantages of Single Axis PID

The following analysis traces *why* each disadvantage arises mathematically.

### Disadvantage 1 — Integral Windup

**Phenomenon:** When the actuator saturates at $u_{max}$, the error $e(t)$ continues to grow, and the integrator keeps accumulating a huge $\int e \, dt$ value.

**Mathematical demonstration:**

$$u_{sat}(t) = \text{sat}\bigl(u(t)\bigr) = \begin{cases} u_{max}, & u(t) > u_{max} \\ u(t), & \vert u(t) \vert \le u_{max} \\ -u_{max}, & u(t) < -u_{max} \end{cases}$$

The integrator still uses the *unsaturated* $u(t)$ internally, so:

$$I(t) = K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau \longrightarrow \infty \quad \text{as} \quad t \longrightarrow t_{sat}$$

When the error finally reverses sign, the controller must first *discharge* this enormous accumulated integral before the actuator responds, producing a massive **overshoot** and possible oscillation.

### Disadvantage 2 — Derivative Kick

**Phenomenon:** A sudden step change in the setpoint $r(t)$ produces a mathematically infinite $\dot{e}(t)$ spike.

**Mathematical demonstration:**

If $r(t) = R \cdot \mathbf{1}(t)$ where $\mathbf{1}(t)$ is the unit step, then:

$$\dot{r}(t) = R \, \delta(t) \quad \text{(Dirac delta distribution)}$$

Therefore the derivative term becomes:

$$u_d(t) = K_d \frac{d}{dt}\bigl(r(t) - y(t)\bigr) = K_d \cdot R \cdot \delta(t) - K_d \dot{y}(t)$$

This impulsive spike of infinite magnitude can physically damage actuators, saturate DAC outputs, and trigger protective shutdowns.

### Disadvantage 3 — High-Frequency Noise Amplification

**Phenomenon:** The derivative branch has an $s$ factor, so its frequency response magnitude grows linearly with $\omega$.

**Frequency response derivation:**

$$\bigl\vert C_d(j\omega) \bigr\vert = \bigl\vert K_d \cdot j\omega \bigr\vert = K_d \omega$$

$$\text{Gain in dB} = 20 \log_{10}(K_d \omega)$$

A 10× increase in noise frequency produces a **20 dB** amplification. In robotic encoders with quantization noise, this can render the controller useless without a low-pass filter on the derivative.

### Disadvantage 4 — Single Axis Decoupling Failure

**Phenomenon:** Robotic joints are dynamically coupled. The torque on joint $i$ depends on positions, velocities, and accelerations of *all other joints*.

**Coupled dynamics equation:**

$$\tau_i = M_{ii}(q) \ddot{q}_i + \sum_{j \neq i} M_{ij}(q) \ddot{q}_j + C_i(q, \dot{q}) + G_i(q)$$

The term $\sum_{j \neq i} M_{ij}(q) \ddot{q}_j$ represents the **coupling inertia**. A single axis PID ignores this term entirely, treating it as a disturbance. As speed increases, this unmodelled disturbance degrades tracking performance severely — this is why **computed-torque control** and **feedback linearisation** exist for high-speed robots.

### Disadvantage 5 — Poor Performance on Non-Linear Systems

The PID law is fundamentally **linear and time-invariant (LTI)**. It cannot adapt to:
- Friction nonlinearities (Coulomb, stiction, viscous)
- Backlash in gears
- Saturation in motors
- Variable payload inertia

### Disadvantage 6 — Sensitivity to Tuning and Time Delays

Plant-model mismatch at high frequencies causes instability. Pure time delay $\tau_d$ reduces the **phase margin** as:

$$\phi_{loss} = \omega_{co} \cdot \tau_d \quad \text{(radians)}$$

Exceeding 60° of phase loss will destabilise the closed loop.

### Disadvantage 7 — No Multi-Objective Optimisation

A single scalar signal $u(t)$ cannot simultaneously:
- Track position,
- Minimise energy consumption,
- Respect actuator rate limits, and
- Avoid obstacles.

These require **model-predictive control (MPC)** or **adaptive control**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of Single Axis PID Control Loop

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete signal flow inside one axis of a robotic joint controller.

```mermaid

    r["R_DESIRED_SETPOINT"]:::inputNode --> sumA["SUMMING_JUNCTION"]
    sumA --> ctrl["PID_CONTROLLER_KP_KI_KD"]:::ctrlNode

    sat --> plant["ROBOT_JOINT_PLANT"]:::plantNode
    plant --> fb["FEEDBACK_SENSOR_ENCODER"]:::fbNode
    fb --> sumA
    fb --> noise["SENSOR_NOISE_INJECTION"]:::noiseNode

    classDef inputNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef ctrlNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef satNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000

```

The next diagram maps each disadvantage to the specific block in the control loop where it originates.

## 4.3 Detailed PID Internal Branching Diagram

```mermaid

    err --> i1["INTEGRATE_OVER_TIME"]:::inode
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the single axis PID controller and write its mathematical expression in both time domain and s-domain.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A single axis PID controller is a linear feedback controller that regulates one independent degree of freedom by computing the control signal as a weighted sum of proportional, integral, and derivative actions on the tracking error.

**Time-domain form:**

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**S-domain form:**

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for time-domain, 1 mark for s-domain).**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List any four disadvantages of single axis PID control in robotic manipulator applications.

**Model Answer (4 disadvantages for 3 marks — any four):**

1. **Integral windup** during actuator saturation causing large overshoot.
2. **Derivative kick** producing impulsive control spikes during setpoint changes.
3. **High-frequency noise amplification** in the derivative branch.
4. **Inability to handle inter-joint coupling torques** in multi-DOF manipulators.
5. **Poor performance on nonlinear systems** (friction, backlash, variable payload).
6. **Sensitivity to time delays** in feedback loop causing phase-margin loss.
7. **No multi-objective optimisation** capability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the operation of a single axis PID controller for a robotic joint. Discuss in detail its major disadvantages when applied to multi-DOF robotic manipulators.

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Operation (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A single axis PID controller for a robotic joint consists of the following functional blocks in a unity-feedback closed loop:

1. **Setpoint generator** $r(t)$: provides the desired joint angle $q_d$.
2. **Error detector**: computes $e(t) = q_d(t) - q_a(t)$, where $q_a$ is the actual joint angle from the encoder.
3. **PID controller**: processes the error into three parallel branches.
   - Proportional branch multiplies $e(t)$ by $K_p$.
   - Integral branch integrates $e(t)$ and multiplies by $K_i$.
   - Derivative branch differentiates $e(t)$ and multiplies by $K_d$.
4. **Actuator** (servo motor + drive): receives the control voltage/torque signal, subject to saturation limits.
5. **Plant** (the robotic link): produces mechanical motion $q_a(t)$.
6. **Sensor** (optical encoder / resolver): measures the actual joint position for feedback.

**Signal flow equation:**

$$\tau(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \dot{e}(t)$$

This torque is applied to the joint whose dynamics follow:

$$J_{eff} \ddot{q}_a + B \dot{q}_a + \tau_{dist} = \tau(t)$$

where $J_{eff}$ is effective inertia, $B$ is damping, and $\tau_{dist}$ contains all disturbance torques (gravity, coupling, friction).

**Block diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.) — *3 marks for the diagram, 4 marks for the textual explanation.*

#### Part (b) — Major Disadvantages (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Integral Windup (2 marks)**
When the joint motor saturates, the integrator keeps accumulating error. Once the error finally reverses, the controller takes a long time to "unwind" the integrator, producing a large overshoot. Mitigation: anti-windup using back-calculation or conditional integration.

**(ii) Derivative Kick and Noise Amplification (2 marks)**
Sudden setpoint changes inject impulsive derivative spikes, and the $K_d s$ term amplifies sensor noise by 20 dB per decade. Mitigation: filter the derivative with a first-order low-pass filter, or compute derivative on the measured output only.

**(iii) Inability to Handle Joint Coupling (2 marks)**
In an $n$-DOF manipulator, the torque on joint $i$ depends on the accelerations of *all* other joints through the off-diagonal inertia terms $M_{ij}$. Single axis PID treats these as unmeasured disturbances and degrades in tracking accuracy as speed increases.

**(iv) Linear Assumption Breakdown (1 mark)**
Friction (Coulomb + stiction), backlash, and saturation are nonlinearities that a fixed-gain linear PID cannot optimally compensate.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Derive the transfer function of a single axis PID controller. Explain integral windup phenomenon with mathematical justification and describe two practical methods to prevent it.

#### Part (a) — Transfer Function Derivation (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Starting with the time-domain PID control law:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply Laplace transform assuming zero initial conditions.

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + K_i \cdot \frac{E(s)}{s} + K_d \cdot s E(s)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Factor out $E(s)$.

$$U(s) = \left[ K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \right] E(s)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Combine terms over common denominator $s$.

$$U(s) = \frac{K_p s + K_i + K_d s^2}{s} \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Rearrange in descending powers of $s$.

$$U(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s} \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Write the controller transfer function.

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Closed-loop transfer function with plant $G(s)$ in unity feedback.

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s) G(s)}{1 + C(s) G(s)} = \frac{(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i) G(s)}{s + (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i) G(s)}$$

#### Part (b) — Integral Windup Explanation and Prevention (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Mathematical Justification of Windup (3 marks):**
When the actuator saturates at $u_{max}$, the actual applied signal is:

$$u_{applied}(t) = \text{sat}\bigl(u(t)\bigr)$$

But the integrator continues updating using the unsaturated error:

$$I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$$

For a step input of magnitude $R$ with saturation active for duration $\Delta t$:

$$I(\Delta t) = K_i \cdot R \cdot \Delta t$$

This integral term grows without bound. When the setpoint is finally reached and the error reverses, the controller must satisfy:

$$u_{applied} = u_{max} \implies K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e} = u_{max}$$

The accumulated integral keeps $u_{applied} = u_{max}$ for some "discharge time" $t_d$ during which the position overshoots significantly.

**Prevention Method 1 — Back-Calculation Anti-Windup (2 marks):**
The integrator input is modified by feeding back the difference between the saturated and unsaturated control signals:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = K_i e(t) + \frac{1}{T_t}\bigl(u_{sat}(t) - u(t)\bigr)$$

where $T_t$ is the tracking time constant. This drains the integrator at a rate proportional to the saturation gap.

**Prevention Method 2 — Conditional Integration / Clamping (2 marks):**
The integration is suspended (held constant) whenever the actuator is saturated *and* the error sign would continue to increase the integral. This is also called "integrator clamping" and is computationally simple to implement in digital controllers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting the $s$-domain final form:** Many students write $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ but **fail to combine it into a single rational function** $\frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$. Both forms are accepted, but the combined form is required to identify the closed-loop characteristic polynomial. **Lose 1 mark** if you do not show the closed-loop denominator.
> - **Skipping anti-windup techniques:** A common KTU expectation is that you **name at least one mitigation strategy** (back-calculation, clamping, or conditional integration). Merely defining windup without mitigation costs you 1 to 2 marks.
> - **Confusing "setpoint derivative" with "measurement derivative":** In real industrial practice, derivative is taken on $y(t)$ only to avoid derivative kick. If a question asks "how is derivative kick prevented?", the answer is "compute derivative on the output, not on the error" — **not** "increase $K_d$".
> - **Ignoring joint coupling:** In a 14-mark question, simply listing seven disadvantages without mathematical linkage to the manipulator equation $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ is treated as a **superficial answer**. Always anchor your disadvantages to a physical equation.
> - **Units mismatch:** When $K_p$ is in N·m/rad and $K_d$ in N·m·s/rad, do **not** numerically add them; they are mathematically added inside the controller but you must respect units in any dimensional analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Single axis PID** is a **Type-1, linear, time-invariant** controller with transfer function $C(s) = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$.
- The **proportional term** reacts to *present* error, **integral** to *accumulated past* error, **derivative** to *predicted future* error.
- The controller is **inherently single-input single-output (SISO)** and **ignores inter-axis coupling**, which is a critical limitation in multi-DOF robots.
- **Integral windup** occurs when the actuator saturates; the integrator keeps accumulating error and produces a delayed, overshooting response. Mitigations include **back-calculation** and **clamping**.
- **Derivative kick** is an impulsive spike caused by step changes in the setpoint; the standard remedy is to differentiate the **measured output** rather than the error.
- The **derivative branch** amplifies high-frequency sensor noise with a 20 dB/decade slope; a first-order low-pass filter on the derivative is mandatory in practice.
- The PID is **linear**; it cannot optimally compensate **nonlinear friction, backlash, or variable payloads** without gain scheduling.
- **Time delays** in feedback (sampling, computation, communication) reduce the **phase margin** by $\omega_{co} \tau_d$ radians; excessive delay causes instability.
- For high-speed, high-accuracy robotic manipulators, single axis PID is replaced by **computed-torque control**, **feedback linearisation**, **adaptive control**, or **model-predictive control**.
- The Ziegler-Nichols closed-loop tuning method gives a starting point but is **not optimal** for robotic systems; manual refinement or LQR-based synthesis is preferred.
- In KTU examinations, always **draw the block diagram**, **derive the transfer function step-by-step**, and **link every disadvantage to a physical equation** to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PD Gravity Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PD Gravity Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**PD Gravity Control** (also called **PD Control with Gravity Compensation**) is an *independent joint / decoupled position control strategy* used in robotic manipulators, where the classical Proportional-Derivative (PD) control law is augmented with an explicit **feed-forward gravity compensation term** $g(q)$ to cancel out the gravitational torques acting on the robot's links.

The control law in **joint space** is given by:

$$\tau = K_p \tilde{q} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + g(q)$$

where:
- $\tilde{q} = q_d - q$ is the position error,
- $\dot{\tilde{q}} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$ is the velocity error,
- $K_p, K_v$ are positive-definite diagonal gain matrices (PD gains),
- $g(q)$ is the gravity vector of the manipulator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is essential for understanding **Set-Point Regulation** and **Trajectory Tracking** in robot manipulators. It is a direct application of the **robot dynamic equation**:
> $$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + g(q) = \tau$$

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are trying to hold a **heavy bowling ball** at arm's length. Your muscles (the *actuators*) must constantly produce a force equal to the weight of the ball; otherwise, your arm will droop. A **pure PD controller** is like trying to hold the ball using only the "spring + damper" feedback of your muscles — it will *always* sag slightly (a **steady-state error**) because gravity is a *persistent disturbance*.

Now, imagine you wear an **exoskeleton** that pre-emptively lifts the ball for you. Your muscles only need to make *small corrections* to keep the ball at the exact target position. That exoskeleton is the **gravity compensation term $g(q)$** — it cancels gravity *before* it even affects the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Pure PD control works for a *frictionless, weightless* robot. Real robots have mass, so we **add gravity compensation** to remove the steady-state deflection caused by gravity on each link.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The **gravitational acceleration** $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ is treated as a constant vector in the world frame.
- Robot link masses are typically measured in **kilograms (kg)**.
- Joint torques are in **Newton-meters (N·m)**.
- PD gains $K_p$ and $K_v$ are **diagonal positive-definite** matrices for decoupled control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Pure PD Control Fails Under Gravity

The **Euler-Lagrange equation** governing an n-DOF serial manipulator is:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + g(q) = \tau$$

where:
- $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$: Symmetric, positive-definite **inertia matrix**.
- $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$: **Coriolis and centrifugal** torque vector.
- $g(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$: **Gravitational torque** vector.
- $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$: Generalized torque applied at the joints.

For a **pure PD controller** $\tau = K_p \tilde{q} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}}$, if we set $\dot{q}_d = 0$ and $\ddot{q}_d = 0$ (regulation task), the **steady-state error** $\tilde{q}_{ss}$ satisfies:

$$g(q_d - \tilde{q}_{ss}) = K_p \tilde{q}_{ss} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tilde{q}_{ss} \neq 0$$

The robot **sags** under gravity because the PD law cannot produce a sustained output proportional to a constant disturbance (similar to a Type-0 system responding to a step input).

---

## 2.2 The PD + Gravity Compensation Solution

By **pre-loading** the controller with the exact gravitational torque, we cancel the disturbance:

$$\tau = K_p \tilde{q} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + \hat{g}(q)$$

If $\hat{g}(q) = g(q)$ (perfect model), the closed-loop dynamics reduce to:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} = K_p \tilde{q} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}}$$

Gravity is **completely eliminated** from the closed loop, and the system behaves like a set of independent second-order linear systems — one per joint.

---

## 2.3 Stability Analysis (Lyapunov Approach)

To prove **asymptotic stability**, define the candidate Lyapunov function:

$$V(\tilde{q}, \dot{\tilde{q}}) = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\tilde{q}}^T M(q) \dot{\tilde{q}} + \frac{1}{2}\tilde{q}^T K_p \tilde{q}$$

This function is **positive-definite** and **radially unbounded**.

Its time derivative along trajectories is:

$$\dot{V} = \dot{\tilde{q}}^T M(q) \ddot{\tilde{q}} + \frac{1}{2}\dot{\tilde{q}}^T \dot{M}(q) \dot{\tilde{q}} + \dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_p \tilde{q}$$

Using the skew-symmetric property $\dot{q}^T [\frac{1}{2}\dot{M}(q) - C(q,\dot{q})]\dot{q} = 0$ and the closed-loop dynamics, we obtain:

$$\dot{V} = -\dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} \leq 0$$

By **LaSalle's Invariance Principle**, this implies $\tilde{q} \to 0$ and $\dot{\tilde{q}} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$, i.e., **global asymptotic stability**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Formula** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units / Property** |
|---|---|---|
| $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + g(q) = \tau$ | Robot dynamic equation (Euler-Lagrange) | Vector / Matrix equation |
| $\tau_{PD} = K_p \tilde{q} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}}$ | Pure PD control law | N·m |
| $\tau_{PD+g} = K_p \tilde{q} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + g(q)$ | PD + Gravity compensation | N·m |
| $\tilde{q} = q_d - q$ | Position tracking error | rad or m |
| $\dot{\tilde{q}} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$ | Velocity tracking error | rad/s or m/s |
| $K_p = \text{diag}(k_{p1},\dots,k_{pn})$ | Proportional gain (positive-definite) | N·m/rad |
| $K_v = \text{diag}(k_{v1},\dots,k_{vn})$ | Derivative gain (positive-definite) | N·m·s/rad |
| $V = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\tilde{q}}^T M \dot{\tilde{q}} + \frac{1}{2}\tilde{q}^T K_p \tilde{q}$ | Lyapunov candidate | Positive scalar (Joules) |
| $\dot{V} = -\dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} \leq 0$ | Negative semi-definite $\Rightarrow$ Stability | Energy dissipation rate |
| $\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})$ | Skew-symmetric matrix property | Used in stability proof |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** PD + Gravity control is the **industrial standard** for robot manipulators in tasks like pick-and-place, welding, and assembly — wherever the robot must hold a position precisely against gravity.

---

## 2.5 Design Guidelines (Practical Insights)

- $K_p$ should be large enough for **stiffness** (small position error) but not so large that it excites unmodelled dynamics.
- $K_v = 2\sqrt{K_p} \cdot \zeta$ with damping ratio $\zeta \approx 1$ gives a **critically damped** response.
- The gravity model $g(q)$ must be **accurate**; mismatch causes residual steady-state error.
- The controller requires **joint position and velocity feedback** (encoders + tachometers / observers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Error Dynamics under PD + Gravity Control

### Step 1 — Start with the manipulator dynamic model
The general equation of motion of a serial-chain rigid robot is:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + g(q) = \tau$$

**Explanation:** This is the **fundamental equation of robot dynamics** derived from the Euler-Lagrange formulation. The inertia matrix $M(q)$ captures the mass distribution; $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ captures velocity-dependent (Coriolis + centrifugal) effects; and $g(q)$ is the conservative gravitational torque.

---

### Step 2 — Substitute the PD + Gravity control law
The control torque applied is:

$$\tau = K_p \tilde{q} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + g(q)$$

where $\tilde{q} = q_d - q$ and $\dot{\tilde{q}} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$.

**Explanation:** The proportional term $K_p \tilde{q}$ acts like a virtual spring pulling each joint toward its desired position; the derivative term $K_v \dot{\tilde{q}}$ acts as a virtual damper; and $g(q)$ cancels the gravitational torque exactly.

---

### Step 3 — Substitute into the dynamic equation
Plugging the control law into the open-loop dynamics:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + g(q) = K_p \tilde{q} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + g(q)$$

**Explanation:** We replace $\tau$ on the right-hand side with our control law. The gravity term $g(q)$ appears on **both sides** of the equation.

---

### Step 4 — Cancel the gravity terms
Subtracting $g(q)$ from both sides yields:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} = K_p (q_d - q) + K_v (\dot{q}_d - \dot{q})$$

**Explanation:** This is the critical step — gravity has been **exactly cancelled**, leaving only the inertial and Coriolis terms on the left. The closed-loop system now behaves like a **linear spring-damper system in each joint**.

---

### Step 5 — Rearrange into the standard second-order form
Moving all state-dependent terms to the left:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + K_v \dot{q} + K_p q = K_v \dot{q}_d + K_p q_d$$

**Explanation:** This is the closed-loop error dynamics. The right-hand side represents the *desired* forcing (a feed-forward reference), and the left-hand side describes the *actual* motion governed by the closed-loop stiffness and damping.

---

### Step 6 — Express everything in terms of the tracking error
Define $\tilde{q} = q_d - q$. Taking two time derivatives:

$$\dot{\tilde{q}} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}, \qquad \ddot{\tilde{q}} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q}$$

Substitute $q = q_d - \tilde{q}$, $\dot{q} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{\tilde{q}}$, and $\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{\tilde{q}}$ into the closed-loop equation:

$$M(q)[\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{\tilde{q}}] + C(q,\dot{q})[\dot{q}_d - \dot{\tilde{q}}] + K_v [\dot{q}_d - \dot{\tilde{q}}] + K_p [q_d - \tilde{q}] = K_v \dot{q}_d + K_p q_d$$

---

### Step 7 — Simplify using the open-loop dynamics
Recall the open-loop dynamics for the **desired trajectory** (assuming it satisfies the model):

$$M(q_d)\ddot{q}_d + C(q_d,\dot{q}_d)\dot{q}_d + g(q_d) = \tau_d$$

Under exact model matching, the terms involving $q_d, \dot{q}_d, \ddot{q}_d$ on the left-hand side cancel with the right-hand side, leaving:

$$M(q)\ddot{\tilde{q}} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{\tilde{q}} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + K_p \tilde{q} = 0$$

**Explanation:** This is the **final closed-loop error dynamics**. It is a nonlinear second-order differential equation in the error $\tilde{q}$. The matrix $M(q)$ is positive-definite, $K_p$ and $K_v$ are positive-definite gains, and $C(q,\dot{q})$ only multiplies the velocity error.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Lyapunov Stability Proof

### Step 1 — Define the candidate Lyapunov function

$$V(\tilde{q}, \dot{\tilde{q}}) = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\tilde{q}}^T M(q) \dot{\tilde{q}} + \frac{1}{2}\tilde{q}^T K_p \tilde{q}$$

**Explanation:** The first term is the **kinetic energy** of the error motion (always $\geq 0$); the second term is the **potential energy** stored in the virtual spring (always $\geq 0$). $V = 0$ if and only if $\tilde{q} = 0$ and $\dot{\tilde{q}} = 0$.

---

### Step 2 — Differentiate $V$ with respect to time

$$\dot{V} = \dot{\tilde{q}}^T M(q) \ddot{\tilde{q}} + \frac{1}{2}\dot{\tilde{q}}^T \dot{M}(q) \dot{\tilde{q}} + \dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_p \tilde{q}$$

**Explanation:** We use the product rule on the first term and the fact that $M(q)$ depends on $q$ (and therefore on time through $\tilde{q}$). The last term comes from differentiating the spring potential.

---

### Step 3 — Substitute the error dynamics
Replace $M(q)\ddot{\tilde{q}}$ using the error equation $M(q)\ddot{\tilde{q}} = -C(q,\dot{q})\dot{\tilde{q}} - K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} - K_p \tilde{q}$:

$$\dot{V} = \dot{\tilde{q}}^T \left[-C(q,\dot{q})\dot{\tilde{q}} - K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} - K_p \tilde{q}\right] + \frac{1}{2}\dot{\tilde{q}}^T \dot{M}(q) \dot{\tilde{q}} + \dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_p \tilde{q}$$

---

### Step 4 — Group and simplify

$$\dot{V} = \dot{\tilde{q}}^T \left[\frac{1}{2}\dot{M}(q) - C(q,\dot{q})\right] \dot{\tilde{q}} - \dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} - \dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_p \tilde{q} + \dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_p \tilde{q}$$

The first bracket vanishes due to the **skew-symmetry property** of robot dynamics:

$$\dot{\tilde{q}}^T \left[\frac{1}{2}\dot{M}(q) - C(q,\dot{q})\right] \dot{\tilde{q}} = 0$$

The two $\dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_p \tilde{q}$ terms also cancel, leaving:

$$\dot{V} = -\dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_v \dot{\tilde{q}}$$

---

### Step 5 — Conclude stability
Since $K_v$ is positive-definite:

$$\dot{V} = -\dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} \leq 0$$

**Explanation:** $\dot{V} \leq 0$ means $V$ is non-increasing — the system is **stable** in the sense of Lyapunov. Furthermore, by **LaSalle's Invariance Principle**, since $\dot{V} = 0$ only when $\dot{\tilde{q}} = 0$, the largest invariant set is the equilibrium $\tilde{q} = 0, \dot{\tilde{q}} = 0$, proving **global asymptotic stability**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Property** | **Pure PD Control** | **PD + Gravity Compensation** |
|---|---|---|
| Steady-state error under gravity | $\tilde{q}_{ss} \neq 0$ (sag) | $\tilde{q}_{ss} = 0$ (exact) |
| Closed-loop dynamics | Nonlinear with $g(q)$ | Linear-like, gravity-free |
| Stability proof | Requires $g(q) = 0$ | Always asymptotically stable |
| Computational cost | Low | Requires $g(q)$ computation |
| Model dependency | None | Requires accurate $g(q)$ model |
| Use case | Frictionless / horizontal joints | All real manipulators |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — PD Control with Gravity Compensation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Desired Position qd] --> B[Comparator]
    A1[Desired Velocity qd_dot] --> B1[Comparator]
    FB[Actual Position q] --> B
    FB1[Actual Velocity q_dot] --> B1
    B --> E1[Error Signal e = qd - q]
    B1 --> E2[Error Signal e_dot = qd_dot - q_dot]
    E1 --> P[Kp Gain Block]
    E2 --> D[Kv Gain Block]
    P --> SUM[Summation Node]
    D --> SUM
    GCM[Gravity Model g of q] --> SUM
    SUM --> TAU[Control Torque tau]
    TAU --> ROBOT[Robot Manipulator M C g]
    ROBOT --> FB
    ROBOT --> FB1
    ROBOT --> OUT[Output q and q_dot]
```

**Explanation of the block diagram:**
- The **comparators** compute the position and velocity errors.
- $K_p$ and $K_v$ act on these errors to produce spring and damper torques.
- The **Gravity Model** block computes $g(q)$ in real time from joint angles.
- All three signals are summed and sent to the robot as the **control torque $\tau$**.
- The robot produces the actual motion $q, \dot{q}$, which is fed back to close the loop.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Pure PD Control (for Comparison)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A2[Desired Position qd] --> B2[Comparator]
    A3[Desired Velocity qd_dot] --> B3[Comparator]
    FB2[Actual Position q] --> B2
    FB3[Actual Velocity q_dot] --> B3
    B2 --> P2[Kp Gain Block]
    B3 --> D2[Kv Gain Block]
    P2 --> SUM2[Summation Node]
    D2 --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> TAU2[Control Torque tau PD]
    TAU2 --> ROBOT2[Robot Manipulator M C g]
    ROBOT2 --> FB2
    ROBOT2 --> FB3
    ROBOT2 --> OUT2[Output with Steady State Error]
```

**Explanation:** Notice that **gravity is NOT cancelled** — the gravitational torque $g(q)$ is treated as a disturbance that the PD controller must fight. This leads to a steady-state position error (sag) in vertical joints.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Stability Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Define Error Vectors tilde q and tilde q dot] --> S2[Write Closed Loop Dynamics]
    S2 --> S3[Construct Lyapunov Function V]
    S3 --> S4[Differentiate V along Trajectories]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Skew Symmetry Property]
    S5 --> S6[Obtain V dot negative semidefinite]
    S6 --> S7[Apply LaSalle Invariance Principle]
    S7 --> S8[Conclude Global Asymptotic Stability]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by PD control with gravity compensation in robotic manipulators? Why is the gravity term added to the control law?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
PD control with gravity compensation is a joint-space control law of the form $\tau = K_p \tilde{q} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + g(q)$. The gravity term $g(q)$ is added to **cancel the gravitational torques** acting on the robot links, thereby eliminating the steady-state position error (sag) that would otherwise occur in vertical joints under pure PD control. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the equation, 1 for justification)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the Lyapunov function used to prove the stability of PD control with gravity compensation and write its time derivative.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Lyapunov candidate is:
$$V = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\tilde{q}}^T M(q)\dot{\tilde{q}} + \frac{1}{2}\tilde{q}^T K_p \tilde{q}$$
Its time derivative is $\dot{V} = -\dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} \leq 0$, which is negative semi-definite, proving stability. (3 marks: 1 for V, 1 for V-dot, 1 for conclusion)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) Derive the closed-loop error dynamics of an n-DOF robot manipulator under PD control with gravity compensation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Start with the Euler-Lagrange dynamic model:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + g(q) = \tau$$

**Step 2:** Apply the PD + Gravity control law:

$$\tau = K_p(q_d - q) + K_v(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) + g(q)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the dynamic equation:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + g(q) = K_p(q_d - q) + K_v(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) + g(q)$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $g(q)$ from both sides:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} = K_p(q_d - q) + K_v(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q})$$

**Step 5:** Express in terms of error $\tilde{q} = q_d - q$:

$$M(q)\ddot{\tilde{q}} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{\tilde{q}} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + K_p \tilde{q} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** The closed-loop error dynamics is a second-order nonlinear differential equation with positive-definite mass matrix and positive-definite gain matrices.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per logical step, 1 for final equation, 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### (b) Using Lyapunov's direct method, prove that the PD + gravity controller ensures global asymptotic stability of the tracking error. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Candidate function:**

$$V(\tilde{q}, \dot{\tilde{q}}) = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\tilde{q}}^T M(q)\dot{\tilde{q}} + \frac{1}{2}\tilde{q}^T K_p \tilde{q}$$

$V(0,0) = 0$ and $V > 0$ otherwise (positive-definite).

**Step 2 — Differentiate:**

$$\dot{V} = \dot{\tilde{q}}^T M(q)\ddot{\tilde{q}} + \frac{1}{2}\dot{\tilde{q}}^T \dot{M}(q)\dot{\tilde{q}} + \dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_p \tilde{q}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute error dynamics** $M(q)\ddot{\tilde{q}} = -C\dot{\tilde{q}} - K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} - K_p \tilde{q}$:

$$\dot{V} = \dot{\tilde{q}}^T\left[\frac{1}{2}\dot{M} - C\right]\dot{\tilde{q}} - \dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} - \dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_p \tilde{q} + \dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_p \tilde{q}$$

**Step 4 — Apply skew-symmetry** (first bracket vanishes) and cancel the $K_p$ terms:

$$\dot{V} = -\dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} \leq 0$$

**Step 5 — Invoke LaSalle's Invariance Principle:** Since $\dot{V} = 0$ only when $\dot{\tilde{q}} = 0$, and the largest invariant set in this case is $\tilde{q} = 0$, we conclude **global asymptotic stability**.

*(7 marks: 1 for V, 1 for V-dot derivation, 1 for skew-symmetry, 1 for cancellation, 1 for negative semi-definiteness, 1 for LaSalle's principle, 1 for final conclusion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**

#### (a) Explain why pure PD control exhibits a steady-state error in the presence of gravity. Show mathematically that $\tilde{q}_{ss} \neq 0$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Under pure PD control $\tau = K_p \tilde{q} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}}$, the closed-loop dynamics is:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + g(q) = K_p(q_d - q) + K_v(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q})$$

**Step 2:** At steady state, $\dot{q} = 0, \ddot{q} = 0, \dot{q}_d = 0$ (set-point regulation). The dynamics reduce to:

$$g(q_{ss}) = K_p(q_d - q_{ss})$$

**Step 3:** This means the steady-state error must balance gravity:

$$\tilde{q}_{ss} = q_d - q_{ss} = K_p^{-1} g(q_{ss})$$

**Step 4:** Since $g(q_{ss}) \neq 0$ for any non-horizontal configuration, $\tilde{q}_{ss} \neq 0$. The robot sags until the spring torque $K_p \tilde{q}_{ss}$ exactly balances gravity.

**Conclusion:** Pure PD control always has a non-zero steady-state error whenever gravity is non-zero, which is why the gravity compensation term is essential.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for steady-state condition, 2 for the error formula, 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### (b) Compare PD control and PD + gravity control in terms of steady-state accuracy, model dependency, and stability. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A structured comparison table:

| **Criterion** | **Pure PD Control** | **PD + Gravity Control** |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady-state error** | $\tilde{q}_{ss} = K_p^{-1} g(q)$ — Non-zero under gravity | $\tilde{q}_{ss} = 0$ — Exact regulation |
| **Model dependency** | Independent of robot model | Requires exact $g(q)$ computation |
| **Computational load** | Low — just two gain multiplications | Moderate — must compute $g(q)$ at each sample |
| **Stability** | Stable only for $g = 0$ or $q_d$ small | Globally asymptotically stable (Lyapunov) |
| **Trajectory tracking** | Good for slow trajectories | Excellent even for fast trajectories |
| **Robustness** | Robust to model errors | Sensitive to $g(q)$ model mismatch |
| **Implementation** | Simple — encoder + gain block | Needs dynamic model in controller |

**Conclusion:** PD + gravity control is the **superior** choice for precise set-point regulation in real industrial robots, at the cost of needing a reasonably accurate dynamic model. (7 marks: 6 for table contents, 1 for concluding remark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $K_p \tilde{q}$ cancellation** in the V-dot derivation — students often leave a stray $K_p$ term and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $K_p$ and $K_v$ units** — they are NOT the same dimension; $K_p$ has units of N·m/rad, $K_v$ has N·m·s/rad. Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing this.
> 3. **Not stating the skew-symmetry property** explicitly in the Lyapunov proof — it is a mandatory step.
> 4. **Using $\dot{q}$ instead of $\dot{\tilde{q}}$** in the error dynamics — mixing up actual and error velocities is a common error worth 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention LaSalle's Invariance Principle** — without it, you only prove *Lyapunov stability*, not *asymptotic stability*. Examiners specifically test for this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Law:** $\tau = K_p \tilde{q} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + g(q)$ — the *only* addition over pure PD is the gravity vector.
- **Robot Dynamics Equation:** Always start derivations from $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + g(q) = \tau$.
- **Pure PD Fails** under gravity because the steady-state equation $g(q_{ss}) = K_p \tilde{q}_{ss}$ has $\tilde{q}_{ss} \neq 0$.
- **Closed-loop error dynamics** (final form): $M(q)\ddot{\tilde{q}} + C\dot{\tilde{q}} + K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} + K_p \tilde{q} = 0$.
- **Lyapunov function:** $V = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\tilde{q}}^T M \dot{\tilde{q}} + \frac{1}{2}\tilde{q}^T K_p \tilde{q}$ — kinetic + potential energy.
- **Lyapunov derivative:** $\dot{V} = -\dot{\tilde{q}}^T K_v \dot{\tilde{q}} \leq 0$ — energy always dissipates.
- **Skew-symmetry property:** $\dot{q}^T [\frac{1}{2}\dot{M} - C]\dot{q} = 0$ — a key identity for the proof.
- **Stability conclusion:** Global asymptotic stability via **LaSalle's Invariance Principle**.
- **Gain tuning:** $K_v$ ensures damping; $K_p$ ensures stiffness. $K_v = 2\sqrt{K_p}$ gives critical damping (when $M$ is normalized).
- **Model accuracy matters:** Errors in $g(q)$ directly cause steady-state position errors.
- **Industrial use:** This controller is the backbone of point-to-point motion in **CNC machines, welding robots, pick-and-place systems, and SCARA arms**.
- **Decoupling property:** With perfect gravity compensation, each joint behaves like an **independent second-order linear system** — a major advantage in design and analysis.
- **Boundary condition:** If $q_d$ is constant (regulation), $\dot{q}_d = 0$ and $\ddot{q}_d = 0$ throughout the motion.
- **KTU favorite question pattern:** "Derive the closed-loop dynamics and prove stability" — be ready to write both parts back-to-back in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computed Torque Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computed Torque Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computed Torque Control (CTC)** is a model-based, nonlinear feedback linearization strategy for robotic manipulators in which the joint torque vector $\tau(t)$ is computed as a function of the robot's *measured* inertial matrix $\mathbf{M}(q)$, the *Coriolis/centripetal* vector $\mathbf{C}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$, and the *gravitational* vector $\mathbf{G}(q)$, together with a *servo-level* tracking error. The technique effectively cancels the nonlinear coupling between joints, converting the original $n$-DOF nonlinear plant into a set of $n$ independent linear double-integrators, which are then stabilized by an outer PD (or PID) loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Computed Torque Control belongs to the *Nonlinear Control of Robot Manipulators* cluster and is the most frequently asked derivation-based topic (full 14-mark derivations appear in almost every University ESE cycle).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Intelligent Driver"

Imagine driving a fully loaded truck on a hilly, slippery road. A naive driver would press the brake with the *same force* regardless of slope or cargo, causing skidding or under-braking. An **expert driver** internally computes:

> *"Truck is heavy → press harder; road is downhill → press even harder; cargo has shifted → compensate for new center of mass."*

Computed Torque Control is exactly this **"expert driver"** for a robot arm. At every instant ($T_s \approx 1$–$\mathbf{1}$ **ms**), the controller:

1. *Senses* the current joint state $(q,\dot{q})$.
2. *Computes* the instantaneous physical loading on every joint from the **inverse dynamics model**.
3. *Pre-compensates* the torque so the arm behaves like a *simple, predictable unit mass* (a double integrator) that any linear PD law can govern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (textbook standard):** CTC operates at a typical servo bandwidth of $\omega_n = \mathbf{10}$ to $\mathbf{50}$ rad/s with a damping ratio $\zeta = \mathbf{1}$ (critically damped) for industrial manipulators. The model's inverse-dynamics calculation is **O($n^3$)** in floating-point operations, dominated by the inversion of $\mathbf{M}(q)$.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Design Parameters

The following quantities govern a CTC design and will appear repeatedly in derivations and exam questions:

| Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Unit | Common Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Degrees of freedom (number of joints) | dimensionless | $\mathbf{2} \le n \le \mathbf{7}$ |
| $\mathbf{M}(q)$ | Configuration-dependent mass/inertia matrix | $\mathbf{kg \cdot m^2}$ | Symmetric, $n \times n$, positive-definite |
| $\mathbf{K}_p$ | Proportional gain matrix (diagonal) | $\mathbf{N \cdot m / rad}$ | $\omega_n^2$ |
| $\mathbf{K}_d$ | Derivative gain matrix (diagonal) | $\mathbf{N \cdot m \cdot s / rad}$ | $2\zeta\omega_n$ |
| $\omega_n$ | Natural frequency of the closed loop | $\mathbf{rad/s}$ | $\mathbf{10}$ to $\mathbf{50}$ |
| $\zeta$ | Damping ratio (dimensionless) | dimensionless | $\mathbf{1}$ (critically damped) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Governing Robot Dynamics (Starting Point)

The **Lagrangian-Euler** or **Newton-Euler** formulation yields the general manipulator equation:

$$
\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot{q} \;+\; \mathbf{C}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} \;+\; \mathbf{G}(q) \;+\; \mathbf{F}(\dot{q}) \;=\; \tau
$$

- $\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot{q}$ — Inertial torque term (configuration-dependent, $\mathbf{O}(n^2)$ terms).
- $\mathbf{C}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ — Coriolis and centripetal coupling torque ($\mathbf{O}(n^3)$ terms).
- $\mathbf{G}(q)$ — Gravitational loading torque.
- $\mathbf{F}(\dot{q})$ — Friction torque (often linear in $\dot{q}$).
- $\tau$ — Generalized joint torque vector ($n \times 1$).

For compactness, the *nonlinear internal forces* are bundled into a single vector:

$$
\mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q}) \;=\; \mathbf{C}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} \;+\; \mathbf{G}(q) \;+\; \mathbf{F}(\dot{q})
$$

The plant equation becomes:

$$
\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot{q} \;+\; \mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q}) \;=\; \tau
$$

### 2.2 Philosophy of Computed Torque Control — Why and How

**Why is it needed?**
A conventional **independent joint PD controller** treats each joint as a decoupled second-order linear system. This is physically *wrong* for any robot with $n \ge 2$ because joint $i$'s motion exerts reaction torques on joint $j$ through $\mathbf{M}_{ij}(q)$ and $\mathbf{C}_{ij}(q,\dot{q})$. At low speeds the errors are small, but at higher speeds (e.g., $\dot{q} > \mathbf{1}$ rad/s) the coupling torques become large and the robot *oscillates, overshoots, or becomes unstable*.

**How does CTC fix this?**
CTC uses a *two-loop architecture*:

1. **Inner loop (Nonlinear Compensation):** Cancels $\mathbf{M}(q)$ and $\mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q})$ *exactly* by exploiting the model. This step is called **"Feedback Linearization"** or **"Inverse Dynamics Feedforward"**.
2. **Outer loop (Linear Servo):** After linearization, the plant is reduced to a unit-mass double integrator per joint. A standard PD law closes the loop, choosing gains for desired transient response.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Utility:** CTC is the *workhorse* of high-performance industrial robotics — used in painting robots (e.g., Dürr, ABB IRB series), surgical robots (da Vinci Research Kit), and space manipulators (Canadarm 2) where trajectory-tracking accuracy must stay below $\mathbf{0.1}$ mm.

### 2.3 The Control Law (Compact Form)

$$
\tau \;=\; \mathbf{M}(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{e} + \mathbf{K}_p e\bigr] \;+\; \mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q})
$$

with the tracking error vector:

$$
e \;=\; q_d \;-\; q
$$

### 2.4 Resulting Error Dynamics

Substituting the control law into the plant equation:

$$
\ddot{e} \;+\; \mathbf{K}_d \dot{e} \;+\; \mathbf{K}_p e \;=\; 0
$$

This is a **linear, decoupled, second-order** differential equation for each joint — exactly what PD control on a unit mass would produce.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the *minimum memorizable* set for any KTU 2024 exam on this topic.** Master it before any derivation.

| # | Equation / Rule | Meaning | KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot{q} + \mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q}) = \tau$ | Compact plant model | Starting point of every derivation |
| 2 | $\tau = \mathbf{M}(q)\,a + \mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q})$ | Generic feedback-linearization law | Definition of $a$ as auxiliary input |
| 3 | $a = \ddot{q}_d + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{e} + \mathbf{K}_p e$ | Outer PD servo law | Gives the final CTC law |
| 4 | $\ddot{e} + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{e} + \mathbf{K}_p e = 0$ | Closed-loop error dynamics | *Always the answer* to stability Qs |
| 5 | $\mathbf{K}_p = \operatorname{diag}(\omega_{n_i}^2)$ | Proportional gain for joint $i$ | Tuning under critical damping |
| 6 | $\mathbf{K}_d = \operatorname{diag}(2\zeta_i\omega_{n_i})$ | Derivative gain for joint $i$ | Tuning under critical damping |
| 7 | $\zeta_i = 1 \;\Rightarrow\;$ critically damped | No overshoot, fastest non-oscillatory | Industrial default |
| 8 | $e(t) = e^{-(\zeta\omega_n)t}\bigl[\cos(\omega_d t) + \tfrac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\sin(\omega_d t)\bigr]e(0)$ | Underdamped response | Transient analysis |
| 9 | Routh: $\mathbf{K}_p > 0$ **and** $\mathbf{K}_d > 0$ | Necessary and sufficient for stability | Stability proof in 7-mark Qs |
| 10 | Inverse-dynamics cost: $\mathbf{O}(n^3)$ | Computational complexity | Justifies Newton-Euler recursive form |
| 11 | Singularity: $\det\mathbf{M}(q) = 0$ | Cannot occur — $\mathbf{M}$ is PD always | Explanation of *why* inversion is safe |
| 12 | Tracking error bound: $\vert e \vert \le \mu / \lambda_{\min}(\mathbf{K}_p)$ | Robustness to bounded disturbance $\mu$ | Advanced 14-mark Qs |

> [!NOTE]
> **All gain matrices $\mathbf{K}_p$ and $\mathbf{K}_d$ must be positive-definite.** This is the **single most-tested statement** in KTU valuation keys for the stability part.

### 2.6 Why Choose $\mathbf{K}_p = \omega_n^2$ and $\mathbf{K}_d = 2\zeta\omega_n$?

For a single joint, the closed-loop characteristic polynomial is:

$$
s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 \;=\; 0
$$

This is the canonical form of a 2nd-order system whose:
- **Natural frequency** is $\omega_n$ (sets the speed of response — *larger $\omega_n$ = faster but more aggressive actuator demand*).
- **Damping ratio** is $\zeta$ (sets the overshoot — *$\zeta = 1$ gives no overshoot*).

For a multi-DOF system with $\mathbf{K}_p, \mathbf{K}_d$ chosen diagonal, the characteristic polynomial *factors* into $n$ independent 2nd-order polynomials, one per joint — hence the system is **decoupled**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Computed Torque Control Law

**Given:**
The general $n$-DOF manipulator dynamics:

$$
\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot{q} \;+\; \mathbf{C}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} \;+\; \mathbf{G}(q) \;+\; \mathbf{F}(\dot{q}) \;=\; \tau \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

with $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ being the joint position vector, and $\mathbf{M}(q)$ being symmetric, positive-definite for all $q$.

**Step 1 — Bundle the nonlinear internal forces.**

Define the nonlinear-in-velocity and gravity vector:

$$
\mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q}) \;=\; \mathbf{C}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} \;+\; \mathbf{G}(q) \;+\; \mathbf{F}(\dot{q})
$$

The compact plant equation is:

$$
\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot{q} \;+\; \mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q}) \;=\; \tau \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the tracking error and its derivatives.**

$$
e \;\triangleq\; q_d \;-\; q
$$

$$
\dot{e} \;=\; \dot{q}_d \;-\; \dot{q}
$$

$$
\ddot{e} \;=\; \ddot{q}_d \;-\; \ddot{q}
$$

**Step 3 — Propose a desired linear error dynamics.**

We *want* the closed loop to behave like a critically-tunable, decoupled 2nd-order system:

$$
\ddot{e} \;+\; \mathbf{K}_d \dot{e} \;+\; \mathbf{K}_p e \;=\; 0 \quad \text{...(iii)}
$$

where $\mathbf{K}_p, \mathbf{K}_d \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ are symmetric positive-definite gain matrices. Substituting the error derivatives:

$$
\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q} \;+\; \mathbf{K}_d(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) \;+\; \mathbf{K}_p(q_d - q) \;=\; 0
$$

Solving for $\ddot{q}$:

$$
\ddot{q} \;=\; \ddot{q}_d \;+\; \mathbf{K}_d \dot{e} \;+\; \mathbf{K}_p e \quad \text{...(iv)}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the auxiliary control input $a$.**

Recognizing that (iv) has the form $\ddot{q} = a$, define the *auxiliary control input*:

$$
a \;\triangleq\; \ddot{q}_d \;+\; \mathbf{K}_d \dot{e} \;+\; \mathbf{K}_p e
$$

If we can force $\ddot{q} = a$, the desired error dynamics (iii) are guaranteed.

**Step 5 — Design the torque to enforce $\ddot{q} = a$.**

Substituting $\ddot{q} = a$ into (ii) and solving for the required $\tau$:

$$
\mathbf{M}(q)\, a \;+\; \mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q}) \;=\; \tau
$$

$$
\tau \;=\; \mathbf{M}(q)\, a \;+\; \mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q}) \quad \text{...(v)}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the expression for $a$.**

$$
\tau \;=\; \mathbf{M}(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + \mathbf{K}_d \dot{e} + \mathbf{K}_p e\bigr] \;+\; \mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q}) \quad \text{...(vi)}
$$

This is the **Computed Torque Control Law** — the master equation of this module.

**Step 7 — Verify cancellation.**

Plug (vi) into (ii):

$$
\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot{q} \;+\; \mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q}) \;=\; \mathbf{M}(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + \mathbf{K}_d \dot{e} + \mathbf{K}_p e\bigr] \;+\; \mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q})
$$

Cancel $\mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q})$ from both sides:

$$
\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot{q} \;=\; \mathbf{M}(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + \mathbf{K}_d \dot{e} + \mathbf{K}_p e\bigr]
$$

Since $\mathbf{M}(q)$ is invertible (always positive-definite for revolute joints), left-multiply by $\mathbf{M}^{-1}(q)$:

$$
\ddot{q} \;=\; \ddot{q}_d + \mathbf{K}_d \dot{e} + \mathbf{K}_p e
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q} \;+\; \mathbf{K}_d \dot{e} + \mathbf{K}_p e \;=\; 0
$$

$$
\ddot{e} + \mathbf{K}_d \dot{e} + \mathbf{K}_p e \;=\; 0 \quad \text{...(vii)}
$$

$\blacksquare$ — *The system is now linear and decoupled.*

### 3.2 Decoupled Per-Joint Error Dynamics

When $\mathbf{K}_p$ and $\mathbf{K}_d$ are chosen as *diagonal* matrices, equation (vii) decomposes into $n$ independent scalar equations:

$$
\ddot{e}_i(t) + K_{d_i}\,\dot{e}_i(t) + K_{p_i}\,e_i(t) \;=\; 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

Each joint behaves *identically and independently* — there is **no cross-coupling** in the closed loop. The $i$-th joint's response is fully characterized by the pair $(K_{p_i}, K_{d_i})$ via standard 2nd-order system theory.

### 3.3 Gain Selection via Canonical Form

The canonical 2nd-order characteristic polynomial is:

$$
s^2 + 2\zeta_i \omega_{n_i}\, s + \omega_{n_i}^2 \;=\; 0
$$

Matching coefficients with $s^2 + K_{d_i}\,s + K_{p_i} = 0$:

$$
K_{p_i} \;=\; \omega_{n_i}^2
$$

$$
K_{d_i} \;=\; 2\zeta_i \omega_{n_i}
$$

For **critically damped** response (industrial default — zero overshoot):

$$
\zeta_i \;=\; 1
\;\Rightarrow\;
K_{d_i} \;=\; 2\omega_{n_i}
$$

### 3.4 Stability Analysis via Routh-Hurwitz

The closed-loop characteristic polynomial for joint $i$ is:

$$
p(s) \;=\; s^2 + K_{d_i}\,s + K_{p_i}
$$

The Routh array is:

| Row | Column 1 | Column 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^2$ | $1$ | $K_{p_i}$ |
| $s^1$ | $K_{d_i}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K_{p_i}$ | — |

**Routh-Hurwitz condition for asymptotic stability:**

- All first-column entries positive:
$$
K_{d_i} > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad K_{p_i} > 0
$$
- Since gains are chosen as $K_{p_i} = \omega_{n_i}^2 > 0$ and $K_{d_i} = 2\zeta_i\omega_{n_i} > 0$ (with $\omega_{n_i} > 0$, $\zeta_i > 0$), **the system is asymptotically stable for every joint $i$**.

### 3.5 Transient Response (Underdamped Case, $0 < \zeta_i < 1$)

When $\zeta_i < 1$ the system exhibits damped oscillation with damped natural frequency:

$$
\omega_{d_i} \;=\; \omega_{n_i}\sqrt{1 - \zeta_i^2}
$$

The step response with initial error $e_i(0) = e_0$ and $\dot{e}_i(0) = 0$ is:

$$
e_i(t) \;=\; \frac{e_0}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta_i^2}}\; e^{-\zeta_i \omega_{n_i} t}\, \sin\!\bigl(\omega_{d_i} t + \phi\bigr)
$$

where $\phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta_i)$. The 2% settling time is:

$$
t_s \;\approx\; \frac{4}{\zeta_i \omega_{n_i}}
$$

For a CTC tuned at $\omega_n = \mathbf{10}$ rad/s and $\zeta = 1$, the closed-loop settling time is approximately $t_s = \mathbf{0.4}$ seconds.

### 3.6 Robustness Sketch (Beyond Perfect Model)

In practice, the *estimated* model $\hat{\mathbf{M}}, \hat{\mathbf{h}}$ differs from the true plant $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{h}$. Substituting the *true* dynamics with the *estimated* control law yields:

$$
\ddot{e} + \mathbf{K}_d \dot{e} + \mathbf{K}_p e \;=\; \mathbf{M}^{-1}(q)\bigl[(\mathbf{M}(q) - \hat{\mathbf{M}}(q))a + (\mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q}) - \hat{\mathbf{h}}(q,\dot{q}))\bigr]
$$

The right-hand side is a *bounded disturbance*. With $\mathbf{K}_p, \mathbf{K}_d > 0$, Lyapunov analysis gives the steady-state tracking bound:

$$
\|e\|_\infty \;\le\; \frac{\mu}{\lambda_{\min}(\mathbf{K}_p)}
$$

where $\mu$ bounds the model-mismatch term. Larger gains ⇒ *smaller* tracking error, but the gains are upper-bounded by unmodeled high-frequency dynamics (e.g., joint flexibility, sample rate).

### 3.7 Numerical Example (Decoupled 2-DOF, KTU Typical)

**Problem:** A 2-DOF manipulator has $\mathbf{M}(q) = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{C} = 0$, $\mathbf{G} = 0$. Design a CTC with $\omega_n = 5$ rad/s and $\zeta = 1$ for each joint.

**Solution:**
- $K_{p_i} = \omega_n^2 = 25$
- $K_{d_i} = 2\zeta\omega_n = 10$
- $\mathbf{K}_p = 25\mathbf{I}_2$, $\mathbf{K}_d = 10\mathbf{I}_2$
- $\mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q}) = 0$ (no Coriolis, no gravity, no friction)
- $\tau = \mathbf{M}(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + 10\dot{e} + 25\,e\bigr]$

The closed loop is two independent critically-damped 2nd-order systems with $\omega_n = 5$ rad/s.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Computed Torque Control — Functional Block Architecture

The block diagram below shows the *closed-loop* CTC architecture. Notice the **two parallel feedback paths** (state feedback into the PD block, and the *implicit* use of $q,\dot{q}$ inside the inverse-dynamics model).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Reference Trajectory Generator qd, qdotd, qddotd] --> B[Outer PD Servo Law]
    J[Measured Joint Position q] --> K[Error Computer: e = qd - q]
    N[Measured Joint Velocity qdot] --> K
    A --> K
    K --> B
    B --> C[Auxiliary Acceleration a = qddotd + Kd edot + Kp e]
    J --> D[Inverse Dynamics Model Mqhqdotqdot]
    N --> D
    D --> E[Computed Torque Vector tau = Mq a + hqqdot]
    E --> F[Robot Manipulator Plant]
    F --> J
    F --> N
    styleA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD
    styleB fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    styleD fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    styleF fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

### 4.2 Equivalent Linearized View (After Feedback Linearization)

Once the inner loop compensates $\mathbf{M}(q)$ and $\mathbf{h}(q,\dot{q})$, the *outer* perception of the plant is **decoupled, unit-mass double integrators** — one per joint. The design problem collapses to choosing a PD law.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Joint i Desired qdi] --> B1[Summing Junction Minus]
    G1[Actual qi Feedback] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Error ei]
    C1 --> D1[Proportional Gain Kpi]
    C1 --> E1[Derivative Gain Kdi]
    H1[edoti] --> E1
    D1 --> F1[Summing Junction Plus]
    E1 --> F1
    I1[Feedforward qddoti] --> F1
    F1 --> J1[Auxiliary Input ai]
    J1 --> K1[Linear Plant 1 over s squared]
    K1 --> L1[Actual Joint Position qi]
    L1 --> G1
    L1 --> H1
    styleK1 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    styleJ1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    styleA1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD
```

### 4.3 Signal-Flow View of Error Dynamics

The closed-loop characteristic equation $\ddot{e}_i + K_{d_i}\dot{e}_i + K_{p_i}e_i = 0$ is the **master stability test** for the entire CTC design. The diagram below illustrates how choosing $K_{p_i}, K_{d_i}$ in canonical form guarantees the desired transient response.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Select Natural Frequency wni] --> B[Kpi = wni squared]
    A --> C[Select Damping Ratio zetai]
    C --> D[Kdi = 2 zetai wni]
    B --> E[Closed Loop Error Dynamics]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Settle Time ts approx 4 over zetai wni}
    E --> G{Overshoot Mp percent = exp negative pi zetai over sqrt 1 minus zetai squared times 100}
    E --> H[Stability Routh Hurwitz Both Gains Positive Definite]
    styleE fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD
    styleH fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Diagram Reading Tip (KTU Exam):** When asked to draw the CTC block diagram, always include the **two distinct feedback paths** — one carrying $(q,\dot{q})$ into the inverse-dynamics model, another carrying the tracking error into the PD block. Examiners explicitly allocate 2 marks for this distinction.
