topics.title: Key Issues for Locomotion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Key Issues for Locomotion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Locomotion** in mobile robotics refers to the mechanism by which an articulated, multi-body system (the robot) generates controlled motion through its environment using internal actuators. The **key issues for locomotion** encompass the fundamental engineering problems that govern how a robot physically moves, including: stability against tipping, energy efficiency of the gait cycle, degrees of freedom of the appendages, contact mechanics with the terrain, and the geometry of the support polygon.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus outcomes, this topic directly maps to **CO4 (Analyze locomotion mechanisms and design considerations for mobile robots)** and addresses the foundational constraints that differentiate successful autonomous ground vehicles from stationary manipulators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition to Memorize:**
> "Locomotion is the process of causing a robot to move through its environment by deforming the robot's shape in a controlled manner, or by generating propulsive forces through cyclic interactions with the ground or surrounding medium."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview
Think of locomotion as **the engineering problem of "pushing yourself off the ground in a controlled way"**.

Imagine you are standing on a slippery ice surface. Now imagine you need to walk across a room. To do so, you must:
1. **Push** against the ground (the ground pushes back — Newton's 3rd Law).
2. Keep your **center of mass** over your feet so you don't fall (stability).
3. Coordinate your **limbs** in a rhythm (gait).
4. Do all this without wasting too much **energy**.

A robot faces the *exact same four challenges*. The only difference is the robot uses motors, joints, wheels, or legs instead of human muscles and bones.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> There is no single "best" locomotion method. The choice depends on the **terrain** (smooth floor vs rocky hill), the **task** (precise positioning vs fast travel), and the **energy budget** (battery life). This is why KTU tests you on comparing legged vs. wheeled systems.

### 1.3 The Five Fundamental Issues for Locomotion

| # | Issue | Engineering Question |
|---|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Stability** | How many contact points are needed to keep the robot upright? |
| 2 | **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** | How many independent joint movements does each leg/wheel need? |
| 3 | **Contact Forces & Friction** | Can the wheels/legs grip the surface without slipping? |
| 4 | **Energy Efficiency** | What is the metabolic or electrical cost per meter traveled? |
| 5 | **Terrain Adaptability** | Can the robot handle obstacles, steps, and uneven ground? |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU Frequently Tested)

- **Stability Margin (SM):** Shortest distance from the projected Center of Gravity (COG) to the boundary of the support polygon. Unit: **meters (m)**.
- **Specific Resistance (SR):** Energy consumed per unit weight per unit distance. Unit: dimensionless.
- **Number of legs (n):** Directly affects static stability. $n \geq 3$ required for static walking.
- **Stride Length (L):** Distance traveled per gait cycle. Unit: **meters (m)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Three Pillars of Locomotion Theory

#### Pillar 1: Support Polygon and Static Stability

A **support polygon** is the convex hull formed by the contact points (feet/wheels) of the robot on the ground. For a robot to be **statically stable** (not fall over without active control), the vertical projection of its **Center of Gravity (COG)** must lie strictly *inside* this support polygon.

**Why this matters (The "Why"):**
- A standing human on two feet has a small support polygon (just the area between the two feet).
- This is why humans must continuously adjust — we are *dynamically* stable, not statically stable.
- A four-legged table is statically stable because its COG projection always lies within the rectangle of four leg contacts.

**The "How":**
1. Determine the coordinates of all ground contact points.
2. Compute the convex hull of these points.
3. Project the COG vertically downward onto the ground plane.
4. Use the **point-in-polygon test** to verify the projection lies inside.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite Concept:** Static stability requires **$n \geq 3$ legs** because a polygon needs at least 3 non-collinear points to enclose an area.

#### Pillar 2: Gaits and Leg Coordination

A **gait** is the cyclic, coordinated pattern of leg lifting (swing phase) and pushing (stance phase). The duty factor $\beta$ is the fraction of a gait cycle that a given leg spends on the ground:

$$\beta = \frac{T_{stance}}{T_{cycle}}$$

where $T_{cycle}$ is the total time for one complete leg cycle, and $T_{stance}$ is the time spent in ground contact.

- If $\beta > 0.5$: **Statically stable gait** (at least 3 legs on the ground at all times — like a slow-walking quadruped).
- If $\beta = 0.5$: **Critically stable gait** (alternating tripods, like an insect).
- If $\beta < 0.5$: **Dynamically stable gait** (running, galloping — relies on momentum).

#### Pillar 3: Energy Efficiency and Specific Resistance

The **Specific Resistance (SR)** is a dimensionless metric comparing locomotion efficiency across vastly different robot sizes:

$$SR = \frac{P}{M \cdot g \cdot v}$$

where:
- $P$ = Power consumption in **Watts (W)**
- $M$ = Robot mass in **kilograms (kg)**
- $g$ = Gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $v$ = Forward velocity in **m/s**

Lower SR means more efficient locomotion. Walking robots typically have SR between **0.1 and 0.5**; well-designed wheeled robots can achieve SR as low as **0.05**.

### 2.2 Locomotion Categories (KTU Classification)

| Category | Mechanism | Key Advantage | Key Disadvantage |
|----------|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Wheeled** | Rolling contact | Energy efficient, simple control | Poor on rough terrain |
| **Legged** | Discrete ground contacts | Excellent terrain adaptability | Complex control, high energy cost |
| **Tracked** | Continuous belt | High traction, good on soft ground | Heavy, slow |
| **Sliding/Snake** | Whole-body undulation | Highly maneuverable in tight spaces | Very slow, high friction |
| **Hopping** | Impulsive ground reaction | Can clear large obstacles | Extremely difficult to control |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Units | KTU Use Case |
|---------|-------------|-------|--------------|
| $\beta = T_{stance} / T_{cycle}$ | Duty factor | dimensionless | Gait classification |
| $SR = P / (M g v)$ | Specific resistance | dimensionless | Efficiency comparison |
| $L = 2 \cdot r$ | Stride length (one-wheel rotation) | meters | Wheel design |
| $N_{stable} \geq 3$ | Static stability leg count | legs | Stability analysis |
| $F_{friction} \leq \mu \cdot N$ | Coulomb friction limit | Newtons | Wheel slip analysis |
| $\omega_{max} = v_{max} / r$ | Max wheel angular velocity | rad/s | Motor selection |
| $P_{climb} = M g v \sin\theta$ | Power to climb incline | Watts | Slope negotiation |
| $COG = \sum m_i \vec{r}_i / \sum m_i$ | Center of gravity | meters | Stability margin |

**Real-world Engineering Utility:**
These formulas are used in:
- **Search and rescue robots** (locomotion on rubble — legged systems).
- **Autonomous warehouse robots** (wheeled — Amazon Kiva robots).
- **Planetary rovers** (NASA's Mars rovers use rocker-bogie, a 6-wheeled legged-wheeled hybrid).
- **Military reconnaissance** (Boston Dynamics' quadrupeds like Spot).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Specific Resistance (SR) — Energy Efficiency of Locomotion

We want a metric that is **independent of robot size**, so we can compare a 1 kg desktop robot to a 1000 kg lunar rover.

**Step 1: Define power consumption.**
The electrical power consumed by the robot to maintain forward motion at constant velocity $v$ is $P$ (in Watts).

**Step 2: Recognize that useful work is against gravity over a horizontal distance.**
The work done to move mass $M$ a distance $d$ is:
$$W = M g d$$

This represents the *minimum* useful work (just lifting the robot's weight across the reference plane).

**Step 3: Form a ratio of power input to useful work rate.**
The rate of doing useful work is:
$$\dot{W}_{useful} = M g v$$

The specific resistance is the ratio of input power to the rate of useful work:
$$SR = \frac{P_{input}}{M g v}$$

**Step 4: Verify dimensional consistency.**
- Numerator: $[P] = W = J/s = kg \cdot m^2 / s^3$
- Denominator: $[M g v] = kg \cdot (m/s^2) \cdot (m/s) = kg \cdot m^2 / s^3$
- Result: **dimensionless** ✓

**Step 5: Physical interpretation.**
If $SR = 0$, the robot is 100% efficient (impossible in practice). If $SR = 1$, the robot burns as much energy as if it were simply lifting its own weight straight up at the same vertical rate. Real robots have $SR$ in the range 0.05 to 2.0.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stability Margin for a Quadruped Robot

Consider a rectangular quadruped robot with four legs at the corners of a rectangle of width $W$ and length $L$. The COG is at the geometric center (assume uniform mass distribution).

**Step 1: Identify the support polygon vertices.**
The four contact points in the ground plane (x, y) are:
- $(0, 0)$, $(L, 0)$, $(0, W)$, $(L, W)$

**Step 2: Determine COG projection.**
The vertical projection of the COG onto the ground is the center of the rectangle:
$$\vec{r}_{COG} = \left(\frac{L}{2}, \frac{W}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3: Compute the stability margin.**
The support polygon is a rectangle. The shortest distance from the COG to the boundary is the perpendicular distance to the nearest edge.

For a rectangle of dimensions $L \times W$, the minimum distance from the center to an edge is:
$$SM = \min\left(\frac{L}{2}, \frac{W}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Generalize to an n-legged robot with a regular support polygon.**
For a regular n-gon support polygon with circumradius $R$ (distance from center to vertex), the inradius (distance from center to edge) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{in} &= R \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{n}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

The stability margin equals this inradius.

**Step 5: Numerical example for KTU.**
A quadruped ($n = 4$) with leg span giving a circumradius of $R = 0.5$ m has:

$$SM = 0.5 \times \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = 0.5 \times 0.707 = 0.354 \text{ m}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Legged vs. Wheeled Trade-offs

| Criterion | Wheeled (3+ wheels) | Legged (4+ legs) |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Static stability** | Excellent (always) | Requires gait planning |
| **Energy efficiency** | Best (SR ≈ 0.05) | Poor (SR ≈ 0.5) |
| **Obstacle clearance** | Limited to wheel radius | Can step over tall obstacles |
| **Control complexity** | Low | High |
| **Discrete terrain** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Speed** | High | Moderate |
| **Mechanical complexity** | Simple | Complex (many joints) |
| **Cost** | Low | High |

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Wheel Climbing Ability

A wheel of radius $r$ can climb a step of height $h$ only if $h \leq r$. Derivation:

**Step 1: Geometric constraint.**
For the wheel to roll over the edge of a step, the line connecting the wheel's center to the corner of the step must be at an angle $\leq 90°$ from vertical. This requires:

$$h \leq r$$

**Step 2: Active climbing with leg/wheel coordination.**
If a leg is used to lift the wheel, the required torque at the wheel hub to lift the robot's weight $W = Mg$ over a step of height $h$ is:

$$\tau = W \cdot \sqrt{r^2 - (r-h)^2} = Mg\sqrt{2rh - h^2}$$

**Step 3: Power requirement at climbing velocity $v_{climb}$.**
$$P_{climb} = \tau \cdot \omega = Mg\sqrt{2rh - h^2} \cdot \frac{v_{climb}}{r}$$

This shows why climbing is energy-expensive and why large wheels are preferred for rough terrain.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Locomotion Taxonomy Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    A[LOCOMOTION SYSTEMS] --> B[Rolling Contact]

    A --> D[Legged Contact]
    A --> E[Hybrid Systems]
    
    B --> B1[Single Wheel]
    B --> B2[Multi-Wheel]
    B --> B3[Tracked]
    
    C --> C1[Whole Body Sliding]
    C --> C2[Snake-like Undulation]
    
    D --> D1[Bipedal 2 legs]
    D --> D2[Quadruped 4 legs]
    D --> D3[Hexapod 6 legs]
    D --> D4[Octopod 8 legs]
    
    E --> E1[Wheel-Leg Hybrid]
    E --> E2[Active Suspension]
    E --> E3[Rocker Bogie]
    
    D2 --> F{Stability Test}
    F --> F1[Static: COG inside support polygon]
    F --> F2[Dynamic: ZMP inside support polygon]
    
    D3 --> G[GAIT PATTERNS]

```

```mermaid

    A1 -->|Low| F1[Static Stability]

    C1 -->|Medium| F1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the terms "support polygon" and "static stability margin" in the context of legged locomotion. State the minimum number of legs required for a robot to be statically stable.*

**Model Answer:**
The **support polygon** is defined as the convex hull formed by connecting all the ground contact points (feet) of the robot on the supporting surface. A robot is **statically stable** when the vertical projection of its center of gravity (COG) lies strictly inside this support polygon.

The **static stability margin** is the shortest Euclidean distance from the COG projection to the nearest edge of the support polygon, measured along the ground plane. Mathematically:

$$SM = \min_{i} \left\| \vec{r}_{COG} - \vec{p}_i \right\|$$

where $\vec{p}_i$ are the vertices of the support polygon.

**Minimum number of legs required for static stability is 3 (three)**, because a polygon requires at least three non-collinear points to enclose a non-zero area within which the COG can be located. A 2-legged (bipedal) robot cannot achieve static stability — it must rely on dynamic balancing (like humans).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the concept of "duty factor" in a legged robot's gait. What is the significance of $\beta = 0.5$?*

**Model Answer:**
The **duty factor ($\beta$)** is defined as the fraction of a complete gait cycle for which a particular leg remains in ground contact (stance phase):

$$\beta = \frac{T_{stance}}{T_{cycle}}$$

where $T_{stance}$ is the time the foot is on the ground, and $T_{cycle}$ is the total time for one full periodic leg motion (swing + stance).

**Significance of $\beta = 0.5$:** When $\beta = 0.5$, each leg spends exactly half its time in stance and half in swing. For an even number of legs, this enables the **alternating tripod gait** (used by insects), where the legs are divided into two groups of three, with one group's three legs in stance while the other group's three legs are in swing. This gait is **critically stable** — any faster and the robot would tip.

For $\beta > 0.5$, the gait is **statically stable** (at least three legs always on the ground). For $\beta < 0.5$, the gait is **dynamically stable** (running).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Full 14-Mark ESE Pattern

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the five key issues that must be addressed when designing the locomotion system of a mobile robot. Discuss how each issue affects the choice between wheeled and legged configurations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A quadruped robot has its four feet at coordinates $(0,0)$, $(0.6, 0)$, $(0, 0.4)$, and $(0.6, 0.4)$ meters, with the center of gravity located at the geometric center. Calculate: (i) the support polygon area, (ii) the static stability margin, and (iii) whether the robot is statically stable. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Five Key Issues (7 marks):**

The five key issues for locomotion are:

1. **Stability:** Wheeled robots are inherently stable (3+ wheels define a polygon with COG inside). Legged robots need careful gait planning. For rough terrain, legged systems are preferred despite stability challenges.

2. **Energy Efficiency:** Wheeled systems have lower Specific Resistance (SR $\approx 0.05$) because rolling friction is small. Legged systems have higher SR (0.3–0.5) due to repeated acceleration/deceleration of leg masses. For long missions, wheels win.

3. **Maneuverability:** Legged robots can step sideways, climb stairs, and cross gaps. Wheeled robots are limited to smooth surfaces. For indoor human environments, legged robots are more practical.

4. **Mechanical Complexity:** A wheel needs 1 actuator (drive) and optionally 1 for steering. Each leg needs 2–3 actuators (hip, knee, ankle). For cost-sensitive applications, wheels are chosen.

5. **Terrain Adaptability:** Legged robots can choose discrete foot placement, avoiding obstacles. Wheeled robots must roll over obstacles (limited to wheel radius). For unstructured outdoor environments, legged or hybrid systems (like Mars rovers' rocker-bogie) excel.

| Issue | Wheeled Advantage | Legged Advantage |
|-------|-------------------|------------------|
| Stability | ✓ Always stable | — Needs planning |
| Energy | ✓ Low SR | — High SR |
| Terrain | — Limited to wheel r | ✓ Steps over |
| Complexity | ✓ Simple | — Many actuators |
| Speed | ✓ Fast | — Slower |

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** Four feet at $A(0,0)$, $B(0.6, 0)$, $C(0, 0.4)$, $D(0.6, 0.4)$ m. COG at geometric center.

**(i) Support Polygon Area:**

The four points form a rectangle of length $L = 0.6$ m and width $W = 0.4$ m.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{polygon} &= L \times W \\
&= 0.6 \times 0.4 \\
&= 0.24 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Static Stability Margin:**

The COG is at the geometric center:
$$\vec{r}_{COG} = \left(\frac{0.6}{2}, \frac{0.4}{2}\right) = (0.3, 0.2) \text{ m}$$

The shortest distance from $(0.3, 0.2)$ to the nearest edge:
- Distance to edge $y = 0$: $0.2$ m
- Distance to edge $y = 0.4$: $0.2$ m
- Distance to edge $x = 0$: $0.3$ m
- Distance to edge $x = 0.6$: $0.3$ m

$$SM = \min(0.2, 0.3) = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Static Stability Verification:**

Since the COG projection $(0.3, 0.2)$ lies strictly inside the rectangle $[0, 0.6] \times [0, 0.4]$, and the stability margin is positive (0.2 m), **the robot IS statically stable**.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Compare wheeled and legged locomotion systems based on: (i) stability, (ii) energy efficiency, (iii) terrain adaptability, and (iv) mechanical complexity. Justify your answer with suitable examples. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 6-legged (hexapod) robot is walking with a tripod gait. Each leg has a stride length of 0.3 m. Calculate the time taken to traverse a distance of 10 meters if the gait cycle period is 2 seconds. Also, find the duty factor if each leg is in stance phase for 1.2 seconds. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks):**

A detailed comparison is presented in the table from Section 3.3. The justifications are:

- **Stability:** Wheeled (3+ wheels) is statically stable by default; legged systems need coordinated gait. Example: A 3-wheeled differential drive robot is stable without any control.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Rolling friction is much lower than the impact loading in legged systems. Example: A Mars rover's rocker-bogie system uses wheels for 99% energy efficiency.
- **Terrain Adaptability:** Legged systems can pick discrete foot placements. Example: Boston Dynamics' Spot (quadruped) climbs stairs that a wheeled robot cannot.
- **Mechanical Complexity:** Each leg needs 2–3 DOF (3 DOF minimum for full 3D foot placement); a wheel needs 1–2 DOF. Example: A simple differential drive car has 2 motors; Spot has 12.

**Part (b) — Hexapod Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** Stride length $L_s = 0.3$ m, Gait cycle $T_{cycle} = 2$ s, Total distance $D = 10$ m, Stance time $T_{stance} = 1.2$ s.

**(i) Time to traverse 10 meters:**

Number of strides required:
$$N = \frac{D}{L_s} = \frac{10}{0.3} = 33.33$$

Since the robot completes one stride per gait cycle, the total time is:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= N \times T_{cycle} \\
&= 33.33 \times 2 \\
&= 66.67 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Duty factor:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{T_{stance}}{T_{cycle}} \\
&= \frac{1.2}{2.0} \\
&= 0.6
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\beta = 0.6 > 0.5$, this is a **statically stable gait** (at least 3 legs on the ground at all times).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT confuse static stability margin with the support polygon AREA.** They are completely different — margin is a *distance*, area is in *m²*. Many students write $0.24$ m² as the stability margin and lose 2 marks.
> - **For duty factor, remember $\beta > 0.5$ = static, $\beta = 0.5$ = critical, $\beta < 0.5$ = dynamic.** This is a top-3 favorite question.
> - **Always state units in numerical answers.** Writing "SM = 0.2" without "meters" loses 0.5 marks.
> - **For legged robots, do not forget the minimum 3 legs for static stability** — a 2-legged robot is dynamically stable, not statically.
> - **Tripod gait = 6-legged alternating, not 3-legged.** Each "tripod" is a set of 3 legs (2 on one side + 1 in middle, or front-back alternation).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Locomotion** = controlled motion through environment via internal actuators.
- [x] **Five key issues**: stability, DOF, contact friction, energy efficiency, terrain adaptability.
- [x] **Support polygon** = convex hull of ground contact points.
- [x] **Static stability** = COG projection inside support polygon.
- [x] **Minimum 3 legs** required for static stability.
- [x] **Stability Margin (SM)** = shortest distance from COG to polygon edge (in meters).
- [x] **Duty factor $\beta$** = $T_{stance} / T_{cycle}$.
- [x] **$\beta > 0.5$** → statically stable; **$\beta = 0.5$** → critical (tripod); **$\beta < 0.5$** → dynamic (running).
- [x] **Specific Resistance (SR)** = $P / (M g v)$ — dimensionless, lower is better.
- [x] **Wheel radius = max step height** for rolling.
- [x] **Wheeled systems**: more efficient, simpler, less terrain-capable.
- [x] **Legged systems**: more versatile terrain, higher cost, higher energy.
- [x] **Common gaits**: Wave (slow, stable), Tripod (fast, insects), Ripple (medium).
- [x] **ZMP (Zero Moment Point)** = advanced concept where the net moment of ground reaction forces is zero; must lie inside support polygon for dynamic stability.
- [x] **Snake robots** use whole-body undulation; very low speed but high maneuverability.
- [x] **Hybrid systems** (e.g., Mars rovers) combine wheels with active suspension for the best of both worlds.

**High-Yield Formulas to Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam:**

$$\beta = \frac{T_{stance}}{T_{cycle}}, \quad SR = \frac{P}{M g v}, \quad SM = \min_i \|\vec{r}_{COG} - \vec{p}_i\|, \quad h_{step} \leq r_{wheel}$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Legged Mobile Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Legged Mobile Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LEGGED MOBILE ROBOT (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *Legged Mobile Robot* is a discrete, articulated locomotion platform that uses a series of mechanical limbs (legs) — each comprising one or more kinematic linkages powered by actuators — to propel itself across a surface. Unlike wheeled or tracked robots, legged robots achieve mobility through a *sequence of foot–ground contact events* (support phases) interspersed with *swing phases* where the leg is repositioned in the air. This produces *gait*, which is the spatiotemporal pattern of leg lifting and placement.

**Key Design Drivers (per KTU Robotics Syllabus):**
- **Number of legs** $n$ (biped $n=2$, quadruped $n=4$, hexapod $n=6$, octopod $n=8$).
- **Degrees of freedom (DOF)** per leg $d$.
- **Total actuators** = $n \times d$ plus payload/head actuators.
- **Stability regime** — static (S) or dynamic (D).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine climbing a staircase in the dark.**
> Each time you step forward, **one leg is in the air** (the *swing leg*) while **the other supports your entire body weight** (the *stance leg*). A legged robot operates on exactly this principle: at any moment, a subset of its legs is pushing against the ground to hold the body up, while the rest are *repositioning* for the next step. The body therefore "walks" through space as a *sequence of stable, balanced postures*, the same way you keep your coffee upright while crossing a cluttered room.

A simple table-driven overview (kept pipe-free for markdown safety):

**Table 1.1 — Intuitive Comparison: Wheeled vs Legged Locomotion**

| Aspect | Wheeled Robot | Legged Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Contact with ground | Continuous (rolling) | Discrete (foot-falls) |
| Terrain adaptability | Low (needs smooth floor) | Very high (stairs, rubble, sand) |
| Mechanical complexity | Low | High (joints + actuators per leg) |
| Energy efficiency on flat terrain | High | Lower |
| Obstacle clearance | Limited by wheel radius | Limited by leg reach and step height |
| Stability | Always stable (3+ wheels) | Conditional on gait and posture |

> [!NOTE]
> **Body Mass of an Adult Human ≈ 70 kg.** A bipedal humanoid must solve the *inverted pendulum* problem in real time to prevent falling — the same problem a unicycle rider faces but with two stacked pendulums (leg + torso).

---

## 1.3 Why Study Legged Robots? (KTU High-Yield Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Rough-terrain mobility** — search-and-rescue, planetary exploration, forestry.
> - **Human-centred environments** — stairs, ladders, doors, narrow corridors.
> - **Mimicking biological locomotion** for biomechanics, prosthetics, and exoskeletons.
> - **Energy recovery** through elastic leg compliance (e.g., Boston Dynamics' hydraulic quadrupeds).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Decomposition of a Legged Robot

A legged robot is decomposed into three layers:

1. **Body (chassis / torso)** — the rigid mass $m_b$ that carries sensors, payload, batteries. Its pose is described by $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ in world frame.
2. **Legs** — each leg is a serial chain of rigid links connected by *revolute* (R) or *prismatic* (P) joints. Common leg kinematic configurations:
   - **1-DOF leg** — a single prismatic or revolute joint (simplest hopper / Klann linkage).
   - **2-DOF leg** — a pantograph or planar RR linkage (good for planar walkers).
   - **3-DOF leg** — RR:R or RR:PRR style (mammalian-inspired, e.g., BigDog's leg).
   - **4+ DOF leg** — fully 3-D spatial positioning + ankle orientation (humanoid legs in ASIMO, Atlas).
3. **Feet / end-effectors** — point contacts or flat pads that determine the *support polygon* vertices.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"More legs ⇒ easier static stability, but harder to coordinate."*

---

## 2.2 Stability: The Two Fundamental Regimes

### 2.2.1 Static Stability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A robot is *statically stable* at an instant if, with all motors frozen, it does not topple. Equivalently, the vertical projection of its **Center of Gravity (CoG)** onto the ground must lie **inside the convex hull of its support feet** (the *support polygon*).

**Stability Margin (SM):**
The shortest distance from the CoG projection to any edge of the support polygon.

$$
S M = \min_{i=1}^{N_f} d\left(P_{CoG}, e_i\right)
$$

where $N_f$ is the number of feet in contact, $P_{CoG}$ is the foot-projection of the CoG, and $e_i$ is the $i$-th edge of the convex hull.

### 2.2.2 Dynamic Stability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A robot is *dynamically stable* if it does not topple over time, even if at some instants it is statically unstable. Bipeds running and quadrupeds trotting fall in this regime — the **Zero-Moment Point (ZMP)** must remain inside the support polygon.

**ZMP Criterion (Vukobratović, 1969):**
For planar motion,

$$
x_{ZMP} = x - \frac{z \cdot \ddot{x}}{\ddot{z} + g}, \qquad
y_{ZMP} = y - \frac{z \cdot \ddot{y}}{\ddot{z} + g}
$$

For stability: $P_{ZMP} \in \text{SupportPolygon}$.

---

## 2.3 Gaits — The Heart of Legged Locomotion

> [!NOTE]
> **Gait = the temporal sequence in which each leg switches between *swing phase* (lifted) and *stance phase* (pushing on the ground).**

Two canonical gaits:

- **Symmetric gait** — leg pairs move in mirror symmetry (e.g., trot, pace, bound, gallop).
- **Asymmetric / wave gait** — used by insects (e.g., stick insect wave gait).

### 2.3.1 Hexapod Gaits (n = 6)

| Gait | Legs down at any time | Stability | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wave gait** | $\geq 3$ | Always statically stable | Slow, careful walking |
| **Ripple gait** | $3$–$4$ | Statically stable | Moderate speed |
| **Tripod gait** | $3$ (alternating triangles) | Marginally stable | Fast walking, running |

### 2.3.2 Quadruped Gaits (n = 4)

| Gait | Footfall sequence | Speed |
|---|---|---|
| **Crawl / Walk** | 3 legs down always | Slowest, max SM |
| **Pace** | LF+RF then LH+RH | Medium (camels, horses) |
| **Trot** | Diagonal pairs (LF+RH, RF+LH) | Medium-fast (most dogs) |
| **Bound** | Front pair then hind pair | Fast (rabbits, hares) |
| **Gallop** | Asymmetric rapid bound | Fastest (horses) |

### 2.3.3 Biped Gaits (n = 2)

- **Static walking** — both feet on ground during double-support phase (ZMP always inside polygon).
- **Dynamic walking** — passive dynamic walkers (McGeer's compass-gait robot) with no actuation; gravity + geometry produce stable gait.
- **Running** — flight phase where *no foot touches the ground*; ZMP concept breaks; **Spring-Loaded Inverted Pendulum (SLIP)** model applies.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Pipe-Safe)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula you must memorise for Module-4 university questions. No vertical pipes are used inside cells; absolute values are written as $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ for full markdown compatibility.

**Table 2.1 — Essential KTU Legged-Robot Formulas**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static stability margin | $SM = \min_{i} d(P_{CoG}, e_i)$ | metres; $\ge 0$ means stable |
| 2 | CoG of a multi-body | $P_{CoG} = \dfrac{\sum_i m_i \, r_i}{\sum_i m_i}$ | metres; vector form |
| 3 | ZMP x-coordinate (planar) | $x_{ZMP} = x - \dfrac{z \, \ddot{x}}{\ddot{z} + g}$ | metres; $g \approx 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ |
| 4 | ZMP y-coordinate (planar) | $y_{ZMP} = y - \dfrac{z \, \ddot{y}}{\ddot{z} + g}$ | metres |
| 5 | Stride length | $L_s = 2 \, \lambda$ | metres; $\lambda$ = step length |
| 6 | Duty factor (leg $j$) | $\beta_j = \dfrac{t_{\text{stance},j}}{T}$ | $0 < \beta_j \le 1$ |
| 7 | Relative phase | $\phi_j = t_{\text{lift},j}/T$ | $0 \le \phi_j < 1$ |
| 8 | Average forward speed | $v = L_s / T$ | m/s |
| 9 | Minimum legs for static stability | $N_{\min} = 3$ (on flat ground) | discrete |
| 10 | Number of actuators (knee joint) | $A = n \cdot d$ | integer; $n$ legs $\times$ $d$ DOF |
| 11 | Static stability of $n$-legged robot | Stable iff $n \ge 3$ in **non-collinear** stance | combinatorial |
| 12 | Energy stored in compliant leg (SLIP) | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} k \, \Delta^2$ | joules; $k$ = leg stiffness |
| 13 | Leg tip position (2-D RR leg) | $x = l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12}$, $z = l_1 s_1 + l_2 s_{12}$ | metres |
| 14 | Pitch stability of biped | $\ddot{\theta} \le g/L_{com} \cdot \sin\theta$ | rad/s$^2$ |
| 15 | Workspace of 2-DOF leg | Annulus, $r \in [\lvert l_1 - l_2 \rvert, l_1 + l_2]$ | metres |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Robot | Type | DOF/leg | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Honda ASIMO | Biped | 6 | Humanoid assistance |
| Boston Dynamics BigDog | Quadruped | 3 | Military load carriage |
| Boston Dynamics Spot | Quadruped | 3 | Industrial inspection |
| Boston Dynamics Atlas | Biped | 6 + arms | Search-and-rescue |
| MIT Cheetah | Quadruped | 3 | High-speed research |
| NASA's ATHLETE | Hexapod | 6 | Lunar/planetary roving |
| Dante II | 8-leg walker | 3 | Volcanic exploration |
| Plustech Walking Forest Harvester | 4/6-leg | hydraulic | Forestry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always state the **duty factor $\beta$** when describing a gait; many students omit it and lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step is written out explicitly. Multi-line equations use `aligned` blocks. Each derivation is preceded by a brief English explanation of *why* the step is taken.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CoG of a Multi-Rigid-Body Legged Robot

**Context:** A quadruped robot consists of one body of mass $m_b$ and four legs each of mass $m_\ell$ uniformly distributed. Find the height of the overall centre of mass.

**Step 1 — Set up the system.**
Let the body centre be at height $h_b$ and each leg's centre of mass be at height $h_\ell$ (the leg's mid-length). The total mass is

$$
M = m_b + 4\, m_\ell
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CoG formula** from Table 2.1, row 2:

$$
P_{CoG} = \frac{\sum_i m_i \, r_i}{\sum_i m_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the masses and heights** (assuming symmetric posture so $x, y$ components cancel):

$$
h_{CoG} = \frac{m_b \, h_b + 4 \, m_\ell \, h_\ell}{m_b + 4 \, m_\ell}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** Let $m_b = 30$ kg, $m_\ell = 2$ kg, $h_b = 0.6$ m, $h_\ell = 0.25$ m:

$$
h_{CoG} = \frac{30 \cdot 0.6 + 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.25}{30 + 8}
= \frac{18 + 2}{38}
= \frac{20}{38}
= 0.5263 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The body dominates the height because $m_b \gg m_\ell$. Reducing leg mass lowers the CoG, which *increases* static stability against tipping.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Static Stability Margin for a 3-Legged Tripod

**Context:** A hexapod robot is in a *tripod gait* where legs 1, 3, 5 form one triangle on the ground. Compute the stability margin for a CoG projection at point $P = (x_p, y_p)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the support triangle vertices** (in the body frame):

$$
A = (a, 0, 0), \quad B = \left(-\tfrac{a}{2}, \tfrac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{2}, 0\right), \quad C = \left(-\tfrac{a}{2}, -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{2}, 0\right)
$$

where $a$ is the triangle's side length. (This is an equilateral triangle of side $a$.)

**Step 2 — Compute the equations of the three edges.** General line through points $P_1, P_2$ in 2D:

$$
(y_2 - y_1)x - (x_2 - x_1)y + (x_2 - x_1)y_1 - (y_2 - y_1)x_1 = 0
$$

For edge $AB$ with $A = (a, 0)$, $B = (-a/2, \sqrt{3}\,a/2)$:

$$
\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{2} - 0\right) x - \left(-\frac{a}{2} - a\right) y + \ldots = 0
$$

Working out:

$$
\frac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{2}\, x + \frac{3a}{2}\, y - \frac{\sqrt{3}\,a^2}{2} = 0
$$

Dividing by $a/2$:

$$
\sqrt{3}\, x + 3\, y - \sqrt{3}\, a = 0
$$

Perpendicular distance from $P = (x_p, y_p)$ to this line:

$$
d_{AB} = \frac{\lvert \sqrt{3}\, x_p + 3\, y_p - \sqrt{3}\, a \rvert}{\sqrt{3 + 9}} = \frac{\lvert \sqrt{3}\, x_p + 3\, y_p - \sqrt{3}\, a \rvert}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\lvert \sqrt{3}\, x_p + 3\, y_p - \sqrt{3}\, a \rvert}{2\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — By symmetry, the other two edges yield similar expressions** $d_{BC}, d_{CA}$.

**Step 4 — Stability margin** is the minimum of the three:

$$
SM = \min\left(d_{AB},\, d_{BC},\, d_{CA}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Special case: $P$ is the centroid.** The centroid of the equilateral triangle is

$$
P_C = \left(0,\, 0\right)
$$

Substitute into $d_{AB}$:

$$
d_{AB} = \frac{\lvert -\sqrt{3}\, a \rvert}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\, a}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{a}{2}
$$

By symmetry, all three distances equal $a/2$, so

$$
SM = \frac{a}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** For an equilateral support triangle of side $a$, the maximum achievable static stability margin is $a/(2\sqrt{3})$ from a *centroid-ward* safe zone. Always check the **sign** inside the absolute value to confirm the CoG projection lies *inside* the polygon.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Two-Link (RR) Leg Inverse Kinematics

**Context:** A planar 2-DOF leg has link lengths $l_1$ (hip-to-knee) and $l_2$ (knee-to-foot). The foot's desired Cartesian position is $(x_f, z_f)$ in the hip frame. Derive the joint angles $\theta_1$ (hip) and $\theta_2$ (knee).

**Step 1 — Write the forward-kinematic map.**

$$
x_f = l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$
$$
z_f = l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Square and add the two equations.**

$$
x_f^{\,2} + z_f^{\,2} = l_1^{\,2} + l_2^{\,2} + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\cos\theta_2$ (the knee angle).**

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x_f^{\,2} + z_f^{\,2} - l_1^{\,2} - l_2^{\,2}}{2 l_1 l_2}
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left(\frac{x_f^{\,2} + z_f^{\,2} - l_1^{\,2} - l_2^{\,2}}{2 l_1 l_2}\right)
$$

The $\pm$ corresponds to **elbow-up** (+) and **elbow-down** (-) configurations.

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta_1$ using the atan2 formulation.**

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(z_f, x_f) - \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(l_2 \sin\theta_2,\; l_1 + l_2 \cos\theta_2\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.** $l_1 = 0.4$ m, $l_2 = 0.4$ m, foot target $(x_f, z_f) = (0.6, -0.3)$ m.

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{0.36 + 0.09 - 0.16 - 0.16}{2 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.4} = \frac{0.13}{0.32} = 0.40625
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \arccos(0.40625) \approx 1.151 \text{ rad} \approx 65.95^{\circ}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(-0.3, 0.6) - \operatorname{atan2}(0.4 \sin 1.151,\ 0.4 + 0.4 \cdot 0.40625)
$$

Computing inner terms:

$$
\operatorname{atan2}(-0.3, 0.6) = -0.4636 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\sin 1.151 = 0.9138,\quad l_2 \sin\theta_2 = 0.4 \cdot 0.9138 = 0.3655
$$

$$
l_1 + l_2 \cos\theta_2 = 0.4 + 0.4 \cdot 0.40625 = 0.5625
$$

$$
\operatorname{atan2}(0.3655,\ 0.5625) = 0.5759 \text{ rad}
$$

Therefore

$$
\theta_1 = -0.4636 - 0.5759 = -1.0395 \text{ rad} \approx -59.55^{\circ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Plug back into forward kinematics — you obtain $(0.6, -0.3)$ m. (This is a recommended self-check step in the KTU answer sheet.)

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — ZMP for a Biped in Single-Support Phase

**Context:** During single-support, only one foot touches the ground. The biped's mass $M$ acts at CoG $(x, z)$. Derive the ZMP $x_{ZMP}$.

**Step 1 — Start from the rotational moment equilibrium about the ZMP.**

The ZMP is defined as the point on the ground where the *net moment of the inertial and gravitational forces is zero*.

**Step 2 — Sum moments about the ZMP (planar, sagittal plane).**

$$
M (\ddot{z} + g) \, x_{ZMP} = M (\ddot{z} + g) \, x - M \ddot{x}\, z
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $M (\ddot{z} + g)$ (assume $\ddot{z} + g \neq 0$ on ground).**

$$
x_{ZMP} = x - \frac{z \, \ddot{x}}{\ddot{z} + g}
$$

This is the ZMP formula in Table 2.1, row 3. The same procedure in the lateral plane yields $y_{ZMP}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability requirement:** $\;x_{ZMP} \in [x_{\text{ankle,front}},\, x_{\text{ankle,back}}]$ for a flat foot.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Legged Robot Architectures

**Table 3.1 — KTU Comparison Matrix (Pipe-Safe, Markdown Stable)**

| Parameter | Biped (2 legs) | Quadruped (4 legs) | Hexapod (6 legs) | Octopod (8 legs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static stability | No (dynamic only) | Marginal (crawl gait only) | Excellent (3+ feet) | Excellent |
| Typical DOF per leg | 5–6 | 3 | 3 | 2–3 |
| Total actuators | 10–12 | 12 | 18 | 16–24 |
| Redundancy after losing leg | 0 — falls | Tolerable | Tolerable | High |
| Typical speed | 1.0 m/s walk | 1.5 m/s trot | 0.5 m/s wave | 0.3 m/s |
| Power efficiency | Moderate (humanoid) | High (gallop) | Moderate | Lower |
| Control complexity | Very high | High | Moderate | High |
| Examples | ASIMO, Atlas | BigDog, Spot | ATHLETE | Dante II |
| Best terrain | Stairs, urban | Mixed outdoor | Rough/debris | Sub-terranean |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid blocks obey the Node Identifier Alpha Rule (purely alphanumeric + letter prefix) and Label Formatting Restriction (no markdown/HTML inside double-quoted labels). Reserved keywords are never used as node names.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Legged Locomotion System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Planner / Task Layer] --> B[Gait Pattern Generator]
    B --> C[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    C --> D[Joint Trajectory Interpolator]
    D --> E[Servo / Hydraulic Driver]
    E --> F[Mechanical Leg Chain RR or RRR]
    F --> G[Foot Ground Contact Sensor]
    G --> H[Force Torque Feedback Unit]
    H --> I[Balance Controller ZMP or CoM]
    I --> B
    J[IMU Inertial Measurement Unit] --> I
    K[CoG Estimator] --> I
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **planner** sends high-level velocity / heading commands.
- The **gait generator** decides which legs are in swing vs stance.
- The **inverse-kinematics solver** maps foot targets to joint angles.
- The **balance controller** continuously adjusts body posture using ZMP / CoM feedback.
- Loop closes via IMU + CoG + ground contact feedback.

---

## 4.2 Hexapod Tripod Gait — Footfall State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Tripod1
    Tripod1: Tripod 1 Down Legs 1 3 5 On Ground
    Tripod1 --> Tripod2: Lift Legs 1 3 5
    Tripod2: Tripod 2 Down Legs 2 4 6 On Ground
    Tripod2 --> Tripod1: Lift Legs 2 4 6
    Tripod1 --> [*]
```

**Legend (raw uppercase, no markdown inside labels):**
- **Tripod 1:** Right-Front, Left-Middle, Right-Hind on ground.
- **Tripod 2:** Left-Front, Right-Middle, Left-Hind on ground.

---

## 4.3 Quadruped Gait Comparison Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Quadruped Gait Footfall Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s
    section Crawl Gait Slow Static
    Leg1 LF :a1, 0, 3
    Leg2 RF :a2, 1, 3
    Leg3 LH :a3, 2, 3
    Leg4 RH :a4, 0, 3
    section Trot Gait Fast Diagonal
    Leg1b LF :b1, 0, 2
    Leg2b RF :b2, 2, 2
    Leg3b LH :b3, 0, 2
    Leg4b RH :b4, 2, 2
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **crawl gait**, at least 3 feet are on the ground at any instant (maximum static stability). In the **trot gait**, alternating diagonal pairs lift together — only 2 feet down per phase (dynamic stability only).

---

## 4.4 ZMP Stability Region Inside the Support Polygon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Polygon[Support Foot Region]
        A1[Foot A Vertex]
        A2[Foot B Vertex]
        A3[Foot C Vertex]
        A4[Foot D Vertex]
        A5[ZMP Stable Zone Interior]
    end
    Polygon --- P[CoG Projection]
    P -. Outside Polygon Means Falling .-> F[TIP OVER]
    P -. Inside Polygon Means Stable .-> S[ROBOT STABLE]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Mantra:** *"Draw the polygon first, plot the CoG projection second, and ask: is the point inside the shape?"*

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Six-Legged Walking Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Central Control CPU] --> B[Gait Scheduler FSM]
    B --> C1[Leg 1 Controller]
    B --> C2[Leg 2 Controller]
    B --> C3[Leg 3 Controller]
    B --> C4[Leg 4 Controller]
    B --> C5[Leg 5 Controller]
    B --> C6[Leg 6 Controller]
    C1 --> D1[Hip Joint Actuator]
    C1 --> D2[Knee Joint Actuator]
    C1 --> D3[Ankle Joint Actuator]
    C2 --> E1[Hip Joint Actuator]
    C2 --> E2[Knee Joint Actuator]
    C2 --> E3[Ankle Joint Actuator]
    C3 --> F1[Hip Joint Actuator]
    C3 --> F2[Knee Joint Actuator]
    C3 --> F3[Ankle Joint Actuator]
    C4 --> G1[Hip Joint Actuator]
    C4 --> G2[Knee Joint Actuator]
    C4 --> G3[Ankle Joint Actuator]
    C5 --> H1[Hip Joint Actuator]
    C5 --> H2[Knee Joint Actuator]
    C5 --> H3[Ankle Joint Actuator]
    C6 --> I1[Hip Joint Actuator]
    C6 --> I2[Knee Joint Actuator]
    C6 --> I3[Ankle Joint Actuator]
    A --> J[Terrain Mapping Sensor LIDAR or Stereo]
    A --> K[IMU Body Posture]
    J --> B
    K --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is a **Fallback Functional Architecture** (per the prompt's safety clause) — it maps the interactions between the high-level controller, per-leg joint controllers, and the sensor feedback loops, instead of attempting a physical 3-D leg drawing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part-A question must be answered in **30–50 words** with a precise definition or formula. No derivations.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]

> **Define *statically stable walking*. What is the minimum number of legs required for a robot to be statically stable on flat ground, and why?**

**Model Answer:**
Statically stable walking is a locomotion regime in which the robot's vertical CoG projection remains *inside the convex hull of its support feet* at **every** instant — even with all motors frozen. The minimum number of legs required on flat ground is **3** (forming a non-collinear triangle), because 1 or 2 non-collinear contacts cannot enclose a 2-D region and would allow tipping. *(Word count: 62)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]

> **What is the *Zero-Moment Point* (ZMP)? State the planar formula for $x_{ZMP}$.**

**Model Answer:**
The ZMP is the point on the ground where the **net moment of inertial and gravitational forces equals zero**. Planar formula:

$$
x_{ZMP} = x - \frac{z\,\ddot{x}}{\ddot{z} + g}
$$

where $(x,z)$ is the CoG position, $\ddot{x},\ddot{z}$ its accelerations, and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. The robot is dynamically stable iff $x_{ZMP}$ lies inside the support polygon.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE convention: each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two alternative choices are provided as per Module 4 internal-choice rule.

---

### ✦ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> **Explain the classification of legged robots based on the number of legs. Compare the stability and applications of biped, quadruped, and hexapod robots with neat sketches.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Classification by number of legs $n$:**

| Class | $n$ | Stability regime | Typical mass |
|---|---|---|---|
| Monopod / Hopper | 1 | Dynamic only | < 5 kg |
| Biped | 2 | Dynamic (ZMP / SLIP) | 30–80 kg |
| Triped | 3 | Marginal static | Rare |
| Quadruped | 4 | Static (crawl) / dynamic (trot) | 30–120 kg |
| Hexapod | 6 | Excellent static | 20–200 kg |
| Octopod | 8 | Redundant, very stable | 50–300 kg |

**2. Comparison:**

- **Biped ($n=2$):** Always *dynamically* stable; no static stability. Uses ZMP and inverted-pendulum models. Best for human environments (stairs, doors). Examples: ASIMO, Atlas.
- **Quadruped ($n=4$):** Statically stable in *crawl* gait (3 feet down), dynamically stable in *trot / pace / gallop*. Balanced trade-off between speed and terrain adaptability. Examples: BigDog, Spot.
- **Hexapod ($n=6$):** Always statically stable in wave, ripple, and tripod gaits. Heavy mechanical complexity (18 actuators) but excellent redundancy. Examples: ATHLETE (NASA), commercial crawlers.

**3. Sketches (verbal description):**
- Biped: two vertical articulated legs beneath a torso with arms.
- Quadruped: four legs in a rectangular footprint.
- Hexapod: six legs in two parallel rows of three (insect-like).

**4. Conclusion:** Number of legs is a *trade-off* between static stability, mechanical complexity, and energetic cost.

*(Marks distribution: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> **A 2-DOF planar leg has link lengths $l_1 = 0.5$ m and $l_2 = 0.5$ m. Using inverse kinematics, find the joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ when the foot tip must reach $(x_f, z_f) = (0.7,\ -0.4)$ m. Take the elbow-up configuration.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the cosine-rule formula** for the knee:

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x_f^{\,2} + z_f^{\,2} - l_1^{\,2} - l_2^{\,2}}{2 l_1 l_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{0.7^2 + (-0.4)^2 - 0.5^2 - 0.5^2}{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5} = \frac{0.49 + 0.16 - 0.25 - 0.25}{0.5} = \frac{0.15}{0.5} = 0.30
$$

**Step 3 — Knee angle:**

$$
\theta_2 = \arccos(0.30) \approx 1.2661 \text{ rad} \approx 72.54^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Hip angle using the atan2 formula:**

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(z_f, x_f) - \operatorname{atan2}(l_2 \sin\theta_2,\ l_1 + l_2 \cos\theta_2)
$$

Compute subterms:

$$
\operatorname{atan2}(-0.4,\, 0.7) = -0.5191 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\sin\theta_2 = \sin(1.2661) = 0.9539
$$

$$
l_2 \sin\theta_2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.9539 = 0.4769
$$

$$
l_1 + l_2 \cos\theta_2 = 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot 0.30 = 0.65
$$

$$
\operatorname{atan2}(0.4769,\, 0.65) = 0.6329 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 5 — Final hip angle:**

$$
\theta_1 = -0.5191 - 0.6329 = -1.1520 \text{ rad} \approx -66.0^{\circ}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\theta_1 \approx -1.152\ \text{rad}\ (-66.0^{\circ})$, $\theta_2 \approx 1.266\ \text{rad}\ (72.5^{\circ})$ (elbow-up).

**Verification (forward kinematics):**

$$
x_f = 0.5\cos(-1.152) + 0.5\cos(-1.152 + 1.266) = 0.5(0.405) + 0.5(0.998) = 0.202 + 0.499 = 0.701\ \text{m}\ \checkmark
$$

$$
z_f = 0.5\sin(-1.152) + 0.5\sin(0.114) = 0.5(-0.914) + 0.5(0.114) = -0.457 + 0.057 = -0.400\ \text{m}\ \checkmark
$$

*(Marks distribution: 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 1)*

---

### ✦ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> **Define the term *gait*. With neat phase diagrams, explain the wave gait, ripple gait, and tripod gait of a six-legged robot. State the duty factor for each.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition:** A *gait* is the temporal and spatial pattern in which a legged robot's legs cycle between *swing* (lifted) and *stance* (in contact) phases.

Let $T$ = gait cycle time, $t_{\text{stance}}$ = time foot is on ground. **Duty factor** $\beta = t_{\text{stance}}/T$.

**2. Wave Gait (slowest, most stable):**
- Legs lift one at a time, from rear to front on each side, in a travelling wave.
- **At any instant, 5 of 6 legs are on the ground.**
- $\beta \approx 5/6 \approx 0.83$.
- Walking speed $v = L_s / T$ is minimum.

**Phase order (legs labelled 1 = front-left, 6 = rear-left):**

$$
1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6 \to 1
$$

(stick insect gait)

**3. Ripple Gait (medium speed):**
- Two waves propagate simultaneously, one on each side, offset by half a cycle.
- **At any instant, 4 of 6 legs are on the ground.**
- $\beta \approx 2/3 \approx 0.67$.

**4. Tripod Gait (fastest, marginally stable):**
- Legs pair into two alternating triangles: $\{1, 3, 5\}$ and $\{2, 4, 6\}$.
- One triangle is in stance while the other is in swing, then they swap.
- **At any instant, 3 of 6 legs are on the ground.**
- $\beta = 0.5$.

**Comparison Table:**

| Gait | Legs down | $\beta$ | Stability | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wave | 5 | 5/6 | Highest | Lowest |
| Ripple | 4 | 2/3 | High | Medium |
| Tripod | 3 | 1/2 | Marginal | Highest |

*(Marks distribution: 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> **A quadruped robot has body mass $m_b = 40$ kg and four identical legs each of mass $m_\ell = 3$ kg. In standing posture, the body CoG is at $h_b = 0.7$ m and the leg CoG (uniform) is at $h_\ell = 0.3$ m. Calculate the height of the overall centre of mass and the static stability margin if the four feet form a rectangle of $0.5\ \text{m} \times 0.8$ m and the CoG projection lies at the rectangle's centre.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total mass.**

$$
M = m_b + 4 m_\ell = 40 + 4 \cdot 3 = 40 + 12 = 52\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — CoG height using** the formula from Derivation 1:

$$
h_{CoG} = \frac{m_b h_b + 4 m_\ell h_\ell}{M} = \frac{40 \cdot 0.7 + 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.3}{52} = \frac{28 + 3.6}{52} = \frac{31.6}{52} \approx 0.6077\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Support polygon = rectangle of $a = 0.8$ m (long side) $\times$ $b = 0.5$ m (short side).**

**Step 4 — CoG projection = rectangle centre.** Distances from centre to the four edges:

- To long edges (length $a$): $b/2 = 0.25$ m.
- To short edges (length $b$): $a/2 = 0.40$ m.

**Step 5 — Static stability margin** is the *minimum* of these:

$$
SM = \min(0.25,\ 0.40) = 0.25\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** A perturbation tipping the robot about the *short-side foot pair* (over the 0.5 m edge) would cause loss of stability after only 0.25 m of horizontal CoG displacement. Improving the SM requires either lowering the CoG (heavier feet, lighter legs) or widening the stance along the short axis.

*(Marks distribution: 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always state units.** A formula without $\text{m}$, $\text{kg}$, or $\text{rad}$ is penalised in KTU 2024 marking schemes.
> 2. **In inverse-kinematics answers, draw the leg diagram** and label elbow-up / elbow-down — many students lose 1–2 marks for ambiguity.
> 3. **For stability questions, plot the support polygon first** and mark the CoG projection as a dot; this single step is worth 1–2 marks in the rubric.
> 4. **Mention the duty factor $\beta$** whenever you describe a gait — omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Do not confuse $g = 9.81$ m/s² with $G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11}$ N·m²/kg²** — students sometimes substitute the gravitational constant; this is a fatal error.
> 6. **In ZMP questions, verify that $x_{ZMP}$ lies *inside* the support polygon**, not merely compute the value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Legged Mobile Robots**

- ☐ **Define** legged robot, gait, support polygon, CoG projection.
- ☐ **Recall:** Minimum legs for static stability on flat ground = **3** (non-collinear).
- ☐ **Differentiate** static vs dynamic stability with one crisp sentence each.
- ☐ **List** the four common hexapod gaits and their duty factors: wave ($\beta=5/6$), ripple ($\beta=2/3$), tripod ($\beta=1/2$).
- ☐ **List** the four common quadruped gaits: crawl, pace, trot, bound/gallop.
- ☐ **Write** the ZMP formula in both $x$ and $y$ directions.
- ☐ **Write** the CoG formula for a multi-rigid-body system.
- ☐ **Derive** the 2-DOF planar leg inverse kinematics — knee via cosine rule, hip via atan2.
- ☐ **State** the SLIP model for running robots (one sentence).
- ☐ **Remember** the trade-off: *more legs ⇒ more stability, more actuators, more coordination cost.*
- ☐ **Examples to memorise:** ASIMO (biped), BigDog (quadruped), ATHLETE (hexapod), Dante II (octopod).
- ☐ **Constant to remember:** $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.
- ☐ **Pitfall to avoid:** Confusing **ZMP** with **CoP** (Centre of Pressure) — ZMP is the *theoretical* zero-moment point; CoP is the *actual* contact pressure centroid; they coincide on rigid, flat contact but diverge on edges.
- ☐ **Energy relation:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}k\Delta^2$ for compliant leg energy storage.
- ☐ **Equation to derive on demand:** Stability margin = minimum perpendicular distance from CoG projection to support polygon edge.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheeled Mobile Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wheeled Mobile Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR)** is a locomotion platform whose motion in a planar workspace is generated by the controlled actuation of one or more wheels in contact with the ground. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEBRT612, Module 4), a WMR is formally defined as a *non-linear, time-varying, holonomic or non-holonomic mechanical system whose configuration is completely described by its pose $(x, y, \theta)$ in a 2D Cartesian reference frame, where $x$ and $y$ are the planar coordinates of a reference point on the robot and $\theta$ is the orientation angle with respect to the global X-axis.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Pose of a WMR:** The instantaneous configuration of a wheeled mobile robot in a 2D plane is denoted as $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^T \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$, where $S^1$ is the unit circle representing angular orientation modulo $2\pi$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **remote-controlled toy car** moving on a smooth floor. The wheels are the only parts touching the ground, and they roll (or sometimes slide) to push the car forward, backward, or around a curve. Now, imagine that instead of a toy, you have a **hospital delivery robot**, a **Mars rover**, or an **automated warehouse tug (AGV)**. They all share the same fundamental idea: wheels convert motor rotation into translation of the body. The engineering challenge is figuring out *how the wheels must spin and steer* so that the robot reaches a desired location with a desired heading. This is precisely the science of **wheeled mobile robot kinematics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Wheeled Mobile Robots covers wheel design, wheel configuration, kinematic constraints, instantaneous center of rotation (ICR), maneuverability, and forward/inverse kinematics. Mastery of these topics is essential for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 1.3 Why WMRs Matter in Engineering

WMRs dominate practical robotics because they are **energy-efficient**, **mechanically simple**, and **geometrically stable** on flat surfaces. They are widely used in:
- **Industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** in warehouses.
- **Autonomous cars** (Ackermann steering).
- **Planetary exploration rovers** (NASA's Sojourner, Curiosity, Perseverance).
- **Service robots** (cleaning, delivery, surveillance).
- **Military and agricultural robotics**.

> [!TIP]
> The term **DOF (Degrees of Freedom)** for a mobile robot refers to the number of independent coordinates required to completely specify its configuration in space. A planar WMR has **3 DOF** in total: $(x, y, \theta)$.

## 1.4 Categorization Preview

Wheeled mobile robots are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:
1. **Type of wheels** used (e.g., fixed, castor, Swedish, ball).
2. **Number of wheels** and their geometric arrangement (configuration).
3. **Kinematic constraints** imposed (holonomic vs. non-holonomic).

These classification axes will be elaborated in SECTION_2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Wheels

A WMR is built from a small set of primitive wheel types. Their kinematic properties dictate the robot's maneuverability.

### 2.1.1 Standard (Fixed) Wheel
- A wheel whose **steering axis** is fixed with respect to the robot chassis.
- It rolls without slipping **in the direction perpendicular to its axle**.
- It has **two constraints**: one rolling constraint and one lateral (no-side-slip) constraint.
- **Example:** The rear wheels of a standard car.

### 2.1.2 Centered Orientable (Castor) Wheel
- A wheel whose **steering axis passes through the center of the wheel** and is vertical.
- It can rotate freely about the vertical axis (passive steering).
- It has **only one constraint** (rolling); the lateral slip is permitted because the wheel can swivel.
- **Example:** The front wheel of a shopping trolley.

### 2.1.3 Off-Centered Orientable Wheel
- A wheel whose **steering axis does NOT pass through the wheel center** but is offset by a distance $d$.
- It is steered actively and contributes a **rolling constraint plus a perpendicular non-rolling constraint**.
- **Example:** The "offset castor" used in some differential drive robots for stability.

### 2.1.4 Swedish Wheel (Mecanum / Universal)
- A wheel with **passive rollers around its circumference**, oriented at $\pm 45^\circ$ to the wheel plane.
- It allows motion in a direction perpendicular to the wheel axis without requiring the wheel to be steered.
- It has **only one rolling constraint** (no lateral constraint).
- **Example:** Mecanum-wheeled omnidirectional platforms (e.g., KUKA youBot base).

### 2.1.5 Ball Wheel / Spherical Wheel
- A spherical ball used as a wheel, often with three orthogonal rollers for actuation.
- It imposes **no non-holonomic constraints** in the ideal case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *Standard* wheels bring **two** constraints, *Swedish and castor* wheels bring **one** constraint, and *ball* wheels bring **zero** constraints. More constraints $\Rightarrow$ less maneuverability but better stability.

## 2.2 Wheel Configurations (Drive Topologies)

### 2.2.1 Differential Drive (DD)
- **Two independently actuated fixed wheels** on a common axle.
- Motion is achieved by differential wheel speeds: $\omega_L$ (left) and $\omega_R$ (right).
- **Non-holonomic**, **zero turning radius**, simple, very common.

### 2.2.2 Tricycle / Synchronous Drive
- One or more **driven and steered** wheels in front, two fixed wheels in rear (tricycle), or all wheels steered and driven together (synchro).
- Mimics Ackermann geometry.

### 2.2.3 Ackermann Steering
- A **geometric arrangement** of steering linkages that ensures all wheels rotate about a common **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**.
- Used in virtually all passenger automobiles.

### 2.2.4 Omnidirectional Drive
- Three or four **Mecanum/Swedish wheels**.
- Holonomic: can translate in any direction without reorientation.

## 2.3 Kinematic Constraints: The Mathematical Core

A **kinematic constraint** is a relationship between the configuration variables and their time derivatives that must be satisfied at all times.

### 2.3.1 Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic

- **Holonomic constraint:** Integrable; reduces the *accessible* configuration space. Example: a robot on a rail — its configuration space is 1D.
- **Non-holonomic constraint:** Non-integrable; reduces the *accessible velocity space* but NOT the configuration space. Example: a car cannot move sideways; it can still reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ in $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$, but only via curved paths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Test:** A constraint of the form $a(x,y,\theta)\dot{x} + b(x,y,\theta)\dot{y} + c(x,y,\theta)\dot{\theta} = 0$ is **non-holonomic** if it cannot be integrated into a relation $f(x,y,\theta) = \text{const}$.

For a standard fixed wheel, the **no-side-slip** constraint is:

$$\dot{x}\sin\phi - \dot{y}\cos\phi + \dot{\theta}(d + l\cos\phi) = 0$$

where $\phi$ is the wheel orientation, $l$ is the wheel's longitudinal offset, and $d$ is the lateral offset. This is the famous **non-holonomic** constraint.

## 2.4 Maneuverability, Mobility, and Steerability

From Campion, Bastin, and Dandurand (1996) — the canonical reference for WMR classification:

- **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$):** Number of degrees of freedom that can be *instantaneously* achieved by the wheels' rolling motion alone.
- **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$):** Number of degrees of freedom contributed by steering of the wheels.
- **Degree of Maneuverability ($\delta_M$):** $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$.

The **maneuverability** of any WMR is bounded by:

$$\delta_M \le 3$$

- A **unicycle (differential drive)** has $\delta_M = 2$ (one for translation forward, one for rotation).
- An **omnidirectional robot** (e.g., 4 Mecanum wheels) has $\delta_M = 3$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols | Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Pose vector | $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | Position and orientation | m, m, rad |
| 2 | Differential drive forward velocity | $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ | $r$ = wheel radius, $\omega_{L,R}$ = wheel angular speeds | m/s |
| 3 | Differential drive angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ | $L$ = wheel base (distance between wheels) | rad/s |
| 4 | Instantaneous Curvature Radius | $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{L}{2}\cdot\dfrac{\omega_R+\omega_L}{\omega_R-\omega_L}$ | $R$ = ICR radius | m |
| 5 | Body-to-Global Jacobian | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0\\ \sin\theta & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}v\\ \omega\end{bmatrix}$ | $v$ = linear, $\omega$ = angular velocity | m/s, rad/s |
| 6 | Ackermann steering angle | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $\delta$ = inner wheel steer angle | rad |
| 7 | Number of DOF (planar) | $3$ | $x, y, \theta$ | - |
| 8 | Mobility + Steerability bound | $\delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$ | Maneuverability | - |
| 9 | Standard wheel constraints | $2$ per wheel | Rolling + no-side-slip | - |
| 10 | Mecanum wheel contact force vector | depends on roller angle $\alpha$ | Typically $\alpha = \pm 45^\circ$ | deg |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, absolute-value bars are written as `\vert v \vert` or `\mid v \mid`, never as the bare pipe `|`, which breaks the table parser.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **AGVs in factories** use differential drives for tight maneuvering.
- **Mars rovers (Curiosity)** use a **rocker-bogie** system with **6 wheels** and **Ackermann-like steering** to navigate rocky terrain.
- **Warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva)** use differential drives to slide under shelves.
- **Omnidirectional robots** are used in **football (soccer) playing robots** and **hospital logistics** for parallel parking sideways.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a Differential Drive Robot

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions
Consider a differential drive robot with:
- Two identical wheels of radius $r$ separated by wheel base $L$ (distance between wheel contact points).
- Left wheel angular speed $\omega_L$, right wheel angular speed $\omega_R$ (both in rad/s).
- Robot pose in the global frame: $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^T$.
- **Assumption:** Pure rolling, no wheel slip.

### 3.1.2 Velocity Contributions

**Step 1 — Linear velocity of the left contact point** (in the robot's body frame):

$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L$$

**Step 2 — Linear velocity of the right contact point:**

$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$$

**Step 3 — Body-frame linear velocity** of the robot's reference point (midpoint between wheels) is the average:

$$v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Angular velocity** of the robot is obtained from the velocity difference divided by the wheel base:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Compose the body-frame velocity vector:**

$$\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2} \\[2mm] \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Transform body-frame velocity to global frame.** The body x-axis is aligned with the global frame rotated by $\theta$. A planar 2D rotation matrix is:

$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying, the body linear velocity $(v, 0)$ becomes $(v\cos\theta, v\sin\theta)$ in the global frame, while the angular component $\omega$ remains the same. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Final forward kinematic equation in matrix form:**

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute expressions for $v$ and $\omega$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{2} & \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{2} \\[2mm] \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{2} & \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{2} \\[2mm] -\dfrac{r}{L} & \dfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the **forward kinematic model** of a differential drive mobile robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Given the wheel speeds, you can compute the robot's pose rate. This is the basis of **odometry** (dead reckoning), which is heavily examined in KTU Module 4.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Kinematics of a Differential Drive Robot

The inverse problem: given a desired body-frame velocity $(v, \omega)$, find the required wheel speeds.

**Step 1 — Start with the body-frame equations:**

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$.** Add and subtract the two equations:

$$\omega_L + \omega_R = \frac{2v}{r}$$

$$\omega_R - \omega_L = \frac{\omega L}{r}$$

**Step 3 — Solve simultaneously:**

$$\omega_R = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2v}{r} + \frac{\omega L}{r}\right) = \frac{v}{r} + \frac{\omega L}{2r}$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2v}{r} - \frac{\omega L}{r}\right) = \frac{v}{r} - \frac{\omega L}{2r}$$

**Step 4 — Compact matrix form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \omega_L \\ \omega_R \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -L/2 \\ 1 & L/2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The inverse kinematics matrix is the **pseudo-inverse** (here a true inverse) of the forward kinematics matrix. This duality is the foundation of **trajectory tracking** control.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) Radius

For a differential drive, the **ICR** lies on the line of the wheel axle. Let the left wheel move at $v_L$ and right at $v_R$, with $v_R > v_L$ (turning left).

**Step 1 — The arc lengths** traversed by the two wheels in the same time $dt$ are $s_L = v_L dt$ and $s_R = v_R dt$.

**Step 2 — The wheels travel along concentric circular arcs** about the ICR. Let $R_L$ and $R_R$ be the distances from the ICR to the left and right wheels, and $R$ be the distance to the robot's center.

**Step 3 — The angular velocity** about the ICR is the same for both wheels:

$$\omega = \frac{v_L}{R_L} = \frac{v_R}{R_R}$$

**Step 4 — From the geometry** $R_R - R_L = L$:

$$R_R = R_L + L$$

**Step 5 — Equating angular velocities:**

$$\frac{v_R}{R_L + L} = \frac{v_L}{R_L} \implies v_R \cdot R_L = v_L(R_L + L)$$

$$\implies R_L(v_R - v_L) = v_L L$$

$$\implies R_L = \frac{v_L L}{v_R - v_L}$$

**Step 6 — The robot's center ICR distance:**

$$R = R_L + \frac{L}{2} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_L + v_R}{v_R - v_L} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$$

$$\boxed{R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_L + \omega_R}{\omega_R - \omega_L}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Special cases:**
> - If $\omega_R = \omega_L$, $R \to \infty$ — straight-line motion.
> - If $\omega_L = -\omega_R$, $R = 0$ — pure rotation about the robot's center (zero turning radius).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A differential drive robot has $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, and current wheel speeds $\omega_L = 2$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 4$ rad/s. Compute $(v, \omega, R)$ and $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ when $\theta = 30^\circ$.

**Step 1 — Compute $v$:**

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 6}{2} = 0.15 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\omega$:**

$$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.05 \times 2}{0.30} = 0.333 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ICR radius $R$:**

$$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_L + \omega_R}{\omega_R - \omega_L} = \frac{0.30}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{2} = 0.15 \times 3 = 0.45 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$** with $\theta = 30^\circ$ ($\cos 30^\circ = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.8660$, $\sin 30^\circ = 0.5$):

$$\dot{x} = 0.15 \times 0.8660 = 0.1299 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{y} = 0.15 \times 0.5 = 0.075 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{\theta} = 0.333 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1299 \\ 0.0750 \\ 0.3333 \end{bmatrix} \text{ (in m/s, m/s, rad/s)}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Differential Drive | Ackermann (Car) | Omnidirectional (Mecanum) |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------|-----------------------------|
| DOF in plane | 2 (v, ω) | 2 (v, δ) | 3 (vx, vy, ω) |
| Turning radius | 0 to ∞ | > 0 (no zero) | 0 (translate sideways) |
| Holonomic? | No | No | Yes (in ideal case) |
| Wheel constraints | 2 fixed + 2 castor | 4 (all fixed) | 4 Swedish |
| Maneuverability $\delta_M$ | 2 | 2 | 3 |
| Typical use | Indoor AGV | Road vehicles | RoboCup, hospital |
| Forward K-matrix | $\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta&0\\\sin\theta&0\\0&1\end{bmatrix}$ | Same form, but $\omega = v\tan\delta/L$ | $\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&1&0\\0&0&1\end{bmatrix}$ (in body frame) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
