# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 4]**
**Differentiate between holonomic and non-holonomic constraints in the context of wheeled mobile robots. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **holonomic constraint** is an equation that can be expressed purely in terms of the configuration variables $(x, y, \theta)$ and their time derivatives can be integrated to a position-level relation. It reduces the *accessible* configuration space of the robot.
- *Example:* A robot constrained to move on a circular track — the relation $x^2 + y^2 = R^2$ is holonomic.

A **non-holonomic constraint** is a velocity-level equation that **cannot be integrated** to a position-level equation. It restricts the *velocities* but the robot can still reach any point in the configuration space via some path.
- *Example:* The no-side-slip constraint of a car: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.

A differential drive WMR is **non-holonomic** because it cannot translate sideways.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1.5 marks for holonomic definition + example; 1.5 marks for non-holonomic definition + example.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP, Module 4]**
**Define the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) for a wheeled mobile robot. What is its significance in differential drive kinematics?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is the point in the plane (which may lie inside, outside, or at infinity relative to the robot) about which the entire rigid body is instantaneously rotating. For a differential drive robot moving along a curved trajectory, the ICR lies on the line extending the wheel axle.

**Significance:**
1. It determines the **radius of curvature** $R$ of the robot's path.
2. It unifies all wheels — every wheel must roll along an arc centered at the ICR.
3. It is the geometric foundation of **Ackermann steering**, where the inner and outer wheel steer angles are chosen so that their respective extensions pass through the ICR.

The ICR radius for a differential drive with $L$ as the wheel base is:

$$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_L + \omega_R}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark definition, 1 mark significance, 1 mark formula.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Two alternatives are given below; the student answers **ONE** complete option.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted, Module 4]**

**(a)** Classify the different **types of wheels** used in wheeled mobile robots. With neat sketches, explain the rolling and lateral constraints for each. Mention the number of constraints each type imposes on the robot's motion. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **forward kinematic equations** of a differential drive mobile robot. Given a robot with $r = 0.04$ m, $L = 0.20$ m, $\omega_L = 5$ rad/s, and $\omega_R = 7$ rad/s, compute its linear velocity, angular velocity, and radius of curvature at an orientation of $\theta = 60^\circ$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The five standard wheel types are:

1. **Fixed (Standard) Wheel** — Axle fixed to chassis. Two constraints:
   - **Rolling:** The wheel's rotational speed $\dot{\phi}$ is related to the contact-point velocity by $r\dot{\phi} = v_{contact}$.
   - **No-side-slip:** The lateral velocity at the contact point is zero.
2. **Centered Orientable (Castor) Wheel** — Steerable but steering axis passes through wheel center. One constraint (rolling only).
3. **Off-Centered Orientable Wheel** — Steerable with offset $d$. Two constraints.
4. **Swedish (Mecanum) Wheel** — Has passive rollers at $\pm 45^\circ$. One rolling constraint along an *effective* axis; no lateral constraint.
5. **Ball Wheel** — Spherical; ideal case has zero constraints.

Number of constraints determines the degree of mobility. For a robot with $N_f$ fixed, $N_c$ castor, and $N_s$ Swedish wheels, the total constraints are:

$$N_{total} = 2N_f + N_c + N_s$$

The number of degrees of mobility is $\delta_m = 3 - N_{total}$ (if $N_{total} < 3$), else $\delta_m = 0$.

A neat schematic with labeled contact points, wheel orientation angle $\phi$, longitudinal offset $l$, and lateral offset $d$ should accompany the answer.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark per wheel type (5 marks), 1 mark constraint table, 1 mark degree of mobility expression.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematic Equations** (refer to Section 3.1 derivation):

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

where:

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix}\dot{x}\\\dot{y}\\\dot{\theta}\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\dfrac{r\cos\theta}{2} & \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{2}\\[2mm] \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{2} & \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{2}\\[2mm] -\dfrac{r}{L} & \dfrac{r}{L}\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\omega_L\\\omega_R\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Computation** with $r = 0.04$ m, $L = 0.20$ m, $\omega_L = 5$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 7$ rad/s:

$$v = \frac{0.04 \times (5+7)}{2} = \frac{0.04 \times 12}{2} = 0.24 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{0.04 \times (7-5)}{0.20} = \frac{0.04 \times 2}{0.20} = 0.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_L+\omega_R}{\omega_R-\omega_L} = \frac{0.20}{2} \cdot \frac{12}{2} = 0.10 \times 6 = 0.60 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Pose rate at $\theta = 60^\circ$:** Using $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$, $\sin 60^\circ = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.8660$:

$$\dot{x} = 0.24 \times 0.5 = 0.12 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{y} = 0.24 \times 0.8660 = 0.2078 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{\theta} = 0.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

> **Marking Scheme:** 2 marks for derivation of forward K, 2 marks for Jacobian matrix, 1.5 marks for numerical $v, \omega$, 1.5 marks for $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Loses 1–2 marks):** Students often confuse the **wheel base $L$** with the wheel **diameter**. The wheel base is the *distance between the two wheel contact points*, not the wheel diameter. Also, students sometimes forget to convert $\theta$ from degrees to radians when computing trigonometric values.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **degree of mobility, degree of steerability, and degree of maneuverability** for a WMR. Using the inequality $\delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$, classify a differential drive, an Ackermann-steered car, and a 4-Mecanum omnidirectional robot. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **inverse kinematics** of a differential drive robot. A robot must follow a trajectory requiring $v = 0.5$ m/s and $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s. If $r = 0.05$ m and $L = 0.30$ m, find the required wheel angular speeds. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Definitions:**
- **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$):** The number of DOF the robot can achieve **instantaneously** by changing wheel speeds alone. It equals $3 - N_c$, where $N_c$ is the number of independent non-rolling (i.e., lateral) constraints.
- **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$):** The number of DOF contributed by **active steering** of orientable wheels.
- **Degree of Maneuverability ($\delta_M$):** $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$.

**Classification:**

| Robot | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $\delta_M$ | Type |
|-------|------------|------------|------------|------|
| Differential Drive | 2 (forward + rotation) | 0 | 2 | Non-holonomic |
| Ackermann Car | 2 (forward + rotation) | 1 (front wheel steer) | 3 in principle, but only 2 independently | Non-holonomic |
| 4-Mecanum Omnidirectional | 3 (vx, vy, ω) | 0 | 3 | Holonomic |

A differential drive achieves $\delta_M = 2$ because the two wheel speeds control $v$ and $\omega$ — there is no steering. The Ackermann car uses a steered front wheel plus a longitudinal velocity for effective $\delta_M = 2$ as well (despite 3 actuators, only 2 independent inputs). The Mecanum robot achieves full $\delta_M = 3$ because it can translate in any direction in the body frame.

> **Marking Scheme:** 2 marks for definitions, 3 marks for the classification table, 2 marks for the inequality explanation.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Derivation** (refer to Section 3.2):

From forward kinematics:

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$\omega_R = \frac{v}{r} + \frac{\omega L}{2r}, \qquad \omega_L = \frac{v}{r} - \frac{\omega L}{2r}$$

**Numerical Computation** with $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s, $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m:

$$\omega_R = \frac{0.5}{0.05} + \frac{0.2 \times 0.30}{2 \times 0.05} = 10 + 0.6 = 10.6 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{0.5}{0.05} - \frac{0.2 \times 0.30}{2 \times 0.05} = 10 - 0.6 = 9.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Verification:** $v = 0.05 \times (9.4 + 10.6)/2 = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5$ m/s ✓

> **Marking Scheme:** 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for final symbolic expressions, 2 marks for numerical computation and verification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Loses 1–2 marks):** Students frequently swap $L$ (wheel base) with $2L$ or forget the factor of 2 in the ICR formula. Another common mistake is using **diameter** $D = 2r$ in formulas that call for **radius** $r$. Always re-read the question and label your quantities.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Wheeled Mobile Robots:**

- [ ] **Pose of a WMR:** $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^T \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$. Three DOF in the plane.
- [ ] **Wheel types and constraint counts:** Fixed (2), Castor (1), Off-centered (2), Swedish (1), Ball (0).
- [ ] **Holonomic vs. Non-holonomic:** A car-style WMR is non-holonomic; a Mecanum-wheeled WMR is (approximately) holonomic.
- [ ] **Differential drive forward K:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
- [ ] **Differential drive inverse K:** $\omega_R = v/r + \omega L/(2r)$, $\omega_L = v/r - \omega L/(2r)$.
- [ ] **ICR radius:** $R = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\omega_L + \omega_R}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$.
- [ ] **Maneuverability bound:** $\delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$.
- [ ] **Ackermann geometry:** $\tan \delta = L/R$ for the inner steered wheel.
- [ ] **Special cases:** $\omega_R = \omega_L \Rightarrow R = \infty$ (straight); $\omega_R = -\omega_L \Rightarrow R = 0$ (in-place rotation).
- [ ] **Common units:** $r$ in meters, $\omega$ in rad/s, $v$ in m/s, $L$ in meters.
- [ ] **Drives to remember:** Differential, Tricycle, Ackermann, Synchro, Omnidirectional (Mecanum), Rocker-Bogie.
- [ ] **Symbols used:** $r$ = wheel radius; $L$ = wheel base; $d$ = lateral offset of off-centered wheel; $l$ = longitudinal offset; $\phi$ = wheel orientation; $\alpha$ = Swedish wheel roller angle.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 favourite questions:** Forward K derivation, ICR radius, holonomic vs. non-holonomic, and wheel constraint counts.
- [ ] **Pitfall to avoid:** Mixing up wheel **diameter** with wheel **radius**, and confusing $L$ (wheel base) with chassis length.
- [ ] **Real-world anchors:** AGVs (differential), cars (Ackermann), Mars rovers (rocker-bogie), RoboCup (Mecanum).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aerial Mobile Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aerial Mobile Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aerial Mobile Robots (AMRs)** are a class of **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs)** that combine mechanical flight platforms with onboard sensing, computation, and autonomous control systems. They are formally defined under **KTU 2024 syllabus** as robotic systems capable of achieving **three-dimensional locomotion** (6 Degrees of Freedom) through aerodynamic force generation, without an onboard human pilot, while executing **perception–planning–action** loops in real-time.

According to the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Anderson & Krathwohl, 2001)** cognitive level **L2 (Understand)**, a KTU student must be able to differentiate aerial mobile robots from other mobile robots based on three defining attributes:

| Defining Attribute | Description |
|---|---|
| **Uninhabited Flight** | No onboard human pilot; flight is fully autonomous, semi-autonomous, or remotely piloted (RPAS). |
| **6-DOF Body Motion** | The rigid body has 3 translational DoF $(x, y, z)$ and 3 rotational DoF $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ (roll, pitch, yaw). |
| **Aerodynamic Lift** | Lift force is generated by rotors (rotary-wing) or fixed wings (aerofoils) — never by ground contact forces. |

**Common KTU Classification Nomenclature:**
- **UAV** – Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (the airframe alone).
- **UAS** – Unmanned Aerial System (UAV + GCS + datalink + payload).
- **RPAS** – Remotely Piloted Aircraft System (regulated term used by ICAO).
- **Drone** – Popular but technically imprecise term (avoid in KTU answers).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Flying Balancing Stick" Analogy**
> Imagine balancing a long stick on the tip of your finger. To keep it upright, you must constantly make tiny corrections — your finger moves forward/backward, left/right, and you even rotate it slightly. A **quadrotor is essentially this same problem in 3D**, but with four spinning rotors instead of a finger, and **gravity** constantly trying to tip it over. The onboard flight controller (often a Pixhawk or similar) is the "brain" that makes these corrections **400–800 times per second**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are **mandatorily tested** in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations:

- **Standard Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (acceleration due to gravity at sea level, in **bold**)
- **Air Density (sea level, ISA):** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Degrees of Freedom:** **6-DOF** for the rigid aerial body
- **Sampling Rate of Flight Controller:** **400 Hz to 8 kHz**
- **Classification by Mass (DGCA, India):** **Nano (<250 g), Micro (250 g–2 kg), Small (2 kg–25 kg), Large (>150 kg)**
- **Dynamic Pressure:** $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ (used in fixed-wing lift/drag)
- **Inertia Tensor Units:** $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a KTU question asks to "classify" or "compare" aerial robots, always present the answer in a **two-column table** with parameters like *Lift Mechanism, Hover Capability, DoF, Energy Efficiency, Payload, and Application*. This earns full methodology marks even if numerical answers have minor errors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Aerial Mobile Robots

KTU 2024 Module 4 explicitly classifies aerial mobile robots into **five families** based on aerodynamic principle:

### 2.1.1 Fixed-Wing UAVs
- **Principle:** Lift from forward airspeed over a fixed aerofoil.
- **Equations:** $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$, $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D$
- **Constraint:** **Cannot hover** — must maintain minimum stall speed.
- **Example:** **Boeing Insitu ScanEagle**, **DRDO Rustom**.

### 2.1.2 Rotary-Wing (Single Rotor / Helicopters)
- **Principle:** Lift from a single main rotor (collective pitch) plus anti-torque tail rotor.
- **Advantage:** **Vertical Take-Off and Landing (VTOL)** + hover.
- **Disadvantage:** Mechanically complex, requires swashplate.

### 2.1.3 Multi-Rotor UAVs (Most Tested in KTU)
- **Sub-types:** **Tricopter (3 rotors), Quadrotor/X4 (4 rotors), Hexacopter (X6), Octocopter (X8).**
- **Quadrotor Configurations:**
  - **"+" Plus Configuration:** Motors at front (M1), back (M2), left (M3), right (M4).
  - **"X" Cross Configuration:** Motors 45° offset from body axes — most stable.
- **Hover Condition:** Total thrust $T = 4 \cdot F_i = m \cdot g$ (for symmetric hover).

### 2.1.4 Hybrid VTOL
- **Principle:** Tiltrotor or tail-sitter combining fixed-wing cruise efficiency with multi-rotor hover.
- **Example:** **Bell V-280 Valor**, **Aurora Flight Sciences XV-24A**.

### 2.1.5 Flapping-Wing / Ornithopters
- Bio-inspired; uses unsteady aerodynamics (Weis-Fogh, delayed stall).

## 2.2 Why the Quadrotor Dominates KTU 2024 Syllabus

| Reason | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Mechanically Simple** | 4 fixed-pitch rotors — no swashplate, no collective pitch actuator. |
| **4 Actuators vs 6 DoF** | Underactuated system: 4 control inputs, 6 DoF — a key KTU concept. |
| **Symmetric Geometry** | Inertia matrix $J$ is approximately **diagonal** in body frame. |
| **Cheap & Educational** | Standard platform for KTU labs (e.g., Crazyflie, DJI Tello). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept: Underactuation**
> A quadrotor has **4 independent control inputs** (the four rotor speeds $\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4$) but must control **6 degrees of freedom** $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$. This is the definition of a **second-order underactuated system**. The position $(x, y)$ is controlled **indirectly** through the roll $(\phi)$ and pitch $(\theta)$ angles — never directly. This is a **guaranteed KTU Part B (14-mark) question**.

## 2.3 Reference Frames — The Mathematical Backbone

Two coordinate frames are mandatory for any aerial robot derivation:

| Frame | Notation | Axes | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inertial / World Frame** | $\mathcal{F}_I = \{O_I, \vec{\imath}_I, \vec{\jmath}_I, \vec{k}_I\}$ | $X^I, Y^I, Z^I$ | Fixed to earth; $Z^I$ points **up**, opposite to gravity. |
| **Body Frame** | $\mathcal{F}_B = \{O_B, \vec{\imath}_B, \vec{\jmath}_B, \vec{k}_B\}$ | $X^B, Y^B, Z^B$ | Attached to UAV center of mass; $X^B$ typically points forward. |

**Transformation:** Any vector expressed in $\mathcal{F}_B$ can be rotated to $\mathcal{F}_I$ using the **Rotation Matrix** $R \in SO(3)$:

$$R = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)$$

Where $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ are the **Euler ZYX angles** — roll, pitch, yaw respectively.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU ESE 2024 — every formula here is a potential Part B (14-mark) question.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Newton's 2nd Law (translation)** | $m\ddot{\vec{r}}^I = R \cdot \vec{F}^B + m\vec{g}$ | $m$ in kg, $\vec{g} = [0,0,-g]^T$ |
| 2 | **Euler's Equation (rotation)** | $J\dot{\vec{\omega}}^B + \vec{\omega}^B \times J\vec{\omega}^B = \vec{\tau}^B$ | $J$ is inertia tensor, kg·m² |
| 3 | **Individual Rotor Thrust** | $F_i = k_f \omega_i^2$ | $k_f$ = thrust coefficient (N·s²) |
| 4 | **Individual Rotor Torque** | $\tau_i = k_m \omega_i^2$ | $k_m$ = moment coefficient (N·m·s²) |
| 5 | **Total Thrust (Quadrotor)** | $T = \sum_{i=1}^{4} F_i = k_f(\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2)$ | Newtons |
| 6 | **Roll Torque** | $\tau_\phi = L \cdot k_f(-\omega_2^2 + \omega_4^2)$ | $L$ = arm length (m) |
| 7 | **Pitch Torque** | $\tau_\theta = L \cdot k_f(\omega_1^2 - \omega_3^2)$ | Newton-metres |
| 8 | **Yaw Torque** | $\tau_\psi = k_m(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_4^2)$ | Newton-metres |
| 9 | **Hover Thrust** | $T_{hover} = mg$ | For $m$ kg vehicle |
| 10 | **Hover Rotor Speed** | $\omega_{hover} = \sqrt{\dfrac{mg}{4k_f}}$ | rad/s |
| 11 | **Lift (Fixed-Wing)** | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$ | $S$ = wing area, $C_L$ = lift coefficient |
| 12 | **Drag (Fixed-Wing)** | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D$ | $C_D$ = drag coefficient |
| 13 | **Aerodynamic Force/Length** | $f = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 b c_L$ | Per unit span |
| 14 | **Body Rates from Euler** | $\begin{bmatrix}\dot{\phi}\\\dot{\theta}\\\dot{\psi}\end{bmatrix} = W^{-1}\vec{\omega}^B$ | $W^{-1}$ = Euler Jacobian |
| 15 | **Geometric / Kinematic Constraint** | $\ddot{x} = \tfrac{T}{m}(\cos\phi\sin\theta\cos\psi + \sin\phi\sin\psi)$ | Sub-component of (1) |
| 16 | **Endurance (Battery)** | $t = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{hover}}$ | Hours |
| 17 | **Momentum Theory Hover Power** | $P_{hover} = T \cdot v_i = \sqrt{\tfrac{T^3}{2\rho A_{disk}}}$ | $v_i$ = induced velocity |
| 18 | **Static Thrust Margin** | $\eta_T = \dfrac{T_{max} - T_{hover}}{T_{hover}} \times 100\%$ | KTU design parameter |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerial Mobile Robots** are revolutionizing **precision agriculture** (DJI Agras), **search and rescue** (post-earthquake), **last-mile delivery** (Zipline medical drones), and **infrastructure inspection** (power lines, bridges).
- **Military Applications:** **DRDO Rustom** (India), **IAI Heron** (Israel) — strategic reconnaissance.
- **KTU Research Relevance:** IITs and NITs use quadrotors as benchmark platforms for testing **nonlinear control** algorithms (backstepping, sliding-mode, MPC) because the system is **affine in control** and **Lagrangian**-formulable.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Tip:** When KTU asks "design a quadrotor for 1 kg payload with 15 min endurance", work backward from $T_{hover} = (m_{frame} + m_{payload})g$, compute $P_{hover}$ from momentum theory, then size battery via Equation (16). Always show units in every step.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rotation Matrix from Euler ZYX Angles

The rotation matrix $R$ transforms a vector from the body frame $\mathcal{F}_B$ to the inertial frame $\mathcal{F}_I$ using three intrinsic rotations in the order **Z-Y-X** (yaw → pitch → roll).

**Step 1 — Rotate about $Z^B$ by yaw $\psi$:**

$$R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Rotate about the *new* $Y$ by pitch $\theta$:**

$$R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Rotate about the *new* $X$ by roll $\phi$:**

$$R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compose the three rotations** (extrinsic ZYX, intrinsic XYZ — same result):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi) \\
&= \begin{bmatrix}
c\theta c\psi & s\phi s\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi & c\phi s\theta c\psi + s\phi s\psi \\
c\theta s\psi & s\phi s\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi & c\phi s\theta s\psi - s\phi c\psi \\
-s\theta & s\phi c\theta & c\phi c\theta
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $c\theta \equiv \cos\theta$, $s\theta \equiv \sin\theta$, and so on. This $R$ maps a body-fixed vector $\vec{v}^B$ to $\vec{v}^I = R\vec{v}^B$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quadrotor Equations of Motion (Newton–Euler Formalism)

We treat the quadrotor as a **single rigid body** of mass $m$ and inertia tensor $J$ (assumed diagonal in the body frame).

**Step 1 — Define state vector.** Let $\vec{\xi} = [x, y, z]^T$ be position in $\mathcal{F}_I$ and $\vec{\eta} = [\phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ be orientation (Euler ZYX).

**Step 2 — Translational dynamics from Newton's 2nd Law.** The forces on the body are:
- Aerodynamic thrust $\vec{T}^B = [0, 0, T]^T$ (along $Z^B$).
- Gravity $\vec{G}^I = [0, 0, -mg]^T$ (in $\mathcal{F}_I$).

Applying Newton's second law in $\mathcal{F}_I$:

$$m\ddot{\vec{\xi}} = R \cdot \vec{T}^B + \vec{G}^I$$

Expanding the $Z^B$ column of $R$ gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ddot{x} &= \frac{T}{m}(\cos\phi\sin\theta\cos\psi + \sin\phi\sin\psi) \\
\ddot{y} &= \frac{T}{m}(\cos\phi\sin\theta\sin\psi - \sin\phi\cos\psi) \\
\ddot{z} &= \frac{T}{m}(\cos\phi\cos\theta) - g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rotational dynamics from Euler's equation.** For a body rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}^B = [p, q, r]^T$ in body frame:

$$J\dot{\vec{\omega}}^B + \vec{\omega}^B \times (J\vec{\omega}^B) = \vec{\tau}^B$$

Assuming diagonal $J = \text{diag}(I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz})$ and cross-product expansion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{p} &= \frac{1}{I_{xx}}\left[\tau_\phi + qr(I_{yy} - I_{zz})\right] \\
\dot{q} &= \frac{1}{I_{yy}}\left[\tau_\theta + pr(I_{zz} - I_{xx})\right] \\
\dot{r} &= \frac{1}{I_{zz}}\left[\tau_\psi + pq(I_{xx} - I_{yy})\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Kinematic coupling (body rates to Euler rates).** The relationship between $\vec{\omega}^B$ and $\dot{\vec{\eta}}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\psi} \end{bmatrix} &= W^{-1}(\phi,\theta) \begin{bmatrix} p \\ q \\ r \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix}
1 & \sin\phi\tan\theta & \cos\phi\tan\theta \\
0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\
0 & \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos\theta} & \dfrac{\cos\phi}{\cos\theta}
\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p \\ q \\ r \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matrix is **singular at $\theta = \pm 90°$** — a key KTU caveat called **gimbal lock**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hover Thrust and Rotor Speed

**Problem:** A quadrotor of mass $m = 1.5 \text{ kg}$ uses four identical rotors with thrust coefficient $k_f = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N·s}^2$. Find the required rotor speed for level hover and verify the static thrust margin if each motor can spin at $\omega_{max} = 8000 \text{ rpm}$.

**Step 1 — Compute hover total thrust.** At level hover, $\ddot{z} = 0$ and $\cos\phi\cos\theta \approx 1$, so:

$$T_{hover} = mg = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Distribute among 4 rotors equally** (symmetric hover):

$$F_i = \frac{T_{hover}}{4} = \frac{14.715}{4} = 3.679 \text{ N per rotor}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for rotor speed** using $F_i = k_f \omega_i^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{hover} &= \sqrt{\frac{F_i}{k_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.679}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}} \\
&= \sqrt{306{,}583.3} \approx 553.7 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to RPM** (the units KTU examiners often demand):

$$\omega_{hover_{rpm}} = 553.7 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 5287 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute static thrust margin.** Maximum thrust per rotor at $8000 \text{ rpm} = 837.76 \text{ rad/s}$:

$$F_{max} = k_f \omega_{max}^2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times (837.76)^2 \approx 8.42 \text{ N}$$

Total max thrust: $T_{max} = 4 \times 8.42 = 33.68 \text{ N}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_T &= \frac{T_{max} - T_{hover}}{T_{hover}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{33.68 - 14.715}{14.715} \times 100\% \approx 128.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> A static thrust margin of **> 100%** is the industry standard for **agile quadrotors** (racing drones) and is what allows the UAV to climb at full throttle and recover from aggressive maneuvers. KTU questions on "design" almost always require this computation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Control Allocation

The quadrotor **control allocation matrix** maps desired body moments + total thrust to individual rotor speeds. For an "X" configuration with arm length $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} T \\ \tau_\phi \\ \tau_\theta \\ \tau_\psi \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix}
k_f & k_f & k_f & k_f \\
0 & -Lk_f & 0 & Lk_f \\
Lk_f & 0 & -Lk_f & 0 \\
k_m & -k_m & k_m & -k_m
\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_1^2 \\ \omega_2^2 \\ \omega_3^2 \\ \omega_4^2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The $4 \times 4$ matrix is **invertible** (assuming $k_f, k_m \neq 0$ and $L \neq 0$), giving the **mixing law** that every flight controller firmware (PX4, ArduPilot) uses.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the "+" and "X" configurations in the mixing matrix. In **"X"**, the front-right rotor (M1) is **between** the $X^B$ and $Y^B$ axes, so it contributes to BOTH roll AND pitch torques. In **"+"**, M1 is purely along $X^B$ and contributes only to pitch. This single sign error costs **2 of 14 marks** in typical KTU solutions.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Quadrotor (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SetP["Desired Trajectory x_d, y_d, z_d, psi_d"] --> OuterLoop
    IMU["IMU 6-axis Gyroscope plus Accelerometer"] --> StateEst
    GPS["GNSS GPS Receiver"] --> StateEst
    Baro["Barometric Altimeter"] --> StateEst
    OptFlow["Optical Flow Sensor Optional"] --> StateEst
    StateEst["Extended Kalman Filter EKF State Estimator"] --> OuterLoop
    OuterLoop["Outer Loop Position Controller PD"] --> InnerLoop
    InnerLoop["Inner Loop Attitude Controller PID"] --> Mixer
    Mixer["Control Allocation Mixer"] --> ESC1["ESC 1"]
    Mixer --> ESC2["ESC 2"]
    Mixer --> ESC3["ESC 3"]
    Mixer --> ESC4["ESC 4"]
    ESC1 --> M1["Brushless Motor M1 Front Right"]
    ESC2 --> M2["Brushless Motor M2 Rear Right"]
    ESC3 --> M3["Brushless Motor M3 Rear Left"]
    ESC4 --> M4["Brushless Motor M4 Front Left"]
    M1 --> Force["Aerodynamic Forces and Moments"]
    M2 --> Force
    M3 --> Force
    M4 --> Force
    Force --> Dynamics["Rigid Body Dynamics Newton Euler"]
    Dynamics --> IMU
    Dynamics -.-> |"Feedback"| StateEst
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram represents the **closed-loop control architecture** implemented in PX4 / ArduPilot. The **outer loop** (position, ~50 Hz) and **inner loop** (attitude, ~250–1000 Hz) are cascaded — a **standard KTU 2024 Module 4 question asks to draw this block diagram**.

## 4.2 Quadrotor Configuration Diagram (X-Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph QuadX[Quadrotor X Configuration Top View]
        direction TB
        M1["M1 Front Right CW"] --- C["Center of Mass CG"]
        C --- M2["M2 Rear Right CCW"]
        C --- M3["M3 Rear Left CW"]
        C --- M4["M4 Front Left CCW"]
    end
    XAxis["X_B Forward Axis"] --- C
    YAxis["Y_B Right Axis"] --- C
    Roll["Roll torque about X_B"] -.-> M1
    Roll -.-> M4
    Pitch["Pitch torque about Y_B"] -.-> M1
    Pitch -.-> M3
    Yaw["Yaw torque about Z_B"] -.-> M1
    Yaw -.-> M2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation:** CW = Clockwise rotation, CCW = Counter-clockwise. **Adjacent rotors spin in opposite directions** to cancel net reactive torque — this is the reason a quadrotor yaw is controlled by the **difference** $\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_4^2$ (note alternating signs).

## 4.3 State Estimation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMUraw["Raw IMU 1 kHz"] --> Pref["Mahony Complementary Filter"]
    GPSraw["Raw GPS 10 Hz"] --> Pref
    BaroRaw["Raw Barometer 50 Hz"] --> Pref
    Pref["Attitude Estimate 1 kHz"] --> EKF["EKF Position and Velocity 100 Hz"]
    EKF --> Ctrl["Control Inputs to Mixer"]
```

This block diagram illustrates why **sensor fusion** is essential: GPS is accurate but slow, IMU is fast but drifts — a **Kalman filter** combines them optimally to produce a state estimate at the rate the controller demands.

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Drawing Convention:** Use **rectangles for blocks**, **arrows for signal flow**, and **always label every arrow** with the signal name and units. A well-labelled diagram earns at least **3 of 14 marks** in ESE answers, even if numerical parts have errors.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A expects **2-mark definition** + **1-mark application/example**. Answer length: **30–50 words**.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Unmanned Aerial Vehicle" (UAV). Differentiate between a rotary-wing and a fixed-wing UAV.**

**Model Answer:**
A UAV is an aerial vehicle that flies without an onboard human pilot, executing missions via autonomous control or remote command. A **rotary-wing** UAV (e.g., quadrotor) uses spinning rotors for lift, enabling **VTOL and hover**. A **fixed-wing** UAV (e.g., ScanEagle) uses aerofoil lift, requiring forward motion and **cannot hover**.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three applications of aerial mobile robots in civilian domain.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Precision agriculture** — crop monitoring and pesticide spraying (DJI Agras).
2. **Disaster response** — search and rescue in collapsed structures.
3. **Aerial surveying** — 3D mapping using LiDAR + photogrammetry (Pix4D).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks, split into two 7-mark sub-parts. **Internal choice between sub-parts is NOT permitted** — only the full-question choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the Newton–Euler equations of motion for a quadrotor UAV in 3D space, clearly defining the inertial frame, body frame, and Euler angles. Show that the system is **underactuated** with 4 inputs controlling 6 degrees of freedom. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quadrotor has $m = 2 \text{ kg}$, arm length $L = 0.25 \text{ m}$, $k_f = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N·s}^2$, and $k_m = 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N·m·s}^2$. Compute the **hover rotor speed in rad/s and RPM**, and determine the **roll torque** when $\omega_4 = 600 \text{ rad/s}$ and $\omega_2 = 500 \text{ rad/s}$ (others at hover). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Step 1 — Frame definitions.** Inertial frame $\mathcal{F}_I$ with $Z^I$ pointing up; body frame $\mathcal{F}_B$ fixed at CG with $X^B$ forward.

**Step 2 — Translational equation:**

$$m\ddot{\vec{\xi}} = R \cdot [0,0,T]^T + [0,0,-mg]^T$$

**Step 3 — Rotational equation:**

$$J\dot{\vec{\omega}}^B + \vec{\omega}^B \times J\vec{\omega}^B = \vec{\tau}^B$$

**Step 4 — Control inputs.** Only four independent rotor speeds $\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4$ → 4 control inputs. But the rigid body has **6 DoF** $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ → **Underactuated by 2**. Positions $x, y$ are controlled **indirectly** via $\phi, \theta$.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Hover thrust:** $T_{hover} = mg = 2 \times 9.81 = 19.62 \text{ N}$

**Step 2 — Per rotor:** $F_i = 19.62/4 = 4.905 \text{ N}$

**Step 3 — Hover speed:**

$$\omega_{hover} = \sqrt{\frac{4.905}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}} = \sqrt{490{,}500} \approx 700.36 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting: $700.36 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 6688 \text{ rpm}$

**Step 4 — Roll torque (X-config, M4 right, M2 left):**

$$\tau_\phi = L \cdot k_f (-\omega_2^2 + \omega_4^2) = 0.25 \times 10^{-5} \times (-500^2 + 600^2)$$

$$= 0.25 \times 10^{-5} \times 110{,}000 = 0.275 \text{ N·m}$$

The positive sign indicates a **clockwise roll** about the body $X^B$ axis (right side lifting).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 (Alternative Choice)]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **"X" and "+" configurations** of a quadrotor. Compare them in terms of **maneuverability, sensor placement, and fault tolerance.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a fixed-wing UAV cruising at $V = 25 \text{ m/s}$ with wing area $S = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$, $C_L = 0.6$, $C_D = 0.05$, and $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, calculate the **lift, drag, and lift-to-drag ratio (L/D)**. Comment on the **aerodynamic efficiency** of this design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Diagram (textual representation):**
- **"+" config:** Motors 1, 2, 3, 4 aligned with body $X^B, Y^B$ axes. Front/back motors are along $X^B$.
- **"X" config:** Motors at **45°** offset from body axes. M1 at front-right, M2 at rear-right, M3 at rear-left, M4 at front-left.

| Parameter | "Plus" (+) | "Cross" (X) |
|---|---|---|
| Maneuverability | Lower (smaller effective moment arm) | Higher (effective arm = $L/\sqrt{2}$ × $\sqrt{2} = L$) — same physical, better yaw control |
| Sensor Mounting | Clear front/back for camera/gimbal | Camera must be mounted on front arm, not directly forward |
| Fault Tolerance | Standard | Slightly better yaw authority |

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Step 1 — Dynamic pressure:**

$$q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 25^2 = 382.81 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Lift:**

$$L = q \cdot S \cdot C_L = 382.81 \times 0.5 \times 0.6 = 114.84 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Drag:**

$$D = q \cdot S \cdot C_D = 382.81 \times 0.5 \times 0.05 = 9.57 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — L/D ratio:**

$$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{0.6}{0.05} = 12$$

**Comment:** An L/D of 12 is **moderate** for a small UAV — typical training aircraft achieve 15–20, while high-altitude long-endurance (HALE) drones reach 25–30. The design is **aerodynamically acceptable** for short-range surveillance missions but **not optimal** for long endurance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of torques** — Students frequently lose **2 marks** by writing $\tau_\phi = Lk_f(\omega_2^2 - \omega_4^2)$ instead of the correct $(-\omega_2^2 + \omega_4^2)$. **Draw the rotors and identify which side lifts** — never memorize blind.
> 2. **Unit conversion** — KTU expects **both rad/s AND rpm** in numerical answers. Forgetting to convert loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Frame labelling** — Always label gravity as $mg$ acting in $-Z^I$ (inertial), not $-Z^B$ (body). Conflating frames is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Euler angle order** — The ZYX convention is *de facto* standard in KTU. If you use XYZ, declare it explicitly or lose 1 mark for "ambiguous convention".
> 5. **Stating the underactuation property** — A 7-mark derivation question that does **not** mention the word "underactuated" or "4 inputs vs 6 DoF" is considered **incomplete**, losing **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read before every KTU exam:**

- [ ] An aerial mobile robot is a UAV with **6 DoF** and aerodynamic lift generation.
- [ ] A quadrotor has **4 rotors, 4 inputs, 6 DoF → underactuated**.
- [ ] **ZYX Euler convention**: $\psi$ (yaw) → $\theta$ (pitch) → $\phi$ (roll).
- [ ] Rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$ has $\det R = 1$ and $R^{-1} = R^T$.
- [ ] Hover condition: $T = mg$, equally distributed across rotors.
- [ ] Rotor force model: $F_i = k_f \omega_i^2$ (quadratic in speed).
- [ ] **CW and CCW rotors alternate** to cancel yaw reaction torque.
- [ ] Static thrust margin $\eta_T > 100\%$ for agile quadrotors.
- [ ] **Gimbal lock** occurs at $\theta = \pm 90°$ — use quaternions to avoid.
- [ ] Two main frames: **inertial $\mathcal{F}_I$** (world) and **body $\mathcal{F}_B$** (vehicle).
- [ ] **EKF** is the standard sensor fusion algorithm for state estimation.
- [ ] **Cascade control** architecture: outer position loop (~50 Hz) + inner attitude loop (~250+ Hz).
- [ ] Fixed-wing UAVs **cannot hover**; L/D ratio indicates aerodynamic efficiency.
- [ ] **Standard KTU constants:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{SL} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- [ ] **Always show units** in every line of numerical derivation — partial credit depends on it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** Questions on **aerial mobile robots** almost always combine a **derivation** (EOMs, rotation matrix) with a **numerical problem** (hover speed, thrust, drag). Practice the mixing matrix in both "+" and "X" configurations, and **memorize the sign convention** of body torques. For 14-mark answers, follow the structure: **Diagram → Assumptions → Derivation → Numerical Substitution → Final Answer with Units**. This pattern alone secures at least **11 of 14 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Robot Kinematics (Differential Drive Robot)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mobile Robot Kinematics (Differential Drive Robot)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mobile Robot Kinematics** is the branch of robotics that studies the mathematical description of a mobile robot's motion in its configuration space, *independent of the forces and torques that produce the motion*. For a **Differential Drive Robot (DDR)**, kinematics deals with the geometric relationship between the wheel actuation inputs (wheel angular velocities) and the resulting motion of the robot's chassis (linear and angular velocity, pose).

A differential drive robot is a **non-holonomic** wheeled mobile robot equipped with two independently driven wheels mounted on a common axis, plus one or more passive caster wheels for balance. "Differential" refers to the fact that the robot's turning motion is generated by the *difference* in velocities applied to the left and right wheels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The focus is restricted to **kinematic modeling** (geometric/algebraic relationships). Dynamics (forces, torques, inertia, motor modeling) belong to later modules. Students are expected to derive both the **forward kinematics** (wheel speeds → chassis velocity) and **inverse kinematics** (chassis velocity → wheel speeds), and to express the result in a *posture/pose* vector form.

### 1.2 The Robot's Configuration (Posture)

The configuration (or *pose*) of a mobile robot on a 2D plane is fully described by three independent variables:

$$ q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} $$

where:
- $x$ = position of the robot along the global X-axis (in **meters**)
- $y$ = position along the global Y-axis (in **meters**)
- $\theta$ = heading angle measured from X-axis to robot's forward direction (in **radians**)

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Office Chair Test"

Imagine sitting on a swivel office chair with your feet off the ground (wheels free). Your two hands grip the rim of the wheels.

- **Push both hands forward equally** → you roll straight ahead (pure translation).
- **Push the right hand forward, hold the left still** → the chair pivots to the **left** around the stationary left wheel.
- **Push both hands equally but in opposite directions** → the chair spins in place (pure rotation about its center).

> [!NOTE]
> This is *exactly* the principle of a differential drive robot. There is **no steering wheel** and **no need to align a wheel** before turning. The robot can change direction *instantly* by varying the ratio of the two wheel speeds. The trade-off is that the robot **cannot move sideways** — it is constrained to roll forward or backward along its instantaneous heading axis. This is the famous **non-holonomic constraint**.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

- It is the **foundation of every autonomous ground vehicle** built on a 2-wheel differential base (warehouse AGVs, TurtleBot, Roomba, Mars rovers — *Sojourner, Curiosity, Perseverance*).
- It feeds directly into **odometry** (dead-reckoning localization).
- It is a mandatory question in KTU's robotics, mechatronics, and automation papers, with heavy weightage on the Jacobian, the posture equation, and the inverse kinematic formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Robot's Physical Setup

Consider a differential drive robot with:
- Two **active (driven)** wheels of radius $r$, separated by a distance $L$ (the *wheelbase* or *axle length*).
- One or more **passive casters** for static balance (no kinematic role).
- A **local reference frame** $\lbrace R \rbrace$ attached to the robot, with origin at the **midpoint of the drive axle**, X-axis pointing forward, Y-axis pointing left.

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** of the robot always lies on the line joining the two drive wheels.

### 2.2 The Two Fundamental Wheel Constraints

For an ideal, rigid wheel that **rolls without slipping**, two constraints apply:

| # | Constraint Name | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Rolling constraint** | $\dot{x}_c \cos\theta + \dot{y}_c \sin\theta = r\dot{\phi}$ | The velocity of the contact point along the wheel's plane equals $r \dot{\phi}$. |
| 2 | **Lateral / No-slip constraint** | $-\dot{x}_c \sin\theta + \dot{y}_c \cos\theta = 0$ | The wheel cannot slide sideways (the hallmark of non-holonomic behavior). |

### 2.3 Deriving the Forward Kinematics — Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Assign contact-point velocities.**
Let the right wheel contact point have velocity $(\dot{x}_R, \dot{y}_R)$ and the left wheel $(\dot{x}_L, \dot{y}_L)$ in the world frame.

**Step 2 — Apply the rolling constraint to BOTH wheels.**
Because the wheels are rigidly fixed to the chassis and separated by $L$:
- Right wheel contact: $\dot{x}_R = \dot{x} + (L/2)\dot{\theta}\sin\theta$ and $\dot{y}_R = \dot{y} - (L/2)\dot{\theta}\cos\theta$
- Left wheel contact: $\dot{x}_L = \dot{x} - (L/2)\dot{\theta}\sin\theta$ and $\dot{y}_L = \dot{y} + (L/2)\dot{\theta}\cos\theta$

**Step 3 — Apply the no-slip lateral constraint to BOTH wheels** (gives identical equation for both, because the wheelbase is symmetric about the origin).

**Step 4 — Solve the resulting 2×2 linear system** for $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ in terms of $\dot{\phi}_R$ and $\dot{\phi}_L$.

**Step 5 — Recognize that $\theta$-axis motion is governed by the wheel-speed *difference*.**

### 2.4 The Forward Kinematics Equations (Posture Update)

> [!NOTE]
> **These three equations are the single most-tested result in the entire Mobile Robot Kinematics chapter at KTU. Memorize them as a block.**

$$ \dot{x} = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)\cos\theta $$

$$ \dot{y} = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)\sin\theta $$

$$ \dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L) $$

In compact matrix form (the **Forward Kinematics / Posture Equation**):

$$ \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \frac{r}{L} & -\frac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix} $$

Define the **forward linear velocity** $v$ and the **forward angular velocity** $\omega$ of the chassis:
$$ v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L), \qquad \omega = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L) $$

Then the posture equation becomes the elegant universal mobile-robot form:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} $$

### 2.5 The Inverse Kinematics

To find the wheel speeds needed to achieve a desired chassis velocity $(v, \omega)$:

$$ \dot{\phi}_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}, \qquad \dot{\phi}_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r} $$

### 2.6 The Jacobian Matrix

The matrix $J(\theta)$ that maps the *actuation* vector $[\dot{\phi}_R \ \dot{\phi}_L]^T$ into the *configuration-space velocity* is called the **Jacobian**:

$$ J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \frac{r}{L} & -\frac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix}_{3 \times 2} $$

- It is a **3×2** matrix (3 configuration coordinates, 2 actuators) — hence the system is **redundantly constrained** in the configuration space but **non-holonomic** in the workspace.
- The **determinant of the top-left 2×2 submatrix** is **zero**, which is the *algebraic* signature of a non-holonomic system: the rank is 2, not 3, meaning the robot cannot instantaneously move in every direction of its configuration space.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use \vert for absolute-value bars inside any table cell to avoid breaking markdown syntax.**

| # | Quantity / Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward linear velocity $v$ | $v = \tfrac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)$ | m/s | Range: $[-\infty, +\infty]$ |
| 2 | Forward angular velocity $\omega$ | $\omega = \tfrac{r}{L}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)$ | rad/s | Positive = counter-clockwise (CCW) by convention |
| 3 | Posture X-velocity $\dot{x}$ | $\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$ | m/s | Component along world X-axis |
| 4 | Posture Y-velocity $\dot{y}$ | $\dot{y} = v \sin\theta$ | m/s | Component along world Y-axis |
| 5 | Inverse: right wheel speed | $\dot{\phi}_R = \tfrac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$ | rad/s | Required actuation |
| 6 | Inverse: left wheel speed | $\dot{\phi}_L = \tfrac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$ | rad/s | Required actuation |
| 7 | Non-holonomic constraint | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | — | Equivalent to "velocity $\perp$ lateral axis" |
| 8 | Turning radius $R_c$ | $R_c = \tfrac{v}{\omega} = \tfrac{L}{2} \cdot \tfrac{\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L}{\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L}$ | m | $\vert R_c \vert \rightarrow \infty$ when $\omega \to 0$ (straight line) |
| 9 | Pure rotation (in place) | $\dot{\phi}_R = -\dot{\phi}_L$, so $v=0$, $\omega \neq 0$ | — | $R_c = 0$ |
| 10 | Discrete-time pose update (Euler) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \Delta t \cos\theta_k$ | m | Used in odometry |
| 11 | Discrete-time pose update (Euler) | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \Delta t \sin\theta_k$ | m | Used in odometry |
| 12 | Discrete-time pose update (Euler) | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$ | rad | Used in odometry |
| 13 | Singularity warning | $\dot{\phi}_R = \dot{\phi}_L \neq 0 \Rightarrow \omega = 0$ | — | Pure translation only |
| 14 | Singularity warning | $\dot{\phi}_R = -\dot{\phi}_L \neq 0 \Rightarrow v = 0$ | — | Pure rotation only |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mars Rover navigation (NASA JPL):** The *Sojourner* rover (1997) used exactly this kinematic model; the same family of equations was scaled up for *Curiosity* and *Perseverance*.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics):** Differential drive is the dominant base because of its zero turning radius — critical in narrow aisles.
- **Educational platforms (TurtleBot 3, Pololu 3pi):** Used worldwide in robotics labs to teach these exact derivations.
- **Self-driving cars (Ackermann base, related):** Ackermann steering can be *approximated* by a differential drive for low-speed path planning, and the Ackermann Jacobian reduces to the differential-drive Jacobian when the steering angle is held fixed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Forward Kinematics

**Setting up the wheel-contact velocities.** Place the world frame $\lbrace W \rbrace$ at an arbitrary origin. Let the robot's pose in this frame be $(x, y, \theta)$. The right wheel contact point is located at world position:

$$ P_R = \begin{bmatrix} x_R \\ y_R \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + R(\theta)\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -L/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x + (L/2)\sin\theta \\ y - (L/2)\cos\theta \end{bmatrix} $$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$ \dot{x}_R = \dot{x} + (L/2)\dot{\theta}\cos\theta, \qquad \dot{y}_R = \dot{y} + (L/2)\dot{\theta}\sin\theta $$

Similarly for the left wheel (sign change on the lateral offset):

$$ \dot{x}_L = \dot{x} - (L/2)\dot{\theta}\cos\theta, \qquad \dot{y}_L = \dot{y} - (L/2)\dot{\theta}\sin\theta $$

**Apply the rolling constraint to the right wheel.** The rolling constraint requires that the component of the contact-point velocity along the wheel's rolling axis (the robot's local X-axis, which makes angle $\theta$ with the world X-axis) equals $r\dot{\phi}_R$:

$$ \dot{x}_R\cos\theta + \dot{y}_R\sin\theta = r\dot{\phi}_R $$

Substitute the expressions above:

$$ [\dot{x} + (L/2)\dot{\theta}\cos\theta]\cos\theta + [\dot{y} + (L/2)\dot{\theta}\sin\theta]\sin\theta = r\dot{\phi}_R $$

Expand and group:

$$ \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta + (L/2)\dot{\theta}(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = r\dot{\phi}_R $$

Using $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$ \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta + (L/2)\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\phi}_R \quad \text{...(R1)} $$

**Apply the rolling constraint to the left wheel.** By identical logic (replacing $R \to L$ and $L/2 \to -L/2$):

$$ \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta - (L/2)\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\phi}_L \quad \text{...(L1)} $$

**Solve the linear system.** Add (R1) and (L1):

$$ 2(\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta) = r(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L) $$

$$ \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L) \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)} $$

Subtract (L1) from (R1):

$$ L\dot{\theta} = r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L) \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)} $$

**Apply the no-slip (lateral) constraint.** For a properly aligned wheel, the velocity of the contact point along the local Y-axis must be zero:

$$ -\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0 \quad \text{...(Eq. 3)} $$

**Solve Eq. 1 and Eq. 3 simultaneously for $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$.** Write them in matrix form:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$

The coefficient matrix is an orthogonal rotation matrix $R(\theta)^T$. Its inverse is $R(\theta)$ (its transpose):

$$ \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$

Multiplying out:

$$ \dot{x} = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)\cos\theta $$

$$ \dot{y} = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)\sin\theta $$

Combined with Eq. 2, the **Forward Kinematics Posture Equation** is:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \frac{r}{L} & -\frac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this result is non-holonomic:** The mapping from the 2-D input space $[\dot{\phi}_R, \dot{\phi}_L]^T$ to the 3-D configuration velocity $[\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$ is **non-integrable**: the sideways velocity $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta \equiv 0$ is a *velocity-level* constraint that **cannot be integrated into a position-level constraint**. Hence the robot is *not* globally controllable in every direction of $(x,y,\theta)$ — it is locally controllable but globally path-dependent.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Inverse Kinematics

**Starting point:** We are given the desired chassis velocities $(v, \omega)$ and the geometric parameters $(r, L)$. We must solve for $(\dot{\phi}_R, \dot{\phi}_L)$.

From the forward-kinematics definitions:

$$ v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L) $$

$$ \omega = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L) $$

These form a 2×2 linear system in $(\dot{\phi}_R, \dot{\phi}_L)$. Solve for $\dot{\phi}_R$:

$$ \dot{\phi}_R = \frac{v}{r} + \frac{\omega L}{2r} = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r} $$

Solve for $\dot{\phi}_L$:

$$ \dot{\phi}_L = \frac{v}{r} - \frac{\omega L}{2r} = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r} $$

**Verification of a special case — pure rotation in place.** If the user requests $v = 0$ and $\omega \neq 0$:

$$ \dot{\phi}_R = \frac{\omega L}{2r}, \qquad \dot{\phi}_L = -\frac{\omega L}{2r} $$

The two wheel speeds are **equal in magnitude and opposite in sign** — exactly matching the office-chair "twist the rim in opposite directions" intuition.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

> **Problem:** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheelbase $L = 0.30$ m, and current heading $\theta = \pi/6$ rad (30°). The right wheel is driven at $\dot{\phi}_R = 8$ rad/s, the left at $\dot{\phi}_L = 6$ rad/s. Compute (a) the linear and angular chassis velocities, (b) the world-frame velocities, (c) the pose after 1 second of motion (Euler integration), and (d) the wheel speeds needed to move straight at $v = 0.3$ m/s.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Chassis velocities.**

$$ v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L) = \frac{0.05}{2}(8 + 6) = 0.025 \times 14 = 0.35 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \omega = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L) = \frac{0.05}{0.30}(8 - 6) = 0.1\overline{6} \times 2 = 0.3\overline{3} \text{ rad/s} $$

**Part (b) — World-frame velocities.**

$$ \dot{x} = v\cos\theta = 0.35 \times \cos(30°) = 0.35 \times 0.8660 \approx 0.3031 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta = 0.35 \times \sin(30°) = 0.35 \times 0.5 = 0.175 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \dot{\theta} = \omega \approx 0.3333 \text{ rad/s} $$

**Part (c) — Pose after 1 s (Euler, $\Delta t = 1$ s).**

$$ x(1) = 0 + 0.3031 \times 1 = 0.3031 \text{ m} $$

$$ y(1) = 0 + 0.175 \times 1 = 0.175 \text{ m} $$

$$ \theta(1) = \pi/6 + 0.3\overline{3} \times 1 \approx 0.5236 + 0.3333 \approx 0.8569 \text{ rad} \approx 49.1° $$

**Part (d) — Inverse kinematics for $v = 0.3$ m/s, $\omega = 0$ (straight line).**

$$ \dot{\phi}_R = \frac{2(0.3) + 0 \times 0.30}{2 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.6}{0.10} = 6.0 \text{ rad/s} $$

$$ \dot{\phi}_L = \frac{2(0.3) - 0 \times 0.30}{2 \times 0.05} = 6.0 \text{ rad/s} $$

Both wheels at **6 rad/s** — confirms that "straight ahead" requires equal wheel speeds.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Examiners frequently ask for both the *forward* AND *inverse* problem in a single 14-mark question. Practice recognizing the trigger words: "wheel speeds are given, find chassis velocity" = forward; "chassis velocity is given, find wheel speeds" = inverse.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> *Mermaid physical-geometry renderings are not supported reliably in markdown pipelines, so the diagram below is a faithful **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the signal flow from actuation to configuration update.*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Desired Chassis Velocity v omega"] --> B["Inverse Kinematics Solver Block"]
    B --> C["Required Wheel Speeds phiR phiL"]
    C --> D["Motor Driver Stage Left Motor Right Motor"]
    D --> E["Differential Drive Plant r L theta"]
    E --> F["Wheel Rotation phiR phiL measured"]
    E --> G["Chassis Linear v and Angular omega velocity"]
    G --> H["Forward Kinematics Block J theta"]
    H --> I["Configuration Velocity Derivative xdot ydot thetadot"]
    I --> J["Integrator Block x y theta"]
    J --> K["Updated Robot Pose"]
    K --> H
    F --> L["Encoder Feedback Block"]
    L --> M["Discrete Pose Estimate Odometry"]
    M --> B
```

### 4.2 Schematic Description of the DDR Physical Layout

(For students visualizing the equations — read this with the formula sheet side-by-side.)

```
                World Frame {W}
                       |
                       |  y_W
                       |
                       |__________ x_W
                      /
                     / θ
                    /_______________________
                   /  |                  ^
                  /   |   L               |
                 /    | <---------->      |
                /     v                  |
               /  +---+-----+-----+---+  |  Robot Body
              /   |   |     |  o  |   |  |  (Local Frame {R})
             /    |   |  o  |     |   |  |
            /     |   |     |  o  |   |  |
           /  ----+---+-----+-----+---+--+
          /       φ_L  Center   φ_R
         /
        v  x_W

      Where:
        - The two outer wheels (driven) are separated by distance L.
        - The center 'o' is the reference point (mid-axle).
        - θ is measured CCW from world X-axis to robot's forward axis.
        - v is the linear velocity of the center along the robot's X-axis.
        - ω = θ̇ is the angular velocity about the center.
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is on the line connecting the two wheel contact points, located at perpendicular distance $R_c = v/\omega$ from the robot's center. When $\omega \to 0$ (straight motion), the ICR recedes to infinity.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the term **non-holonomic constraint** as it applies to a differential drive robot. Write the mathematical form of the constraint for a robot with pose $(x, y, \theta)$ and explain its physical meaning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **non-holonomic constraint** is one that restricts the *velocity* of the system but cannot be reduced to a constraint on the *configuration* (position) alone. For a differential drive robot, it is the **no-sideways-slip** condition:

$$ \dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0 $$

**Physical meaning:** The robot's instantaneous velocity vector must always lie *along* the robot's forward axis; it can never have a component perpendicular to its heading. Hence the robot cannot translate sideways — to move "left", it must first rotate, then translate. This constraint is non-integrable (cannot be written as a relation purely among $x, y, \theta$), making the system non-holonomic.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*For a differential drive robot, write the expressions for the linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ in terms of wheel angular velocities $\dot{\phi}_R$ and $\dot{\phi}_L$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$ v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L) \quad \text{(linear velocity of the chassis, m/s)} $$

$$ \omega = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L) \quad \text{(angular velocity of the chassis, rad/s)} $$

where $r$ is the wheel radius and $L$ is the distance between the two drive wheels.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU's 14-mark questions in this module conventionally split into two 7-mark sub-parts. One sub-part is usually a *derivation*, the other a *numerical problem* or *qualitative design analysis*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** With the aid of a labeled schematic, derive the **forward kinematics equations** of a differential drive robot in terms of the wheel angular velocities $\dot{\phi}_R$ and $\dot{\phi}_L$, the wheel radius $r$, the wheelbase $L$, and the heading angle $\theta$. Clearly state the two wheel constraints you apply. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot has $r = 0.04$ m and $L = 0.25$ m. It is currently at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (1.0, 0.5, 0)$ m, rad. The wheel speeds are $\dot{\phi}_R = 10$ rad/s and $\dot{\phi}_L = 4$ rad/s. Compute:
- (i) The chassis linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$.
- (ii) The world-frame velocity components $\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}$.
- (iii) The pose of the robot after 2 seconds (use Euler integration with $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, 20 steps).
- (iv) The wheel speeds $\dot{\phi}_R, \dot{\phi}_L$ required to follow a circular arc of radius $R_c = 0.5$ m at speed $v = 0.2$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Schematic and coordinate frame.** [1 mark for diagram and frame description]

[Full derivation as given in Section 3.1 of these notes — all algebraic steps, both wheel constraints, matrix form of the Jacobian.]

**Key equations (must be present in answer):**

$$ \dot{x} = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)\cos\theta $$

$$ \dot{y} = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)\sin\theta $$

$$ \dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{L}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L) $$

**State the two constraints applied:** [0.5 marks each]
1. **Rolling constraint:** $\dot{x}_c\cos\theta + \dot{y}_c\sin\theta = r\dot{\phi}$
2. **Lateral no-slip constraint:** $-\dot{x}_c\sin\theta + \dot{y}_c\cos\theta = 0$

[Full marks require the substitution, expansion, the use of $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$, and the final matrix form.]

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Chassis velocities (2 marks):**

$$ v = \frac{0.04}{2}(10 + 4) = 0.02 \times 14 = 0.28 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \omega = \frac{0.04}{0.25}(10 - 4) = 0.16 \times 6 = 0.96 \text{ rad/s} $$

**(ii) World-frame velocities (1.5 marks):** With $\theta = 0$, so $\cos\theta = 1$, $\sin\theta = 0$:

$$ \dot{x} = 0.28 \times 1 = 0.28 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \dot{y} = 0.28 \times 0 = 0 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \dot{\theta} = 0.96 \text{ rad/s} $$

**(iii) Pose after 2 s (Euler, $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, 20 steps) (2 marks):**

The general Euler update:

$$ x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t $$

$$ y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t $$

$$ \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t $$

Because $v$ and $\omega$ are constant (open-loop), we can use the closed-form kinematic integrals (or the sum over 20 steps). Using $v\cos\theta$ integrated numerically over the 20-step loop:

| Step k | $\theta_k$ (rad) | $\cos\theta_k$ | $x_k$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 |
| 1 | 0.096 | 0.9954 | 1.0278 |
| 2 | 0.192 | 0.9816 | 1.0553 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 20 | 1.920 | -0.3457 | ≈ 1.244 |

[Full credit: student may compute exactly using the closed-form expressions $x(t) = x_0 + \tfrac{v}{\omega}[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega t) - \sin\theta_0]$, $y(t) = y_0 - \tfrac{v}{\omega}[\cos(\theta_0 + \omega t) - \cos\theta_0]$.]

Final approximate pose:
$$ x(2) \approx 1.24 \text{ m}, \quad y(2) \approx 0.78 \text{ m}, \quad \theta(2) \approx 1.92 \text{ rad} \approx 110° $$

**(iv) Wheel speeds for $R_c = 0.5$ m, $v = 0.2$ m/s (1.5 marks):**

First, find the required $\omega$:

$$ \omega = \frac{v}{R_c} = \frac{0.2}{0.5} = 0.4 \text{ rad/s} $$

Then apply inverse kinematics:

$$ \dot{\phi}_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r} = \frac{2(0.2) + 0.4(0.25)}{2(0.04)} = \frac{0.4 + 0.1}{0.08} = \frac{0.5}{0.08} = 6.25 \text{ rad/s} $$

$$ \dot{\phi}_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r} = \frac{0.4 - 0.1}{0.08} = \frac{0.3}{0.08} = 3.75 \text{ rad/s} $$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice – KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** Derive the **inverse kinematics** of a differential drive robot. Show that pure rotation in place requires equal and opposite wheel speeds, and pure translation requires equal wheel speeds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a differential drive mobile robot with $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.40$ m:
- (i) The robot must follow a path requiring $v = 0.5$ m/s and $\omega = 0.25$ rad/s. Find the required $\dot{\phi}_R$ and $\dot{\phi}_L$.
- (ii) Compute the radius of curvature $R_c$ of the resulting path.
- (iii) For a new task, the robot must turn through $90°$ in 4 seconds while staying at the same position. Find $\dot{\phi}_R$ and $\dot{\phi}_L$.
- (iv) Comment on what happens if both wheel speeds are set equal to a non-zero value — can the robot turn? Justify using the formula. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) — Inverse Kinematics Derivation (7 marks):**

[Full derivation as in Section 3.2 of these notes.]

**Special cases:**

- **Pure rotation in place** ($v = 0$, $\omega \neq 0$): Set $v = 0$ in the inverse formulas: $\dot{\phi}_R = \tfrac{\omega L}{2r}$, $\dot{\phi}_L = -\tfrac{\omega L}{2r}$. The wheel speeds are **equal in magnitude, opposite in sign** — verifying the statement.

- **Pure translation** ($\omega = 0$, $v \neq 0$): Set $\omega = 0$ in the inverse formulas: $\dot{\phi}_R = \dot{\phi}_L = \tfrac{v}{r}$ — the two wheels turn at the **same speed** and the robot moves straight.

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Wheel speeds for $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega = 0.25$ rad/s (2 marks):**

$$ \dot{\phi}_R = \frac{2(0.5) + 0.25(0.40)}{2(0.05)} = \frac{1.0 + 0.1}{0.10} = \frac{1.1}{0.10} = 11.0 \text{ rad/s} $$

$$ \dot{\phi}_L = \frac{2(0.5) - 0.25(0.40)}{0.10} = \frac{1.0 - 0.1}{0.10} = 9.0 \text{ rad/s} $$

**(ii) Radius of curvature (1.5 marks):**

$$ R_c = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.5}{0.25} = 2.0 \text{ m} $$

**(iii) In-place 90° turn in 4 s (2 marks):**

The required angular velocity:

$$ \omega = \frac{\pi/2}{4} = \frac{\pi}{8} \approx 0.3927 \text{ rad/s}, \quad v = 0 $$

Wheel speeds:

$$ \dot{\phi}_R = \frac{0 + \omega L}{2r} = \frac{0.3927 \times 0.40}{0.10} = 1.5708 \text{ rad/s} \approx \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad/s} $$

$$ \dot{\phi}_L = -\frac{\omega L}{2r} = -1.5708 \text{ rad/s} \approx -\frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad/s} $$

**(iv) Equal non-zero wheel speeds (1.5 marks):**

If $\dot{\phi}_R = \dot{\phi}_L = \phi \neq 0$:

$$ \omega = \frac{r}{L}(\phi - \phi) = 0 \text{ rad/s} $$

$$ v = \frac{r}{2}(\phi + \phi) = r\phi \neq 0 $$

**Conclusion:** The robot moves in a **straight line** with no rotation. It cannot turn. The turning motion is generated *solely* by the *difference* in wheel speeds; if both speeds are identical, the difference is zero and the robot has no angular velocity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this exact topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up which wheel is RIGHT vs LEFT in inverse formulas.** The plus sign is conventionally on $\dot{\phi}_R$. Students who write $\dot{\phi}_L = \tfrac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$ lose 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ factors** when going from the $(v, \omega)$ representation to the world-frame velocities. The chassis is moving *along its own heading*, not the world axes.
> 3. **Confusing the wheelbase $L$ with the wheel diameter.** $L$ is the distance between wheel *centers*, not the wheel size.
> 4. **Treating $\theta$ as a constant** in time-integration problems. The heading evolves as $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \omega t$, so $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ must be re-evaluated at each Euler step.
> 5. **Writing the non-holonomic constraint as $\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2 = 0$** (a common confusion with stationary constraints). The correct form is $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
> 6. **Omitting units** in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing or wrong unit on numerical sub-questions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Robot pose:** $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — three scalars, two of which form a non-holonomic constraint.
- **Forward kinematics** maps **wheels → world** using the Jacobian $J(\theta)$, a $3 \times 2$ matrix.
- **Inverse kinematics** maps **world → wheels** via the closed-form formulas $\dot{\phi}_R = \tfrac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$ and $\dot{\phi}_L = \tfrac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$.
- **Chassis velocities** are $v = \tfrac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)$ and $\omega = \tfrac{r}{L}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)$.
- **Non-holonomic constraint** (no sideways slip): $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
- **Pure translation** requires equal wheel speeds; **pure rotation** requires equal-and-opposite wheel speeds.
- **Turning radius** is $R_c = v/\omega$, and tends to infinity for straight-line motion.
- **The Jacobian has rank 2, not 3** — the algebraic fingerprint of a non-holonomic system.
- **Discrete pose update (Euler):** $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \Delta t$, $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \Delta t$, $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$. This is the basis of **dead-reckoning odometry**.
- **Always include the heading angle $\theta$** when converting $(v, \omega)$ to world-frame velocities — it is the most-skipped step in KTU answer sheets.
- **Dimensions check:** $r$ in meters, $L$ in meters, $\dot{\phi}$ in rad/s, $v$ in m/s, $\omega$ in rad/s, $\theta$ in radians. Inconsistency = mark loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Robot Workspace - Degree of Freedom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mobile Robot Workspace - Degree of Freedom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mobile Robot Workspace — Formal Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBRT612 – Fundamentals of Robotics), the **Workspace** of a mobile robot is defined as the set of all points **$(x, y, \theta)$** in the robot's *Configuration Space (C-space)* that the robot's reference point can physically reach, subject to non-holonomic and holonomic kinematic constraints imposed by its wheel arrangement.

Unlike a fixed-base manipulator whose workspace is bounded by link lengths and joint limits, a **wheeled mobile robot (WMR)** typically operates on a planar (2D) ground and its workspace is governed by:
- The robot's geometry (wheelbase, track width, wheel radius).
- The **number, type, and arrangement of wheels** (fixed, steerable, castor).
- The **kinematic constraints** these wheels impose on the chassis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Configuration Space (C-Space) of a Mobile Robot**
> For a planar WMR on a flat surface, the C-space is **$SE(2)$** — the Special Euclidean Group in 2D. Every configuration is represented as:
>
> $$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> where **$(x, y)$** is the position of the reference point and **$\theta$** is the orientation angle. The *unconstrained* C-space therefore has **3 DOF** intrinsically.

## 1.2 Degree of Freedom (DOF) — Formal Definition

The **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a mobile robot is the number of **independent motions** (translations + rotations) the robot can execute at any given instant without violating the kinematic constraints imposed by its wheels and the ground.

> [!NOTE]
> **Grumbach & Shin (1995) Maneuverability Framework**
> The total **Degree of Maneuverability ($\delta_M$)** of a WMR is decomposed as:
>
> $$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$
>
> where **$\delta_m$** is the *Degree of Mobility* (independent translational/rotational motions of the chassis) and **$\delta_s$** is the *Degree of Steerability* (independent steering rotations of the wheels).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **pushing a shopping cart** in a supermarket:
- A shopping cart has **2 fixed rear wheels** and **2 castor front wheels** that swivel freely. You can drive it forward/backward and rotate it by pushing one wheel harder — but you **cannot move it sideways** like a crab. This is a **Differential Drive** with $\delta_M = 1$ (non-holonomic).

Now imagine driving a **Maruti car**:
- The front two wheels are steerable (you turn the steering wheel), and the rear two are fixed. The car can move forward/backward and turn (rotate), but it **cannot translate sideways**. This is an **Ackermann / Car Drive** with $\delta_M = 2$.

Finally, picture a **3-wheeled office chair** with omni-wheels or a mecanum-wheeled robot — it can move forward, sideways, and rotate independently. This is **Holonomic** with $\delta_M = 3$.

> [!TIP]
> **Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic — The Big Picture**
> A robot is **Holonomic** if $\delta_M = 3$ (equal to the DOF of $SE(2)$). It is **Non-Holonomic** if $\delta_M < 3$. A non-holonomic robot can still reach *any* $(x, y, \theta)$ in its C-space, but it cannot do so with arbitrary *velocity directions* — it must execute *paths* (e.g., parallel-park a car).

## 1.4 Standard Wheel Types & Their Contributions

| Wheel Type | Symbol | Constraints ($N_c$ contribution) | Steerability ($\delta_s$ contribution) | Real Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Standard Wheel** | F | **1** | 0 | Rear wheels of a car |
| **Steerable Standard Wheel** | S | **1** | 1 (shared axis reduces this) | Front wheels of a car |
| **Castor Wheel** | C | **0** | 0 | Front wheels of a shopping cart |
| **Swedish / Omni Wheel** | O | **0** | 0 | Mecanum-wheeled robots |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Castor wheel** has *no* fixed rolling axis — it can spin freely about its vertical swivel, so it imposes *no* kinematic constraint on the chassis. This is why a shopping cart glides effortlessly in any direction your hand pushes it.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Grumbach–Shin Framework — Step by Step

The framework quantifies maneuverability by counting **independent kinematic constraints** imposed on the chassis.

### Step 1: Count the Constraints ($N_c$)

Each **Fixed (F)** wheel contributes **1 constraint** (it cannot slip sideways).
Each **Steerable (S)** wheel contributes **1 constraint** + steering degree of freedom.
Each **Castor (C)** wheel contributes **0 constraints**.
Each **Swedish/Omni (O)** wheel contributes **0 constraints**.

The total number of constraints is the sum over all wheels:

$$N_c = \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i$$

where $c_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the constraint count of the $i^{th}$ wheel.

### Step 2: Compute the Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$)

The mobility measures how many of the 3 motions of $SE(2)$ survive the constraints:

$$\delta_m = 3 - N_c$$

- If $N_c = 2 \Rightarrow \delta_m = 1$ (drive forward/backward only).
- If $N_c = 1 \Rightarrow \delta_m = 2$ (drive + strafe).
- If $N_c = 0 \Rightarrow \delta_m = 3$ (free planar motion — omni-directional).

### Step 3: Compute the Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$)

Steerability is the number of **independent steering axes**. If $N_s$ steerable wheels are arranged such that they share a common steering axis, they contribute only **1** to $\delta_s$:

$$\delta_s \leq N_s \quad \text{(equality holds when steering axes are independent)}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Degree of Maneuverability ($\delta_M$)

$$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s = 3 - N_c + \delta_s$$

### Step 5: Classify the Robot

| $\delta_M$ Value | Classification | Kinematic Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **3** | **Holonomic** | Can move in any direction instantaneously |
| **2** | **Non-holonomic** | Can drive + rotate, but not strafe |
| **1** | **Highly Non-holonomic** | Can only drive forward/backward + rotate |
| **0** | **Immobile** | Robot cannot move |

## 2.2 Worked Examples of Common WMRs

| Robot Type | Wheels | $N_c$ | $\delta_s$ | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_M$ | Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Differential Drive** | 2F + 1C (or 2C) | 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | Non-holonomic |
| **Tricycle / Ackermann** | 1F + 2S (shared axis) | 3 | 1 | 0 → 1* | 1 | Non-holonomic |
| **Car (Ackermann 4W)** | 2F + 2S (shared) | 4 → 3* | 1 | 0 → 1* | 1 | Non-holonomic |
| **Synchro-Drive** | 3S (all synchronized) | 3 | 1 | 0 → 2* | 2 | Non-holonomic |
| **Omni-Directional (4 omni)** | 4O | 0 | 0 | 3 | 3 | **Holonomic** |
| **Mecanum Drive (4)** | 4 Omni-mech | 0 | 0 | 3 | 3 | **Holonomic** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Asterisk (*) Explained**
> For the Car, Ackermann, and Synchro-Drive cases, the *geometric dependency* of the wheels (e.g., all 4 car wheels must rotate in a coordinated Ackermann geometry) reduces the **effective** constraint count below the naïve sum. The framework uses the *rank* of the constraint Jacobian — not just the wheel count — to compute $\delta_m$. This is a classic KTU question trap.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mobility | $\delta_m = 3 - N_c$ | Dimensionless integer |
| 2 | Steerability | $\delta_s \leq N_s$ | $N_s$ = # steerable wheels |
| 3 | Maneuverability | $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | **< 3 ⟹ Non-holonomic** |
| 4 | C-space DOF (planar) | $\dim(SE(2)) = 3$ | $(x, y, \theta)$ |
| 5 | Workspace area (no obstacles) | $A_w = \pi R_{max}^2$ | $R_{max}$ = max reach from origin |
| 6 | Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) | $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\phi)}$ | $L$ = wheelbase, $\phi$ = steer angle |
| 7 | Forward Velocity (car) | $v = \dfrac{\omega_w r}{N}$ | $\omega_w$ = wheel angular vel, $r$ = wheel radius |
| 8 | Turning Radius (Ackermann) | $R = \dfrac{L}{\sin(\delta)}$ | $\delta$ = avg steering angle |
