&= 2\;\text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Tremor amplitude reaching the slave (3 marks):**

Magnitude of 1st-order LPF at $f = 10$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 10 = 62.83\;\text{rad/s} \\
\omega \tau &= 62.83 \times 0.1 = 6.283 \\
\vert H(j\omega)\vert &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (6.283)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 39.48}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{40.48}} \\
&= \frac{1}{6.362} \approx 0.157
\end{aligned}
$$

Scaled tremor amplitude at slave:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= k \cdot \vert H \vert \cdot A_m \\
&= 0.1 \times 0.157 \times 1\;\text{mm} \\
&\approx 0.0157\;\text{mm} \;\approx\; 15.7\;\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The 1 mm tremor at the hand becomes only **~15 µm** at the tool tip — well below human surgical resolution, demonstrating the effectiveness of scaling + filtering.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Classify **medical robots** based on their function. Explain in detail the working principle of a **rehabilitation robot** with a suitable example. State any **three kinematic/performance specifications** a rehabilitation robot must satisfy.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **tele-surgery system**. Derive the **force-scaling relation** for haptic feedback, stating the condition for *symmetric feel*.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification + Rehabilitation Robot (7 marks):**

**Classification (3 marks):**
1. **Surgical Robots** (e.g., Da Vinci, Mako) — perform or assist invasive procedures.
2. **Rehabilitation Robots** (e.g., Lokomat, Armeo) — restore motor function post-stroke/spinal injury.
3. **Assistive / Prosthetic Robots** (e.g., i-Limb) — replace missing limbs.
4. **Hospital Service Robots** (e.g., TUG, Xenex) — logistical and hygiene tasks.
5. **Diagnostic / Imaging Robots** (e.g., Airo) — image-guided biopsy and intervention.

**Rehabilitation Robot — Lokomat (4 marks):**
The **Lokomat** is a **gait-training exoskeleton** used for patients with spinal cord injury or stroke. It comprises:
- A **treadmill** (base) and an **overhead body-weight support** (BWS).
- A **bilateral robotic orthosis** strapped to the patient's legs, with actuators at the **hip** and **knee** joints (2 active DOF per leg).
- **Force sensors** at the foot plate to record patient effort.
- A **PC-based virtual-reality display** providing biofeedback.

**Working principle (2 marks):**
A pre-recorded **gait trajectory** (hip/knee angles vs. time) is generated from a normal-walking database. The robot's controllers track this trajectory in **position-controlled mode** for severely impaired patients, or in **impedance-controlled mode** for patients with partial recovery — allowing them to *initiate* the motion while the robot provides only the *difference* (assist-as-needed paradigm). Performance metrics (gait symmetry, step length, muscle activity via EMG) are logged for therapist review.

**Three Required Specifications (1 mark):**
1. **Workspace:** hip flexion 0°–120°, knee flexion 0°–130° to match physiological range.
2. **Force capacity:** support body weights up to **135 kg** with peak joint torques of **~80 Nm** at the hip.
3. **Backdrivability / Compliance:** impedance control with adjustable stiffness $K$ (10–1000 N/m) for safe human interaction.

**(b) Tele-surgery + Force-scaling derivation (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 flow above (Surgeon → Controller → Network → Robot → Patient → back).

**Working (2 marks):** The surgeon operates a master console; commands are encoded, transmitted over a high-bandwidth link (fibre-optic or 5G), and decoded at the patient-side cart. Stereo video from the endoscope is streamed back. **Haptic feedback** uses force sensors at the slave to recreate touch sensation at the master.

**Force-scaling derivation (3 marks):**

Let the master experience force $F_m$ for displacement $dx_m$ and the slave experience $F_s$ for $dx_s$.

Energy/work conservation in scaled coordinates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dW_{master} &= dW_{slave} \\
F_m \cdot dx_m &= F_s \cdot dx_s
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply motion scaling $dx_s = k \cdot dx_m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_m \cdot dx_m &= F_s \cdot (k \cdot dx_m) \\
F_m &= k \cdot F_s
\end{aligned}
$$

The **force-scaling gain** is therefore $s_f = k$. This is the **symmetric-feel condition**: the surgeon perceives the operation as if working directly on the tissue.

> Hence, if $k = 0.1$ (motion scaled 10× down), the force is also scaled 10× down — a 1 N tissue force is felt as 0.1 N at the hand, which is the desired scaling.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Medical Robotics Topic**

- ✅ **Definition**: A medical robot is a *reprogrammable, mechatronic* device that performs surgical / rehabilitative / diagnostic / assistive tasks under regulatory standards (ISO 13482, IEC 80601).
- ✅ **Master–Slave architecture** = three blocks: Surgeon Console + Control Unit + Patient-Side Cart.
- ✅ **RCM (Remote Centre of Motion)** = pivot point at the trocar; reduces 6-DOF master input to **4 effective DOF** at the tool tip (+ grip).
- ✅ **Motion scaling**: $x_s = k \cdot x_m$; typical $k = 2{:}1$ to $10{:}1$.
- ✅ **Velocity scaling**: $v_s = k \cdot v_m$.
- ✅ **Force scaling**: $F_m = s_f \cdot F_s$; **symmetric feel** requires $s_f = k$.
- ✅ **Tremor filter** = 1st-order LPF, transfer function $H(j\omega) = 1/(1 + j\omega\tau)$, with $f_c \approx 1$–$2$ Hz to cut physiological tremor at 8–12 Hz.
- ✅ **Surgical robot DOF** = **7** per arm (6 pose + 1 grip), with workspace of $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ around the RCM.
- ✅ **Positioning accuracy** in neurosurgery = **±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm**; general MIS = **±1 mm**.
- ✅ **Round-trip latency** for tele-surgery must be **< 200 ms** (preferably < 100 ms for force feedback stability).
- ✅ **Classifications**: Surgical, Rehabilitation, Assistive/Prosthetic, Hospital Service, Diagnostic, Training.
- ✅ **Examples to memorize**: Da Vinci (surgical), Lokomat (rehab), ReWalk (exoskeleton), i-Limb (prosthetic), TUG (hospital), CyberKnife (radio-surgery).
- ✅ **Safety layers**: hardware stops + software virtual fixtures + watchdog timer (< 50 ms fault response).
- ✅ **Kinematic constraints** of surgical robots include RCM, joint-limit avoidance, and singularity-free workspace within the body cavity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Material Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — MATERIAL HANDLING

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Handling (MH)** in robotics refers to the automated movement, protection, storage, and control of materials and products throughout the manufacturing, warehousing, and distribution process using robotic manipulators, grippers, conveyors, AGVs, and integrated sensor systems.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBRT612)** syllabus, Material Handling encompasses the use of industrial robots for tasks such as **pick-and-place, palletizing, depalletizing, packaging, machine loading/unloading, and conveying**. It is one of the most commercially significant application areas of robotics, accounting for the **largest installed base of industrial robots globally** (typically **30–40%** of all robotic deployments).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of a **massive Amazon warehouse**: thousands of shelves, packages, and conveyor belts. Now imagine a single super-precise mechanical "arm with fingers" picking a box from one conveyor and placing it on a pallet, a truck, or a different conveyor — **without ever getting tired, distracted, or making a placement error**. That mechanical arm, equipped with the right "hand" (gripper), the right "eyes" (sensors), and the right "brain" (controller), is the essence of a robotic material handling system.

Just as a human warehouse worker uses:
- **Eyes** → to locate the object
- **Hands** → to grasp and lift
- **Body** → to transport
- **Judgment** → to place carefully

A robotic material handling cell replicates each of these using **vision systems, end effectors, articulated arms, and intelligent controllers**.

### 1.3 Core Sub-Domains of Material Handling

| Sub-Domain | Function | Typical Robot Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Pick-and-Place** | Transfer parts between two locations | Cartesian, SCARA, 6-DOF |
| **Palletizing / Depalletizing** | Stacking/unstacking on pallets | Heavy-payload articulated |
| **Machine Tending** | Loading/unloading CNC, presses, injection moulding | Articulated, gantry |
| **Packaging** | Boxing, wrapping, sealing | Delta, SCARA, articulated |
| **Conveyor Tracking** | Synchronization with moving belts | Vision-guided 6-DOF |
| **Warehouse Automation** | Order picking, sorting | AGVs/AMRs with manipulators |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Material Handling

- **Payload Capacity (kg)** — maximum weight the robot can manipulate at rated performance.
- **Repeatability (mm)** — typically **±0.02 mm to ±0.5 mm** for industrial robots.
- **Cycle Time (s)** — time for one complete pick-transport-place operation.
- **Throughput (units/hour)** — production rate.
- **Reach / Work Envelope (m)** — spatial volume accessible by the end effector.
- **Duty Cycle (%)** — proportion of operational time the robot is actively moving.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently test the *classification* of material handling applications and the *types of end effectors* used. Memorize the table above and the gripper classifications in Section 1.5.

### 1.5 Classification of Material Handling Robots

By **kinematic structure**:
1. **Cartesian (Gantry)** — 3 linear axes; high payload, large workspace.
2. **Cylindrical** — 1 rotary + 2 linear axes.
3. **Spherical (Polar)** — 2 rotary + 1 linear axes.
4. **SCARA** — 2 rotary + 1 linear; excellent for vertical pick-and-place.
5. **Articulated (6-DOF)** — most flexible; dominates modern MH applications.
6. **Parallel (Delta)** — very high speed; used in packaging and food handling.
7. **AGV / AMR** — mobile platforms for transport across long distances.

By **function**:
- **Pick-and-Place Robots** (high speed, low payload).
- **Palletizing Robots** (medium-high payload, long reach, 4 axes typical).
- **Heavy Material Handling Robots** (payloads up to **2300 kg**, found in steel, foundry, and logistics industries).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 End Effectors (The "Hands" of the Robot)

The end effector is the device mounted on the robot's wrist flange that physically interacts with the workpiece. For material handling, end effectors are classified as:

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Grippers
- **Two-Finger Parallel Jaw Gripper** — most common; jaws move linearly.
- **Angular (V-Block) Gripper** — jaws rotate; good for cylindrical parts.
- **Three-Finger Concentric Gripper** — for round or spherical objects.
- **Multiple-Finger / Adaptive Grippers** — for irregular shapes.

### 2.1.2 Vacuum (Suction) Grippers
- Use **negative pressure** created by a vacuum pump or venturi.
- Suited for flat, smooth surfaces (sheet metal, glass, cardboard boxes).
- Generators: **pneumatic ejector, electric vacuum pump**.
- Standard vacuum level: **−0.6 to −0.9 bar**.

### 2.1.3 Magnetic Grippers
- **Electromagnetic** — require continuous current; risk of drop on power loss.
- **Permanent Magnet (with Electro-release)** — fail-safe, energy-efficient.
- Used for ferromagnetic workpieces.

### 2.1.4 Special End Effectors
- **Nail grippers, hook grippers, scoop grippers, adhesive grippers**, and **soft robotics grippers** for delicate or variable-geometry items.

## 2.2 Fundamental Physics of Grasping — The "Why" Behind the Math

When a gripper lifts an object, the analysis proceeds through these logical steps:

- **Step 1:** Identify the contact type (point, line, or area).
- **Step 2:** Apply Newton's third law — the gripper jaws exert a *normal force* $N$ on the object; the object pushes back equally.
- **Step 3:** Friction at the contact surface resists slipping. The friction force $F_f = \mu N$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction.
- **Step 4:** For the object to be held against gravity (and any inertial force), the *total* upward friction must equal the *downward* load.
- **Step 5:** If two jaws are used, **both** contribute friction, so $2\mu N \geq W + m a$.
- **Step 6:** If the gripper is accelerating, include the inertial term $ma$ in the equilibrium.
- **Step 7:** To find the *actuator* force $F$, account for the **mechanical advantage (MA)** of the gripper linkage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **coefficient of friction** is the single most important practical parameter. Typical values: rubber on steel $\mu \approx 0.7$–$1.0$, smooth steel on steel $\mu \approx 0.2$–$0.3$, soft foam $\mu \approx 0.4$–$0.6$. A 2× increase in $\mu$ halves the required gripping force.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all equations** a student is expected to know for KTU 2024 ESE on Material Handling. **Memorize the symbols, the boundary conditions, and the units.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum grip force (static, 2-jaw) | $N = \dfrac{W}{2\mu}$ | $N$: jaw normal force, $W$: weight, $\mu$: coeff. of friction | N, kg, – | Horizontal contact |
| 2 | Grip force with acceleration | $N = \dfrac{m(g+a)}{2\mu}$ | $m$: mass, $a$: vertical acceleration | N, kg, m/s² | Used for vertical lift |
| 3 | Actuator force with mechanical advantage | $F_{act} = \dfrac{N}{MA}$ | $F_{act}$: actuator input force, $MA$: mech. advantage | N | Linkage ratio $L_1/L_2$ |
| 4 | General grasp with $n$ contact points | $n\mu N \geq W + ma$ | $n$: number of contact patches | N | Includes irregular grippers |
| 5 | Vacuum holding force | $F_v = \Delta P \cdot A_{eff}$ | $\Delta P$: pressure differential, $A_{eff}$: effective suction area | N, Pa, m² | $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ |
| 6 | Required vacuum area | $A_{eff} = \dfrac{m(g+a) \cdot SF}{\Delta P \cdot n_c}$ | $SF$: safety factor (1.5–3), $n_c$: number of cups | m² | Cup diameter $d=\sqrt{4A/\pi}$ |
| 7 | Magnetic holding force | $F_m = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | $B$: flux density, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m | N, T, m² | Theoretical peak |
| 8 | Pallet boxes per pallet | $B = L \times W \times H_p$ | $L,W$: boxes per row, per column; $H_p$: layers | count | Excludes overhang |
| 9 | Cycle time per box | $t_b = \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{N_{boxes}}$ | $T_{cycle}$: total cycle, $N_{boxes}$: boxes handled | s | For batch process |
| 10 | Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{3600}{t_b}$ | Throughput in units/hour | boxes/hr | Plant KPI |
| 11 | Robot reach requirement | $R_{min} = \sqrt{d^2 + h^2}$ | $d$: horizontal pick distance, $h$: vertical pick height | m | For pallet edge |
| 12 | Required payload | $m_p = m_{obj} + m_{gripper} + SF$ | Includes safety margin | kg | Typically 1.2–1.5× |
| 13 | Work envelope volume (cylindrical) | $V = \pi R^2 H$ | $R$: reach, $H$: vertical travel | m³ | For Cartesian robot |
| 14 | Inertial load (lift accel) | $F_i = m \cdot a$ | Effective additional force | N | For accel > 1g, consider jerk |
| 15 | Energy to lift | $E = m g h$ | Per lift | J | For power calculations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** In all grip force derivations, examiners expect the **safety factor (SF)** explicitly written. Use $SF = 1.5$ to $3.0$ depending on the application (light packaging → $1.5$; heavy industrial → $2.5$–$3.0$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Palletizing tire rims, engine blocks, body panels using 6-axis articulated robots with **payloads from 50 kg to over 1,000 kg**.
- **Food and Beverage:** Delta robots with vacuum grippers handle biscuits, chocolates, and bottles at speeds of **>120 picks/minute**.
- **E-Commerce Warehousing:** Kiva/Amazon robots combine AMR mobility with shelving manipulation for **order picking rates >1,000 orders/hour**.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Cleanroom-compatible stainless steel grippers handle vials and blister packs.
- **Steel and Foundries:** Heavy-duty robots with magnetic grippers handle red-hot billets (**up to 800 °C**) using heat shielding.

> [!NOTE]
> The same physics equations above are used to *select* a robot for a real factory. A typical industrial cell designer computes the required payload, reach, grip force, and cycle time, then matches them against manufacturer catalogues (e.g., FANUC, ABB, KUKA, Yaskawa).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **exhaustive and KTU-board-ready**. Each algebraic transition is justified with a *conversion-logic* text line. Multi-line equations use a single `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` block.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Gripping Force for a Two-Jaw Parallel Gripper

**Problem Setup:** A parallel-jaw gripper holds a rectangular block of mass $m$ vertically (lifted against gravity with acceleration $a$). The coefficient of friction between the jaw and the block is $\mu$. Derive the minimum normal gripping force $N$ that must be applied at each jaw.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram (FBD) Identification**

Let us identify the forces acting on the block:
- Weight of block acting downward: $W = m g$.
- Inertial force due to upward acceleration: $F_i = m a$.
- Normal force at left jaw acting to the right: $N_1$.
- Normal force at right jaw acting to the left: $N_2$.
- Friction force at left jaw acting upward: $f_1 = \mu N_1$.
- Friction force at right jaw acting upward: $f_2 = \mu N_2$.

**Step 2 — Apply Symmetry**

Because the block is symmetrically held by identical jaws, the normal forces are equal:
$N_1 = N_2 = N$. The friction forces are also equal: $f_1 = f_2 = \mu N$.

**Step 3 — Apply Vertical Equilibrium**

For the block not to slip, the total upward friction force must equal (or exceed) the total downward load (weight + inertia):
$2 \mu N \geq m g + m a$.

**Step 4 — Factor out the Mass**

Group the gravitational and inertial terms:
$2 \mu N \geq m (g + a)$.

**Step 5 — Solve for N**

Divide both sides by $2\mu$:
$N \geq \dfrac{m (g + a)}{2 \mu}$.

**Step 6 — Final Result**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{min} &= \frac{m (g + a)}{2 \mu} \cdot SF
\end{aligned}
$$

where $SF$ is the design safety factor (typically $1.5$ to $2.0$).

**Step 7 — Numerical Illustration**

Suppose $m = 5$ kg, $a = 2$ m/s², $\mu = 0.4$, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $SF = 2.0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{min} &= \frac{5 \cdot (9.81 + 2)}{2 \cdot 0.4} \cdot 2.0 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5 \cdot 11.81}{0.8} \cdot 2.0 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{59.05}{0.8} \cdot 2.0 \\[4pt]
&= 73.8125 \cdot 2.0 \\[4pt]
&= 147.625 \text{ N per jaw}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total actuator force depends on the linkage's mechanical advantage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic — Why divide by 2?** Because friction acts at *both* jaws. A common student error is to write $N = m(g+a)/\mu$ (forgetting the factor of 2). Examiners will deduct marks for this.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mechanical Advantage and Actuator Force

**Problem Setup:** A toggle-linkage gripper has the geometry where the input link is $L_1 = 80$ mm and the output link is $L_2 = 20$ mm. Find the mechanical advantage and the required pneumatic cylinder force $F_{act}$ if the gripping force needed at the jaw is $N = 150$ N.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition of Mechanical Advantage**

The mechanical advantage of a toggle linkage is the ratio of the output link length to the input link length:
$MA = \dfrac{L_2}{L_1}$ (for a force-amplifying toggle).

**Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MA &= \frac{20 \text{ mm}}{80 \text{ mm}} = 0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this gives $MA < 1$, which is wrong. A gripper toggle amplifies force, so the input link should be longer. Re-examining: for **force amplification** in a toggle mechanism, the geometry gives:

$MA = \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$ (the input link is the longer link moving through a small angle to produce a large output force on the short link).

**Step 3 — Correct Application**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MA &= \frac{L_1}{L_2} = \frac{80}{20} = 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Required Actuator Force**

The output (gripping) force $N$ equals the actuator force $F_{act}$ multiplied by the mechanical advantage:
$N = F_{act} \cdot MA$.

Solve for $F_{act}$:
$F_{act} = \dfrac{N}{MA} = \dfrac{150}{4.0} = 37.5$ N.

**Step 5 — Result with Safety Factor**

Applying $SF = 1.5$ for cylinder friction and seal drag:
$F_{act, design} = 37.5 \times 1.5 = 56.25$ N.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vacuum Gripper Suction Cup Sizing

**Problem Setup:** A vacuum gripper must lift a glass sheet of mass $m = 10$ kg. The vacuum generator produces $\Delta P = 60$ kPa below atmospheric. Using 4 suction cups and a safety factor of 2.5, find the required diameter of each cup.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total Lifting Force Required**

The total holding force must exceed the weight multiplied by the safety factor:
$F_{total} = m \cdot g \cdot SF = 10 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 = 245.25$ N.

**Step 2 — Force Per Cup**

Distribute equally over $n_c = 4$ cups:
$F_{cup} = \dfrac{F_{total}}{n_c} = \dfrac{245.25}{4} = 61.3125$ N.

**Step 3 — Effective Area Per Cup**

Using $F_{cup} = \Delta P \cdot A_{eff}$:
$A_{eff} = \dfrac{F_{cup}}{\Delta P} = \dfrac{61.3125}{60{,}000} = 1.0219 \times 10^{-3}$ m² = $1021.9$ mm².

**Step 4 — Diameter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{eff} &= \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \\[4pt]
d &= \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{eff}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1021.9}{\pi}} = \sqrt{1301.1} \approx 36.07 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Selection**

Round up to a standard cup size: $d = 40$ mm. This is a common KTU-style question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Palletizing Throughput and Cycle Time

**Problem Setup:** A palletizing robot stacks cardboard boxes onto a standard pallet. Each box footprint is $400 \times 300$ mm. The pallet is $1200 \times 1000$ mm. Boxes are stacked 5 layers high. The robot completes one full pallet in 90 seconds. Find: (a) boxes per pallet, (b) cycle time per box, (c) hourly throughput.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Boxes per Layer (along Length)**

$n_L = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1200}{400} \right\rfloor = 3$ boxes (length direction).

**Step 2 — Boxes per Layer (along Width)**

$n_W = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1000}{300} \right\rfloor = 3$ boxes (width direction).

**Step 3 — Boxes per Layer**

$n_{layer} = n_L \times n_W = 3 \times 3 = 9$ boxes.

**Step 4 — Total Boxes per Pallet**

$B = n_{layer} \times H_p = 9 \times 5 = 45$ boxes.

**Step 5 — Cycle Time per Box**

$t_b = \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{B} = \dfrac{90}{45} = 2.0$ s/box.

**Step 6 — Throughput**

$\eta = \dfrac{3600}{t_b} = \dfrac{3600}{2.0} = 1800$ boxes/hour.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Robot Reach and Workspace Volume for Palletizing

**Problem Setup:** A 4-axis palletizing robot must reach the far corner of a pallet placed at distance $d = 1.8$ m horizontally and $h = 1.6$ m vertically from the robot base. Find the minimum reach $R_{min}$ and the cylindrical work-envelope volume.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Reach via Diagonal**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \sqrt{d^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{(1.8)^2 + (1.6)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{3.24 + 2.56} = \sqrt{5.80} \approx 2.408 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Select Robot**

Standard industrial reach ≥ $2.5$ m is selected (allowing 5% margin).

**Step 3 — Work Envelope Volume**

For a cylindrical envelope of radius $R = 2.5$ m and height $H = 3.0$ m:
$V = \pi R^2 H = \pi (2.5)^2 (3.0) = \pi \cdot 6.25 \cdot 3.0 = 58.9$ m³.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The diagonal formula $R = \sqrt{d^2 + h^2}$ comes from the Pythagorean theorem applied to the right triangle formed by horizontal pick distance and vertical lift height. The reach must cover the **3D Euclidean distance** from the robot's base flange center to the *farthest* pick point.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Gripper Selection

| Application | Best Gripper | Why | Typical Payload | Cycle Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bottles in beverage line | Vacuum / Soft | Flat, smooth, fast | 0.5–2 kg | < 0.4 s |
| Steel sheets in press shop | Magnetic (EM) | Flat, ferromagnetic | up to 500 kg | 1.5–3 s |
| Cartons in warehouse | Mechanical (parallel) | Variable sizes | 1–25 kg | 0.8–2 s |
| Engine block transfer | Mechanical (3-finger) | Heavy, round-ish | 50–250 kg | 4–6 s |
| Bakery bread handling | Vacuum (foam seal) | Soft, porous | 0.2–1 kg | < 0.5 s |
| Glass panels | Vacuum (multi-cup) | Smooth, fragile | 5–50 kg | 2–4 s |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Material Handling Cell

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram shows the functional flow and data/control pathways between subsystems of a typical robotic material handling work cell.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[INCOMING CONVEYOR] --> B1[PART PRESENCE SENSOR]
    B1 --> C1[VISION INSPECTION UNIT]
    C1 --> D1[ROBOT CONTROLLER CPU]
    C1 --> E1[PART ORIENTATION MODULE]
    D1 --> F1[ROBOTIC ARM 6 DOF]
    F1 --> G1[END EFFECTOR GRIPPER]
    G1 --> H1[WORKPIECE]
    H1 --> I1[OUTGOING CONVEYOR]
    D1 --> J1[SAFETY PLC]
    J1 --> K1[EMERGENCY STOP]
    J1 --> L1[LIGHT CURTAIN]
    D1 --> M1[HMI TOUCHSCREEN]
    D1 --> N1[IO MODULE FIELDBUS]
    N1 --> O1[REMOTE SCADA MONITOR]
    I1 --> P1[QUALITY CHECK STATION]
    P1 --> D1
    M1 --> D1
    L1 --> J1
    K1 --> J1
```

### Reading the Diagram
- **A1 → B1 → C1**: A workpiece arrives, is detected, and is visually inspected.
- **D1**: The central controller coordinates all subsystems in real time.
- **F1 → G1 → H1**: The arm moves the gripper to grasp the workpiece.
- **H1 → I1**: The object is placed on the outgoing conveyor.
- **J1 (Safety PLC)**: Receives emergency stops and light-curtain signals.
- **M1 / O1**: Operator interface and remote monitoring.

---

## 4.2 Palletizing Work-Cell Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[INFEED CONVEYOR] --> B2[ROBOT BASE PEDESTAL]
    B2 --> C2[PALLET STACKING AREA 1]
    B2 --> D2[PALLET STACKING AREA 2]
    B2 --> E2[SLIP SHEET DISPENSER]
    C2 --> F2[OUTFEED ROLLER CONVEYOR]
    D2 --> F2
    F2 --> G2[WRAPPING MACHINE]
    G2 --> H2[LABELING AND DISPATCH]
```

### Reading the Diagram
- The **pedestal-mounted robot** serves two pallet stations alternately, maximizing duty cycle.
- The **slip sheet dispenser** inserts cardboard between layers for stability.
- The **outfeed roller conveyor** moves completed pallets to wrapping and labeling.

---

## 4.3 Material Handling Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START] --> S2[Identify workpiece geometry]
    S2 --> S3[Determine mass and surface]
    S3 --> S4{Workpiece shape}
    S4 -->|Flat Smooth| S5[Vacuum Gripper]
    S4 -->|Ferromagnetic| S6[Magnetic Gripper]
    S4 -->|Irregular 3D| S7[Mechanical Multi-finger]
    S4 -->|Cylindrical| S8[Angular 3-jaw Gripper]
    S4 -->|Delicate Soft| S9[Soft Pneumatic Gripper]
    S5 --> S10[Select robot reach and payload]
    S6 --> S10
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Compute cycle time and throughput]
    S11 --> S12[Validate safety and work envelope]
    S12 --> S13[Deploy in work cell]
```

---

## 4.4 End Effector Selection Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Material Handling Robot Cell] --> Q2[End Effector Subsystem]
    Q2 --> R1[Mechanical Subtype]
    Q2 --> R2[Vacuum Subtype]
    Q2 --> R3[Magnetic Subtype]
    Q2 --> R4[Special Subtype]
    R1 --> R1A[Parallel Jaw]
    R1 --> R1B[Angular 3 Jaw]
    R1 --> R1C[Adaptive Multi-finger]
    R2 --> R2A[Single Cup]
    R2 --> R2B[Multi Cup Bellow]
    R3 --> R3A[Electromagnet]
    R3 --> R3B[Permanent with Electro Release]
    R4 --> R4A[Needle Gripper for Textiles]
    R4 --> R4B[Adhesive Gripper]
    R4 --> R4C[Scoop for Granular]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Sample]**
> **Q1. Define material handling in the context of industrial robotics and list four major sub-applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Material handling in robotics refers to the automated movement, storage, protection, and control of materials and products using robotic manipulators and integrated sensor systems. Four major sub-applications are: (i) **Pick-and-Place**, (ii) **Palletizing / Depalletizing**, (iii) **Machine Tending** (loading/unloading), and (iv) **Packaging / Conveying**. *(Full 3 marks requires definition + 4 sub-applications.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Sample]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between a mechanical gripper and a vacuum gripper. State one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **mechanical gripper** uses jaws (2-finger, 3-finger, or multi-finger) that physically clamp the workpiece; it relies on friction between jaw and part. Typical application: grasping engine blocks, cartons, or irregular parts. A **vacuum gripper** uses negative pressure to hold a workpiece against a suction cup; it requires a smooth, non-porous surface. Typical application: lifting glass sheets or cardboard boxes. Mechanical grippers are versatile for varied geometries; vacuum grippers excel on flat, smooth, delicate items.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Sample]**
> **(a)** Explain the different types of end effectors used in robotic material handling, with diagrams and suitable applications. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** A two-jaw parallel gripper is required to lift a steel block of mass **8 kg** vertically with an acceleration of **1.5 m/s²**. The coefficient of friction between jaw and block is **0.35**. Determine: (i) the minimum gripping force per jaw, (ii) the required pneumatic cylinder force if the toggle linkage has a mechanical advantage of **4**, and (iii) the cylinder force with a safety factor of **1.8**. Use $g = 9.81$ m/s². *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Types of End Effectors (7 marks):**

- **Mechanical Grippers** — two-jaw, three-jaw, multi-finger; used for variable geometries.
- **Vacuum (Suction) Grippers** — flat, smooth parts (glass, sheet metal, cardboard).
- **Magnetic Grippers** — ferromagnetic parts (steel sheets, iron castings).
- **Special End Effectors** — needle, scoop, adhesive, soft robotics.
- Diagrams (2-finger parallel, 3-jaw angular, vacuum cup, electromagnet) — 1 mark each block.
- Applications — 1 mark each.

**(b) Gripping Force Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $m = 8$ kg, $a = 1.5$ m/s², $\mu = 0.35$, $MA = 4$, $SF = 1.8$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Minimum gripping force per jaw:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{min} &= \frac{m(g+a)}{2\mu} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8 \times (9.81 + 1.5)}{2 \times 0.35} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8 \times 11.31}{0.7} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{90.48}{0.7} \\[4pt]
&= 129.26 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cylinder force (without SF):**

$F_{act} = \dfrac{N}{MA} = \dfrac{129.26}{4} = 32.31$ N.

**Step 3 — Cylinder force (with SF = 1.8):**

$F_{act, design} = 32.31 \times 1.8 = 58.16$ N.

**Final Answer:** $N_{min} \approx 129.26$ N, $F_{act} \approx 32.31$ N, $F_{act, design} \approx 58.16$ N.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a robotic material handling work cell. Identify and explain the role of at least **six** subsystems. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** A palletizing robot stacks boxes of size $400 \times 300 \times 200$ mm on a standard pallet of $1200 \times 1000$ mm. The stack height is 5 layers. If the robot completes a full pallet in **75 seconds**, calculate: (i) total boxes per pallet, (ii) cycle time per box, and (iii) throughput in boxes/hour. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Functional Architecture (7 marks):** Diagram as in Section 4.1. Six subsystems to explain:

1. **Infeed conveyor** — delivers parts.
2. **Part presence sensor** — inductive / photoelectric.
3. **Vision system** — locates and inspects parts.
4. **Robot controller (CPU)** — central coordination.
5. **Robot arm + end effector** — physical manipulation.
6. **Safety PLC + light curtain + E-stop** — operator protection.
7. **HMI / SCADA** — operator interface and monitoring.

(1 mark per subsystem explanation; 1 mark for diagram.)

**(b) Palletizing Calculations (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Boxes per layer (length):** $n_L = 1200 / 400 = 3$ boxes.

**Step 2 — Boxes per layer (width):** $n_W = 1000 / 300 = 3.33 \rightarrow 3$ boxes (use floor for fit).

**Step 3 — Boxes per layer:** $n_{layer} = 3 \times 3 = 9$.

**Step 4 — Total boxes per pallet:** $B = 9 \times 5 = 45$ boxes.

**Step 5 — Cycle time per box:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_b &= \frac{75}{45} = 1.667 \text{ s/box}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Throughput:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{3600}{1.667} \approx 2160 \text{ boxes/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** 45 boxes/pallet, $\approx 1.67$ s/box, $\approx 2160$ boxes/hour.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor (SF)** in grip force and vacuum calculations — examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for omission.
> 2. **Using the wrong "2" in the denominator** — students often confuse $N = m(g+a)/\mu$ with $N = m(g+a)/(2\mu)$. The factor of 2 is from the **two jaws**, not from arbitrary symmetry.
> 3. **Unit errors** — mixing kgf and N, or omitting $\Delta P$ units (Pa vs kPa). Always convert kPa → Pa ($1$ kPa $= 1000$ Pa) before computation.
> 4. **Ignoring the mechanical advantage direction** — in a toggle linkage, MA = $L_1/L_2$ (long over short) gives force amplification. Using the inverse produces a *fractional* MA, which is physically meaningless for force amplification.
> 5. **Pallet pattern errors** — using the *full* decimal of $1000/300 = 3.33$ as 3.33 boxes instead of taking the floor (3 boxes) for a *fit*-based pattern.
> 6. **Not writing the conversion-logic sentence** — KTU valuation keys expect a one-line justification for each algebraic step.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following bullets the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **Material Handling (MH)** = automated movement, protection, storage, and control of materials using robots.
- ✅ **Largest robotic application segment** (~30–40% of installations).
- ✅ **Six sub-applications**: Pick-and-Place, Palletizing, Depalletizing, Packaging, Machine Tending, Conveyor Tracking.
- ✅ **Six robot types for MH**: Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, SCARA, Articulated, Parallel (Delta), plus AGV/AMR.
- ✅ **End Effector Categories**: Mechanical, Vacuum, Magnetic, Special.
- ✅ **Mechanical Subtypes**: 2-finger parallel, angular 3-jaw, multi-finger adaptive, needle.
- ✅ **Vacuum Gripper Equation**: $F_v = \Delta P \cdot A_{eff}$. Typical $\Delta P$ = 60–80 kPa.
- ✅ **Magnetic Gripper**: Use permanent magnet with electro-release for fail-safe operation.
- ✅ **Minimum Grip Force (Vertical)**: $N = m(g+a)/(2\mu)$.
- ✅ **Actuator Force**: $F_{act} = N / MA$, where $MA = L_1/L_2$ for toggle linkage.
- ✅ **Safety Factor**: Always include $SF = 1.5$ to $3.0$ in design calculations.
- ✅ **Pallet Pattern**: $B = n_L \times n_W \times H_p$ (with floor function for partial fits).
- ✅ **Throughput**: $\eta = 3600 / t_b$ (boxes/hour).
- ✅ **Robot Reach**: $R = \sqrt{d^2 + h^2}$ (diagonal distance).
- ✅ **Work Envelope**: $V = \pi R^2 H$ for cylindrical envelope.
- ✅ **Typical friction coefficients**: rubber on steel = 0.7–1.0; steel on steel = 0.2–0.3; foam = 0.4–0.6.
- ✅ **Standard reach for palletizing robots**: 2.5 m to 3.2 m.
- ✅ **Standard payload for palletizing**: 50 kg to 1,500 kg.
- ✅ **Industrial examples**: FANUC M-2000iA (2,300 kg payload), ABB IRB 760 (450 kg, 3.2 m reach), KUKA KR 1000 (1,000 kg).
- ✅ **Safety standards**: ISO 10218 (industrial robot safety), ISO/TS 15066 (collaborative robots), ANSI/RIA R15.06.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pick and Place Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pick and Place Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Pick and Place Robot** is a class of **servo-controlled or non-servo (pneumatic/hydraulic) industrial robot** whose primary function is the **gripping (picking) of a payload from a defined source location and the subsequent positioning (placing) of that payload at a defined destination location**, following a pre-programmed, often open-loop or semi-closed-loop, point-to-point (PTP) trajectory. According to the KTU 2024 scheme for *OEBRT612 – Fundamentals of Robotics*, a pick and place robot is classified as a **low-degree-of-freedom (typically 2 to 4 DOF)**, **repetitive-task industrial manipulator** used extensively in **transferring, sorting, loading/unloading, and packaging operations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Pick and place robots are a direct application of the **kinematic chain**, **end-effector design**, and **PTP motion control** concepts studied in earlier modules. They are frequently asked in KTU examinations as **application-level questions (CO3, CO4 mapping).**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Factory Arm" Analogy — Think of a Human Hand Reaching for a Chocolate on a Conveyor Belt:**
> Imagine you are standing beside a moving conveyor belt carrying assorted chocolates. You reach out (**arm motion**), open your hand (**gripper opens**), close your fingers around one chocolate (**pick operation**), lift it (**vertical motion**), swing your arm sideways (**horizontal motion**), and drop it into a packing box (**place operation**). You then return your hand to its original position to pick the next chocolate.
>
> A **pick and place robot does EXACTLY this — but it is:**
> - **Faster** (cycle time often 2–6 seconds).
> - **More accurate** (repeatability of $\pm 0.05$ mm in servo versions).
> - **Tireless** (works 24/7 in industrial environments).
> - **Programmable** (the motion is stored as a sequence of waypoints).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea (Board-Exam Favourite):**
> A pick and place robot is essentially a **"smart arm"** that performs the **most elementary industrial task — moving an object from point A to point B — with high speed, precision, and repeatability.** It serves as the **gateway application** that introduces students to industrial robotics, CNC-like positioning, and pneumatic/servo actuation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics for Pick and Place Robots (as per ISO 9283 and KTU reference):**
> - **Payload Capacity:** Typically **0.5 kg to 50 kg** for standard industrial pick and place units.
> - **Repeatability:** Ranges from **$\pm 0.025$ mm (high-precision servo)** to **$\pm 1$ mm (low-cost pneumatic)**.
> - **Reach (Working Envelope Radius):** From **100 mm (mini)** to **over 3000 mm (gantry)**.
> - **Cycle Time:** **2 to 6 seconds per pick-and-place cycle** is industry standard.
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Typically **2 to 4 DOF** ($X, Y, Z$, plus optional wrist roll).
> - **Standard Operating Speed:** **1 m/s to 3 m/s** in Cartesian configurations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown — Anatomy of a Pick and Place Robot

A pick and place robot is composed of **five (5) principal subsystems**. The following bulleted hierarchy mirrors the KTU board examiner's expected answer structure:

### A. Mechanical Structure (Arm / Manipulator)
- The **kinematic chain** provides the physical reach and motion.
- Common configurations: **Cartesian (PPP)**, **Cylindrical (RPP)**, **Spherical/Polar (RRP)**, **Articulated (RRR)**, and **SCARA (RRP + vertical)**.
- For pick and place, the **SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)** and **Cartesian gantry** dominate due to their **rigidity in the vertical axis** and **compliance in the horizontal plane**.

### B. Actuation System (Prime Mover)
- **Pneumatic actuators** (compressed air, 4–8 bar) — used in **non-servo, bang-bang (end-to-end) pick and place** units. Cheap, fast stroke, but limited to two fixed end positions per axis.
- **Servo-electric motors (BLDC / Stepper with encoder feedback)** — used in **servo pick and place** for variable positioning along the path.
- **Hydraulic actuators** — rare in modern pick and place, used only for very high payloads (>50 kg).

### C. End Effector (Gripper)
- **Mechanical grippers** (two-finger parallel, three-finger angular) — most common.
- **Vacuum grippers** (suction cup) — for smooth, flat surfaces (glass, sheet metal, PCB).
- **Magnetic grippers** — for ferrous materials.
- **Soft/compliant grippers** — for delicate or irregular items (fruits, bakery).

### D. Sensing System
- **Limit switches / Proximity sensors** — define the two end positions in pneumatic versions.
- **Encoders (rotary / linear)** — provide positional feedback in servo versions.
- **Vision systems (2D/3D cameras)** — for **random bin picking** advanced applications.
- **Force/Torque sensors** — for adaptive gripping force.

### E. Controller
- **PLC-based** (ladder logic) for pneumatic pick and place.
- **Microcontroller / Embedded board (Arduino, PLC, dedicated robot controller)** for servo versions.
- Stores the **sequence of I/O signals and motion commands**.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Trigger / Start Signal:** A conveyor sensor detects an incoming object (photoelectric / inductive / capacitive).
2. **Approach Phase:** The arm moves from the **HOME position** to the **PICK position** along a pre-defined trajectory.
3. **Gripper Actuation:** The end effector closes (or vacuum is applied) to grasp the object with a controlled gripping force $F_g$.
4. **Lift / Retract:** The arm retracts vertically to clear obstacles and avoid collision.
5. **Transfer Phase:** Horizontal movement of the arm from PICK location to PLACE location.
6. **Place Phase:** The arm descends to the PLACE position, the gripper opens (or vacuum is released), and the object is deposited.
7. **Return Phase:** The arm returns to HOME, ready for the next cycle.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM READY — All High-Yield Equations for Pick and Place Robots:**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Degrees of Freedom (Gruebler's Eq.)** | $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | dimensionless | For planar mechanisms; $n$ = links, $j_1$ = full joints, $j_2$ = half joints |
| 2 | **Workspace Volume (Cartesian)** | $V = L_x \times L_y \times L_z$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Cartesian/gantry pick and place |
| 3 | **Cycle Time** | $T_c = t_{pick} + t_{transfer} + t_{place} + t_{return}$ | seconds (s) | Productivity / throughput calculation |
| 4 | **Throughput (Picks per Hour)** | $\eta = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | picks/hour | Production line planning |
| 5 | **Gripper Gripping Force** | $F_g = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \mu}$ | Newtons (N) | Design of mechanical gripper; $W$ = weight, $n$ = fingers, $\mu$ = friction coefficient |
| 6 | **Cylinder Thrust (Pneumatic)** | $F_{thrust} = P \cdot A_{piston} - P_{back} \cdot A_{rod}$ | Newtons (N) | Sizing pneumatic actuator |
| 7 | **Servo Motor Torque** | $\tau = I \cdot \alpha + \tau_{friction} + \tau_{load}$ | N·m | Joint torque requirement |
| 8 | **Resolution (Linear)** | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{\text{Lead}}{\text{Steps per rev}}$ | mm/step | Stepper-driven axis positioning |
| 9 | **Repeatability vs Accuracy** | $\text{Repeatability} \le \text{Accuracy}$ (generally) | mm | Performance specification |
| 10 | **SCARA Reach (Radial)** | $r = L_1 + L_2$ (max), $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ (min) | m | SCARA robot arm dimensioning |

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Character Note for Tables:** All absolute-value expressions (e.g., $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$) have been written with the `\vert` macro to prevent breaking the markdown table. The same convention is used in all tables below.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters in the industry:**
> Pick and place robots are the **backbone of modern manufacturing**:
> - **Automotive industry:** Pick and place of engine components, dashboard panels, glass.
> - **Electronics (SMT):** Surface Mount Technology pick and place machines place components onto PCBs at speeds exceeding **50,000 components per hour**.
> - **Food & Beverage:** Sorting, packaging, palletizing.
> - **Pharmaceuticals:** Vial and blister-pack handling.
> - **Warehousing / Logistics:** Amazon-style bin picking and conveyor transfer.
>
> **Economic impact:** Reduces labour cost by **40–60%**, increases throughput by **200–300%**, and lowers defect rates by eliminating human error.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition below is written explicitly. No steps are skipped. The student should be able to reproduce the entire derivation in a KTU University Exam (ESE) answer sheet.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gripper Gripping Force for a Two-Finger Parallel Gripper

### Problem Setup
A pick and place robot uses a **two-finger parallel jaw gripper** to lift a steel component of mass $m = 2.0$ kg. The coefficient of friction between the gripper pads and the object surface is $\mu = 0.45$. The gravitational acceleration is $g = 9.81$ m/s². Determine the **minimum gripping force per finger** such that the object does not slip during vertical lift.

### Given Data
- Mass of object: $m = 2.0$ kg
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.45$
- Number of fingers in contact: $n = 2$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the weight of the object (downward force to be supported).**

The weight $W$ of the object is the product of its mass and the gravitational acceleration:

$$W = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values to compute $W$.**

$$W = 2.0 \times 9.81 = 19.62 \text{ N}$$

This is the **total downward force** that the gripping friction must overcome.

**Step 3: Write the friction force equilibrium condition.**

For the object to be held without slipping, the **total static friction force** generated by both fingers must be greater than or equal to the weight. With $n$ fingers, each applying a normal gripping force $F_g$, the total friction force is:

$$F_{friction} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F_g$$

**Step 4: Apply the no-slip condition (limit equilibrium).**

At the verge of slipping, friction equals weight:

$$n \cdot \mu \cdot F_g = W$$

**Step 5: Solve for the gripping force per finger $F_g$.**

Rearranging the equation for $F_g$:

$$F_g = \frac{W}{n \cdot \mu}$$

**Step 6: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$F_g = \frac{19.62}{2 \times 0.45} = \frac{19.62}{0.90}$$

**Step 7: Final numerical evaluation.**

$$F_g = 21.80 \text{ N (per finger)}$$

### Engineering Insight
> [!TIP]
> In practice, a **safety factor (SF) of 2 to 3** is applied to account for dynamic loading (acceleration during lift), vibration, and surface irregularities. So the design force per finger becomes:
> $$F_{g,\text{design}} = SF \cdot F_g = 2.5 \times 21.80 = 54.5 \text{ N}$$
> This is the force the pneumatic cylinder or servo motor must deliver through the gripper mechanism.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cycle Time and Throughput of a Pneumatic Pick and Place Unit

### Problem Setup
A 3-DOF Cartesian (gantry) pick and place robot must transfer components from a feeder (Station A) to an inspection conveyor (Station B). The horizontal transfer distance is $L = 1.2$ m. The vertical pick stroke is $h_p = 0.15$ m, and the vertical place stroke is $h_d = 0.15$ m. The horizontal speed of the gantry is $v_h = 1.5$ m/s, and the vertical speed (pneumatic extension/retraction) is $v_v = 0.5$ m/s. The gripper open/close time is $t_g = 0.3$ s, and the dwell time at each station is $t_d = 0.2$ s. Compute the **cycle time** and the **throughput in picks per hour**.

### Given Data
- Horizontal travel distance: $L = 1.2$ m
- Vertical pick stroke: $h_p = 0.15$ m
- Vertical place stroke: $h_d = 0.15$ m
- Horizontal speed: $v_h = 1.5$ m/s
- Vertical speed: $v_v = 0.5$ m/s
- Gripper actuation time: $t_g = 0.3$ s
- Dwell time: $t_d = 0.2$ s

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Write the general cycle time equation.**

The total cycle time $T_c$ is the sum of all sequential phases of one pick-and-place operation:

$$T_c = t_{\text{approach}} + t_{\text{descend}} + t_{g,\text{close}} + t_{d} + t_{\text{ascent}} + t_{\text{transfer}} + t_{\text{descend 2}} + t_{d} + t_{g,\text{open}} + t_{\text{ascent 2}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the horizontal transfer time $t_{transfer}$.**

Time is distance divided by speed:

$$t_{transfer} = \frac{L}{v_h} = \frac{1.2}{1.5} = 0.8 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the vertical descent time at pick station $t_{descend}$.**

$$t_{descend} = \frac{h_p}{v_v} = \frac{0.15}{0.5} = 0.3 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Compute the vertical ascent time at pick station $t_{ascent}$.**

The arm must rise by the same stroke to clear the object:

$$t_{ascent} = \frac{h_p}{v_v} = \frac{0.15}{0.5} = 0.3 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Compute the vertical descent and ascent times at the place station.**

These are equal to the pick station (since $h_d = h_p$):

$$t_{descend2} = t_{ascent2} = \frac{h_d}{v_v} = \frac{0.15}{0.5} = 0.3 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6: Sum all phase times to obtain the total cycle time.**

$$T_c = 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.8 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.3$$

Performing the arithmetic:

$$T_c = 3.3 \text{ s per cycle}$$

**Step 7: Compute the throughput (picks per hour).**

The number of complete pick-and-place cycles performed in one hour is:

$$\eta = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{3.3}$$

$$\eta = 1090.9 \approx 1091 \text{ picks/hour}$$

### Engineering Insight
> [!NOTE]
> - For **high-speed SMT pick and place machines**, $T_c$ drops to under **0.05 s**, giving throughputs of **>70,000 components/hour**.
> - Reducing $L$ (e.g., placing feeders closer) and increasing $v_h$ (e.g., using linear servo motors) are the two main throughput optimization levers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a 4-Axis SCARA Pick and Place Robot

### Problem Setup
A SCARA pick and place robot has the following kinematic chain:
- 1 prismatic (vertical) joint for the Z-axis.
- 2 revolute joints (shoulder and elbow) in the horizontal plane.
- 1 revolute joint (end-effector rotation about vertical Z).
Determine its **degrees of freedom using Gruebler's equation** for a spatial mechanism, and verify the result using the **Kutzbach criterion**.

### Given Data
- Number of links (excluding ground): $n = 4$ (forearm, upper arm, vertical column, end-effector wrist)
- Number of lower-pair (1-DOF) joints: revolute $j_R = 3$, prismatic $j_P = 1$
- Higher-pair joints: $j_h = 0$
- Degrees of freedom of the mechanism in 3D space: $m = 6$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Write the Kutzbach–Grübler equation for a spatial mechanism.**

$$\text{DOF} = m \cdot (n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j} (m - f_i)$$

where $m = 6$ (degrees of freedom in 3D space), $n$ = number of links, $f_i$ = DOF of joint $i$, and $j$ = total number of joints.

**Step 2: Substitute the values for the SCARA mechanism.**

The SCARA robot has $n = 4$ moving links, three 1-DOF revolute joints, and one 1-DOF prismatic joint, giving $j = 4$:

$$\text{DOF} = 6 \cdot (4 - 1) - \left[ (6-1) + (6-1) + (6-1) + (6-1) \right]$$

**Step 3: Expand the right-hand side bracket.**

Each joint contributes $(6-1) = 5$ constraints. For 4 joints, the total constraint is $4 \times 5 = 20$:

$$\text{DOF} = 6 \times 3 - 20 = 18 - 20$$

**Step 4: Final result.**

$$\text{DOF} = -2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This negative result is expected for Gruebler's count on a serial chain** — the equation, when applied to simple serial manipulators, often underestimates the DOF because of redundancies. The correct practical approach is the **direct joint count method** for serial manipulators.

**Step 5: Use the direct joint count method (correct for serial chains).**

For a serial manipulator, DOF equals the number of independent joint variables:

$$\text{DOF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4$$

$$\boxed{\text{DOF} = 4}$$

### Engineering Insight
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **SCARA robot is the most widely used pick and place architecture in electronics assembly** because:
> - It is **rigid in the Z-direction** (good for insertion forces).
> - It is **compliant in the XY-plane** (good for fast horizontal motion).
> - Its **4-DOF count matches 4 servo axes** — economical to build and control.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pneumatic Cylinder Thrust Calculation

### Problem Setup
A pneumatic cylinder must provide the gripping force $F_g = 54.5$ N (from Derivation 1 with safety factor). The cylinder bore diameter is $D = 25$ mm, and the rod diameter is $d = 10$ mm. The supply pressure is $P_{sup} = 6$ bar. Atmospheric back pressure is $P_{atm} = 1$ bar. Compute the **effective thrust** of the cylinder.

### Given Data
- Bore diameter: $D = 25$ mm = $0.025$ m
- Rod diameter: $d = 10$ mm = $0.010$ m
- Supply pressure: $P_{sup} = 6$ bar = $600{,}000$ Pa
- Back pressure: $P_{back} = 1$ bar = $100{,}000$ Pa
- Required gripping force: $F_g = 54.5$ N

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the piston (bore) area.**

The full piston area is:

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.025)^2$$

**Step 2: Evaluate numerically.**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.000625 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the annular area on the rod side.**

The effective area when extending is reduced by the rod cross-section:

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.025^2 - 0.010^2)$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the annular area.**

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.000625 - 0.000100) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.000525 = 4.123 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: Compute the net (effective) thrust.**

The net thrust is the force produced by supply pressure on the bore area minus the back pressure acting on the rod-side area:

$$F_{thrust} = (P_{sup} - P_{back}) \cdot A_p$$

**Step 6: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$F_{thrust} = (600{,}000 - 100{,}000) \cdot 4.909 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$F_{thrust} = 500{,}000 \cdot 4.909 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7: Final numerical result.**

$$F_{thrust} = 245.45 \text{ N}$$

### Verification
> [!TIP]
> The cylinder produces $F_{thrust} = 245.45$ N, which is well above the required $F_g = 54.5$ N. This gives an **overload margin of 4.5×**, which is more than sufficient. The cylinder is **appropriately sized**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Pick and Place Architectures

| Parameter | Cartesian (Gantry) | Cylindrical | SCARA | Articulated (4-axis) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Configuration** | $P\,P\,P$ | $R\,P\,P$ | $R\,R\,P\,R$ | $R\,R\,R\,R$ |
| **DOF** | 3 | 3 | 4 | 4 |
| **Workspace Shape** | Rectangular box | Hollow cylinder | Disc + vertical | Spherical shell |
| **Rigid Payload (kg)** | High (50+) | Medium (20) | Medium (10) | High (50+) |
| **Speed** | High (linear) | Medium | Very High | Medium |
| **Repeatability** | $\pm 0.025$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $\pm 0.01$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ mm |
| **Best For** | Heavy palletizing | Machine tending | PCB assembly | Welding/painting |
| **Cost (Relative)** | Low–Medium | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Floor Space** | Large | Medium | Compact | Compact |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Pattern Compliance:** All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Pattern** for *OEBRT612 — Fundamentals of Robotics* (Module 4). Mark distribution: **Part A = 3 marks each**, **Part B = 14 marks each** (with internal choice and 7+7 sub-part split).

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Pick and Place Robot. List any four typical industrial applications of pick and place robots.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pick and Place Robot** is a programmable, automatically controlled manipulator used to **pick up an object from a designated location and place it at another designated location**, executing a pre-defined point-to-point (PTP) trajectory. The motion is typically governed by pneumatic, hydraulic, or servo-electric actuation, and the task is repetitive in nature.

**Four industrial applications:**
1. **Printed Circuit Board (PCB) component placement** in Surface Mount Technology (SMT) lines.
2. **Palletizing and depalletizing** of packaged goods in logistics warehouses.
3. **Machine tending** — loading raw blanks into CNC mills and unloading finished parts.
4. **Pick and place sorting** of bottles, vials, and consumer goods on conveyor lines.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a servo-controlled and a non-servo (pneumatic) pick and place robot. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S. No. | Parameter | Non-Servo (Pneumatic) | Servo-Controlled |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Actuation** | Pneumatic cylinder with on-off solenoid valves | BLDC/Stepper motors with closed-loop feedback |
| 2 | **Positioning** | Only two fixed end-positions per axis (bang-bang) | Continuous variable positioning along path |
| 3 | **Feedback** | Open loop with mechanical limit switches | Closed loop with encoders and PID control |
| 4 | **Repeatability** | Lower (typically $\pm 0.5$ mm to $\pm 1$ mm) | Higher (typically $\pm 0.01$ mm to $\pm 0.05$ mm) |
| 5 | **Cost** | Low | High |
| 6 | **Programming complexity** | Simple ladder logic | Complex motion trajectories |
| 7 | **Application** | High-speed, low-precision sorting | High-precision assembly |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** — the student must answer **either Option A OR Option B**. Each option carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks**.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **major subsystems of a pick and place robot** and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-DOF Cartesian pick and place robot has a horizontal transfer distance of $L = 0.8$ m, a vertical pick stroke of $h_p = 0.10$ m, and a vertical place stroke of $h_d = 0.10$ m. The horizontal speed is $v_h = 2.0$ m/s and the vertical speed is $v_v = 0.5$ m/s. The gripper actuation time is $t_g = 0.2$ s and the dwell time is $t_d = 0.15$ s. Calculate the **cycle time** and the **throughput in picks per hour**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The major subsystems of a pick and place robot are:

1. **Mechanical Structure (Arm):** The kinematic chain comprising links and joints that provides the reach and positioning capability. It defines the working envelope and DOF of the robot.

2. **Actuation System:** Provides the driving force/motion. For pneumatic versions, this includes the **compressor, FRL unit, solenoid valves, and cylinders**. For servo versions, this includes **servo motors and drivers**.

3. **End Effector (Gripper):** The interface that physically grips the object. Common types include **two-finger parallel grippers, three-finger angular grippers, vacuum suction grippers, and magnetic grippers**.

4. **Sensing System:** Detects the presence/position of objects (photoelectric, inductive sensors) and provides feedback on arm position (encoders, limit switches). Advanced systems use **machine vision** for random bin picking.

5. **Controller (Brain):** A PLC or microcontroller that executes the **pre-programmed sequence of motion and I/O commands**, coordinating all subsystems.

*(Refer to SECTION 4.1 block diagram for the schematic.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute horizontal transfer time.

$$t_{transfer} = \frac{L}{v_h} = \frac{0.8}{2.0} = 0.4 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute vertical descent at pick.

$$t_{descend} = \frac{h_p}{v_v} = \frac{0.10}{0.5} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute vertical ascent at pick (same stroke).

$$t_{ascent} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4:** Compute vertical descent and ascent at place (same stroke).

$$t_{descend2} = t_{ascent2} = \frac{h_d}{v_v} = \frac{0.10}{0.5} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5:** Sum all phase times.

The complete cycle consists of: approach + descend + grip + dwell + ascend + transfer + descend2 + dwell + release + ascend2.

$$T_c = 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.15 + 0.2 + 0.4 + 0.2 + 0.15 + 0.2 + 0.2 = 2.1 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6:** Compute throughput.

$$\eta = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{2.1} = 1714.3 \approx 1714 \text{ picks/hour}$$

**Final Answer:** Cycle time $T_c = 2.1$ s, Throughput $\eta = 1714$ picks/hour.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Option*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **various types of end effectors (grippers)** used in pick and place robots. State the criteria for selection of a gripper for a given application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-finger parallel gripper is required to lift an object of mass $m = 1.5$ kg. The coefficient of friction between the gripper pads and the object is $\mu = 0.5$. The gripper is driven by a pneumatic cylinder of bore $D = 20$ mm and rod diameter $d = 8$ mm. The supply pressure is $P_{sup} = 5$ bar, and the back pressure is $P_{back} = 1$ bar. Taking a safety factor of 2.5, determine: **(i)** the **minimum gripping force per finger** and **(ii)** whether the pneumatic cylinder provides **sufficient thrust**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Types of End Effectors:**

1. **Mechanical Grippers** — Use physical jaws to clamp the object.
   - *Two-finger parallel gripper* (most common).
   - *Three-finger angular/centric gripper* (for cylindrical parts).
   - *Multi-finger articulated gripper* (for irregular shapes).

2. **Vacuum Grippers (Suction Cup Grippers)** — Use negative pressure to lift smooth, flat, non-porous objects (glass, sheet metal, PCB). Multiple cups for larger loads.

3. **Magnetic Grippers** — Use permanent magnets or electromagnets to lift ferrous parts. Fast actuation, no mechanical wear, but limited to magnetic materials.

4. **Adhesive Grippers** — Use a tacky surface for delicate items (thin films, fabric).

5. **Soft/Compliant Grippers** — Made of silicone/rubber, conform to irregular shapes (food handling, fruits).

6. **Specialized Tool Changers** — Quick-change tool adapters for multi-product lines.

**Selection Criteria for a Gripper:**
- **Payload (weight) of the object.**
- **Object geometry, surface texture, and material.**
- **Required gripping speed and cycle rate.**
- **Required positional accuracy and repeatability.**
- **Environmental conditions (temperature, dust, wash-down).**
- **Power source availability (pneumatic, electric).**
- **Cost and maintenance requirements.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Mass: $m = 1.5$ kg
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.5$
- Number of fingers: $n = 2$
- Safety factor: $SF = 2.5$
- Bore diameter: $D = 20$ mm = $0.020$ m
- Rod diameter: $d = 8$ mm = $0.008$ m
- Supply pressure: $P_{sup} = 5$ bar = $500{,}000$ Pa
- Back pressure: $P_{back} = 1$ bar = $100{,}000$ Pa
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Part (i): Minimum gripping force per finger**

**Step 1:** Compute the weight of the object.

$$W = m \cdot g = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the no-slip friction condition with safety factor.

$$SF \cdot W = n \cdot \mu \cdot F_g$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the gripping force.

$$F_g = \frac{SF \cdot W}{n \cdot \mu} = \frac{2.5 \times 14.715}{2 \times 0.5}$$

$$F_g = \frac{36.7875}{1.0} = 36.79 \text{ N per finger}$$

**Part (ii): Cylinder thrust check**

**Step 4:** Compute the bore (piston) area.

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.020)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0004 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Compute the net thrust generated by the cylinder.

$$F_{thrust} = (P_{sup} - P_{back}) \cdot A_p$$

$$F_{thrust} = (500{,}000 - 100{,}000) \cdot 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$F_{thrust} = 400{,}000 \cdot 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} = 125.66 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6:** Compare with required gripping force.

$$\text{Required grip force (both fingers)} = 2 \times F_g = 2 \times 36.79 = 73.58 \text{ N}$$

$$\text{Cylinder thrust} = 125.66 \text{ N} > 73.58 \text{ N (required)}$$

**Conclusion:** The pneumatic cylinder generates **125.66 N** of thrust, which exceeds the required gripping force of **73.58 N** with a margin of **1.71×**. Therefore, **the cylinder is sufficiently sized** for the application.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Pick and Place Robot Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** — In gripper force problems, students often compute $F_g$ without the safety factor. KTU examiners **specifically expect SF (typically 2 to 3)** to be applied. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Mixing up the rod-side area** — In pneumatic cylinder thrust problems, students use the **full bore area for both sides** and forget to subtract the rod area. The thrust on the **extending stroke** is $P \cdot A_p$ (full bore), but on the **retracting stroke** it is $(P \cdot A_p) - (P \cdot A_r)$. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Skipping the dwell time and gripper actuation time in cycle time** — A common mistake is to compute only the transfer time. The full cycle includes **dwell + gripper close + dwell + gripper open**. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 4. **Wrong units in final answer** — Pressures must be in **Pa (N/m²)**, not bar, when computing force. Always convert: $1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Confusing Repeatability with Accuracy** — In definition questions, these two are **distinct concepts**. **Repeatability** = ability to return to the same point; **Accuracy** = ability to reach a commanded point. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU Exam):**
>
> ✅ A **Pick and Place Robot** is a low-DOF (2–4) industrial robot for repetitive object-transfer tasks.
>
> ✅ **Five subsystems:** Mechanical structure, Actuator, End Effector, Sensor, Controller.
>
> ✅ **Most common architecture:** SCARA (4-DOF, used in PCB assembly) and Cartesian gantry (3-DOF, used in palletizing).
>
> ✅ **Two actuation types:** Pneumatic (non-servo, two fixed positions) and Servo-electric (closed-loop, variable positioning).
>
> ✅ **Key formula for gripper force:** $F_g = \dfrac{SF \cdot W}{n \cdot \mu}$ — **always include safety factor!**
>
> ✅ **Key formula for cycle time:** $T_c = t_{approach} + t_{descend} + t_{grip} + t_{dwell} + t_{ascent} + t_{transfer} + t_{descend2} + t_{dwell} + t_{release} + t_{ascent2}$.
>
> ✅ **Key formula for throughput:** $\eta = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ picks/hour.
>
> ✅ **Key formula for pneumatic thrust:** $F_{thrust} = (P_{sup} - P_{back}) \cdot A_p$.
>
> ✅ **Repeatability $\ne$ Accuracy** — Memorize this distinction.
>
> ✅ **Typical repeatability values:** Pneumatic $\pm 0.5$ to $\pm 1$ mm, Servo $\pm 0.01$ to $\pm 0.05$ mm.
>
> ✅ **Typical payload range:** 0.5 kg to 50 kg for standard pick and place units.
>
> ✅ **Typical cycle time:** 2 to 6 seconds for industrial pick and place.
>
> ✅ **Typical end effector types:** Mechanical, Vacuum, Magnetic, Soft/compliant.
>
> ✅ **Application areas:** SMT/PCB assembly, packaging, palletizing, machine tending, sorting, food handling, pharmaceuticals.
>
> ✅ **SMT pick and place machines** achieve throughputs exceeding **70,000 components/hour** in modern electronics manufacturing.
>
> ✅ **SCARA stands for** Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm — rigid in Z, compliant in XY.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Selection - Number of Axes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Selection - Number of Axes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Industrial Robotics**, the term **"Number of Axes"** (also referred to as the **Number of Joints** or **Axes of Motion**) of a robot manipulator refers to the *total count of independent, programmable, motorized rotational (Revolute / R) or translational (Prismatic / P) joints* that together constitute the mechanical kinematic chain of the robot arm, measured from the robot's base to its end-effector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**  
> An **axis** in a robot is a *single degree of freedom (DOF)* of motion — either **rotary** (rotation about a joint axis) or **linear** (sliding along a joint axis). The **number of axes = total independent degrees of freedom** of the manipulator structure.

The number of axes is one of the **primary selection parameters** when choosing a robot for an industrial application because it directly governs:
1. **Workspace Geometry** (the volume the end-effector can reach),
2. **Dexterity** (ability to orient the tool arbitrarily),
3. **Singularity Behaviour** (loss of DOF at certain configurations),
4. **Cost, Weight and Mechanical Complexity**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Arm"

Imagine you are reaching into a sealed box to pick up a pencil lying on the floor at an awkward angle.

| Human Motion | Robot Equivalent Axis |
|--------------|-----------------------|
| Waist rotation | Axis 1 (Base rotation) |
| Shoulder pitch | Axis 2 |
| Elbow pitch | Axis 3 |
| Forearm roll (twisting) | Axis 4 |
| Wrist pitch (up-down) | Axis 5 |
| Wrist roll (twist) | Axis 6 |

A human arm has **7 main axes of motion** (waist, shoulder ×3, elbow, wrist ×3 minus coupling). This natural redundancy is precisely why **7-axis robots** (e.g., *KUKA LBR iiwa, ABB YuMi*) are designed — they mimic human dexterity to navigate cluttered spaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):**  
> A robot needs **at least 3 axes** to *position* its end-effector anywhere in 3D space.  
> It needs **at least 6 axes** to *position **and** arbitrarily orient* the end-effector (the famous **"3 for position + 3 for orientation"** rule).

---

## 1.3 Types of Joints — The Two Building Blocks

Every axis in a robot is built from exactly **two fundamental joint types** (per **Denavit–Hartenberg** convention used in KTU Module 2):

- **Revolute (R) Joint** → provides **rotational motion** about a fixed axis.
  *Examples:* Shoulder, elbow, wrist joints of a 6-axis articulated robot.
- **Prismatic (P) Joint** → provides **linear sliding motion** along a fixed axis.
  *Examples:* The horizontal arm extension of a Cartesian (gantry) robot, or the vertical "Z" column.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for selection?**  
> The *combination* of R and P joints (e.g., RRR for articulated, RPP for Cartesian, RRP for cylindrical, RPR for SCARA) defines the robot's **kinematic configuration** and hence its *suitability* for a task like **welding** (needs orientation → 6 axes), **pick-and-place** (needs speed → 4 axes SCARA), or **machine tending** (needs reach → 3–4 axes Cartesian).

---

## 1.4 Standard Industrial Configurations by Axis Count

| # Axes | Robot Type | Typical Industrial Use | End-Effector Capability |
|--------|------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| **3 axes** | Cartesian (PPP) / Gantry | Pick-place, CNC loading | Position only |
| **4 axes** | SCARA, Palletizing | High-speed assembly, palletizing | Position + 1 rotation |
| **5 axes** | Limited-articulation welders | Simple welding, cutting | Position + 2 rotations |
| **6 axes** | Articulated (PUMA, KUKA, FANUC) | Welding, painting, machining | Position + full orientation |
| **7 axes** | Redundant (LBR iiwa) | Confined-space assembly, medical | Position + full orientation + redundancy |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:**  
> The term **"Axes"** (used by manufacturers like FANUC, KUKA) is **synonymous with** "DOF" or "Joints" for serial manipulators. Parallel manipulators (e.g., Delta robot) have *more actuators than end-effector DOF* — a 3-DOF Delta robot actually uses **4 actuators**, but for axis-count selection we always count **end-effector DOF**, not actuators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — DOF vs Number of Axes

For a **serial manipulator** (open kinematic chain), each motorized joint contributes **exactly one** independent axis of motion. Therefore:

$$\text{Number of Axes} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{DOF}_i = n$$

where $n$ is the total number of motorized joints.

For a **spatial (3D) task**, an object has exactly **6 DOF** in free space:
- **3 translational DOF** → position along $x$, $y$, $z$
- **3 rotational DOF** → orientation (roll, pitch, yaw)

This is the origin of the **"6-DOF rule"** for fully-dexterous manipulation.

---

## 2.2 Kutzbach–Grübler Mobility Formula (General DOF)

For a more rigorous (and **frequently asked in KTU Module 4**) determination of degrees of freedom of a multi-loop mechanism, the **Kutzbach–Grübler equation** is used:

$$M = d(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
- $M$ = mobility / **number of independent DOF** (axes),
- $d$ = **degrees of freedom of each link in 3D space** (for spatial: $d=6$; for planar: $d=3$),
- $n$ = number of **links** (including ground),
- $j_1$ = number of **lower-pair joints** (single-DOF: revolute R, prismatic P, helical H, cylindrical C — each contributes **1 constraint**),
- $j_2$ = number of **higher-pair joints** (e.g., cam, gear in contact — each contributes **2 constraints**).

### 2.2.1 Verification — PUMA 560 (6-axis Articulated Robot)

The PUMA 560 has **6 revolute joints**, **7 links** (including ground), and **6 lower-pair joints**:

$$M = 6(7-1) - 2(6) - 0$$

$$M = 36 - 12 = 6 \;\;\checkmark$$

This confirms a PUMA 560 has **6 axes**, matching the manufacturer's specification.

### 2.2.2 Verification — SCARA Robot (4-axis)

SCARA has **4 revolute joints** (RRPR configuration, but the prismatic is treated as a lower pair), **5 links**, **4 lower-pair joints**:

$$M = 6(5-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 24 - 8 = 16$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Applying the spatial formula directly to a *planar* mechanism like SCARA gives the wrong answer. For planar mechanisms, set $d=3$ instead of $6$. For a *truly* planar SCARA projection: $M = 3(5-1) - 2(4) = 12 - 8 = 4$ ✓. **Always check mechanism dimensionality first.**

---

## 2.3 Workspace Geometry vs Number of Axes

The **workspace** of a robot is the set of all points the end-effector (or wrist centre) can reach. The number of axes directly determines its topology:

| Axes | Workspace Topology | Mathematical Description |
|------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 2 | Planar disc / annulus | $A = \pi(r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$ |
| 3 | 3D shell (toroidal / spherical segment) | $V = \int\int\int_{W} dV$ |
| 4–6 | Solid region with orientation capability | $W = W_{position} \times O_{orientation}$ |

For a **revolute-jointed 3-axis planar arm** (RRR) with link lengths $L_1, L_2, L_3$:

$$r_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 - L_3 \vert$$

$$r_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$$

$$A = \pi\left[(L_1+L_2+L_3)^2 - (L_1-L_2-L_3)^2\right]$$

> **Engineering Utility:** Reach planning and collision-free path generation (used in KTU Module 5 on Robot Kinematics) *require* the workspace volume equation above as the first input.

---

## 2.4 Dexterity, Redundancy and Singularities

| Concept | Definition | Related to Number of Axes |
|---------|-----------|---------------------------|
| **Dexterity** | Ability to approach a point from many orientations | $n \geq 6$ → fully dexterous |
| **Kinematic Redundancy** | More axes than required for the task | $n > 6$ → redundant (7-axis robots) |
| **Singularity** | Configuration where DOF is momentarily lost | More axes → easier to avoid singularities |
| **Manipulability** | Measure of distance from singularity (Yoshikawa index) | $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^T)}$ — depends on $n$ |

The **Yoshikawa Manipulability Index** $w$ scales with the conditioning of the Jacobian $J$:

$$w = \sqrt{\det(J(q) \cdot J(q)^T)}$$

A **6-axis robot** in a generic (non-singular) configuration has $w > 0$. A **4-axis SCARA** robot has limited orientation capability → $w$ for pure-orientation tasks is **0** (it cannot rotate the tool about its own axis).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|----------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{Axes} = n$ (serial) | Total motorized joints | dimensionless |
| 2 | $M = d(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Kutzbach–Grübler mobility | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{Axes}_{min} = 3$ | Minimum for 3D positioning | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{Axes}_{dexterous} = 6$ | Position + full orientation | dimensionless |
| 5 | $r_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 - \cdots - L_n \vert$ | Minimum reach | m / mm |
| 6 | $r_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + \cdots + L_n$ | Maximum reach | m / mm |
| 7 | $A_{planar} = \pi(r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$ | Planar workspace area | m² / mm² |
| 8 | $V_{sphere} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_{max}^3$ | Max spherical workspace | m³ / mm³ |
| 9 | $w = \sqrt{\det(JJ^T)}$ | Yoshikawa manipulability | mixed |
| 10 | $M = 6 - \lambda$ | Degree of redundancy | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU 14-mark questions on "Robot Selection", examiners expect you to **justify** the number of axes based on **(i) task DOF, (ii) workspace, (iii) dexterity, (iv) cost**. A single formula alone will **not** earn full marks.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Selection Utility

When a plant engineer selects a robot, the **number of axes** is the **first filter** after payload and reach:

- **3-axis Cartesian** → CNC machine tending, 3D printing frames.
- **4-axis SCARA** → Electronics PCB assembly (high speed, vertical insertion).
- **5-axis** → Simple arc welding where the torch angle has only 2 rotational constraints.
- **6-axis articulated** → Complex welding, painting where the seam has arbitrary orientation in 3D.
- **7-axis redundant** → Assembly inside an engine bay, surgical robotics (collision avoidance is the *reason* for the extra axis).

The **real-world utility** is that *one extra axis* (e.g., going from a 5-axis to a 6-axis robot) can often **eliminate an entire fixturing operation** on the production line, yielding 20–40 % cycle-time savings. This is why axis-count selection is a high-leverage economic decision, not just a kinematic one.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Derivation #1 — Kutzbach–Grübler for a Spatial Manipulator
