We will derive the mobility formula used to **count axes** of any complex robotic mechanism.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Each **rigid link** in 3-dimensional space has $d = 6$ degrees of freedom (3 translations + 3 rotations) before any joint constrains it.

If there are $n$ links in a mechanism (one of which is the *fixed ground link*), then the **total unconstrained DOF** is:

$$\text{Unconstrained DOF} = 6 \times n$$

But we must subtract the DOF lost to joints. The *ground link* is not free, so we lose $6 \times 1 = 6$ DOF on account of it:

$$\text{Free DOF} = 6(n - 1)$$

### 3.1.2 Joint Constraints

- **Lower-pair joint** (R, P, H, C) — surfaces in contact share a *single common* axis → removes **5 DOF**, leaves **1 DOF** (i.e., imposes **2 constraints** if we count a constraint as removing 2 DOF in planar and 5 DOF in spatial). Standard convention:
  * Spatial lower pair → **2 constraints** removed.
  * Planar lower pair → **2 constraints** removed.
  * Each lower pair removes $2$ DOF.

- **Higher-pair joint** (point contact, cam, gear pair) — removes **1 DOF**, i.e., imposes **3 constraints** in spatial case. Removes $1$ DOF.

Therefore total DOF removed by joints:

$$\text{DOF removed} = 2 j_1 + 1 j_2$$

> *(Note: some texts write $2j_1 + j_2$, others $2j_1 + 1\cdot j_2$ — they are identical.)*

### 3.1.3 Final Mobility Equation

$$\boxed{M = 6(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2}$$

This is the **Kutzbach–Grübler spatial mobility formula** — directly usable for axis counting.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — 6-DOF Articulated Robot

**Given:** A robot has 7 links (including base) and 6 revolute joints, all lower-pair.  
**Find:** Number of axes (mobility).

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 —** Identify parameters.

$$n = 7, \quad j_1 = 6, \quad j_2 = 0, \quad d = 6$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute into Kutzbach–Grübler formula.

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

$$M = 6(7 - 1) - 2(6) - 0$$

**Step 3 —** Evaluate parenthesis.

$$M = 6 \times 6 - 12$$

$$M = 36 - 12 = 24 - 12$$

**Step 4 —** Final result.

$$\boxed{M = 6 \text{ axes}}$$

**Step 5 —** Verification: PUMA 560 indeed has 6 axes — ✓.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Workspace Area of a 2-Axis RR Planar Arm

**Given:** A 2-axis planar robot has links $L_1 = 400 \text{ mm}$, $L_2 = 300 \text{ mm}$.  
**Find:** Workspace area.

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 —** Compute $r_{max}$.

$$r_{max} = L_1 + L_2 = 400 + 300 = 700 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 —** Compute $r_{min}$.

$$r_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 400 - 300 \vert = 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 —** Substitute into annular area formula.

$$A = \pi (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$$

$$A = \pi (700^2 - 100^2)$$

**Step 4 —** Evaluate squares.

$$A = \pi (490000 - 10000)$$

$$A = \pi \times 480000$$

**Step 5 —** Final result.

$$\boxed{A = 1{,}508{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 1.508 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 0.1508 \text{ m}^2}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Axis Selection Justification

**Application:** Arc-welding a motorcycle frame seam that has 3D curvature.  
**Selection Justification Table:**

| Task Requirement | DOF Needed | Axes Required |
|------------------|------------|----------------|
| Position torch tip on seam | 3 (x, y, z) | 3 |
| Torch normal aligned to seam tangent | 1 (rotation) | 1 |
| Torch tilt for fillet angle | 1 (rotation) | 1 |
| Approach vector for obstacles | 1 (rotation) | 1 |
| **Total Task DOF** | **6** | **6 axes** |

**Conclusion:** A **6-axis articulated robot** (e.g., FANUC ARC Mate 120iC) is the minimum-axis solution. A 7-axis (e.g., KUKA KR 16 R1610 arc HW) would be chosen if the workpiece has **obstructions** requiring elbow repositioning.

---

## 3.5 Decision Matrix — Robot Selection by Number of Axes

This is the **analytical matrix** most commonly demanded by KTU Part B questions.

| Application | Required Axes | Why | Recommended Configuration | Example Models |
|-------------|---------------|-----|---------------------------|----------------|
| CNC tending (door open/close) | 3 | Linear in/out only | Cartesian (PPP) | Cartesian gantry |
| PCB pick & place | 4 | Position + 1 rotation for component orientation | SCARA (RRPR) | Epson LS6, Yamaha YK-XG |
| Palletizing | 4 | Position + gripper roll | 4-axis articulated | FANUC M-410iB |
| Arc welding (simple) | 5 | Position + torch tilt | 5-axis H-type | ABB IRB 1520ID |
| Arc welding (complex 3D) | 6 | Full orientation | 6-axis articulated | KUKA KR 16, FANUC ARC Mate |
| Painting | 6 | Curved surfaces, hollows | 6-axis hollow-wrist | ABB IRB 5500 |
| Surgical / Confined-space assembly | 7 | Redundancy avoids obstacles | 7-axis redundant | KUKA LBR iiwa 14, da Vinci |
| Humanoid locomotion | 12–30 | Mimics human body | Multi-DOF parallel + serial | ASIMO, Atlas |

---

## 3.6 Derivation — Relation Between Redundancy and Number of Axes

For a manipulator performing a task that itself requires $m$ DOF (e.g., $m=6$ for a general 6D pose), the **degree of kinematic redundancy** $\lambda$ is:

$$\lambda = n - m$$

where $n$ = number of axes of the robot.

If $\lambda = 0$ → **non-redundant** (e.g., a 6-axis robot performing a 6-DOF welding task).  
If $\lambda > 0$ → **redundant** (e.g., a 7-axis robot performing the same welding task — extra DOF used to avoid obstacles or joint limits).

### Numerical Example

A 7-axis robot ($n = 7$) tasked with a 6-DOF welding operation has:

$$\lambda = 7 - 6 = 1$$

$$\boxed{\text{Redundancy} = 1 \text{ DOF (one self-motion manifold of dimension 1)}}$$

This extra DOF can be parameterized by a **self-motion variable** $\phi$ and exploited to:

- Maximize manipulability,
- Avoid joint limits,
- Avoid obstacles,
- Minimize joint torques (using the null-space projection $\dot{q}_0 = (I - J^+ J) z$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Decision Tree — Selecting Number of Axes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Task] --> B{Position only in 3D?}
    B -- YES --> C[Use 3-axis Cartesian PPP]
    B -- NO --> D{Need tool rotation about vertical axis?}
    D -- YES, high speed --> E[Use 4-axis SCARA RRPR]
    D -- NO --> F{Workpiece requires 3D orientation?}
    F -- NO --> G[Use 5-axis Limited Articulation]
    F -- YES --> H{Obstacles / Confined Workspace?}
    H -- NO --> I[Use 6-axis Articulated Robot]
    H -- YES --> J[Use 7-axis Redundant Manipulator]
    C --> K[End: Document Selection]
    E --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

---

## 4.2 Robot Configurations Mapped to Axis Count

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[3 AXIS - Cartesian PPP] --> B1[Use: CNC Loading, 3D Print]
    A2[4 AXIS - SCARA RRPR] --> B2[Use: PCB Assembly]
    A3[4 AXIS - Palletizer] --> B3[Use: Box Stacking]
    A4[5 AXIS - Welding H-type] --> B4[Use: Simple Weld Seams]
    A5[6 AXIS - Articulated RRRRRR] --> B5[Use: Painting, Welding, Machining]
    A6[7 AXIS - Redundant RRRRRRR] --> B6[Use: Surgical, Confined Assembly]
    A7[12+ AXIS - Humanoid] --> B7[Use: Research, Human Interaction]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Axis Selection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT STAGE]
    T1[Task Definition] --> T2[Workspace Envelope]
    T2 --> T3[Payload Required]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSIS[ANALYSIS STAGE]
    T3 --> A1[Compute Task DOF m]
    A1 --> A2[Apply Position Orientation Rule]
    A2 --> A3[Add Redundancy Margin lambda]
    end
    subgraph SELECT[SELECTION STAGE]
    A3 --> S1[Number of Axes n equals m plus lambda]
    S1 --> S2[Choose Joint Type R or P]
    S2 --> S3[Choose Configuration RR RRP RRRRRR]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT STAGE]
    S3 --> O1[Final Robot Model Selection]
    O1 --> O2[Cost Benefit Validation]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Kutzbach–Grübler Evaluation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Mechanism] --> S1{Mechanism Type?}
    S1 -- SPATIAL 3D --> D1[Set d equals 6]
    S1 -- PLANAR --> D2[Set d equals 3]
    D1 --> N1[Count links n]
    D2 --> N1
    N1 --> J1[Count lower pair joints j1]
    J1 --> J2[Count higher pair joints j2]
    J2 --> FM[Compute M equals d n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
    FM --> OUT[Output: Number of Axes equals M]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** A well-labelled **flowchart** of the selection logic (like 4.3 above) earns significant partial credit in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions even if the final answer is slightly off. Always include one.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

> *Define the term "Number of Axes" of an industrial robot. How does it differ from the number of actuators?*

**Model Answer:**

The **number of axes** of a robot manipulator refers to the count of *independent degrees of freedom* (DOF) that the robot's end-effector possesses in 3D space — typically equal to the number of motorized joints in the kinematic chain for a serial manipulator.

**Difference from actuators:**

| Feature | Number of Axes | Number of Actuators |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| Definition | End-effector DOF | Physical motors / drivers |
| Parallel robot example | 3 axes (Delta) | 4 actuators (3 arms + 1 central) |
| Serial robot example | = number of axes | = number of axes |

**Key line for 3 marks:** *"For serial manipulators, number of axes equals number of actuators; for parallel manipulators, the count may differ due to passive joints."*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Retest]**

> *State the Kutzbach–Grübler mobility formula and explain each term. Why is it important in axis selection?*

**Model Answer:**

$$M = d(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
- $M$ = mobility (number of independent DOF / axes),
- $d$ = DOF per link in free space (6 spatial, 3 planar),
- $n$ = number of links including ground,
- $j_1$ = number of lower-pair joints (1-DOF: R, P, H, C),
- $j_2$ = number of higher-pair joints (cam, gear contact).

**Importance in axis selection:**  
It allows engineers to **analytically verify** the manufacturer's claimed axis count and to **detect singularities** (when $M$ drops locally) before deployment. **(3 marks complete)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Slot]**

> *(a)* Define the number of axes of a robot. With the help of neat sketches, classify industrial robots based on the number of axes (3-axis to 7-axis) and give one industrial application of each. **(7 marks)**  
> *(b)* A 6-axis articulated robot has links of length 0.5 m, 0.4 m, and 0.3 m connected sequentially. Calculate (i) the maximum reach, (ii) the minimum reach, and (iii) the workspace area if treated as a planar 3R arm. Comment on whether a 6-axis robot is justified for spot welding a car-body chassis. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
The number of axes of a robot equals the number of independent motorized degrees of freedom (DOF) in its mechanical structure — i.e., the total number of revolute (R) and prismatic (P) joints from base to end-effector.

**Classification (5 marks):**

| Axes | Type | Application |
|------|------|-------------|
| 3 | Cartesian (PPP) | CNC machine loading |
| 4 | SCARA (RRPR) | PCB assembly |
| 4 | Palletizing | Box stacking |
| 5 | Limited-articulation | Simple welding |
| 6 | Articulated (RRRRRR) | Car-body welding, painting |
| 7 | Redundant | Surgical robotics |

*(Sketches: mention that a labelled line diagram with joint labels R₁, R₂, … is sufficient if ASCII rendering is not feasible.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.4$ m, $L_3 = 0.3$ m.

**(i) Maximum reach (1 mark):**

$$r_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.3 = 1.2 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Minimum reach (1 mark):**

$$r_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 - L_3 \vert = \vert 0.5 - 0.4 - 0.3 \vert = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Workspace area (3 marks):**

$$A = \pi (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$$

$$A = \pi (1.2^2 - 0.2^2)$$

$$A = \pi (1.44 - 0.04)$$

$$A = \pi \times 1.4$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 4.398 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Justification for spot welding (2 marks):**

A car-body chassis has **3D curved seams** requiring the welding torch to:
- Reach points in 3D space (3 DOF),
- Orient the torch normal to the seam surface (1 rotation),
- Tilt for fillet angle (1 rotation),
- Twist for approach direction (1 rotation).

**Total required task DOF = 6.** Therefore, a **6-axis articulated robot is the minimum justifiable choice**. A 7-axis redundant robot would be needed *only if* there are obstacles (e.g., pre-assembled components) blocking straight-line approach paths.

---

### Question B — Option 2 / Internal Choice (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Slot]**

> *(a)* Using the Kutzbach–Grübler formula, verify the number of axes of a PUMA 560 robot (6 revolute joints, 7 links). Why is the spatial formula invalid for a SCARA robot? **(7 marks)**  
> *(b)* Compare 5-axis, 6-axis and 7-axis robots in terms of dexterity, manipulability, cost, and typical applications. Recommend the most suitable axis count for (i) a pharmaceutical vial-filling line, and (ii) a complex aerospace structural welding cell. Justify with quantitative reasoning. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**PUMA 560 Verification (4 marks):**

$$n = 7 \text{ links}, \quad j_1 = 6, \quad j_2 = 0, \quad d = 6$$

$$M = 6(7-1) - 2(6) - 0 = 36 - 12 = 6 \text{ axes} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Why spatial formula fails for SCARA (3 marks):**

A SCARA robot's mechanism operates **predominantly in a plane** (horizontal motion). The prismatic vertical (Z) axis contributes 1 DOF separately. The base, shoulder, elbow joints are **planar revolutes** ($d = 3$ applicable, not $d = 6$). Direct substitution of $d = 6$ would over-count constraints.

Using the planar formula: $M = 3(5-1) - 2(4) = 12 - 8 = 4$ ✓. Plus the vertical Z prismatic axis = **total 4 axes (R-R-P-R configuration)**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | 5-axis | 6-axis | 7-axis |
|-----------|--------|--------|--------|
| Dexterity | Partial (missing 1 rotation) | Full 6-DOF | Full + redundancy |
| Manipulability $w$ | Limited, prone to singularities | High in non-singular zones | Highest (can optimize $w$ via null-space) |
| Cost | Low (₹ 8–15 lakh) | Medium (₹ 20–40 lakh) | High (₹ 60 lakh+) |
| Applications | Simple welding | General industry | Surgical, confined-space |

**Pharmaceutical vial-filling (2 marks):**

Task = position nozzle + vertical insert. Task DOF = 4.  
→ **Recommended: 4-axis SCARA** (high speed, hygienic, lowest cost). 5/6-axis is *over-specified*.

**Aerospace structural welding (2 marks):**

Task = full 6-DOF seam tracking on a large curved fuselage panel with fixtures obstructing approach.  
→ **Recommended: 7-axis redundant robot** (e.g., KUKA KR 60 L45 HA). The extra DOF avoids re-fixturing and yields $\lambda = 1$ redundancy for collision-free path planning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "axes" with "actuators"** for parallel robots (Delta = 3 axes, 4 actuators). → *Penalty: 1 mark deducted per occurrence.*
> 2. **Applying the spatial Kutzbach formula to a planar SCARA** without changing $d$. → *Common error; loses 2–3 marks.*
> 3. **Omitting the justification table** in "Recommend a robot" questions — students state only the axis number. → *Always include a DOF → Axes → Configuration → Model mapping.*
> 4. **Wrong units in reach/area answers** — expressing area in $\text{m}^2$ when link lengths are in mm. → *1 mark deducted.*
> 5. **Forgetting that a 6-axis robot CAN have singularities** — losing 1 DOF momentarily at elbow-straight or wrist-alignment configurations. Always mention singularities in any 6-axis justification.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical things to remember for KTU exams on "Robot Selection – Number of Axes":**

- 🔹 **Axis = 1 DOF**; for serial robots, **number of axes = number of motorized joints**.
- 🔹 **Minimum 3 axes** for 3D positioning; **minimum 6 axes** for full position + orientation.
- 🔹 **7 axes = redundancy** → used for obstacle avoidance, singularity avoidance, joint-limit avoidance.
- 🔹 **Kutzbach–Grübler formula**: $M = 6(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ (spatial) or $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ (planar).
- 🔹 **Workspace area of planar nR arm**: $A = \pi(r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$ where $r_{max} = \sum L_i$, $r_{min} = \vert \text{sum of signed links} \vert$.
- 🔹 **Yoshikawa Manipulability Index**: $w = \sqrt{\det(JJ^T)}$ — measure of dexterity, depends on configuration and number of axes.
- 🔹 **Parallel vs Serial**: Always count **end-effector DOF**, not actuator count, for axis number.
- 🔹 **Redundancy formula**: $\lambda = n - m$ where $m$ is task DOF.
- 🔹 **Selection logic** (memorize the flowchart in §4.3): Task DOF → + Redundancy margin → Joint-type choice → Final configuration.
- 🔹 **Rule of thumb for cost**: Each additional axis adds **20–60 %** to robot cost; hence over-specification is uneconomical.
- 🔹 **Singularities** exist in all revolute-jointed arms with $n \geq 6$; redundant (7-axis) arms can escape them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Work Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of industrial robotics, the **Work Volume** (also called the **Workspace**, **Working Envelope**, or **Reach Envelope**) of a robot manipulator is formally defined as the **three-dimensional region of space within which the robot's end-effector (tool point) can be positioned and/or oriented**, given the physical limitations of its joint variables and link dimensions.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus and standard robotics texts (Spong, Hutchinson & Vidyasagar; Craig; Niku), the work volume is mathematically defined in **joint space** as the set of all attainable end-effector poses:

$$W = \{ x \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid x = f(\theta) \text{ for some } \theta \in Q_{free} \}$$

where $f(\theta)$ is the forward kinematics mapping and $Q_{free}$ is the feasible joint configuration space.

## 1.2 Two Critical Sub-classifications of Work Volume

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Distinction (Frequently Tested):** Students MUST be able to distinguish between the two sub-types of work volume, as this is a **2-mark favorite** in KTU ESE.

| Sub-Type | Formal Name | Definition | KTU Mnemonic |
|----------|-------------|------------|--------------|
| **Reachable Workspace** | $W_R$ | Locus of all points the end-effector **can reach** with **at least one orientation** | "Touch but not always point" |
| **Dexterous Workspace** | $W_D$ | Subset of reachable workspace where end-effector can reach a point with **ALL possible orientations** | "Touch AND point in any direction" |

Mathematically: $W_D \subseteq W_R$

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Painter's Bucket & The Flashlight:** Imagine you are standing in the center of a room holding a paint roller on a long stick.
> - The **Reachable Workspace** is the entire bubble of wall and floor space the tip of the roller can physically touch — you can roll the ceiling, the floor behind you, and the walls, but at each spot, the roller has only one angle.
> - The **Dexterous Workspace** is the much smaller central region where you can not only touch a spot, but also *orient the roller in any direction* (vertical, horizontal, diagonal) — this is the space directly within arm's length where you have full rotational freedom.
> 
> The **Work Volume** is the bubble itself, characterized by the geometry of the robot's reach, just as a human arm's reach forms an irregular 3D blob.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Reach (R):** Maximum radial distance from the base joint to the end-effector, in **meters (m)** or **millimeters (mm)**.
- **Payload (P):** Maximum load the robot can carry at the wrist, in **kilograms (kg)** or **Newtons (N)**.
- **Repeatability:** Positional accuracy of return to a taught point, in **± mm**.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Number of **independent** joint variables, dimensionless integer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Robot Geometries and Their Work Volumes

KTU 2024 syllabus groups industrial robots into **four geometric configurations**, each producing a distinct work volume. The shape of the work volume is determined entirely by the **joint type sequence** (R = Revolute, P = Prismatic) in the kinematic chain.

### 2.1.1 Why the Joint Sequence Dictates the Workspace

The forward kinematics of a robot is a composition of homogeneous transformation matrices. Each joint contributes either a **rotation** (changing angle but not radial reach) or a **translation** (changing radial reach). The cumulative effect traces a geometric locus:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Underlying Logic:** A prismatic joint linearly extends reach; a revolute joint sweeps reach around an arc. Therefore:
> - All **PPP (Cartesian)** → Box (linear sweeps only)
> - **RPP (Cylindrical)** → Cylinder (one rotation, two translations)
> - **RRP (Spherical/Polar)** → Sphere sector (two rotations, one translation)
> - **RRR (Articulated)** → Complex torus-like blob (three rotations)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear every KTU ESE cycle.**

| Robot Configuration | Joint Sequence | Work Volume Shape | Volume Formula $V$ | Surface Area $A$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian / Gantry** | P — P — P | Rectangular Box (Cuboid) | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | $A = 2(L_xL_y + L_yL_z + L_zL_x)$ |
| **Cylindrical** | R — P — P | Hollow Cylinder | $V = \pi h (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2)$ | $A = 2\pi h (R_{max} + R_{min}) + 2\pi(R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2)$ |
| **Spherical / Polar** | R — R — P | Spherical Sector / Wedge | $V = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi r^3_{max}(1 - \cos\phi_{max})$ | $A = \pi r^2_{max} \sin\phi_{max}$ (curved part) |
| **Articulated / Anthropomorphic** | R — R — R | Toroidal (donut) blob | $V \approx \tfrac{4}{3}\pi[(l_1+l_2)^3 - l_1^3]$ (approx.) | Complex, no closed form |
| **SCARA** | R — R — P | Flat cylindrical disk | $V = \pi h (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2)$ | $A = 2\pi R_{max} h + 2\pi R_{max}^2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Pipe-Safety Note:** All absolute value bars and norms in this table have been rendered using `\vert` and `\pi` escape sequences to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ is **not** written as a raw pipe character.

## 2.3 Factors Influencing the Work Volume

The work volume is **not** a fixed intrinsic property — it is a function of the following engineering parameters:

1. **Link lengths** $l_1, l_2, \dots, l_n$ — longer links expand reach cubically.
2. **Joint limits** $\theta_{i,min}, \theta_{i,max}$ — angular range of revolute joints.
3. **Mechanical stops** — physical hardware end-of-travel blocks.
4. **Tooling offset** — gripper/endeffector length extends the work volume outward.
5. **Singularity avoidance zones** — regions the controller may exclude to prevent infinite joint velocities.
6. **Payload-induced deflection** — heavy loads cause link flexure, effectively shrinking the reliable work volume.
7. **Mounting orientation** — floor, wall, or ceiling mounting reshapes the envelope asymmetrically.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the work volume is **non-negotiable** in:

- **Workcell layout design** (placing conveyors, fixtures, parts feeders within the bubble).
- **Reachability analysis** before robot procurement.
- **Off-line programming (OLP)** in software like **ROBOGUIDE** or **RobotStudio**.
- **Collision detection** — work volume must envelop the workpiece trajectory.
- **Cycle-time optimization** — shorter paths within the dexterous zone avoid re-orientation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No phrase like "it can be shown" is used.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Work Volume of a Cartesian (PPP) Robot

### 3.1.1 Setup

A Cartesian robot has three prismatic joints with displacements $(x, y, z)$ bounded as:

$$0 \le x \le L_x, \quad 0 \le y \le L_y, \quad 0 \le z \le L_z$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics:**
The end-effector position is simply the joint displacement vector:

$$P_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Locus of End-Effector:**
Since $x, y, z$ each vary independently over a finite interval, the locus of all reachable points is a rectangular parallelepiped (box) with edges $L_x, L_y, L_z$.

**Step 3 — Volume Integral:**
The volume is the triple integral over the reachable region:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cartesian} &= \int_0^{L_x}\int_0^{L_y}\int_0^{L_z} dz\,dy\,dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Inner Integral (over $z$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{L_z} dz = z\,\Big|_0^{L_z} = L_z
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate Middle Integral (over $y$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{L_y} L_z\,dy = L_z \cdot y\,\Big|_0^{L_y} = L_z L_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Outer Integral (over $x$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{L_x} L_z L_y\,dx = L_z L_y \cdot x\,\Big|_0^{L_x}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{cartesian} = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z\,}
$$

The orientation is fixed (no revolute joint), so $W_R = W_D = V_{cartesian}$ — every point can be approached in only one orientation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Work Volume of a Cylindrical (RPP) Robot

### 3.2.1 Setup

A cylindrical robot has one revolute joint $\theta$ (azimuth) and two prismatic joints: radial $r$ and vertical $z$.

$$0 \le r \le R_{max}, \quad 0 \le \theta \le 2\pi, \quad 0 \le z \le h$$

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics in Cylindrical Coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ee} &= \begin{bmatrix} r\cos\theta \\ r\sin\theta \\ z \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Geometric Solid:**
The locus is bounded by: $0 \le r \le R_{max}$ (radial wall), $0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$ (full revolution), $0 \le z \le h$ (vertical span). This describes a **right circular cylinder** of radius $R_{max}$ and height $h$.

**Step 3 — Volume Integral in Cylindrical Coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cyl} &= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^h\int_0^{R_{max}} r\,dr\,dz\,d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Note the Jacobian $r$ comes from the cylindrical coordinate change of variables.

**Step 4 — Evaluate Radial Integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{R_{max}} r\,dr = \tfrac{1}{2}r^2\,\Big|_0^{R_{max}} = \tfrac{1}{2}R_{max}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate Vertical Integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^h \tfrac{1}{2}R_{max}^2\,dz = \tfrac{1}{2}R_{max}^2 \cdot h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Azimuthal Integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{2\pi} \tfrac{1}{2}R_{max}^2 h\,d\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}R_{max}^2 h \cdot 2\pi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{cylindrical} = \pi R_{max}^2 h\,}
$$

**Generalized form** (for inner radius $R_{min} > 0$ due to base obstruction):

$$
V_{cyl,hollow} = \pi h (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2)
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Work Volume of a Spherical / Polar (RRP) Robot

### 3.3.1 Setup

A spherical robot has two revolute joints (azimuth $\theta$, elevation $\phi$) and one prismatic joint (radial $r$).

$$0 \le r \le r_{max}, \quad 0 \le \theta \le 2\pi, \quad 0 \le \phi \le \phi_{max}$$

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics in Spherical Coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ee} &= \begin{bmatrix} r\sin\phi\cos\theta \\ r\sin\phi\sin\theta \\ r\cos\phi \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Geometric Solid:**
The locus is a **spherical sector** (a cone-segmented region of a full sphere). The polar angle $\phi$ is restricted to $[0, \phi_{max}]$, where $\phi_{max} < \pi$.

**Step 3 — Volume Integral in Spherical Coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sph} &= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\phi_{max}}\int_0^{r_{max}} r^2 \sin\phi\,dr\,d\phi\,d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

The Jacobian for spherical coordinates is $r^2 \sin\phi$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate Radial Integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{r_{max}} r^2\,dr = \tfrac{1}{3}r^3\,\Big|_0^{r_{max}} = \tfrac{1}{3}r_{max}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate Polar Integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{\phi_{max}} \sin\phi\,d\phi = -\cos\phi\,\Big|_0^{\phi_{max}} = 1 - \cos\phi_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Azimuthal Integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{2\pi} d\theta = 2\pi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply All Three Results:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sph} &= 2\pi \cdot (1 - \cos\phi_{max}) \cdot \tfrac{1}{3}r_{max}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{spherical} = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi r_{max}^3 (1 - \cos\phi_{max})\,}
$$

**Special Case (full hemisphere, $\phi_{max} = \pi/2$):** $V = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi r_{max}^3$, which is exactly half the volume of a sphere $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$.

**Special Case (full sphere, $\phi_{max} = \pi$):** $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r_{max}^3$ — the familiar sphere volume.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Approximate Work Volume of an Articulated (RRR) Robot

### 3.4.1 Setup

For a planar 2-link articulated arm (extension to 3D gives toroidal shape), the links are $l_1$ (shoulder) and $l_2$ (elbow). With base rotation about vertical axis included, the 3D shape is a **torus**.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Maximum Reach:**
With both links collinear, the maximum reach is:

$$
R_{max} = l_1 + l_2
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum Reach:**
With the elbow folded fully (links opposing):

$$
R_{min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Outer Torus Volume (Big Radius $R_{max}$, Tube Radius $l_2$):**
Using the volume of a torus $V = 2\pi^2 R r^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{outer} = 2\pi^2 R_{max} l_2^2 = 2\pi^2 (l_1 + l_2) l_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Inner Torus Volume (Big Radius $R_{min}$, Tube Radius $l_2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{inner} = 2\pi^2 R_{min} l_2^2 = 2\pi^2 \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert l_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Effective Work Volume (Difference):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{art} &= V_{outer} - V_{inner} \\
&= 2\pi^2 l_2^2 [(l_1 + l_2) - \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Result (for $l_1 \ge l_2$):**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{articulated} = 4\pi^2 l_1 l_2^2\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is an **approximation** treating the 2-link cross-section as circular. The actual work volume has an indented "waist" near the base, so the true volume is slightly less.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (For Quick Exam Recall)

| Feature | Cartesian | Cylindrical | Spherical | Articulated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Joint Types | PPP | RPP | RRP | RRR |
| Workspace Shape | Box | Cylinder | Sphere sector | Donut / Torus |
| Dexterous Subset | None (1-orientation only) | Vertical axis | Center point | None (in 3D) |
| Rigidity | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest |
| Reach-to-Footprint Ratio | Lowest | Low | Medium | **Highest** |
| Typical Application | Pick-place, CNC loading | Machine tending | Welding, painting | Assembly, complex paths |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Work Volume Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROBOT WORK VOLUME W] --> B[REACHABLE WORKSPACE W sub R]
    A --> C[DEXTEROUS WORKSPACE W sub D]
    B --> D[Cartesian PPP - Cuboid]
    B --> E[Cylindrical RPP - Cylinder]
    B --> F[Spherical RRP - Sphere Sector]
    B --> G[Articulated RRR - Toroid]
    C --> H[Subset of W sub R]
    C --> I[All orientations possible]
    D --> J[Lx times Ly times Lz]
    E --> K[pi R squared h]
    F --> L[2 by 3 pi r cubed 1 minus cos phi]
    G --> M[4 pi squared l1 l2 squared approx]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Robot Configuration Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Application Need] --> B{High Reach Density?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Articulated RRR]
    B -->|No| D{Payload Heavy?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Cartesian PPP]
    D -->|No| F{Compact Reach?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Cylindrical RPP]
    F -->|No| H[Spherical RRP]
    C --> I[Painting, Welding, Assembly]
    E --> J[CNC Tending, Palletizing]
    G --> K[Machine Loading]
    H --> L[Material Handling]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Reachable vs Dexterous Workspace Geometry

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Outer Boundary: W sub R] --> B[Inner Boundary: W sub D]
    A --> C[Points reachable in at least 1 orientation]
    B --> D[Points reachable in ALL orientations]
    B --> E[Generally smaller central core]
    C --> F[Larger boundary volume]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flow — Work Volume Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Step 1: Identify Joint Sequence] --> B[Step 2: Set Joint Limits]
    B --> C[Step 3: Apply Forward Kinematics]
    C --> D[Step 4: Identify Geometric Solid]
    D --> E[Step 5: Apply Volume Integral]
    E --> F[Step 6: Evaluate with Jacobian]
    F --> G[Final V closed form formula]
    style G fill:#d4f4dd
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define Work Volume. Distinguish between Reachable Workspace and Dexterous Workspace with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Work Volume** of a robot is the three-dimensional region of space within which the end-effector can be positioned, given the mechanical limits of the robot's joints and links.

- **Reachable Workspace ($W_R$):** The set of all points in space that the end-effector can reach in **at least one orientation**. It is the **larger, outer** region of the work envelope.
- **Dexterous Workspace ($W_D$):** The set of all points that the end-effector can reach with **all possible orientations** ($\pm 90°$ about every axis). It is the **smaller, inner** region.

Geometrically, $W_D \subset W_R$. For a Cartesian robot, $W_D = \varnothing$ (no orientation freedom). For a spherical robot, $W_D$ is the single central point at the wrist.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2. List the four basic robot configurations with their joint types and the shape of their work volume.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Configuration | Joint Sequence | Work Volume Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cartesian / Gantry | P — P — P | Rectangular Box |
| 2 | Cylindrical | R — P — P | Cylinder |
| 3 | Spherical / Polar | R — R — P | Spherical Sector |
| 4 | Articulated / Anthropomorphic | R — R — R | Toroidal (donut) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Attempt Any One

**Q(a).** Derive the expression for the work volume of a **Spherical (Polar) configuration robot** with maximum reach $r_{max}$ and polar angle limit $\phi_{max}$. What is the special case value when $\phi_{max} = \pi/2$? **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the joint variables and limits:**
- Azimuth angle: $0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$
- Polar (elevation) angle: $0 \le \phi \le \phi_{max}$
- Radial reach: $0 \le r \le r_{max}$

**Step 2 — Write forward kinematics:**

$$
P_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} r\sin\phi\cos\theta \\ r\sin\phi\sin\theta \\ r\cos\phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Set up the volume integral in spherical coordinates with Jacobian $r^2\sin\phi$:**

$$
V_{sph} = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\phi_{max}}\int_0^{r_{max}} r^2 \sin\phi \, dr\,d\phi\,d\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over $r$:**

$$
\int_0^{r_{max}} r^2\,dr = \tfrac{1}{3}r_{max}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over $\phi$:**

$$
\int_0^{\phi_{max}} \sin\phi\,d\phi = 1 - \cos\phi_{max}
$$

**Step 6 — Integrate over $\theta$:**

$$
\int_0^{2\pi} d\theta = 2\pi
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply and write final answer:**

$$
\boxed{V_{spherical} = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi r_{max}^3 (1 - \cos\phi_{max})}
$$

**Step 8 — Special case for $\phi_{max} = \pi/2$ (hemisphere):**
$\cos(\pi/2) = 0$, hence:

$$
V_{hemi} = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi r_{max}^3 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r_{max}^3
$$

This is **exactly half** the volume of a complete sphere, as expected geometrically. **(Verified ✓)**

---

**Q(b).** A **cylindrical robot** has a maximum radial reach of $R_{max} = 1.5$ m, a minimum radial reach of $R_{min} = 0.4$ m (due to the central column), and a vertical stroke of $h = 2.0$ m. **(i)** Calculate the work volume. **(ii)** If the same robot is reconfigured as Cartesian with equivalent bounding box dimensions, what percentage of the Cartesian volume does the cylindrical volume represent? **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Cylindrical Work Volume:**

**Step 1 — Apply the hollow cylinder formula:**

$$
V_{cyl} = \pi h (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
R_{max}^2 = (1.5)^2 = 2.25 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
R_{min}^2 = (0.4)^2 = 0.16 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2 = 2.25 - 0.16 = 2.09 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
V_{cyl} = \pi \cdot 2.0 \cdot 2.09 = 4.18\pi
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical value:**

$$
V_{cyl} \approx 13.13 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Part (ii) — Comparison with Cartesian:**

**Step 5 — Equivalent Cartesian dimensions:**
A cylindrical robot with reach $2R_{max} = 3$ m and height $h = 2$ m encloses a Cartesian bounding box of:

$$
L_x = 3.0 \text{ m}, \quad L_y = 3.0 \text{ m}, \quad L_z = 2.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Cartesian volume:**

$$
V_{cart} = 3.0 \times 3.0 \times 2.0 = 18.0 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 7 — Percentage ratio:**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{V_{cyl}}{V_{cart}} \times 100\% = \frac{13.13}{18.0} \times 100\% \approx 72.94\%
$$

**Step 8 — Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{V_{cyl} \approx 13.13 \text{ m}^3, \quad \text{Ratio} \approx 72.94\%}
$$

This illustrates that the cylindrical configuration wastes about **27%** of its bounding-box volume due to the four corner regions it cannot reach. **(Insight verified ✓)**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q(a).** Explain in detail the **work volume of an articulated (RRR) robot**. Derive an approximate expression for its volume in terms of the link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$. Comment on why the actual work volume differs from this approximation. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup:**
An articulated robot has three revolute joints — base rotation $\theta_1$, shoulder $\theta_2$, elbow $\theta_3$. When viewed in a vertical plane through the base axis, the cross-section is the locus of a 2-link planar arm with links $l_1$ (upper arm) and $l_2$ (forearm).

**Step 2 — Cross-Sectional Boundary:**
In the 2D plane, the reach $r$ ranges from:

$$
R_{min} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert \quad \text{to} \quad R_{max} = l_1 + l_2
$$

**Step 3 — Treat Cross-Section as Annulus:**
The planar cross-section is an **annulus** (ring) with outer radius $R_{max}$ and inner radius $R_{min}$.

**Step 4 — Use Torus Volume Formula:**
When the base joint $\theta_1$ rotates through $2\pi$, this annulus sweeps a **torus** in 3D. The torus volume formula is:

$$
V_{torus} = 2\pi^2 R_{tube} \cdot r_{tube}^2
$$

where $R_{tube}$ is the major (big) radius from the torus center to the tube center, and $r_{tube}$ is the minor (tube) radius.

**Step 5 — Identify Torus Parameters:**
- Major radius (outer): $R_{tube,outer} = l_1 + l_2$
- Major radius (inner): $R_{tube,inner} = \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert$
- Minor radius (tube): $r_{tube} = l_2$

**Step 6 — Compute Outer Torus:**

$$
V_{outer} = 2\pi^2 (l_1 + l_2) l_2^2
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Inner Torus (subtract the unreachable inner hole):**

$$
V_{inner} = 2\pi^2 \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert l_2^2
$$

**Step 8 — Work Volume (Difference):**

$$
V_{art} = 2\pi^2 l_2^2 [(l_1 + l_2) - \vert l_1 - l_2 \vert]
$$

**Step 9 — Special case $l_1 \ge l_2$ (typical industrial design):**

$$
\boxed{V_{articulated} = 4\pi^2 l_1 l_2^2}
$$

**Step 10 — Comment on Approximation Error:**
The actual work volume is **less** than this formula predicts because:
- The base joint $\theta_1$ cannot always reach $\pm 180°$ (mechanical stops at $\pm 170°$ typical).
- The cross-section is not a perfect annulus — the elbow joint limit cuts an inner "C-shape" with two concave indentations near the base.
- Joint limits on $\theta_2$ (shoulder) prevent the full spherical reach near the extremes.

---

**Q(b).** Compare the **work volumes of all four basic robot configurations** in a single comparative table. State **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of the articulated robot configuration for industrial use. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (for 7 marks, a detailed table is essential):**

| Parameter | Cartesian (PPP) | Cylindrical (RPP) | Spherical (RRP) | Articulated (RRR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Work Volume Shape | Rectangular cuboid | Hollow cylinder | Spherical sector | Toroidal / complex |
| Reach Formula | $L_x L_y L_z$ | $\pi h R^2$ | $\tfrac{2}{3}\pi r^3 (1-\cos\phi)$ | $\approx 4\pi^2 l_1 l_2^2$ |
| Dexterous Workspace | None (1 orientation) | Vertical line (axis) | Single central point | None (in 3-D) |
| Footprint Area | Largest | Moderate | Small | **Smallest** |
| Rigidity / Stiffness | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest |
| Joint Complexity | Simple | Simple | Medium | High |
| Programming Complexity | Easiest | Easy | Medium | Hardest |
| Cost | High (long linear guides) | Medium | Medium | Medium-Low |
| Typical Accuracy | $\pm 0.025$ mm | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $\pm 0.1$ mm | $\pm 0.2$ mm |
| Best Applications | CNC, pick-place, assembly | Machine tending, palletizing | Welding, painting | Assembly, complex paths, painting |

**Advantages of Articulated Robots:**

1. **Highest reach-to-footprint ratio:** They can reach into confined spaces (e.g., inside a car body) with a small base footprint, maximizing workspace utilization in a crowded cell.
2. **Maximum flexibility of motion:** With 3 revolute joints, they can orient the end-effector in nearly any pose, making them ideal for complex 3D trajectories like welding seams or spray painting.

**Disadvantages of Articulated Robots:**

1. **Lower rigidity and accuracy:** The cantilevered structure causes link deflection under load, reducing repeatability to $\pm 0.2$ mm or worse compared to $\pm 0.025$ mm for Cartesian.
2. **Complex kinematics and singularities:** They exhibit wrist, elbow, and shoulder singularities where joint velocities can become infinite, requiring careful path planning and controller algorithms.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Work Volume Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Jacobian** when integrating in cylindrical or spherical coordinates — losing **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up Reachable vs Dexterous** workspace definitions — a classic **2-mark trap**.
> 3. **Not stating units** (e.g., $V = 4.18\pi$ m³ vs. just $4.18\pi$) — **0.5–1 mark** deduction.
> 4. **Writing $r^2$ instead of $r^2 \sin\phi$** in the spherical integral — the $\sin\phi$ is mandatory.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention $W_D \subset W_R$** in definitions — loses the strict subset relationship mark.
> 6. **Writing joint sequence incorrectly** (e.g., RPR vs RPP for cylindrical) — full marks cut for configuration identification.
> 7. **No sketch** in definition questions — KTU examiners explicitly award **1 mark** for a labeled diagram.
> 8. **Confusing radius $R$ with diameter** in volume formulas — gives wrong numerical answers in problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Work Volume**

- ✅ **Work Volume** = 3D region reachable by end-effector; also called workspace or envelope.
- ✅ **Two types:** Reachable ($W_R$, larger) and Dexterous ($W_D$, smaller, subset).
- ✅ **Four configurations** — memorize joint sequence + shape:
  - **PPP** → **Box** (Cartesian)
  - **RPP** → **Cylinder** (Cylindrical)
  - **RRP** → **Sphere sector** (Spherical)
  - **RRR** → **Donut/Toroid** (Articulated)
- ✅ **Cartesian volume:** $V = L_x L_y L_z$
- ✅ **Cylindrical volume:** $V = \pi h (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2)$
- ✅ **Spherical volume:** $V = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi r_{max}^3 (1 - \cos\phi_{max})$
- ✅ **Articulated approx:** $V \approx 4\pi^2 l_1 l_2^2$ (for $l_1 \ge l_2$)
- ✅ **Always include the Jacobian** ($r$, $r^2\sin\phi$) in volume integrals for non-Cartesian coordinates.
- ✅ **Dexterous workspace** requires the end-effector to reach with **all orientations**; for Cartesian it is empty.
- ✅ **Factors affecting work volume:** link lengths, joint limits, tool offset, payload deflection, mounting orientation, singularities.
- ✅ **Articulated robots** offer the **best reach-to-footprint ratio** but the **lowest rigidity**.
- ✅ **Industrial applications** are dictated by work volume shape: boxes for pick-place, cylinders for machine tending, sphere sectors for welding, toroids for complex assembly.
- ✅ **Units rule:** Always express volume in $\text{m}^3$ or $\text{mm}^3$ — never leave dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity and Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capacity and Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Capacity in Robotics

**Capacity** in industrial robotics refers to the **maximum payload** that a robot manipulator can carry while maintaining its rated performance specifications (speed, accuracy, and repeatability) throughout its entire workspace. It is one of the most critical specifications that dictates the suitability of a robot for a given industrial application.

> [!NOTE]
> **Official KTU Definition:**
> *Robot capacity (also called payload capacity) is the maximum mass that a robot can manipulate at a specified speed, acceleration, and orientation without violating its structural, actuator, and performance limits. It is expressed in kilograms (kg) and is a static-rated parameter.*

Capacity is **not just a weight limit** — it is a complex envelope that includes:
- **Mass of the end-effector + workpiece**
- **Force/torque moments** generated by the offset (eccentric) loading
- **Inertial effects** during high-speed motion
- **Thermal limits** of the servo motors

## 1.2 Speed in Robotics

**Speed** in robotics denotes the rate at which the robot's end-effector (or Tool Center Point, **TCP**) moves through space, or alternatively, the angular velocity of individual joints. It is fundamentally a **kinematic** quantity that determines cycle time and productivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:**
> Speed must always be specified *with* the payload condition. A robot rated for **2 m/s with 0 kg payload** may only achieve **1.2 m/s with its full rated payload**, because increased mass reduces achievable acceleration.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Delivery Boy on a Bicycle:**
> Imagine a delivery boy on a bicycle. His **capacity** is how much weight he can carry in the basket — say 25 kg. The heavier the load, the **slower** he must pedal uphill, and the **less agile** he becomes in turns. Likewise, in a robot:
> - **Capacity = max basket weight (payload)**
> - **Speed = pedaling rate (TCP velocity)**
> - **Acceleration = how quickly he sprints (joint acceleration)**
> - The manufacturer's spec sheet always gives these values **together**, just like a bicycle's "gear ratio vs. slope" table.

The moment you overload a robot, the controller reduces speed automatically (called **payload-adaptive speed limiting**) to protect the gear trains and maintain trajectory accuracy.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Unit | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Payload Capacity | **kilogram (kg)** | $m_p$ |
| TCP Speed | **meter/second (m/s)** | $v_{tcp}$ |
| Joint Speed | **radian/second (rad/s)** | $\dot{\theta}_i$ |
| Acceleration | **m/s²** | $a$ |
| Cycle Time | **second (s)** | $T_c$ |
| Inertia | **kg·m²** | $I$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Robot Capacity

The **rated payload** $m_p$ of a robot is determined by the weakest of the following limits:

1. **Static Force Limit** — The maximum load the weakest joint actuator (servo motor) can hold against gravity at full reach.
2. **Dynamic (Inertial) Limit** — The maximum mass that can be accelerated/decelerated without exceeding motor torque.
3. **Structural (Mechanical) Limit** — The yield strength of the mechanical structure (links, gearboxes, bearings).
4. **Thermal Limit** — Continuous-duty heating of the motors at rated current.

The **effective payload** that the engineer must consider is:

$$
m_{eff} = m_{workpiece} + m_{end\text{-}effector} + m_{gripper\,fixture}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often test whether students include the **end-effector mass** in the payload calculation. Forgetting this is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.

## 2.2 Speed Specifications — Three Levels

Robots have speed specified at **three distinct levels**, and KTU frequently asks the difference:

### (a) Joint Speed $\dot{\theta}_i$
The angular velocity of an individual joint $i$, in **rad/s**. Limited by motor RPM, gear ratio, and controller bandwidth.

### (b) TCP Speed $v_{tcp}$
The linear velocity of the Tool Center Point in Cartesian space, in **m/s**. This is what the user observes and what datasheets quote (e.g., *"Max TCP speed: 1.5 m/s"*).

### (c) Speed Along a Programmed Path $v_{path}$
The speed along a multi-point trajectory (linear, circular, or spline). For a linear segment of length $L$ traversed in time $T$:

$$
v_{path} = \frac{L}{T} \quad [\text{m/s}]
$$

## 2.3 The Capacity–Speed Trade-off (Core Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is the most-tested concept in KTU Module 4:**
> Capacity and speed are **inversely coupled** through motor torque and power constraints. A robot cannot simultaneously deliver *maximum* payload and *maximum* speed.

The governing relationship comes from Newton's second law for a joint motor delivering torque $\tau$ to move an effective inertia $I_{eff}$:

$$
\tau = I_{eff}\,\ddot{\theta} + \tau_{gravity}(\theta) + \tau_{friction}
$$

At **steady-state maximum speed** (acceleration = 0, gravity balanced), the motor's available torque budget is fully consumed by the load. Hence:

$$
\tau_{motor} \geq \tau_{gravity} = (m_p + m_{link})\,g\,r
$$

If we demand higher acceleration, residual torque is consumed by $I_{eff}\ddot{\theta}$, **reducing permissible $m_p$**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_{eff} = m_p + m_{ee}$ | Effective payload | Capacity check |
| 2 | $v_{tcp} = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2 + \dot{z}^2}$ | Cartesian TCP speed | Trajectory planning |
| 3 | $v_{path} = L/T$ | Path speed | Cycle time |
| 4 | $T_c = T_{motion} + T_{process} + T_{idle}$ | Total cycle time | Productivity |
| 5 | $\tau = I_{eff}\alpha + \tau_g + \tau_f$ | Joint torque eqn. | Capacity limit |
| 6 | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Motor power | Speed-power sizing |
| 7 | $v_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 a d_{brake}}{1}}$ (decel) | Max safe TCP speed | Safety |
| 8 | $\omega_{max} = \dfrac{v_{tcp}}{r_{reach}}$ | Joint speed ↔ TCP speed | Kinematics |
| 9 | $a_{centripetal} = \dfrac{v^2}{r}$ | Cornering acceleration | Path speed limit |
| 10 | $T_c = \dfrac{L_{total}}{v_{avg}}$ | Cycle time (single path) | Quick estimate |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table, do **not** write $\vert x \vert$ with vertical bars inside markdown tables — use $\lvert x \rvert$ (LaTeX `lvert`/`rvert`) to prevent the cell separator `\|` from breaking the table parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive welding lines** require *high payload + moderate speed* (e.g., 100 kg @ 1 m/s).
- **Pick-and-place packaging** requires *low payload + high speed* (e.g., 3 kg @ 5 m/s) — SCARA/Delta robots.
- **Heavy-payload foundry robots** trade speed for *huge payload* (e.g., 500 kg @ 0.5 m/s).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cycle Time for a Pick-and-Place Operation

> **Problem Setup (typical KTU Part B 14-mark pattern):**
> A 6-axis robot moves a workpiece of mass $m_p = 5$ kg along a straight horizontal path of length $L = 1.5$ m, accelerates at $a = 2$ m/s² to a maximum velocity $v_{max} = 1.5$ m/s, then decelerates at $a = -2$ m/s² to rest. Compute the motion time and the cycle time if a gripper operation takes $t_{grip} = 0.6$ s.

**Step 1 — Identify the velocity profile.**

The motion follows a **trapezoidal velocity profile** because $L$ is large enough to reach $v_{max}$:

$$
\text{Profile: Accelerate} \rightarrow \text{Coast at } v_{max} \rightarrow \text{Decelerate}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the distance needed to accelerate to $v_{max}$.**

$$
d_{acc} = \frac{v_{max}^2 - v_0^2}{2a} = \frac{(1.5)^2 - 0}{2 \times 2} = \frac{2.25}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{acc} = 0.5625 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 3 — By symmetry, the deceleration distance equals the acceleration distance.**

$$
d_{dec} = d_{acc} = 0.5625 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the constant-velocity (coasting) distance.**

$$
d_{coast} = L - d_{acc} - d_{dec} = 1.5 - 0.5625 - 0.5625
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{coast} = 0.375 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the acceleration time.**

$$
t_{acc} = \frac{v_{max} - v_0}{a} = \frac{1.5 - 0}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{acc} = 0.75 \text{ s}}
$$

**Step 6 — By symmetry, the deceleration time is the same.**

$$
t_{dec} = t_{acc} = 0.75 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the coasting time.**

$$
t_{coast} = \frac{d_{coast}}{v_{max}} = \frac{0.375}{1.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{coast} = 0.25 \text{ s}}
$$

**Step 8 — Total motion time.**

$$
T_{motion} = t_{acc} + t_{coast} + t_{dec} = 0.75 + 0.25 + 0.75
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{motion} = 1.75 \text{ s}}
$$

**Step 9 — Add process and gripper idle time for total cycle time.**

$$
T_c = T_{motion} + t_{grip} = 1.75 + 0.6
$$

$$
\boxed{T_c = 2.35 \text{ s per cycle}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Joint Torque for a Given Payload (Capacity Check)

> **Problem Setup:**
> A robot's wrist joint must hold a payload of $m_p = 10$ kg at a reach of $r = 0.6$ m from the joint axis, with the arm oriented horizontally. Find the gravitational torque and the motor power needed at joint speed $\omega = 1$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Identify forces acting on the joint.**

The payload creates a moment about the joint axis:

$$
\tau_g = m_p \cdot g \cdot r
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute standard gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s².**

$$
\tau_g = 10 \times 9.81 \times 0.6
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_g = 58.86 \text{ N·m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Add link mass contribution (link mass $m_l = 5$ kg at center of mass $r_l = 0.3$ m).**

$$
\tau_{link} = m_l \cdot g \cdot r_l = 5 \times 9.81 \times 0.3
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{link} = 14.715 \text{ N·m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Total static torque demand.**

$$
\tau_{total} = \tau_g + \tau_{link} = 58.86 + 14.715
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{total} = 73.575 \text{ N·m}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute required motor power at $\omega = 1$ rad/s.**

$$
P = \tau_{total} \cdot \omega = 73.575 \times 1
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 73.575 \text{ W} \approx 0.074 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **static (holding)** calculation. For motion, you must also add the dynamic torque $\tau_{dyn} = I_{eff}\,\alpha$, which depends on link and payload inertias.

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Permissible Speed Around a Curved Path

When the TCP moves along a circular arc of radius $R$ at speed $v$, the centripetal acceleration is:

$$
a_c = \frac{v^2}{R}
$$

The maximum allowed centripetal acceleration for a given payload is fixed by the gripper's holding force. If the gripper can sustain $a_{c,max} = 4g$:

$$
v_{max} = \sqrt{a_{c,max} \cdot R} = \sqrt{4 \cdot 9.81 \cdot R}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{max} = \sqrt{39.24\,R} \quad [\text{m/s}]}
$$

For $R = 0.5$ m: $v_{max} = \sqrt{19.62} \approx 4.43$ m/s.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Scenario | Primary Formula | Secondary Check |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity verification (static) | $\tau_g = m_p g r$ | Motor stall torque |
| Capacity verification (dynamic) | $\tau = I_{eff}\alpha + \tau_g$ | Motor peak torque |
| Single-axis motion time | $v = v_0 + at$ | $d = v_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$ |
| Cycle time (multi-segment) | $T_c = \sum T_{segment} + T_{process}$ | Gripper dwell time |
| TCP speed from joints | $v = J(\theta) \cdot \dot{\theta}$ | Singularities |
| Path speed limit (curved) | $v = \sqrt{a_c R}$ | Payload grip limit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Factors Governing Robot Capacity and Speed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Capacity and Speed] --> B[Mechanical Design]
    A --> C[Actuator Sizing]
    A --> D[Controller Logic]
    A --> E[Payload Conditions]

    B --> B1[Link Length]
    B --> B2[Link Mass]
    B --> B3[Structural Stiffness]
    B --> B4[Joint Type Revolute or Prismatic]

    C --> C1[Servo Motor Torque]
    C --> C2[Motor Power Rating]
    C --> C3[Gear Ratio]
    C --> C4[Thermal Limit]

    D --> D1[Trajectory Planner]
    D --> D2[Speed Override]
    D --> D3[Payload Adaptive Limiter]
    D --> D4[Acceleration Profiler]

    E --> E1[Mass of Workpiece]
    E --> E2[End Effector Mass]
    E --> E3[Eccentric Offset]
    E --> E4[Inertial Coupling]

    B1 --> F[Final Rated Performance]
    B2 --> F
    B3 --> F
    B4 --> F
    C1 --> F
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F

    F --> G[Rated Payload m_p]
    F --> H[Max TCP Speed v_tcp]
    F --> I[Cycle Time T_c]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Capacity–Speed Trade-off Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Payload Demand] --> CTRL{Controller}
    S[Speed Demand] --> CTRL
    A[Acceleration Demand] --> CTRL

    CTRL --> CALC[Torque and Power Calculator]
    CALC --> COMP{Within Actuator Envelope}

    COMP -->|Yes| PERF[Robot Executes Motion at Requested Specs]
    COMP -->|No| LIMIT[Payload Adaptive Speed Limiter Reduces v and a]

    PERF --> OUT1[Achievable TCP Speed v_out]
    LIMIT --> OUT2[Reduced TCP Speed v_limited]

    OUT1 --> PROD[Productivity High]
    OUT2 --> SAFE[Safety and Motor Protection Maintained]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Speed Specification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Robot Speed Specification] --> L1[Level 1 Joint Speed dot theta]
    TOP --> L2[Level 2 TCP Speed v_tcp]
    TOP --> L3[Level 3 Path Speed v_path]

    L1 --> L1A[Limited by Motor RPM]
    L1 --> L1B[Limited by Gear Ratio]
    L1 --> L1C[Limited by Controller Bandwidth]

    L2 --> L2A[Computed via Jacobian J theta]
    L2 --> L2B[Datasheet Spec at 0 kg]
    L2 --> L2C[Reduced at Full Payload]

    L3 --> L3A[Linear Segment Speed]
    L3 --> L3B[Circular Arc Speed]
    L3 --> L3C[Spline Blend Speed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above are **exhaustive enough to score full marks** in a KTU 14-mark descriptive question where the question asks you to *"explain the relationship between robot capacity and speed with a block diagram."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "rated payload" of a robot and list two factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **rated payload** of a robot is the maximum mass, including the end-effector and the workpiece, that the robot can repeatedly handle at rated speed, acceleration, and pose without exceeding any of its design limits (structural, actuator, thermal).

**Two factors affecting rated payload:**
1. **Reach of the robot** — Greater reach increases the gravitational moment arm, reducing permissible payload.
2. **Joint actuator torque** — The motor's continuous and peak torque limits the maximum moment it can sustain against gravity and inertia.

*(Acceptable alternative: payload orientation, mounting configuration, duty cycle, acceleration profile.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between TCP speed and joint speed in a robot.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Joint Speed | TCP Speed |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Angular velocity of an individual joint | Linear velocity of the Tool Center Point in Cartesian space |
| Unit | rad/s | m/s |
| Dependency | Independent per joint | Depends on **all** joint speeds and the **Jacobian** $J(\theta)$ |
| Datasheet | Quoted per axis | Quoted as a single max value |
| Variation with reach | Not affected | **Higher reach → lower TCP speed** at same joint speed |

---

## Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)
### (Module Internal Choice Pattern – KTU 2024 Scheme)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

#### (a) [7 Marks]
A SCARA robot has a rated payload of 6 kg. Its end-effector (gripper) weighs 1.2 kg, and the workpiece to be handled weighs 3.5 kg. The wrist joint is located 0.45 m from the joint axis.
1. Calculate the effective payload.
2. Determine whether the robot can handle this load under gravity, given that the wrist motor's continuous torque is **28 N·m** and the link mass is **2 kg** acting at its center of mass **0.25 m** from the joint axis. Use $g = 9.81$ m/s².

#### (b) [7 Marks]
The same robot must execute a horizontal linear pick-and-place of length **0.9 m** with a trapezoidal velocity profile: acceleration $a = 1.5$ m/s², maximum speed $v_{max} = 1.0$ m/s, deceleration $a = -1.5$ m/s². Compute:
1. The acceleration, coasting, and deceleration distances.
2. The total motion time and the cycle time (gripper actuation takes **0.4 s** at each end).

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

Step 1 — Effective payload:
$$
m_{eff} = m_{workpiece} + m_{gripper} = 3.5 + 1.2 = 4.7 \text{ kg}
$$

Step 2 — Gravitational torque on wrist joint:
$$
\tau_g = m_{eff} \cdot g \cdot r = 4.7 \times 9.81 \times 0.45
$$
$$
\boxed{\tau_g \approx 20.74 \text{ N·m}}
$$

Step 3 — Torque from link mass:
$$
\tau_{link} = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 = 4.905 \text{ N·m}
$$

Step 4 — Total static torque:
$$
\tau_{total} = 20.74 + 4.905 = 25.645 \text{ N·m}
$$

Step 5 — Comparison with motor capacity:
$$
\tau_{motor} = 28 \text{ N·m} > 25.645 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Yes, the robot CAN handle the load (with } \approx 2.35 \text{ N·m safety margin).}}
$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

Step 1 — Acceleration distance:
$$
d_{acc} = \frac{v_{max}^2}{2a} = \frac{(1.0)^2}{2 \times 1.5} = \frac{1.0}{3.0}
$$
$$
\boxed{d_{acc} = 0.333 \text{ m}}
$$

Step 2 — Deceleration distance (by symmetry):
$$
d_{dec} = 0.333 \text{ m}
$$

Step 3 — Coasting distance:
$$
d_{coast} = L - d_{acc} - d_{dec} = 0.9 - 0.333 - 0.333
$$
$$
\boxed{d_{coast} = 0.234 \text{ m}}
$$

Step 4 — Acceleration time:
$$
t_{acc} = \frac{v_{max}}{a} = \frac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.667 \text{ s}
$$

Step 5 — Deceleration time:
$$
t_{dec} = 0.667 \text{ s}
$$

Step 6 — Coasting time:
$$
t_{coast} = \frac{0.234}{1.0} = 0.234 \text{ s}
$$

Step 7 — Total motion time:
$$
T_{motion} = 0.667 + 0.234 + 0.667 = 1.568 \text{ s}
$$

Step 8 — Cycle time (motion + 2 gripper dwells):
$$
T_c = 1.568 + 2 \times 0.4
$$
$$
\boxed{T_c = 2.368 \text{ s}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

#### (a) [7 Marks]
Explain with a neat block diagram the **relationship between robot capacity and speed**. Discuss how increasing the payload affects the maximum achievable TCP speed.

#### (b) [7 Marks]
A 6-DOF articulated robot's joint motor has a stall torque of 50 N·m and a no-load speed of 3000 RPM. The gearbox ratio is 50:1. The joint carries an effective inertia of $I_{eff} = 1.2$ kg·m² (after the gearbox). Calculate:
1. The maximum joint angular speed at the output.
2. The torque available at the joint after the gearbox.
3. The maximum acceleration the joint can deliver to the load.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Explanation:**

Robot **capacity** (payload) and **speed** are tightly coupled through the motor's torque and power budget. The joint torque equation governing the relationship is:

$$
\tau_m = I_{eff}\,\ddot{\theta} + m_{eff}\,g\,r\cos\theta + B\dot{\theta} + \tau_{friction}
$$

where $B\dot{\theta}$ represents viscous friction losses that grow with speed. The block diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes illustrates the functional architecture. As payload increases:

- The gravity term $m_{eff} g r \cos\theta$ increases linearly.
- The remaining torque budget for acceleration decreases.
- Hence $v_{max}$ (steady-state) and $\ddot{\theta}_{max}$ (transient) both drop.
- The controller enforces a **payload-adaptive speed limit** to prevent motor overload.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Output (joint) angular speed from motor no-load speed:
$$
\omega_{joint} = \frac{\omega_{motor}}{N} = \frac{3000 \text{ RPM}}{50}
$$
$$
\omega_{joint} = 60 \text{ RPM} = 60 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 2\pi \text{ rad/s}
$$
$$
\boxed{\omega_{joint} = 6.283 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

Step 2 — Torque at the joint (after gearbox, ideal):
$$
\tau_{joint} = \tau_{stall} \times N = 50 \times 50
$$
$$
\boxed{\tau_{joint} = 2500 \text{ N·m}}
$$

Step 3 — Maximum acceleration (assuming all torque is used for acceleration, gravity balanced):
$$
\alpha_{max} = \frac{\tau_{joint}}{I_{eff}} = \frac{2500}{1.2}
$$
$$
\boxed{\alpha_{max} \approx 2083.3 \text{ rad/s}^2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion**: Students frequently forget to convert **RPM → rad/s**. One RPM = $2\pi/60$ rad/s. Losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Gear ratio direction**: A *speed-reducing* gearbox of ratio 50:1 means output speed is motor speed / 50, but output torque is motor torque × 50. Many students invert this. Losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Cycle time includes both gripper actions**: In a pick-and-place, gripper open + close happens at BOTH ends, so $t_{grip}$ is doubled. Forgetting this is the most common 2-mark error.
> 4. **Effective payload**: Always include end-effector mass. Forgetting it = 1 mark deduction.
> 5. **Trapezoidal vs triangular profile**: If the path is too short to reach $v_{max}$, the profile becomes **triangular** (no coasting). Always check: $2 d_{acc} < L$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Capacity = payload** (kg). Always include end-effector mass in the calculation.
- **Speed has 3 levels**: Joint (rad/s) → TCP (m/s) → Path (m/s).
- **Capacity and speed are inversely coupled** through motor torque budget. Higher payload → lower max speed.
- **Cycle time** = motion time + process time (welding, painting) + gripper dwell.
- **Trapezoidal velocity profile** dominates KTU problems: $d_{acc} = v_{max}^2 / (2a)$, $t_{acc} = v_{max}/a$.
- **Triangular profile** occurs when $2 d_{acc} \geq L$ — the robot never reaches $v_{max}$.
- **Joint torque equation**: $\tau = I_{eff}\alpha + \tau_{gravity} + \tau_{friction}$ is the master relationship.
- **Centripetal limit on curved paths**: $v_{max} = \sqrt{a_c R}$.
- **Always convert RPM to rad/s** in numerical problems.
- **Always include link mass torque** in addition to payload torque for static capacity checks.
- **Gear ratio**: Reduces speed, multiplies torque (for speed-reducing gearboxes used in robots).
- **Damping/friction grows with speed**, so payload-speed trade-off is non-linear at high speeds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stroke and Reach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stroke and Reach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Reach (R)

**Reach** is defined as the **maximum straight-line distance** measured from the **center of the robot's base** (or shoulder origin) to the **farthest point of the end-effector (TCP – Tool Center Point)** when all the manipulator links are **fully extended in a straight line** along a single radial direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Reach (R) is the maximum distance from the robot base coordinate frame to the extreme end of the end-effector when the arm is fully stretched out."*

Mathematically, for an *n*-link serial manipulator:
$$R_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + \dots + L_n$$

where $L_i$ is the length of the *i*-th link of the manipulator.

## 1.2 Stroke (S)

**Stroke** is defined as the **difference between the maximum reach and the minimum reach** of the robot. It quantifies the **range of linear motion** available to the end-effector along the radial direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** Stroke represents the *thickness* of the annular (ring-shaped) region of the workspace — the portion of the radial extent that the end-effector can actually sweep through.

$$S = R_{max} - R_{min}$$

## 1.3 Minimum Reach (R_min)

The **minimum reach** is the **shortest straight-line distance** from the robot's base origin to the end-effector when the manipulator is **fully folded/retracted** into its most compact configuration.

For a serial manipulator with $n$ links:
$$R_{min} = \left| L_1 - (L_2 + L_3 + \dots + L_n) \right|$$

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Arm:**
> - Your **Reach** is the distance from your **shoulder** to your **fingertip** when your arm is fully stretched out sideways.
> - Your **Stroke** is the difference between your **fingertip-when-stretched** and your **fingertip-when-elbow-is-fully-bent**.
> - The **fingertip** sweeps an annular region (donut shape) — never the spot directly under your shoulder (that is the **dead zone**).
> - In robotics, this donut-shaped region is the **workspace envelope** defined by Stroke.

| Parameter | Human Arm Equivalent | Robot Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Reach (R_max) | Shoulder → Fingertip (stretched) | Base → TCP (arm extended) |
| Stroke (S) | Difference between stretched & bent positions | $R_{max} - R_{min}$ |
| Dead Zone | Area under the shoulder (unreachable) | Inner sphere (cannot be reached) |
| Workspace | Donut-shaped 3D envelope | Annular/Complex 3D volume |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Concept of Workspace Envelope

A robot manipulator's **workspace** is the set of all points in 3D space that the end-effector can reach. The reach and stroke parameters define the **outer and inner boundaries** of this workspace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Critical Geometric Boundaries:**
> 1. **Outer Boundary:** A sphere of radius $R_{max}$ centered at the base — defines how *far* the robot can go.
> 2. **Inner Boundary:** A sphere of radius $R_{min}$ centered at the base — defines the **dead zone** (region the robot cannot enter).
> 3. **Stroke (S):** The thickness $R_{max} - R_{min}$ of the accessible annular region.

## 2.2 Reach and Stroke by Robot Configuration

The values of $R_{max}$, $R_{min}$, and $S$ depend on the **robot's kinematic structure**.

### (a) Cartesian (PPP) Robot
- Workspace: Rectangular/Cuboidal box
- Reach: $R = L_x + L_y + L_z$ (cubic diagonal — maximum reach is the corner-to-base distance)
- Stroke in each axis: independent of other axes

### (b) Cylindrical (RPP) Robot
- Workspace: Hollow cylinder
- Outer radius: $R_{max} = r_{max}$
- Inner radius: $R_{min} = r_{min}$ (if revolute joint has limits, otherwise = 0)
- Vertical reach: $L_{prismatic}$

### (c) Polar / Spherical (RRP) Robot
- Workspace: Spherical sector
- $R_{max} = r_1 + r_2$ (revolute + prismatic fully extended)
- $R_{min} = r_1$ (minimum horizontal arm length)

### (d) Articulated (3R) Robot
- Workspace: Complex shape (sphere minus inner dead zone)
- $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$
- $R_{min} = \max(0, \ L_1 - (L_2 + L_3))$

### (e) SCARA (RRP) Robot
- Workspace: Annular region in horizontal plane
- $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$
- $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **all key formulas** that KTU examiners typically test. **Memorize these before any exam.**

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units | Applicable Robot |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum Reach (general serial) | $R_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$ | metres (m) | All serial manipulators |
| 2 | Minimum Reach (general serial) | $R_{min} = \left\vert L_1 - \sum_{i=2}^{n} L_i \right\vert$ | m | All serial manipulators |
| 3 | Stroke (general) | $S = R_{max} - R_{min}$ | m | All manipulators |
| 4 | 2R Planar – Max Reach | $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$ | m | 2R planar arm |
| 5 | 2R Planar – Min Reach | $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ | m | 2R planar arm |
| 6 | 2R Planar – Stroke | $S = 2 \cdot \min(L_1, L_2)$ | m | 2R planar arm |
| 7 | 2R Planar – Workspace Area | $A_w = \pi(R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) = 4\pi L_1 L_2$ | $\text{m}^2$ | 2R planar arm |
| 8 | 3R Articulated – Max Reach | $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$ | m | PUMA-type |
| 9 | 3R Articulated – Min Reach | $R_{min} = \max(0, \ \vert L_1 - L_2 - L_3 \vert)$ | m | PUMA-type |
| 10 | Workspace Volume (spherical shell) | $V_w = \frac{4}{3}\pi(R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3)$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Spherical workspace |
| 11 | Dead Zone Volume | $V_{dz} = \frac{4}{3}\pi R_{min}^3$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Inner unreachable sphere |
| 12 | Joint-space distance (2R) | $r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2$ | $\text{m}^2$ | 2R mechanism |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM RULE:** In the formula $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - \sum L_i \vert$, the result **cannot be negative**. Always take the **maximum** of zero and the absolute value. Writing $-0.1$ m as a "minimum reach" will be marked **WRONG**.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Reach and Stroke Analysis

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in real engineering?**
> - **Workcell Design:** Before installing a robot, engineers must know the *exact* $R_{max}$ to position the conveyor, fixtures, and safety fencing.
> - **Payload Sizing:** The maximum reach determines the **worst-case payload moment** — payload torque is maximum at full extension.
> - **Cycle Time Optimization:** Choosing a robot with the *right stroke* (not over-sized) saves cost and floor space.
> - **Reachability Verification (offline programming):** All programmed waypoints must satisfy $R_{min} \le r \le R_{max}$ — this is checked by inverse kinematics solvers.
> - **Collision Avoidance:** The dead zone (inner sphere) is often where the robot's own base is mounted, so the robot **cannot reach its own pedestal** — critical for self-collision checks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reach and Stroke of a 2R Planar Manipulator

**Problem Setup:** Consider a 2-link revolute (2R) planar manipulator in the XY-plane.

- **Link 1 length:** $L_1$, joint angle $\theta_1$
- **Link 2 length:** $L_2$, joint angle $\theta_2$
- **End-effector position:**
$$x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$
$$y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

### Step 1: Compute the radial distance $r$ from origin

$$r^2 = x^2 + y^2$$

Substitute the expressions for $x$ and $y$:

$$r^2 = [L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)]^2 + [L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]^2$$

### Step 2: Expand both squares

$$r^2 = L_1^2 \cos^2\theta_1 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_2^2 \cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$
$$+ \ L_1^2 \sin^2\theta_1 + 2 L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_2^2 \sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

### Step 3: Apply the Pythagorean identity

Since $\cos^2\alpha + \sin^2\alpha = 1$, the squared terms simplify:

$$L_1^2 \cos^2\theta_1 + L_1^2 \sin^2\theta_1 = L_1^2$$
$$L_2^2 \cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_2^2 \sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) = L_2^2$$

### Step 4: Apply the cosine subtraction identity

The cross-terms collapse using $\cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B = \cos(A - B)$:

$$2 L_1 L_2 [\cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]$$
$$= 2 L_1 L_2 \cos[\theta_1 - (\theta_1+\theta_2)] = 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(-\theta_2) = 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2$$

### Step 5: Final radial distance expression

$$\boxed{\,r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2\,}$$

### Step 6: Maximum reach ($R_{max}$)

The maximum of $r^2$ occurs when $\cos\theta_2$ is maximum, i.e., $\cos\theta_2 = +1$, which means $\theta_2 = 0$ (arm fully extended straight).

$$r_{max}^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 (1) = (L_1 + L_2)^2$$
$$\boxed{\,R_{max} = L_1 + L_2\,}$$

### Step 7: Minimum reach ($R_{min}$)

The minimum of $r^2$ occurs when $\cos\theta_2$ is minimum, i.e., $\cos\theta_2 = -1$, which means $\theta_2 = \pi$ (arm fully folded back).

$$r_{min}^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 (-1) = (L_1 - L_2)^2$$
$$\boxed{\,R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert\,}$$

### Step 8: Stroke

$$S = R_{max} - R_{min} = (L_1 + L_2) - \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$$

If $L_1 \ge L_2$:
$$S = (L_1 + L_2) - (L_1 - L_2) = 2 L_2$$

If $L_2 \ge L_1$:
$$S = (L_1 + L_2) - (L_2 - L_1) = 2 L_1$$

In **either case**:
$$\boxed{\,S = 2 \cdot \min(L_1, L_2)\,}$$

### Step 9: Workspace area

The 2R planar manipulator sweeps an **annular region** (donut) of width equal to the stroke $S$. The accessible area is the area between two concentric circles of radii $R_{min}$ and $R_{max}$:

$$A_w = \pi R_{max}^2 - \pi R_{min}^2 = \pi (L_1+L_2)^2 - \pi (L_1 - L_2)^2$$

Expanding the difference of squares:

$$A_w = \pi \left[(L_1+L_2)^2 - (L_1-L_2)^2\right] = \pi \cdot 4 L_1 L_2$$
$$\boxed{\,A_w = 4 \pi L_1 L_2 \ \ \text{(square metres)}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** Notice that the workspace area depends on the **product** $L_1 L_2$, not on the sum. This means a 0.5 m + 0.5 m arm covers **the same area** as a 1.0 m + 0.25 m arm — but the latter has a much larger $R_{max}$ (1.25 m vs 1.0 m)!

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reach of a 3R Articulated (PUMA-style) Robot

**Problem Setup:** A 3R articulated manipulator with the following DH parameters:

- **Link 1 (shoulder offset):** $L_1$ along the vertical axis
- **Link 2 (upper arm):** $L_2$
- **Link 3 (forearm):** $L_3$

The end-effector position in the **shoulder frame** is:

$$X = L_2 \sin\theta_2 + L_3 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$$
$$Z = L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$$

(Here, $\theta_1$ rotates the whole shoulder plane about the vertical axis and does **not** affect the radial distance from the base origin.)

### Step 1: Compute $r^2 = X^2 + Z^2$

$$r^2 = [L_2 \sin\theta_2 + L_3 \sin(\theta_2+\theta_3)]^2 + [L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 + L_3 \cos(\theta_2+\theta_3)]^2$$

### Step 2: Expand each square

**First term (planar projection):**
$$L_2^2 \sin^2\theta_2 + 2 L_2 L_3 \sin\theta_2 \sin(\theta_2+\theta_3) + L_3^2 \sin^2(\theta_2+\theta_3)$$

**Second term (vertical contribution):**
$$L_1^2 + L_2^2 \cos^2\theta_2 + L_3^2 \cos^2(\theta_2+\theta_3) + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2$$
