$$+ 2 L_1 L_3 \cos(\theta_2+\theta_3) + 2 L_2 L_3 \cos\theta_2 \cos(\theta_2+\theta_3)$$

### Step 3: Group squared terms and apply $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$

The $L_2^2$ and $L_3^2$ terms collapse:
$$L_2^2 (\sin^2\theta_2 + \cos^2\theta_2) + L_3^2 [\sin^2(\theta_2+\theta_3) + \cos^2(\theta_2+\theta_3)] = L_2^2 + L_3^2$$

### Step 4: Group cross-terms and apply cosine subtraction identity

For the $L_2 L_3$ cross-terms:
$$2 L_2 L_3 [\sin\theta_2 \sin(\theta_2+\theta_3) + \cos\theta_2 \cos(\theta_2+\theta_3)]$$
$$= 2 L_2 L_3 \cos[\theta_2 - (\theta_2+\theta_3)] = 2 L_2 L_3 \cos\theta_3$$

### Step 5: Final radial distance expression

Combining all terms:

$$\boxed{\,r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + L_3^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 + 2 L_1 L_3 \cos(\theta_2+\theta_3) + 2 L_2 L_3 \cos\theta_3\,}$$

### Step 6: Maximum reach ($R_{max}$)

Maximum when **all angles are zero** ($\theta_2 = 0, \ \theta_3 = 0$), meaning all links are collinear and pointing straight up (or outward, depending on convention):

$$r_{max}^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + L_3^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 + 2 L_1 L_3 + 2 L_2 L_3$$
$$= (L_1 + L_2 + L_3)^2$$
$$\boxed{\,R_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3\,}$$

### Step 7: Minimum reach ($R_{min}$)

Two cases arise depending on whether the arm can fold back to the base:

**Case A: $L_1 \le L_2 + L_3$ (typical industrial robot)**
The arm can fold completely back so the end-effector reaches the base axis. In this case:
$$R_{min} = 0$$

The dead zone is essentially a point/small region near the base.

**Case B: $L_1 > L_2 + L_3$ (long-shoulder, short-arm configuration)**
The arm cannot reach the base axis even when fully folded. Setting $\theta_2 = \pi, \ \theta_3 = 0$:

$$r_{min}^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + L_3^2 - 2 L_1 L_2 - 2 L_1 L_3 + 2 L_2 L_3 = (L_1 - L_2 - L_3)^2$$
$$\boxed{\,R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 - L_3 \vert\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Unified expression (always correct):**
> $$R_{min} = \max\left(0, \ \vert L_1 - L_2 - L_3 \vert\right)$$

### Step 8: Stroke of the 3R articulated robot

$$S = R_{max} - R_{min} = (L_1 + L_2 + L_3) - \max(0, \ \vert L_1 - L_2 - L_3 \vert)$$

For typical industrial robots where $R_{min} = 0$:
$$S = R_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

**Question:** A 2R planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 0.4$ m and $L_2 = 0.3$ m. Compute (i) Maximum reach, (ii) Minimum reach, (iii) Stroke, (iv) Workspace area.

### Solution:

**Given:** $L_1 = 0.4$ m, $L_2 = 0.3$ m

**(i) Maximum reach:**
$$R_{max} = L_1 + L_2 = 0.4 + 0.3 = \mathbf{0.7 \ m}$$

**(ii) Minimum reach:**
$$R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 0.4 - 0.3 \vert = \mathbf{0.1 \ m}$$

**(iii) Stroke:**
$$S = R_{max} - R_{min} = 0.7 - 0.1 = \mathbf{0.6 \ m}$$

(Verification: $S = 2 \cdot \min(L_1, L_2) = 2 \cdot 0.3 = 0.6$ m ✓)

**(iv) Workspace area:**
$$A_w = 4 \pi L_1 L_2 = 4 \pi (0.4)(0.3) = 0.48 \pi \approx \mathbf{1.508 \ m^2}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Configuration | $R_{max}$ | $R_{min}$ | $S$ | Workspace Shape |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2R Planar ($L_1, L_2$) | $L_1+L_2$ | $\vert L_1-L_2 \vert$ | $2\min(L_1,L_2)$ | Annulus (2D) |
| 3R Articulated ($L_1,L_2,L_3$) | $L_1+L_2+L_3$ | $\max(0, \ \vert L_1 - L_2 - L_3 \vert)$ | $L_1+L_2+L_3 - R_{min}$ | Spherical shell |
| SCARA (R-R-P) | $L_1+L_2$ | $\vert L_1-L_2 \vert$ | $2\min(L_1,L_2)$ | Annular disc (2D) |
| Cartesian (P-P-P) | $\sqrt{L_x^2+L_y^2+L_z^2}$ | $0$ | varies | Cuboid box |
| Polar (R-R-P) | $r_1+r_2$ | $r_1$ (joint limit) | $r_2$ (prismatic stroke) | Spherical sector |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Reach–Stroke–Workspace Relationship

```mermaid
    Q2 --> R

```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: How to Compute Reach for a Given Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST[Start] --> Q1{What is the robot type?}
    Q1 -->|2R Planar| A1[Use Rmax = L1 plus L2]
    Q1 -->|3R Articulated| A2[Use Rmax = L1 plus L2 plus L3]
    Q1 -->|SCARA| A3[Use Rmax = L1 plus L2 in XY plane]
    Q1 -->|Cartesian| A4[Use Rmax = sqrt Lx2 plus Ly2 plus Lz2]
    Q1 -->|Polar| A5[Use Rmax = r1 plus r2]
    A1 --> B1[Compute Rmin = absolute L1 minus L2]
    A2 --> B2[Compute Rmin = max of 0 and absolute L1 minus L2 minus L3]
    A3 --> B3[Compute Rmin = absolute L1 minus L2]
    A4 --> B4[Rmin = 0 for full cuboid]
    A5 --> B5[Rmin = r1 minimum horizontal reach]
    B1 --> C[Compute Stroke S = Rmax minus Rmin]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> END[Report Reach and Stroke]

```

```mermaid
    O --> CIRCLE2[Inner Circle Radius Rmin equals absolute L1 minus L2]

    CIRCLE2 --> SHADED
    SHADED --> STROKE_ARROW[Stroke S equals Rmax minus Rmin equals 2 times min L1 L2]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define reach and stroke of a robotic manipulator. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

**Reach (R):** It is the maximum straight-line distance measured from the centre of the robot's base to the farthest point of the end-effector when all the manipulator links are fully extended in a straight line. For a serial manipulator with $n$ links, $R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$.

**Stroke (S):** It is the difference between the maximum reach and the minimum reach of the manipulator. It represents the radial range of motion available to the end-effector.

**Relationship:** $S = R_{max} - R_{min}$

For a 2R planar arm with links $L_1$ and $L_2$: $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$, $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$, hence $S = 2 \cdot \min(L_1, L_2)$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the "dead zone" in a robot's workspace? Why does it exist?**

**Model Answer:**

The **dead zone** (also called the **inner singularity sphere** or **forbidden region**) is the spherical region of radius $R_{min}$ around the robot's base that **cannot be reached** by the end-effector due to the manipulator's own mechanical structure.

**Reasons for existence:**
1. The arm links have **finite length** and cannot fold to a point.
2. The **base and shoulder hardware** physically occupy the space near the base axis.
3. When the arm is fully folded ($\theta_2 = \pi$), the end-effector is at distance $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - \sum L_i \vert$ from the origin — it cannot penetrate this sphere.
4. **Self-collision** between links and the base prevents reaching this region.

For a 2R planar arm: $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ — points within this radius are unreachable. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** (either Question A **or** Question B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### **(a)** A 3R articulated robot manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.4$ m, and $L_3 = 0.3$ m. Determine:
**(i)** Maximum reach $R_{max}$
**(ii)** Minimum reach $R_{min}$
**(iii)** Stroke $S$
**(iv)** Sketch and label the workspace (outer sphere, inner dead zone, stroke region).

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.4$ m, $L_3 = 0.3$ m.

**(i) Maximum reach:**
$$R_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.3 = \mathbf{0.8 \ m}$$

This occurs when the arm is fully extended ($\theta_2 = 0, \theta_3 = 0$). **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Minimum reach:**
Check if $L_1 \le L_2 + L_3$: $0.5 \le 0.4 + 0.3 = 0.7$ ✓ (arm can fold back to base)

$$R_{min} = \max(0, \ \vert 0.5 - 0.4 - 0.3 \vert) = \max(0, \ \vert -0.2 \vert) = \max(0, 0.2) \ \text{?}$$

Wait — re-evaluating: $\vert L_1 - (L_2 + L_3) \vert = \vert 0.5 - 0.7 \vert = 0.2$ m.

Since $L_2 + L_3 > L_1$, the wrist can in principle reach the base axis, but the **minimum radial reach** with the arm fully folded is:

$$R_{min} = 0$$

This is because the arm can fold back such that the end-effector is at the base axis (within the dead zone). The dead zone degenerates to a point. **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Stroke:**
$$S = R_{max} - R_{min} = 0.8 - 0 = \mathbf{0.8 \ m}$$ **(1 mark)**

**(iv) Workspace sketch:** A sphere of radius 0.8 m centred at the base, with no inner dead zone. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the different types of robot workspaces. Compare the reach and stroke characteristics of Cartesian, Cylindrical, Polar, and Articulated configurations.

**Model Answer:**

**Four primary workspace types:**

1. **Cartesian (PPP) Workspace** — *Rectangular cuboid*
   - The prismatic joints give independent linear motion along X, Y, Z axes.
   - $R_{max} = \sqrt{L_x^2 + L_y^2 + L_z^2}$ (corner diagonal)
   - Workspace volume = $L_x \times L_y \times L_z$ (rectangular box)
   - **Application:** Pick-and-place, CNC loading, assembly lines.

2. **Cylindrical (RPP) Workspace** — *Hollow cylinder with capped ends*
   - Revolute joint rotates about vertical; two prismatic joints extend radially and vertically.
   - $R_{max} = r_{max} + z_{max}$ (corner of cylinder)
   - Workspace volume = $\pi (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) \cdot h$
   - **Application:** Heavy-payload handling, machine tending.

3. **Polar/Spherical (RRP) Workspace** — *Spherical sector*
   - Two revolute joints (waist and shoulder) + one prismatic (extend).
   - $R_{max} = r_1 + r_2$ (full extension)
   - $R_{min} = r_1$ (shortest horizontal arm)
   - **Application:** Forging, casting, injection moulding (high-temperature cells).

4. **Articulated (RRR) Workspace** — *Complex quasi-spherical shape*
   - All three primary joints are revolute.
   - $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$
   - $R_{min} = \max(0, \ \vert L_1 - L_2 - L_3 \vert)$
   - Most dexterous; resembles a human arm.
   - **Application:** Welding, painting, sealing, complex assembly.

**Comparison Table:** *(Student should redraw this in the exam)*

| Configuration | Workspace Shape | $R_{max}$ Formula | Dexterity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian | Cuboid | $\sqrt{L_x^2+L_y^2+L_z^2}$ | Low |
| Cylindrical | Hollow cylinder | $r_{max}+z_{max}$ | Medium |
| Polar | Spherical sector | $r_1+r_2$ | High |
| Articulated | Quasi-sphere | $L_1+L_2+L_3$ | Very High |

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### **(a)** A 2R planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 0.6$ m and $L_2 = 0.4$ m. The end-effector position is given by:
$$x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2), \quad y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

**(i)** Derive the expression for radial distance $r$ from the base origin in terms of $L_1, L_2, \theta_2$.
**(ii)** Hence compute $R_{max}$, $R_{min}$, and Stroke $S$.
**(iii)** Calculate the workspace area $A_w$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Derivation of $r$:**

$$r^2 = x^2 + y^2 = [L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)]^2 + [L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]^2$$

Expanding and using $\sin^2\alpha + \cos^2\alpha = 1$ for squared terms, and $\cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B = \cos(A-B)$ for cross-terms:

$$r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(\theta_1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2)$$
$$r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2$$ **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Reach and Stroke:**

$R_{max}$ when $\cos\theta_2 = +1$ (i.e., $\theta_2 = 0$):
$$R_{max} = L_1 + L_2 = 0.6 + 0.4 = \mathbf{1.0 \ m}$$ **(1 mark)**

$R_{min}$ when $\cos\theta_2 = -1$ (i.e., $\theta_2 = \pi$):
$$R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 0.6 - 0.4 \vert = \mathbf{0.2 \ m}$$ **(1 mark)**

Stroke:
$$S = R_{max} - R_{min} = 1.0 - 0.2 = \mathbf{0.8 \ m}$$

Verification: $S = 2 \min(L_1, L_2) = 2(0.4) = 0.8$ m ✓ **(1 mark)**

**(iii) Workspace area:**

$$A_w = 4 \pi L_1 L_2 = 4 \pi (0.6)(0.4) = 0.96 \pi \approx \mathbf{3.016 \ m^2}$$ **(1 mark)**

---

#### **(b)** Discuss the concept of **dead zone** in robot workspace. How does it affect robot selection for a given application? Illustrate with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The dead zone is the spherical/circular region around the robot's base within radius $R_{min}$ that the end-effector **cannot reach** due to physical limitations of the manipulator structure.

**Causes:**
- Mechanical size of the base, shoulder, and elbow joints.
- Finite link lengths preventing zero-radius folding.
- Self-collision constraints.

**Effect on robot selection:**

| Application | Dead Zone Concern | Recommended Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Inserting components **into** a part near the base | **Critical** — small dead zone needed | Articulated (low $R_{min}$) |
| Loading/unloading conveyor at the side | **Low** — dead zone irrelevant | Cartesian or Cylindrical |
| Working on a **flat table** with no under-table work | **Medium** — depends on reach | SCARA (small planar dead zone) |
| Machining of **deep cavities** | **Critical** — long slender arm needed | Articulated with $L_1 \ge L_2 + L_3$ |

**Example:** Consider **spot welding inside a car body** (underbody). The robot must reach **inside** the car frame near the floor. If an articulated robot with $L_1 = 0.4$ m, $L_2 = 0.6$ m, $L_3 = 0.5$ m is used, then:

$$R_{min} = \max(0, \ \vert 0.4 - 0.6 - 0.5 \vert) = 0$$

The dead zone is essentially zero — the robot can reach very close to the base axis, making it **suitable for underbody welding**.

Conversely, for a **cartesian robot**, the dead zone is the entire base column volume — **unsuitable** for reaching inside cavities near the base.

**Engineering rule of thumb:** Always compute $R_{min}$ and compare it with the **minimum required approach distance** to the workpiece before selecting a robot. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in $R_{min}$:** Writing $R_{min} = -0.2$ m when $L_1 - L_2 - L_3 = -0.2$. A distance **cannot be negative** — always apply the $\max(0, \cdot)$ rule. *(−2 marks penalty if missed)*
> 2. **Forgetting to square in $r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1L_2\cos\theta_2$:** Some students write $r = L_1 + L_2 + 2L_1L_2\cos\theta_2$ — **WRONG**. The $r^2$ form is the correct derivation. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Confusing Reach with Stroke:** Reach is a single distance; Stroke is a *range* (difference between two reaches). Examiners will deduct marks for swapped definitions. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
> 4. **Omitting the Dead Zone discussion:** Whenever a 3R robot is discussed, examiners expect explicit mention of $R_{min}$ and the dead zone. *(−2 marks if missed)*
> 5. **Using $\sin$ instead of $\cos$ in $R_{max}$ calculation:** $R_{max}$ occurs at $\theta_2 = 0$ (cosine = 1), NOT at $\theta_2 = \pi/2$ (where $r = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2}$ — the *average* reach, not the maximum). *(−1 mark)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this before the exam!):**

- ✅ **Reach ($R$)** = Max distance from base to TCP when arm is **fully extended**.
- ✅ **Stroke ($S$)** = $R_{max} - R_{min}$ = radial range of motion of the end-effector.
- ✅ **Dead Zone** = Inner sphere of radius $R_{min}$ that the end-effector **cannot reach**.
- ✅ For a **2R planar arm**: $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$, $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$, $S = 2\min(L_1, L_2)$.
- ✅ For a **3R articulated arm**: $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$, $R_{min} = \max(0, \ \vert L_1 - L_2 - L_3 \vert)$.
- ✅ **Key identity:** $r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1L_2\cos\theta_2$ — derived via $\cos(A-B)$ identity.
- ✅ **Workspace area of 2R:** $A_w = 4\pi L_1 L_2$ — depends on the **product** of link lengths, not the sum.
- ✅ **Workspace volume (spherical shell):** $V_w = \frac{4}{3}\pi(R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3)$.
- ✅ $\theta_2 = 0$ gives **maximum** reach; $\theta_2 = \pi$ gives **minimum** reach.
- ✅ Workspace shape varies by configuration: **Cartesian → cuboid, Cylindrical → hollow cylinder, Polar → spherical sector, Articulated → quasi-spherical shell, SCARA → annular disc.**
- ✅ A **larger $R_{max}$** ⇒ larger payload moment at full extension ⇒ reduced rated payload.
- ✅ Industrial robots (e.g., PUMA) typically have $L_1 < L_2 + L_3$, giving $R_{min} = 0$ (negligible dead zone).
- ✅ Reach is **independent of $\theta_1$** (waist rotation) in a 3R articulated robot — it depends only on $\theta_2$ and $\theta_3$.
- ✅ **Maximum reach ≠ Working reach**: Real workcells use only ~80% of $R_{max}$ to allow for singularities, joint limits, and safety margins.
