# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repeatability (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Repeatability is defined as the measure of the ability of a robot to return to a previously attained position/orientation (pose) when commanded to do so repeatedly under identical conditions of load, speed, path, and environmental setup. It is a **statistical measure of pose clustering** rather than a measure of how close the robot reaches an *absolute* commanded target.

Mathematically, the KTU 2024 Scheme defines repeatability as the **radius of a sphere (or a circle in 2D)** centered on the mean attained position, into which the robot will return for a specified number of consecutive attempts (typically 100 cycles as per **ISO 9283**).

$$R = \bar{x} + k \cdot s$$

Where:
- $R$ = Repeatability (in **mm**)
- $\bar{x}$ = Mean position deviation
- $k$ = Coverage factor (typically $1.96$ for 95% confidence, or $3$ for 99.7%)
- $s$ = Standard deviation of position deviations

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board Distinction (Frequently Asked):**
> - **Repeatability** = How consistently the robot reaches the *same* spot it reached before.
> - **Accuracy** = How close the robot gets to the *commanded* target.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are throwing darts at a dartboard while **blindfolded**:

- **Accuracy** = How close your dart lands to the **bullseye** (the commanded point).
- **Repeatability** = How tightly **all your darts cluster together**, even if that cluster is in the corner of the board.

A robot can have **excellent repeatability but poor accuracy** (darts cluster together, but far from bullseye). It is mathematically possible to have high accuracy only if repeatability is also high, but high repeatability does **not** guarantee high accuracy.

**Real-world Analogy:** Consider a CNC operator who manually resets a machine tool to the same home position 50 times. The variation in that home position over 50 trials is essentially the **repeatability** of the manual system. Industrial robots aim to make this variation as close to **zero** as possible.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | KTU Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pose Repeatability** | $RP$ | **mm** | ISO 9283 |
| **Path Repeatability** | $R_{path}$ | **mm** | ISO 9283 |
| **Cycle Time Repeatability** | $t_{cyc}$ | **seconds** | Manufacturer spec |
| **Angular Repeatability** | $\theta_R$ | **degrees** or **arc-sec** | ISO 9283 |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 examiners specifically look for the **distinction between Pose Repeatability and Path Repeatability** — these are often confused in answers. Pose repeatability is measured at *discrete points*, while path repeatability is measured *along a continuous trajectory*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Repeatability Testing

The KTU 2024 Scheme (per ISO 9283) defines the following logic for evaluating repeatability:

1. **Step 1 — Command the Robot:** Program the robot to move to a specific target pose $P_c$ in its workspace.
2. **Step 2 — Repeat Execution:** Execute the same commanded pose $n$ times (typically $n = 100$ cycles) under **identical conditions**:
   - Same payload
   - Same speed (typically rated maximum)
   - Same environmental temperature
   - Same approach direction
3. **Step 3 — Measure Deviations:** Record the actual attained positions $P_i$ for each cycle $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.
4. **Step 4 — Compute Statistics:** Calculate the mean position $\bar{P}$ and standard deviation $s$ of the deviation vector.
5. **Step 5 — Define Repeatability:** Repeatability $R$ is the radius of the sphere enclosing the cluster of attained positions at a specified confidence level (usually 95%).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why identical conditions?** Because repeatability measures the *system's intrinsic variation*, not external disturbances. Changing load would introduce a separate variable.
- **Why 100 cycles?** A statistically robust sample size that captures the natural distribution of errors.
- **Why confidence factor $k$?** Industrial users need a **guaranteed bound** — not just a mean. A 95% confidence means 95 out of 100 trials will fall within the specified repeatability envelope.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \bar{x} + k \cdot s$ | General repeatability (mean + $k$ std deviations) | **mm** |
| 2 | $R = l_{1-\alpha} \cdot s$ | Where $l_{1-\alpha}$ is the one-sided tolerance limit | **mm** |
| 3 | $\bar{P} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Mean attained pose | **mm** or **deg** |
| 4 | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(P_i - \bar{P})^2}$ | Standard deviation of pose deviations | **mm** or **deg** |
| 5 | $d_i = \vert P_i - \bar{P} \vert$ | Distance of $i^{th}$ attempt from mean | **mm** |
| 6 | $R_{ISO} = \bar{d} + 3\sigma_d$ | ISO 9283 repeatability (mean + 3 std of distances) | **mm** |
| 7 | $A = \vert P_c - \bar{P} \vert$ | Accuracy (distance from commanded to mean actual) | **mm** |
| 8 | $R_{path} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\int_0^{T} \vert p_{cmd}(t) - p_i(t) \vert \, dt$ | Path repeatability integral form | **mm·s** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The `|` (vertical bar) for absolute value is replaced with `\vert` in the table above. In your exam answer sheet, use the standard mathematical notation $\lvert x \rvert$ or write "distance" explicitly to avoid markup errors.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Welding Lines:** Industrial robots perform thousands of spot welds. Each weld must land within a few millimetres of the actual sheet metal joint — this requires repeatability better than **±0.1 mm**.
- **PCB Assembly:** Pick-and-place robots place SMD components. Repeatability of **±0.02 mm** is industry standard.
- **Pharmaceutical Dispensing:** Robotic pipettes must dispense the same volume every cycle — repeatability is more critical than absolute volume.
- **Surgical Robotics (Da Vinci):** Sub-millimetre repeatability is essential for predictable tool placement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ISO 9283 Repeatability Formula

The ISO 9283 standard defines pose repeatability through a rigorous statistical procedure. Let us derive it step by step.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Measurement Framework

A robot is commanded to reach a target pose $P_c$ in Cartesian space. We execute the motion $n$ times and record the attained poses:
$$P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n$$

Each attained pose is a 3D point $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ in the world frame.

### Step 2 — Compute the Mean (Centroid) of Attained Poses

The mean pose is the arithmetic average of all attained positions:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i, \quad \bar{z} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} z_i$$

This centroid $\bar{P} = (\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z})$ represents the **average** location where the robot actually lands over $n$ trials.

### Step 3 — Compute Distances from Each Attempt to the Centroid

For each attempt $i$, compute the Euclidean distance from the attained position to the centroid:

$$d_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}$$

These distances $d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n$ form a positive-valued distribution.

### Step 4 — Compute the Mean Distance and Standard Deviation of Distances

The mean of the distances:
$$\bar{d} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i$$

The standard deviation of the distances:
$$s_d = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(d_i - \bar{d})^2}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the ISO 9283 Definition

ISO 9283 defines pose repeatability as the radius that contains 99.7% of all attempts (a $3\sigma$ bound):

$$R_{ISO} = \bar{d} + 3 \cdot s_d$$

This bound means: 99.7% of the time, the robot will land within a sphere of radius $R_{ISO}$ centered on the mean position.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example

A KTU lab test commands a robot to reach the point $(100, 200, 300)$ mm. Five measurements yield:

| Trial $i$ | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) | $z_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100.05 | 199.92 | 300.03 |
| 2 | 100.02 | 200.01 | 299.98 |
| 3 | 99.98 | 199.99 | 300.04 |
| 4 | 100.04 | 200.03 | 300.00 |
| 5 | 100.01 | 199.95 | 299.99 |

**Compute the means:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{100.05+100.02+99.98+100.04+100.01}{5} = 100.02 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{199.92+200.01+199.99+200.03+199.95}{5} = 199.98 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{z} = \frac{300.03+299.98+300.04+300.00+299.99}{5} = 300.008 \text{ mm}$$

**Compute distances $d_i$:**

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(0.03)^2+(-0.06)^2+(0.022)^2} = \sqrt{0.0009+0.0036+0.000484} \approx 0.0722 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(0.00)^2+(0.03)^2+(-0.028)^2} = \sqrt{0.0009+0.000784} \approx 0.0411 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_3 = \sqrt{(-0.04)^2+(0.01)^2+(0.032)^2} = \sqrt{0.0016+0.0001+0.001024} \approx 0.0522 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_4 = \sqrt{(0.02)^2+(0.05)^2+(-0.008)^2} = \sqrt{0.0004+0.0025+0.000064} \approx 0.0544 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_5 = \sqrt{(-0.01)^2+(-0.03)^2+(-0.018)^2} = \sqrt{0.0001+0.0009+0.000324} \approx 0.0364 \text{ mm}$$

**Compute the mean distance:**

$$\bar{d} = \frac{0.0722+0.0411+0.0522+0.0544+0.0364}{5} = \frac{0.2563}{5} = 0.0513 \text{ mm}$$

**Compute the standard deviation $s_d$:**

$$s_d = \sqrt{\frac{(0.0722-0.0513)^2+(0.0411-0.0513)^2+(0.0522-0.0513)^2+(0.0544-0.0513)^2+(0.0364-0.0513)^2}{5-1}}$$

$$s_d = \sqrt{\frac{0.000437+0.000104+0.0000008+0.0000096+0.000222}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.000773}{4}}$$

$$s_d = \sqrt{0.000193} \approx 0.0139 \text{ mm}$$

**Compute ISO 9283 repeatability:**

$$R_{ISO} = \bar{d} + 3 \cdot s_d = 0.0513 + 3 \times 0.0139 = 0.0513 + 0.0417 = 0.093 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** The robot's pose repeatability is approximately **0.093 mm**, meaning 99.7% of attempts will fall within 0.093 mm of the mean attained position.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Repeatability

| Factor | Effect on Repeatability | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Joint backlash** | Increases pose variation | Use precision gearboxes (harmonic drives) |
| **Encoder resolution** | Limits minimum measurable deviation | High-resolution absolute encoders |
| **Mechanical compliance** | Linkage flex under load | Stiffer materials, FEA-optimised links |
| **Temperature drift** | Thermal expansion alters geometry | Temperature compensation, climate control |
| **Payload variation** | Different deflections per load | Closed-loop force/torque feedback |
| **Controller resolution** | Discrete command steps | Higher CPU bandwidth, interpolation algorithms |
| **Cable management** | Variable tension alters kinematics | Internal cable routing |
| **Friction & Stiction** | Non-linear joint behaviour | Pre-loaded bearings, low-friction coatings |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Repeatability Measurement Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Command Pose Pc] --> B[Robot Controller]
    B --> C[Joint Servo Loops]
    C --> D[Mechanical Linkage System]
    D --> E[End Effector]
    E --> F[External Measurement Device]
    F --> G[Data Acquisition Unit]
    G --> H[Statistical Processor]
    H --> I[Compute Mean Centroid]
    I --> J[Compute Distances di]
    J --> K[Compute Std Deviation sd]
    K --> L[Apply ISO 9283 Formula]
    L --> M[Output Repeatability R]
    H --> N[Store Raw Data]
    N --> O[Generate Report]
```

## 4.2 Repeatability vs Accuracy — Conceptual Cluster Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Case1 [HIGH Repeatability + HIGH Accuracy]
        A1[Commanded Target] --> B1[Cluster tightly at target]
    end
    subgraph Case2 [HIGH Repeatability + LOW Accuracy]
        A2[Commanded Target] --> B2[Cluster tightly but offset]
    end
    subgraph Case3 [LOW Repeatability + HIGH Accuracy]
        A3[Commanded Target] --> B3[Cluster spread around target]
    end
    subgraph Case4 [LOW Repeatability + LOW Accuracy]
        A4[Commanded Target] --> B4[Cluster spread and offset]
    end
```

## 4.3 ISO 9283 Testing Cycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Test Cycle] --> B[Set Payload and Speed]
    B --> C[Command Pose P1]
    C --> D[Record Actual P1 attained]
    D --> E{More Trials?}
    E -- Yes --> C
    E -- No --> F[Compute Statistics]
    F --> G[Move to Next Test Pose]
    G --> H{More Poses?}
    H -- Yes --> C
    H -- No --> I[Generate ISO 9283 Report]
    I --> J[End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid block diagrams map the **functional architecture** of repeatability testing, ISO 9283 procedural flow, and the four possible combinations of accuracy/repeatability performance. These complement (and at exam time, may substitute for) the physically drawn cluster diagrams typically required in KTU answer sheets.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define repeatability. How is it different from accuracy? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Repeatability is the ability of a robot to return to a previously attained position/orientation when commanded repeatedly under identical conditions. It is a statistical measure of the **clustering of attained poses** around a mean.

**Difference from Accuracy:**

| Aspect | Repeatability | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Cluster of attained positions | Distance from commanded target |
| **Measures** | Internal consistency | External correctness |
| **Depends on** | Mechanical precision | Calibration + Kinematic model |
| **Affects** | Cycle-to-cycle variation | Absolute positioning |

(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 2 marks comparison)

---

### Question 2: Name any three factors that affect the repeatability of an industrial robot. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Joint backlash** in gear transmissions
2. **Encoder resolution** of position sensors
3. **Mechanical compliance / link flexibility** under load
4. (Any one additional: Thermal expansion, friction in joints, controller resolution)

(3 marks — 1 mark each, any three correct factors)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the ISO 9283 procedure for measuring pose repeatability of a six-axis industrial robot. Derive the repeatability formula.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robot is commanded to reach a point 5 times. The deviation vectors (in mm) from the mean position for the 5 attempts are: $\vec{d_1} = (0.02, 0.01, 0.03)$, $\vec{d_2} = (-0.01, 0.02, 0.01)$, $\vec{d_3} = (0.03, -0.01, 0.02)$, $\vec{d_4} = (0.01, 0.03, -0.02)$, $\vec{d_5} = (-0.02, 0.01, 0.01)$. Calculate the ISO 9283 repeatability.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ISO 9283 Procedure & Formula Derivation:**

**Procedure:**
1. Place the robot in a test environment with **constant temperature** (typically 20°C).
2. Mount a precision measurement device (laser tracker, coordinate measuring machine, or theodolite).
3. Define a target pose $P_c$ in the robot's workspace.
4. Command the robot to reach $P_c$ a total of $n = 100$ times at rated maximum speed and rated maximum payload.
5. Record each attained position $P_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$.
6. Compute the mean centroid $\bar{P} = (\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z})$.
7. Compute distance $d_i$ from each $P_i$ to $\bar{P}$.
8. Compute mean distance $\bar{d}$ and standard deviation $s_d$.
9. Apply $R_{ISO} = \bar{d} + 3 s_d$.

**Derivation:** (Already shown in Section 3.1 of these notes — reproduce the 6-step derivation in the exam.)

**(7 marks — 4 marks procedure, 3 marks derivation)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Note that these are already *deviation vectors* from the mean. Therefore $\bar{P}$ corresponds to deviations summing to zero (verified by inspection).

Step 2 — Compute magnitudes of each deviation:

$$d_1 = \sqrt{0.02^2+0.01^2+0.03^2} = \sqrt{0.0004+0.0001+0.0009} = \sqrt{0.0014} = 0.0374 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(-0.01)^2+0.02^2+0.01^2} = \sqrt{0.0001+0.0004+0.0001} = \sqrt{0.0006} = 0.0245 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_3 = \sqrt{0.03^2+(-0.01)^2+0.02^2} = \sqrt{0.0009+0.0001+0.0004} = \sqrt{0.0014} = 0.0374 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_4 = \sqrt{0.01^2+0.03^2+(-0.02)^2} = \sqrt{0.0001+0.0009+0.0004} = \sqrt{0.0014} = 0.0374 \text{ mm}$$

$$d_5 = \sqrt{(-0.02)^2+0.01^2+0.01^2} = \sqrt{0.0004+0.0001+0.0001} = \sqrt{0.0006} = 0.0245 \text{ mm}$$

Step 3 — Mean distance:

$$\bar{d} = \frac{0.0374+0.0245+0.0374+0.0374+0.0245}{5} = \frac{0.1612}{5} = 0.0322 \text{ mm}$$

Step 4 — Standard deviation:

$$s_d = \sqrt{\frac{(0.0374-0.0322)^2+(0.0245-0.0322)^2+(0.0374-0.0322)^2+(0.0374-0.0322)^2+(0.0245-0.0322)^2}{4}}$$

$$s_d = \sqrt{\frac{(0.0052)^2 \times 3 + (-0.0077)^2 \times 2}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0000811+0.0001186}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0001997}{4}} = \sqrt{0.0000499} \approx 0.00707 \text{ mm}$$

Step 5 — ISO 9283 Repeatability:

$$R_{ISO} = \bar{d} + 3 s_d = 0.0322 + 3 \times 0.00707 = 0.0322 + 0.0212 = 0.0534 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer: $R_{ISO} \approx 0.053$ mm** (7 marks — 1 mark setup, 2 marks distance calculations, 2 marks mean/std dev, 2 marks final result)

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Distinguish clearly between **pose repeatability** and **path repeatability**. With neat sketches, explain the four possible combinations of accuracy and repeatability in industrial robots.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the mechanical and control factors that limit the repeatability of an industrial robot. Suggest two design-level improvements to enhance repeatability.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pose Repeatability vs Path Repeatability:**

| Aspect | Pose Repeatability | Path Repeatability |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Ability to return to the *same discrete pose* | Ability to *follow the same trajectory* |
| **Measured at** | A single point | Continuously along a path |
| **Typical value** | ±0.02 to ±0.5 mm | ±0.1 to ±1.0 mm |
| **KTU formula** | $R_{ISO} = \bar{d} + 3 s_d$ | $R_{path} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \int \lvert p_{cmd} - p_i \rvert \, dt$ |
| **Used for** | Pick-and-place | Welding, painting, cutting |

**Four Combinations of Accuracy & Repeatability:**

1. **High Accuracy + High Repeatability:** Cluster centered on the commanded target — *Ideal industrial robot* (most modern 6-axis robots).
2. **High Repeatability + Low Accuracy:** Tight cluster but offset from target — *Common in un-calibrated robots* (remedied by kinematic calibration).
3. **Low Repeatability + High Accuracy:** Cluster straddles the target with large spread — *Acceptable for some applications but risky*.
4. **Low Repeatability + Low Accuracy:** Unusable cluster — *Severely worn or mis-calibrated robot*.

(7 marks — 3 marks distinction table, 4 marks four combinations with sketches)

**(b) Factors Limiting Repeatability & Improvements:**

**Mechanical Factors:**
1. **Joint backlash** — clearance between gear teeth causes a dead zone where small reversals do not produce motion.
2. **Linkage compliance** — elastic deflection of links under load alters the end-effector pose.
3. **Friction and stiction** — non-linear resistive forces introduce hysteresis.
4. **Thermal expansion** — temperature rise during operation lengthens links and alters encoder zero-points.
5. **Cable wear and routing** — variable cable tension perturbs joint kinematics.

**Control Factors:**
1. **Encoder resolution** — quantises the joint angle into discrete steps.
2. **Sampling time** — discrete-time control has finite bandwidth.
3. **Servo gain and stability** — poorly tuned PID gains cause oscillation or steady-state error.
4. **Trajectory interpolation** — coarse interpolation steps between path waypoints.

**Design-Level Improvements:**
1. **Use harmonic drive gearboxes** (zero-backlash strain-wave gears) instead of spur gears.
2. **Closed-loop absolute encoders** at each joint for sub-arc-second resolution.
3. **Temperature-compensated frame design** with low thermal expansion coefficient materials (e.g., carbon fibre).
4. **Internal cable routing** to eliminate external cable disturbance.

(7 marks — 3 marks mechanical, 2 marks control, 2 marks improvements)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing repeatability with accuracy** — Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if the student uses these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the multiplier "3" in $R = \bar{d} + 3s_d$** — A very common error; you may write the formula correctly but compute the wrong result.
> 3. **Using Bessel's correction $n-1$ vs $n$** — KTU expects the unbiased estimator with $n-1$ in the denominator. Using $n$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting the units (mm)** — A trivial but frequently penalised omission.
> 5. **Confusing pose vs path repeatability** — Always specify which one your answer refers to.
> 6. **In numerical problems, forgetting to take the square root of the sum of squares** when computing $d_i$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Repeatability = clustering of attained positions; not a measure of *correctness*.
- ⭐ **Statistical formula:** $R_{ISO} = \bar{d} + 3 s_d$ (3-sigma bound, 99.7% confidence).
- ⭐ **Standard:** ISO 9283 governs robot performance testing.
- ⭐ **Two types:** Pose repeatability (discrete point) vs Path repeatability (continuous trajectory).
- ⭐ **Key distinction:** High repeatability does NOT imply high accuracy.
- ⭐ **Units:** Always in **mm** (linear) or **degrees/arc-seconds** (angular).
- ⭐ **Typical values:** Modern industrial robots: **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm** pose repeatability.
- ⭐ **Main factors:** Backlash, encoder resolution, compliance, thermal drift, friction.
- ⭐ **Best gear for repeatability:** Harmonic drive (zero-backlash).
- ⭐ **Coverage factor $k$:** $1.96$ for 95%, $3$ for 99.7% (ISO 9283 uses 3).
- ⭐ **Calibration improves accuracy**; **mechanical precision improves repeatability**.
- ⭐ **Test conditions must be constant:** Payload, speed, temperature, approach direction.
- ⭐ **The centroid $\bar{P}$ is NOT the commanded pose $P_c$** — they may differ (that's the accuracy gap).
- ⭐ **Industrial relevance:** Welding (0.1 mm), PCB pick-and-place (0.02 mm), surgical robotics (<0.1 mm).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precision and Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Precision and Accuracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of industrial robotics, **Precision** and **Accuracy** are two distinct yet interrelated performance parameters defined under the **ISO 9283** standard (Manipulating industrial robots – Performance criteria and related test methods). They quantify the *quality of motion* and *positioning* of the robot's end-effector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Block**
> - **Accuracy** is defined as the ability of a robot to position its end-effector at a *pre-determined target point* in space, measured as the **deviation between the commanded (programmed) position and the mean of the attained positions** across a number of trials.
> - **Precision** (also called **Repeatability** in KTU terminology) is the ability of the robot to return to the *same position* over a number of trials, measured as the **closeness of agreement between the attained positions** themselves, irrespective of the target.
> - **Resolution** is the smallest increment of movement that the robot's control system can reliably command and execute.

Mathematically, the standard KTU notations are:

$$\text{Accuracy } (AP) = \bar{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{x}_c$$

$$\text{Precision } (RP) = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l$$

Where $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ is the mean of attained positions, $\mathbf{x}_c$ is the commanded position, $\sigma_l$ is the standard deviation of distances, and $3\sigma_l$ corresponds to the **three-sigma confidence bound** (99.73% of all data points).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Dartboard Intuition

Imagine a person throwing darts at a dartboard with a single bullseye.

- **Accurate but Imprecise (Systematic Error)**: All darts land *tightly clustered* but *far from the bullseye*. The robot knows where it *thinks* it is, but it is consistently off-target. Fix: **Calibration**.
- **Precise but Inaccurate (Random Error)**: All darts land *around the bullseye* but *spread out widely*. The robot's average position is correct, but trial-to-trial variation is high. Fix: **Mechanical stiffness / sensor upgrade**.
- **Accurate AND Precise (Ideal)**: All darts land *tightly clustered exactly on the bullseye*. This is the goal of every high-performance robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive takeaway for students**: *Precision* answers the question — "If I ask the robot to do the same thing 100 times, will all 100 results agree with each other?" *Accuracy* answers — "If I ask the robot to go to point P, will it actually arrive at P?"

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield Highlights)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Position Repeatability** | $RP$ | $\pm 0.02 \text{ mm}$ to $\pm 0.5 \text{ mm}$ |
| **Pose Accuracy** | $AP_p$ | $\pm 1 \text{ mm}$ to $\pm 10 \text{ mm}$ |
| **Path Accuracy** | $AT$ | $\pm 2 \text{ mm}$ to $\pm 20 \text{ mm}$ |
| **Spatial Resolution** | $r$ | $0.001 \text{ mm}$ to $0.1 \text{ mm}$ |
| **Control Resolution** | $r_c$ | $0.0001^\circ$ to $0.01^\circ$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note**: The term *Precision* in ISO 9283 is formally called **Pose Repeatability**. However, KTU 2024 scheme question papers often use the words *Precision* and *Repeatability* interchangeably. Both refer to the *statistical scatter* of the end-effector positions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Positioning Performance

Robotic positioning performance can be decomposed into **three orthogonal sub-problems**, each studied independently under ISO 9283:

### 2.1.1 Pose Accuracy & Pose Repeatability
- **Pose** = Position (x, y, z) + Orientation (roll, pitch, yaw).
- **Pose Accuracy ($AP_p$)**: distance between the commanded pose and the *mean* of attained poses.
- **Pose Repeatability ($RP_l$)**: the radius of the sphere that contains a specified percentage (typically 99.73%, i.e., $3\sigma$) of all attained poses around their centroid.

### 2.1.2 Path Accuracy & Path Repeatability
- Applied to a *continuous trajectory* (e.g., a welding seam) rather than a discrete point.
- **Path Accuracy ($AT$)**: maximum deviation of the *mean* path from the commanded path.
- **Path Repeatability ($RT$)**: the band of scatter around the mean path.

### 2.1.3 Multi-Directional Accuracy
- Tests whether a pose attained from different approach directions (e.g., from +X, –X, +Y, –Y) yields the *same* result. This reveals **backlash** and **gravity-induced sag**.

## 2.2 Sources of Error in Robotic Systems

The hierarchy of error sources, ordered from macro to micro:

1. **Geometric Errors**: Link length tolerances (manufacturing), misalignment of joint axes.
2. **Kinematic Errors**: Misalignment between actual and nominal Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameters.
3. **Non-Geometric Errors**:
   - *Joint compliance* (gear elasticity, harmonic-drive wind-up).
   - *Link deflection* (cantilever bending under payload).
   - *Thermal expansion* of links and ball-screws.
4. **Control Errors**: Servo lag, steady-state error, quantization (resolution limit).
5. **Sensor Noise**: Encoder quantisation, resolver noise, backlash in reducers.
6. **Environmental**: Temperature drift, vibration, floor settling.

## 2.3 Resolution — The Quantum of Motion

Resolution is the **finest discrete increment** the controller can produce. It has three layers:

- **Control Resolution ($r_c$)**: Determined by encoder bits. For an $n$-bit encoder over $360^\circ$:
$$r_c = \frac{360^\circ}{2^n}$$

- **Spatial Resolution ($r_s$)**: The corresponding linear displacement at the end-effector.
$$r_s = r_c \cdot \vert J(\theta) \vert$$
where $J(\theta)$ is the Jacobian mapping joint motion to Cartesian motion.

- **Programming Resolution ($r_p$)**: The user-defined step size (e.g., a 1 mm increment in a teach pendant).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters**: Resolution sets the *theoretical lower bound* on accuracy. No robot can be more accurate than its resolution.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Mean of attained positions | mm |
| $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Standard deviation (sample) | mm |
| $AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x}-x_c)^2 + (\bar{y}-y_c)^2 + (\bar{z}-z_c)^2}$ | Position Accuracy | mm |
| $RP = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l$ | ISO 9283 Repeatability ($3\sigma$ rule) | mm |
| $\text{RMS}_e = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}$ | Radial scatter (2D circular normal) | mm |
| $r_c = \frac{360}{2^n}$ | Control resolution of $n$-bit encoder | degrees |
| $r_s = r_c \cdot \vert J(\theta) \vert$ | Spatial resolution | mm/pulse |
| $P_{99.7} = 3\sigma$ | Probability of point inside $\pm 3\sigma$ | dimensionless |
| $\Delta P = P_{\text{actual}} - P_{\text{commanded}}$ | Position error vector | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Rule**: In KTU board-marked answers, the symbol $\vert J \vert$ denotes the *determinant/magnitude of the Jacobian* — never use plain `|` inside the markdown table cell. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX form.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive welding**: Tolerances are $\pm 0.5$ mm, demanding high accuracy *and* repeatability.
- **PCB pick-and-place**: Repeatability $\pm 0.02$ mm is essential; accuracy is calibrated offline.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci)**: Sub-millimetre precision is critical for patient safety.
- **Additive manufacturing / 3D printing**: Path accuracy dictates dimensional conformance to CAD.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Repeatability Index from First Principles

The ISO 9283 standard defines pose repeatability from raw data. Let us derive it rigorously.

**Step 1 — Data Collection.**
The robot is commanded to reach a target point $P_c$ a total of $N$ times (typically $N = 30$ or $N = 50$ as per ISO 9283). Each attained position is recorded as $\mathbf{p}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Centroid.**
The mean (centroid) of the cloud of attained points is the best estimate of where the robot *actually* goes on average:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \\[4pt]
\bar{y} &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \\[4pt]
\bar{z} &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Distances from the Centroid.**
For each trial, compute the Euclidean distance $l_i$ from the attained point to the centroid:

$$
l_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Mean and Standard Deviation of $l_i$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{l} &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} l_i \\[4pt]
\sigma_l &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(l_i - \bar{l})^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $N-1$ (Bessel's correction)?** We use the *sample* standard deviation because we are estimating the true population variance from a finite sample. The $N-1$ divisor makes the estimator unbiased.

**Step 5 — Apply the Three-Sigma Rule.**
For a Gaussian (normal) distribution, $99.73\%$ of points lie within $\pm 3\sigma$ of the mean. ISO 9283 uses this confidence level to define:

$$RP_l = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l$$

This value $RP_l$ is the **radius of a sphere** centred on the centroid that contains nearly all of the robot's repeated attempts at the same commanded point.

**Step 6 — Combine with Accuracy.**
The full position error is reported as the pair (Accuracy, Repeatability) = $(AP, RP)$, where

$$
AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2 + (\bar{z} - z_c)^2}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem**: A 6-axis robot is commanded to reach point $P_c = (500.000,\, 300.000,\, 200.000)$ mm. After 5 trials, the attained positions are:
>
> $(500.15, 300.10, 199.92)$, $(499.88, 299.95, 200.18)$, $(500.05, 300.08, 200.05)$, $(499.92, 300.15, 199.90)$, $(500.10, 299.97, 200.10)$ mm.
>
> Compute the **Position Accuracy** and the **Pose Repeatability** ($3\sigma$).

**Solution:**

Step A — Compute the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{500.15 + 499.88 + 500.05 + 499.92 + 500.10}{5} = 500.020 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\bar{y} &= \frac{300.10 + 299.95 + 300.08 + 300.15 + 299.97}{5} = 300.050 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\bar{z} &= \frac{199.92 + 200.18 + 200.05 + 199.90 + 200.10}{5} = 200.030 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step B — Compute the accuracy (deviation from commanded point):

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP &= \sqrt{(500.020 - 500.000)^2 + (300.050 - 300.000)^2 + (200.030 - 200.000)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{(0.020)^2 + (0.050)^2 + (0.030)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{0.0004 + 0.0025 + 0.0009} = \sqrt{0.0038} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0616 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step C — Compute $l_i$ for each trial:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_1 &= \sqrt{(0.130)^2 + (0.050)^2 + (-0.110)^2} = \sqrt{0.0300} = 0.1732 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
l_2 &= \sqrt{(-0.140)^2 + (-0.100)^2 + (0.150)^2} = \sqrt{0.0522} = 0.2285 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
l_3 &= \sqrt{(0.030)^2 + (0.030)^2 + (0.020)^2} = \sqrt{0.0019} = 0.0436 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
l_4 &= \sqrt{(-0.100)^2 + (0.100)^2 + (-0.130)^2} = \sqrt{0.0330} = 0.1817 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
l_5 &= \sqrt{(0.080)^2 + (-0.080)^2 + (0.070)^2} = \sqrt{0.0153} = 0.1237 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step D — Compute $\bar{l}$ and $\sigma_l$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{l} &= \frac{0.1732 + 0.2285 + 0.0436 + 0.1817 + 0.1237}{5} = 0.1501 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\sigma_l &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\left[(0.0231)^2 + (0.0784)^2 + (-0.1065)^2 + (0.0316)^2 + (-0.0264)^2\right]} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.000534 + 0.006147 + 0.011342 + 0.000999 + 0.000697}{4}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.019719}{4}} = \sqrt{0.004930} = 0.0702 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step E — Apply the three-sigma rule:

$$
RP_l = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l = 0.1501 + 3(0.0702) = 0.1501 + 0.2106 = 0.3607 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer (with units, as KTU examiners expect):**
- $AP \approx 0.062$ mm
- $RP_l \approx 0.361$ mm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for the Board Exam**: The robot is *accurate* (centroid within 0.062 mm of target) but has *moderate repeatability* (scatter band of 0.36 mm). Such a robot is suitable for **laser cutting** (where accuracy is critical) but *marginal* for **micro-assembly**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Metric

| Application Domain | Critical Metric | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Spot welding | Repeatability | Robot re-touches the same seam many times |
| Arc welding | Path accuracy | Continuous seam conformance to CAD |
| Pick-and-place | Repeatability + Resolution | High-speed discrete targeting |
| Painting / Coating | Path accuracy | Uniform coverage along a curve |
| Assembly (tolerance 0.1 mm) | Both | Tight fit demands both metrics |
| Inspection (CMM-like) | Accuracy | The measurement *is* the part location |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Precision vs Accuracy — The Dartboard Diagram (ASCII)

```
       Y (Accuracy axis)
       |
   +Y  |      ACCURATE + PRECISE            INACCURATE + PRECISE
       |         (10,10)(10,10)(10,10)         (20,20)(20,20)(20,20)
       |             +                          +
       |                                      (Target at 10,10)
       |
   ----+---------------------------------------> X (Precision axis)
       |
       |      INACCURATE + IMPRECISE         ACCURATE + IMPRECISE
       |        (0,0)(0,0)(0,0)                (15,5)(12,8)(8,15)
       |             +                              +
       |                                      (Target at 10,10)
       |
```

**Legend (KTU Board-Standard):**
- $(+)$ at $(10,10)$ → Commanded target point.
- Each $(x,y)$ triplet → One trial's attained point.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Accuracy Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Commanded Pose P_c] --> B[Trajectory Planner]
    B --> C[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    C --> D[Joint Angle Set theta_i]
    D --> E[Joint Servo Controllers]
    E --> F[Encoder Feedback]
    F --> G[Closed-Loop Position Regulator]
    G --> H[Actual Joint Motion]
    H --> I[Link Compliance and Backlash]
    I --> J[End Effector Pose P_actual]
    J --> K[Statistical Processor]
    K --> L[Mean Centroid]
    K --> M[Variance Sigma Squared]
    L --> N[Compute Accuracy AP]
    M --> O[Compute Repeatability RP]
    N --> P[Performance Report]
    O --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Each block contributes a specific *error budget*. The trajectory planner adds quantisation error, inverse kinematics adds numerical error, servos add lag, encoders add quantisation, and link compliance adds elastic deformation. The final $AP$ and $RP$ are the *statistical aggregates* of all these sub-errors.

## 4.3 Resolution Cascade — From Bit to Micron

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Encoder Resolution n bits] --> B[Control Resolution r_c in deg]
    B --> C[Jacobian J of theta]
    C --> D[Spatial Resolution r_s at end effector in mm]
    D --> E[Programming Resolution r_p in user units]
    E --> F[Effective System Resolution r_eff]
    F --> G[Lower Bound on Accuracy and Repeatability]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight**: The *effective* resolution $r_{\text{eff}}$ is the **maximum** of all three ($r_c, r_s, r_p$). It is a *theoretical floor* below which no metric can ever improve — even with perfect mechanics.

## 4.4 ISO 9283 Test Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Test Cube in Workspace] --> B[Select 5 Poses in Cube]
    B --> C[For each pose, perform N cycles]
    C --> D[Record N attained positions]
    D --> E[Compute centroid for each pose]
    E --> F[Compute accuracy per pose]
    E --> G[Compute repeatability per pose]
    F --> H[Average over 5 poses = AP]
    G --> I[Average over 5 poses = RP]
    H --> J[Final ISO 9283 Report]
    I --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. (3 Marks) — Define Accuracy and Precision in the context of industrial robots. How are they quantified under ISO 9283?

**Model Answer:**
- **Accuracy** is the ability of a robot to position its end-effector at the *commanded (programmed)* target point. Under ISO 9283, it is quantified as the **distance between the commanded pose and the mean of the attained poses** after $N$ trials.
$$AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2 + (\bar{z} - z_c)^2}$$
- **Precision (Repeatability)** is the ability of the robot to *consistently return* to the same position. It is quantified as the **radius of the sphere that contains $99.73\%$ ($3\sigma$ bound) of the attained positions** about their centroid.
$$RP = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l$$

### Q2. (3 Marks) — Differentiate between Spatial Resolution, Control Resolution, and Programming Resolution.

**Model Answer (in tabular form for clarity):**

| Type | Definition | Determined By |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Resolution** | Smallest joint motion the controller can command | Number of encoder bits ($r_c = 360/2^n$) |
| **Spatial Resolution** | Corresponding linear motion at the end-effector | Jacobian $J(\theta)$ multiplied by control resolution |
| **Programming Resolution** | Smallest increment the *programmer* can specify | Teach pendant / language granularity |

The **effective resolution** is the largest of the three; it sets the theoretical lower bound on accuracy and repeatability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 4]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — *(Choose A OR B)*

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain in detail the **ISO 9283 standard** for testing robotic performance. List the **five pose selection criteria** and **three cycles** required.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A robot is tested for position accuracy. The commanded point is $(1000.00,\, 500.00,\, 250.00)$ mm. The 5 attained positions are: $(999.85, 500.10, 249.92)$, $(1000.10, 499.95, 250.05)$, $(999.95, 500.05, 250.10)$, $(1000.05, 499.90, 249.95)$, $(999.90, 500.10, 250.05)$ mm. Calculate the **Position Accuracy ($AP$)** and the **Repeatability ($RP$)** at $3\sigma$ level.

#### Model Solution for A(a):

ISO 9283 — *Manipulating industrial robots – Performance criteria and related test methods* — is the international benchmark.

**Pose Selection Criteria (5 conditions):**
1. The test cube must lie entirely within the robot's rated workspace.
2. One pose must be at the **centre of the workspace**.
3. Four additional poses must be on the **diagonals** of the cube (one per quadrant).
4. The cube should occupy at least $10\%$ of the workspace.
5. Poses must avoid singularities and joint limits (at least $5\%$ margin).

**Test Cycle Requirements (3 cycles):**
1. **Cycle 1** — Each pose is approached *from the same direction* (single-direction approach).
2. **Cycle 2** — Each pose is approached from *different directions* (multi-directional).
3. **Cycle 3** — Pose *stability over time* is measured (drift test).

**Each cycle** typically requires $N = 30$ measurements. The reported values are the **mean** of the metric across all 5 poses.

#### Model Solution for A(b):

**Step 1 — Centroid Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{999.85 + 1000.10 + 999.95 + 1000.05 + 999.90}{5} = 999.970 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\bar{y} &= \frac{500.10 + 499.95 + 500.05 + 499.90 + 500.10}{5} = 500.020 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\bar{z} &= \frac{249.92 + 250.05 + 250.10 + 249.95 + 250.05}{5} = 250.014 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Position Accuracy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP &= \sqrt{(999.970 - 1000.00)^2 + (500.020 - 500.00)^2 + (250.014 - 250.00)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{(-0.030)^2 + (0.020)^2 + (0.014)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{0.0009 + 0.0004 + 0.000196} = \sqrt{0.001496} = 0.0387 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $l_i$ from the centroid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_1 &= \sqrt{(-0.120)^2 + (0.080)^2 + (-0.094)^2} = \sqrt{0.0280} = 0.1673 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
l_2 &= \sqrt{(0.130)^2 + (-0.070)^2 + (0.036)^2} = \sqrt{0.0222} = 0.1490 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
l_3 &= \sqrt{(-0.020)^2 + (0.030)^2 + (0.086)^2} = \sqrt{0.0084} = 0.0916 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
l_4 &= \sqrt{(0.080)^2 + (-0.120)^2 + (-0.064)^2} = \sqrt{0.0226} = 0.1503 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
l_5 &= \sqrt{(-0.070)^2 + (0.080)^2 + (0.036)^2} = \sqrt{0.0114} = 0.1066 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mean and Standard Deviation of $l_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{l} &= \frac{0.1673 + 0.1490 + 0.0916 + 0.1503 + 0.1066}{5} = 0.1330 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\sigma_l &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\left[(0.0343)^2 + (0.0160)^2 + (-0.0414)^2 + (0.0173)^2 + (-0.0264)^2\right]} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.001176 + 0.000256 + 0.001714 + 0.000299 + 0.000697}{4}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.004142}{4}} = \sqrt{0.001036} = 0.0322 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Three-Sigma Repeatability:**

$$
RP = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l = 0.1330 + 3(0.0322) = 0.1330 + 0.0966 = 0.2296 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer:** $AP \approx 0.039 \text{ mm}, \quad RP \approx 0.230 \text{ mm}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Discuss the **sources of errors** affecting the accuracy of an industrial robot. How do **geometric** and **non-geometric** errors differ?

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* With a neat diagram, explain the **concept of Resolution** in robotic systems. A robot's 5th joint uses a **16-bit encoder**. Compute the **control resolution** and, given a Jacobian magnitude of $0.5$ mm per degree at the current configuration, the **spatial resolution** at the end-effector.

#### Model Solution for B(a):

Errors are categorised into three groups:

**1. Geometric Errors** — caused by manufacturing tolerances.
- Link length deviations from nominal DH parameters.
- Misalignment of joint axes (offset, twist).
- Tool centre point (TCP) calibration error.
- *Mitigation*: Closed-loop calibration using laser tracker / vision system.

**2. Non-Geometric Errors** — caused by physical phenomena.
- *Joint compliance*: Harmonic drives wind up under torque.
- *Link deflection*: Cantilever bending of slender links.
- *Thermal expansion*: Ball-screws expand $\sim 12 \;\mu\text{m/m/}^\circ\text{C}$.
- *Gear backlash*: Reversal hysteresis.
- *Mitigation*: Stiffer materials, thermal compensation tables.

**3. Control & Sensor Errors**.
- Quantisation in encoders (resolution limit).
- Servo lag and steady-state error.
- Quantisation noise in ADC channels.
- *Mitigation*: Higher encoder bits, feed-forward control, observer-based state estimation.

**Key Difference:**

| Geometric | Non-Geometric |
|---|---|
| Time-invariant | Time-varying (load, temperature) |
| Identifiable via kinematic calibration | Requires dynamic / thermal models |
| Constant offset | Drift and history-dependent |

#### Model Solution for B(b):

**Resolution Concept Diagram (Textual):**

```
  Joint Encoder  --> Control Resolution r_c (degrees/pulse)
       |
       v
  Multiply by Jacobian |J|
       |
       v
  Spatial Resolution r_s (mm/pulse) at end-effector
       |
       v
  Compare with Programming Resolution r_p (set by user)
       |
       v
  Effective Resolution r_eff = max(r_c, r_s, r_p)
```

**Step 1 — Control Resolution from 16-bit Encoder:**

For a 16-bit encoder, $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ discrete steps over $360^\circ$.

$$
r_c = \frac{360^\circ}{2^{16}} = \frac{360}{65{,}536} = 0.005493^\circ/\text{step}
$$

Converting to radians (for engineering completeness):

$$
r_c = 0.005493 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 9.59 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/step}
$$

**Step 2 — Spatial Resolution at End-Effector:**

Given Jacobian magnitude $\vert J \vert = 0.5$ mm/deg:

$$
r_s = r_c \cdot \vert J \vert = 0.005493^\circ \times 0.5 \text{ mm/deg} = 0.002747 \text{ mm/step}
$$

Converting to microns for industrial readability:

$$
r_s = 0.002747 \text{ mm} = 2.747 \;\mu\text{m/step}
$$

**Final Answer:** $r_c = 0.005493^\circ/\text{step}$ and $r_s \approx 2.75 \;\mu\text{m/step}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing accuracy with precision** — students often *define them as the same*. Always state the **distance-to-target** (accuracy) vs **scatter-of-trail** (repeatability) distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the $3\sigma$ rule** — quoting $\bar{l}$ alone is *not* the repeatability index. ISO 9283 mandates $\bar{l} + 3\sigma_l$.
> 3. **Using sample variance with $N$ instead of $N-1$** — loses 1 mark in numericals.
> 4. **Ignoring units** — always quote accuracy and repeatability in **mm** (or $\mu$m for micro-robotics).
> 5. **Conflating control resolution and spatial resolution** — they are related by the Jacobian, *not* equal.
> 6. **Missing the Jacobian in $r_s$ calculation** — a frequently tested 7-mark question; ensure you write $r_s = r_c \cdot \vert J \vert$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Accuracy** = proximity to the *commanded* target — measured as deviation of the **centroid** from the command.
- **Precision / Repeatability** = proximity among *successive attempts* — measured as **$3\sigma$ scatter** about the centroid.
- **Resolution** = smallest *commandable* increment; sets the theoretical floor.
- **ISO 9283** mandates $N = 30$ trials, 5 poses (centre + 4 corners), and the **3-sigma confidence** criterion.
- **Accuracy** is dominated by *systematic* errors (kinematic calibration can fix it).
- **Repeatability** is dominated by *random* errors (mechanical stiffness, encoder quality fix it).
- **Spatial resolution** = control resolution $\times \vert$Jacobian$\vert$.
- **Effective resolution** = max(control, spatial, programming).
- For KTU numerics, always quote: **mean $\bar{x}$**, **standard deviation $\sigma$**, **$AP$**, and **$RP$** with **correct units (mm)**.
- Three-sigma ($3\sigma$) corresponds to **99.73% confidence interval** in a normal distribution.
- Use **$N-1$ in the denominator** of sample variance (Bessel's correction).
- Industrial robots typically have $RP \in [0.02, 0.5]$ mm and $AP \in [1, 10]$ mm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line memory hook (for KTU vivas)**:
> *"Accuracy is **where you mean to go**; Precision is **how reliably you get there**."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT612 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operating Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBRT612 – Fundamentals of Robotics, Module 4)**, the **Operating Environment** of a robot refers to the complete set of physical, geometrical, sensory, and operational conditions under which a robotic system is designed, deployed, and expected to function. It encapsulates the workspace characteristics, ambient conditions (temperature, pressure, humidity, radiation), terrain, presence of humans, type of task, and the degree of structure available in the surroundings.

According to the standard taxonomy used in KTU-aligned robotics literature, the operating environment is classified along three primary axes:

1. **Degree of Structure** → Structured, Semi-Structured, Unstructured
2. **Medium of Operation** → Terrestrial, Aerial, Underwater, Space
3. **Hazard Level** → Safe (collaborative), Industrial, Hazardous, Extreme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Operating Environment is the *first* topic in Module 4 and acts as the foundation for the subsequent sub-modules on **Sensors, Machine Vision, and Robot Programming Languages**. A clear understanding of *how environment dictates sensor choice* is critical for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

Imagine a **Swiss Army Knife** versus a **specialized surgical scalpel**:
- A Swiss Army Knife works in many environments (camping, office, kitchen) — it is **general-purpose** and operates in a **semi-structured to unstructured** environment, much like a **mobile service robot** in a hospital or warehouse.
- A scalpel, on the other hand, is *highly specialized* for one specific environment (the operating theatre with controlled temperature, sterile air, predictable lighting) — analogous to a **SCARA robot on an assembly line** operating in a **highly structured environment**.

So, the *operating environment* is the **"stage"** on which the robot performs — the more predictable the stage, the simpler the robot's brain (controller) can be; the more chaotic the stage, the smarter the robot must be.

### 1.3 Classification of Robot Operating Environments

| Classification Axis | Category | Defining Property | Example Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Structured | Fixed positions, known dimensions, no surprises | Automotive welding line, PCB assembly |
| **Structure** | Semi-Structured | Mostly known, minor variations | Palletizing, warehouse picking |
| **Structure** | Unstructured | Highly dynamic, unknown obstacles | Search & rescue, planetary exploration |
| **Medium** | Terrestrial | Floor-based, ground reaction available | AGVs, humanoid robots |
| **Medium** | Aerial | 3-D free space, no support | Drones (UAVs), surveillance |
| **Medium** | Underwater | Hydrostatic pressure, low visibility | ROVs, AUVs (subsea cable repair) |
| **Medium** | Space | Vacuum, extreme temperature, micro-gravity | Canadarm, Mars rovers |
| **Hazard** | Collaborative (Cobot) | Human-robot shared workspace | Cobots in assembly assistance |
| **Hazard** | Industrial | Isolated from humans, heavy payloads | Welding, painting robots |
| **Hazard** | Hazardous | Toxic, radioactive, explosive | Nuclear cleanup, bomb disposal |
| **Hazard** | Extreme | Beyond human reach/tolerance | Deep sea, polar, extraterrestrial |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor:** Think **S-S-H** — *Structure, Surrounding medium, Hazard level*. KTU examiners love to ask "Classify the operating environment for a given application" (typically a 3-mark question in Part A).

### 1.4 Key Environmental Parameters Affecting Robot Design

The operating environment dictates several critical design decisions. The following parameters directly influence **sensor selection, material choice, and control architecture**:

- **Temperature Range:** Standard industrial robots operate between **0°C to 50°C**; space-rated systems handle **−150°C to +150°C**.
- **Humidity & Moisture:** Affects electrical enclosures (IP rating — Ingress Protection).
- **Dust & Particulate Density:** Determines sealing requirements (IP65, IP67, IP68).
- **Vibration & Shock:** Important in mobile and aerospace platforms.
- **Electromagnetic Interference (EMI):** Can corrupt sensor signals.
- **Lighting Conditions:** Critical for vision-guided robots.
- **Communication Bandwidth:** Tethered vs. wireless operation in remote environments.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Exam Writing:** Always begin a Part B answer on Operating Environment with a *classification table* and then map the application to the right class. This earns you the **first 2–3 marks of structured presentation** that examiners reward.

### 1.5 Why "Operating Environment" Is Taught Early in Module 4

The placement of this topic in **Module 4** is pedagogically deliberate. Before learning *how* a robot sees (Machine Vision) or *how* it is programmed, the student must understand *what conditions the robot is expected to cope with*. This is the *reverse engineering* approach KTU 2024 Scheme follows:

$$\text{Environment} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Sensors Required} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Programming Paradigm}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

The operating environment of a robot is not a single property — it is a **multi-dimensional vector** of attributes. To design (or analyse) a robot for a given environment, the engineer must work through the following logical sequence:

#### Step 1 — Identify the Workspace Topology
- **Fixed-base vs Mobile:** Does the robot have a stationary base (manipulator) or move freely (mobile robot, drone, AUV)?
- **Degrees of Freedom Required:** A 6-DOF arm is suitable for a structured 3-D workspace; a snake robot may be needed for unstructured search tasks.
- **Reachability Volume:** The reachable workspace $W_r$ must envelop the task volume $W_t$.

$$\begin{aligned}
W_r \supseteq W_t \quad \text{(Reachability Constraint)}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 2 — Quantify the Degree of Structure
The **Structure Index (S)** is a conceptual metric that grades environment predictability on a scale of 0 to 1:

$$\begin{aligned}
S = 1 - \frac{\sigma_{\text{observed}}}{\sigma_{\text{max}}}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\sigma_{\text{observed}}$ is the standard deviation of measured object positions, and $\sigma_{\text{max}}$ is the maximum allowable deviation. A value of **S = 1** indicates a fully structured environment, while **S ≈ 0** indicates a chaotic one.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations, you will not be asked to compute $S$, but you may be asked to *justify* why a particular sensor suite suits a particular $S$ value. Understanding the concept is the key.

#### Step 3 — Select Sensors Based on Environment
The **sensor–environment compatibility matrix** is a critical KTU expectation:

| Environment | Recommended Primary Sensor | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Structured (assembly line) | Limit switches, encoders, photoelectric | High precision, low cost |
| Semi-Structured (warehouse) | LiDAR, depth cameras | Need 3-D mapping |
| Unstructured (outdoor) | Stereo vision, GPS, IMU, radar | Robust to lighting & weather |
| Underwater | Sonar, pressure sensors | Light attenuates in water |
| Space | Star trackers, IMU, sun sensors | No GPS, vacuum constraints |
| Hazardous (nuclear) | Radiation-hardened cameras, force/torque | Remote, radiation tolerant |

#### Step 4 — Choose Materials and Enclosures (IP Rating)
The **Ingress Protection (IP) rating** is the international standard (IEC 60529) defining sealing effectiveness:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IP} \; XY \quad \text{where} \quad X &= \text{solid particle protection (0–6)} \\
Y &= \text{liquid ingress protection (0–9)}
\end{aligned}$$

- **IP65:** Dust-tight, protected against water jets (typical industrial robot).
- **IP67:** Dust-tight, protected against temporary immersion (food-grade robots).
- **IP68:** Dust-tight, protected against continuous immersion (underwater ROVs).

#### Step 5 — Plan for Human–Robot Interaction
Modern operating environments are increasingly **collaborative** (Industry 5.0). KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly recognizes:

- **Coexistence:** Human and robot share space but not task.
- **Cooperation:** Human and robot work on the same task at different times.
- **Collaboration:** Human and robot work on the same task simultaneously (cobot territory).

The choice of operating environment directly determines the **safety integrity level (SIL)** and the **power-and-force-limiting (PFL)** requirements as per **ISO/TS 15066**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reachability Constraint | $W_r \supseteq W_t$ | Geometric containment |
| 2 | Structure Index | $S = 1 - \sigma_{\text{obs}} / \sigma_{\text{max}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le S \le 1$ |
| 3 | Workspace Volume (Spherical) | $V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$ | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 4 | Workspace Volume (Cylindrical) | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 5 | Underwater Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Pa (Pascal) |
| 6 | Hydrostatic Pressure (numerical) | $P \approx P_0 + 0.098\, h$ | d-bar per metre depth |
| 7 | Reynolds Number (Underwater Flow) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Snell's Law (Underwater Vision) | $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ | Refraction index matching |
| 9 | IP Rating Form | $IP\; XY$ | $X \in [0,6],\; Y \in [0,9]$ |
| 10 | Free Space Path Loss (Aerial Comms) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB (d in km, f in MHz) |
| 11 | Blackbody Radiation (Space) | $E = \sigma T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ |
| 12 | Stall Torque (Mobile Base) | $\tau_s \ge \dfrac{m g r \sin\alpha}{\eta}$ | N·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Allocation Tip:** The hydrostatic pressure formula $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ is *favourite KTU territory* for numerical sub-parts in Module 4 questions on underwater environments. Memorize it.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Automation:** The structured environment of car assembly lines allows fixed-path robots (PTP, CP) with simple sensors — drastically reducing cost.
- **Defence & Security:** Unstructured outdoor terrain demands SLAM-enabled mobile robots with multi-sensor fusion (LiDAR + IMU + GPS).
- **Healthcare:** Surgical robots operate in highly structured, sterile environments but require extreme precision — the Da Vinci system operates with a workspace tolerance of **sub-millimetre accuracy**.
- **Space Exploration:** The Mars rovers (Perseverance, Curiosity) operate in arguably the *most unstructured environment* ever attempted — driving the need for AI-driven autonomy.
- **Agriculture:** Semi-structured outdoor environment with variable lighting, terrain, and crop geometry — driving the rise of agricultural robotics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure on an Underwater Robot

This is a KTU-favourite numerical sub-question. We will derive the pressure acting on an underwater ROV operating at a depth of 1500 m, and assess its design implications.

**Given:**
- Sea-water density: $\rho = 1025 \;\text{kg/m}^3$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$
- Atmospheric pressure at surface: $P_0 = 101325 \;\text{Pa}$
- Depth: $h = 1500 \;\text{m}$

**Step 1 — State the governing equation.**

The hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ below a free surface is the sum of the atmospheric pressure pushing down on the surface and the pressure exerted by the column of fluid of height $h$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(h) = P_0 + \rho g h
\end{aligned}$$

This is derived from the hydrostatic equilibrium of an infinitesimal fluid element, where the net force per unit area must vanish in the absence of motion. The pressure gradient with depth is $\dfrac{dP}{dh} = \rho g$, and integrating from $0$ to $h$ gives the linear relation above.

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(1500) &= 101325 + (1025)(9.81)(1500)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the hydrostatic term.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho g h &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1500 \\
        &= 1025 \times 14715 \\
        &= 15{,}082{,}875 \;\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Add the atmospheric pressure.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(1500) &= 101{,}325 + 15{,}082{,}875 \\
        &= 15{,}184{,}200 \;\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to engineering-friendly units.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(1500) &\approx 15.18 \;\text{MPa} \\
        &\approx 151.8 \;\text{bar} \\
        &\approx 150 \;\text{atm}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** A pressure of **150 atm** is enormous. The ROV's hull must be a thick-walled pressure vessel, typically made of titanium or high-grade aluminium alloy. Standard electronics would be crushed; hence the use of pressure-tolerant oil-filled housings.

**Step 6 — Verify using the engineering rule of thumb.**

The empirical rule of thumb is **1 d-bar per metre of depth**:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{approx}} &\approx 0.1 \;\text{bar/m} \times 1500 \;\text{m} = 150 \;\text{bar} = 15 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches our derived value to within 1%, confirming correctness.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Workspace Volume of a Spherical Robot

A spherical underwater robot (e.g., an autonomous profiling float) has a radius $r = 0.4\;\text{m}$. Compute its workspace volume.

**Step 1 — Apply the volume formula for a sphere.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $r = 0.4\;\text{m}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{4}{3} \pi (0.4)^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cube.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.4)^3 &= 0.064 \;\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by constants.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{4}{3} \times 3.14159 \times 0.064 \\
  &= 1.3333 \times 3.14159 \times 0.064 \\
  &= 4.1888 \times 0.064 \\
  &= 0.268 \;\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Express in litres for intuition.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= 0.268 \times 1000 = 268 \;\text{litres}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Use:** If a question asks "why is workspace volume a critical operating environment parameter?", answer: *"The robot's swept volume must not collide with obstacles; in cluttered or unstructured environments, smaller swept volumes are preferred for safety and maneuverability."*

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Environment-to-Design Mapping

| Environment Class | Workspace | Sensor Suite | Material | Power | Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Structured Industrial** | Fixed, bounded | Limit switches, encoders | Mild steel, aluminium | Mains AC | PLC, PID |
| **Semi-Structured Warehouse** | Floor, aisles | LiDAR, wheel encoders | Powder-coated steel | Battery (Li-ion) | SLAM |
| **Unstructured Outdoor** | Open, variable | GPS, IMU, stereo camera | Weatherproof polymer | Solar + Battery | AI/ML autonomy |
| **Aerial (UAV)** | 3-D free space | Barometer, GPS, IMU | Carbon fibre | Li-Po battery | PID + state machine |
| **Underwater (ROV)** | 3-D fluid | Sonar, pressure, camera | Titanium hull | Tethered or battery | Tethered joystick |
| **Space** | Vacuum, micro-g | Sun sensor, star tracker, IMU | Aluminium honeycomb | Solar panels | AI with redundancy |
| **Hazardous (Nuclear)** | Radioactive | Radiation-hard cam, force | Lead-shielded | Tethered | Remote teleop |
| **Collaborative (Cobot)** | Human-shared | Torque, vision | Compliant polymer | Low voltage DC | PFL + ISO/TS 15066 |

### 3.4 Common Numerical Scenarios for KTU

| Scenario | Governing Equation | Typical KTU Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure on underwater hull | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | 3–4 |
| Workspace volume of arm | $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ or $\pi r^2 h$ | 2–3 |
| IP rating selection | $\text{IP}\; XY$ justification | 2 |
| Structure Index justification | $S = 1 - \sigma_{\text{obs}}/\sigma_{\text{max}}$ | 3 |
| Stall torque for mobile base | $\tau_s = \frac{mgr\sin\alpha}{\eta}$ | 4 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Robot Operating Environment — Hierarchical Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROBOT OPERATING ENVIRONMENT] --> B[STRUCTURE AXIS]
    A --> C[MEDIUM AXIS]
    A --> D[HAZARD AXIS]

    B --> B1[Structured]
    B --> B2[Semi-Structured]
    B --> B3[Unstructured]

    C --> C1[Terrestrial]
    C --> C2[Aerial UAV]
    C --> C3[Underwater AUV ROV]
    C --> C4[Space]

    D --> D1[Collaborative Cobot]
    D --> D2[Industrial Isolated]
    D --> D3[Hazardous Nuclear Bomb]
    D --> D4[Extreme Deep Space Polar]

    B1 --> EX1[Example: Car Assembly Welding]
    B2 --> EX2[Example: Warehouse Palletizing]
    B3 --> EX3[Example: Search and Rescue]
    C1 --> EX4[Example: AGV in Factory]
    C2 --> EX5[Example: Drone Surveillance]
    C3 --> EX6[Example: Subsea Cable Repair]
    C4 --> EX7[Example: Mars Rover]
    D1 --> EX8[Example: UR10 Assembly Assist]
    D2 --> EX9[Example: Paint Robot Booth]
    D3 --> EX10[Example: Fukushima Cleanup]
    D4 --> EX11[Example: Cryobot on Europa]
```

### 4.2 Sensor–Environment Decision Flowchart (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Application] --> B{Medium of Operation}
    B -->|Air Terrestrial| C{Structure Level}
    B -->|Water| D[Use Sonar and Pressure Sensor]
    B -->|Air Aerial| E[Use GPS IMU Barometer]
    B -->|Vacuum Space| F[Use Star Tracker Sun Sensor IMU]

    C -->|Structured| G[Use Limit Switch Encoder]
    C -->|Semi Structured| H[Use LiDAR Depth Camera]
    C -->|Unstructured| I[Use Stereo Vision Radar SLAM]

    D --> J[Add Underwater Vision Correction]
    E --> K[Add Wind Vibration Filter]
    F --> L[Add Radiation Hardening]

    G --> M[Select IP54 Enclosure]
    H --> N[Select IP65 Enclosure]
    I --> O[Select IP67 Enclosure]
    J --> P[Select IP68 Pressure Hull]
    K --> Q[Select IP54 with Vibration Mounts]
    L --> R[Select Vacuum Rated Hermetic Seal]

    M --> S[Final Robot Specification]
    N --> S
    O --> S
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
```

### 4.3 Human–Robot Operating Environment Continuum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[No Human Presence] --> B[Coexistence]
    B --> C[Cooperation]
    C --> D[Collaboration]

    A --> A1[Industrial Robot Cage]
    B --> B1[Warehouse AGV + Human Picker]
    C --> C1[Sequential Assembly Line]
    D --> D1[Cobot Lifting with Human]

    A1 -. Power: 50 kW .-> A
    B1 -. Power: 5 kW .-> B
    C1 -. Power: 2 kW .-> C
    D1 -. Power: 0.5 kW + PFL .-> D
```

### 4.4 Design Cascade: From Environment to Sensor to Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[Operating Environment] --> P[Environment Parameters]
    P --> P1[Temperature]
    P --> P2[Pressure]
    P --> P3[Lighting]
    P --> P4[Obstacles]
    P --> P5[Hazard Level]

    P1 --> M1[Material Selection]
    P2 --> M2[Hull Enclosure]
    P3 --> M3[Camera or Sonar]
    P4 --> M4[Mapping SLAM]
    P5 --> M5[Safety Compliance]

    M1 --> R[Robot Hardware Spec]
    M2 --> R
    M3 --> S[Sensor Suite]
    M4 --> S
    M5 --> C[Control Architecture]
    S --> C
    R --> C
    C --> X[Final Robot Design for Environment]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The block diagrams above are *architecture-level abstractions*. In your KTU answer scripts, you may reproduce a simplified version (3–4 boxes) of any one of these flowcharts to earn the **"neat diagram" presentation mark** (typically 1 mark).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Operating Environment" of a robot. List any four environmental parameters that affect robot design.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **operating environment** of a robot is the complete set of physical, geometrical, sensory, and operational conditions (workspace, temperature, medium, hazard level, structure) under which a robot is designed to function.

Four key environmental parameters affecting robot design are:

1. **Temperature Range** — dictates material and electronics selection.
2. **Humidity / Dust / Moisture** — dictates IP rating of enclosures.
3. **Lighting Conditions** — dictates camera/vision sensor selection.
4. **Hazard Level (toxic, radioactive, explosive)** — dictates safety compliance and remote operation.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for parameters; tidy presentation = 1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Classify robot operating environments based on the **degree of structure**. Give one example for each class.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Based on the degree of predictability, robot operating environments are classified into:

| Class | Property | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Structured** | Fixed, known, predictable | Automotive welding line (ABB IRB series) |
| **Semi-Structured** | Mostly known, minor variation | Warehouse palletizing (Kiva robots) |
| **Unstructured** | Dynamic, unknown, chaotic | Search & rescue in collapsed buildings |

*(1 mark per class with example = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a)** Classify robot operating environments along the three axes: *degree of structure*, *medium of operation*, and *hazard level*. For each class, give one real-world robotic application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how the operating environment influences the **sensor selection** for a robot. Support your answer with two detailed examples — one for an **underwater** robot and one for a **space** robot. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The classification is presented as a structured 3 × 4 table (as shown in Section 1.3 of these notes). For full marks, the student must:

- Mention all three axes explicitly.
- Provide at least one *real, named* application (not a generic example).
- Indicate the design implication of each class.

| Axis | Class 1 | Class 2 | Class 3 | Class 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Structure | Structured (Toyota welding) | Semi-Structured (Amazon Kiva) | Unstructured (DARPA SubT) | — |
| Medium | Terrestrial (ASIMO) | Aerial (Phantom UAV) | Underwater ( Alvin ROV) | Space (Canadarm2) |
| Hazard | Collaborative (UR5) | Industrial (KUKA KR16) | Hazardous (PackBot EOD) | Extreme (Mars Rover) |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (drawn in answer script):**

$$\boxed{\text{Environment} \longrightarrow \text{Parameters} \longrightarrow \text{Sensor Choice} \longrightarrow \text{Robot Design}}$$

**Example 1: Underwater Robot (3–4 marks)**
- The underwater medium attenuates light within metres and absorbs radio waves.
- Therefore, **sonar (acoustic) sensors** are used for long-range mapping, and **pressure sensors** for depth estimation.
- Cameras (if used) need a **waterproof housing with a flat optical port** corrected for Snell's law refraction.
- Example: **Remus 6000 AUV** uses side-scan sonar + DVL (Doppler Velocity Log) for seabed mapping at 6000 m depth.

**Example 2: Space Robot (3–4 marks)**
- The space environment has **no GPS, no air, extreme temperature swings, and vacuum**.
- Sensors used: **star trackers** (attitude), **sun sensors** (orientation), **IMUs** (inertial), and **LIDAR** (for asteroid sample collection like OSIRIS-REx).
- Example: **Canadarm2** on the ISS uses force/torque sensors at the end-effector and vision sensors for berthing visiting spacecraft.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**(a)** Explain the concept of **Ingress Protection (IP) rating** for a robot. A robot is to be deployed in a **food processing plant** where it is exposed to high-pressure hot water jets and airborne flour dust. Justify the appropriate IP rating for the robot. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) operates at a depth of **2000 m** in seawater. Calculate the hydrostatic pressure on its hull. Given: $\rho = 1025 \;\text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$, $P_0 = 101.3 \;\text{kPa}$. Comment on the design implications. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**IP Rating Definition (2 marks):**
The IP rating (per IEC 60529) classifies the degree of protection an enclosure provides against intrusion of solid particles (first digit) and liquids (second digit). The format is **IP XY** where $X \in [0,6]$ and $Y \in [0,9]$.

**Justification for Food-Processing Robot (5 marks):**
- **Flour dust** requires complete dust-tight protection → first digit must be **6**.
- **High-pressure hot water jets** require protection against powerful water jets from any direction → second digit must be at least **5**, but for *high-pressure* jets and washdown environments, **6** is preferred.
- Therefore, the appropriate IP rating is **IP66** (dust-tight + protected against powerful water jets).
- For added safety against temporary immersion during floor washdowns, **IP67** may be specified.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Write the hydrostatic pressure equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(h) = P_0 + \rho g h
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(2000) &= 101300 + (1025)(9.81)(2000)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho g h &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2000 = 20{,}110{,}500 \;\text{Pa} \\
P(2000) &= 101{,}300 + 20{,}110{,}500 = 20{,}211{,}800 \;\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &\approx 20.21 \;\text{MPa} \approx 202.1 \;\text{bar} \approx 200 \;\text{atm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Design Implications (2–3 marks):**

- The hull must be a **thick-walled pressure vessel**, typically built from **titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4V)** or aluminium alloy.
- All penetrations (cables, connectors) must use **pressure-rated feedthroughs**.
- Electronics may be housed in **oil-filled compartments** for pressure tolerance and cooling.
- A safety factor of **1.5 to 2.0** is typically applied, meaning the design pressure is **30–40 MPa**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Unit Error (–1 mark):** Students often forget to convert $P_0$ from kPa to Pa, giving a wildly incorrect final answer.
> 2. **Confusing bar with Pa (–1 mark):** Expressing the final answer only in Pa without conversion to bar or atm is acceptable, but mixing units midway is *not*.
> 3. **Skipping Design Implications (–1 mark):** A 7-mark numerical question expects *engineering commentary*, not just arithmetic. Always close with a design remark.
> 4. **Generic Examples (–1 mark):** Saying "an underwater robot uses sonar" without naming a *real* system (e.g., Remus, Alvin) loses the application-oriented mark.
> 5. **No Diagram (–1 mark):** For 7-mark theory sub-parts, a flow diagram or classification table is *expected* by KTU examiners.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Operating Environment**

- ✅ **Definition:** Operating environment = workspace + ambient conditions + structure + medium + hazard level.
- ✅ **Three Classification Axes:** *Structure, Medium, Hazard.* (Remember: **S-M-H**).
- ✅ **Structure Index $S$:** $S = 1 - \sigma_{\text{obs}} / \sigma_{\text{max}}$. Closer to 1 → structured; closer to 0 → unstructured.
- ✅ **Structured environments** → simple sensors (limit switches, encoders); **unstructured** → multi-sensor fusion (LiDAR, vision, IMU).
- ✅ **IP Rating:** Format `IP XY`; first digit = solids, second = liquids. IP65 is the industrial robot default.
- ✅ **Underwater Pressure:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ (master this; it is the most asked numerical).
- ✅ **Space Environment:** No GPS, no air, vacuum, radiation → star trackers, sun sensors, IMU used.
- ✅ **Aerial Environment:** Wind, vibration, GPS dependency → IMU + GPS + barometer + vibration mounts.
- ✅ **Collaborative Robots (Cobots):** ISO/TS 15066 standard; Power and Force Limiting (PFL) required.
- ✅ **Real-world examples to memorize for KTU:** Kiva (warehouse), Canadarm2 (space), Alvin (underwater), UR5 (cobot), PackBot (hazardous), Mars Rover (extreme).
- ✅ **Reachability Constraint:** $W_r \supseteq W_t$ — always mention this in workspace analysis answers.
- ✅ **Blackbody radiation formula** $E = \sigma T^4$ is a great example to cite when discussing thermal design in space environments.
- ✅ **Free Space Path Loss** $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ — quote it for wireless communication in aerial/space environments.
- ✅ **Always close a long answer with a design implication** — KTU rewards application-oriented thinking, not rote definition.

> **End of Module 4 — Operating Environment Notes**
